Fox Charm In the abandoned garden of Fan Water in Pingle, there were many foxes. There was a man named Ning Sheng, who was reclusive and deeply engrossed in books. During the hot summer months, he felt restless and rested in the garden pavilion. A friend tried to dissuade him, but Ning smiled and said, "What harm is there? Foxes have two ways to charm people: those who are lustful, they charm with beauty; those who are greedy, they charm with wealth. I have no interest in either, only in the books on the shelves. Even if their charms are skillful, they will not work on me." The friend replied indifferently and left. After dinner, Ning lay down under the northern window and saw a woman emerge from behind the screen. He knew she was a fox, so he feigned sleep to observe. The woman pointed to the books on the shelves and smiled, saying, "Within the teachings of propriety, there is a place of joy. This boy is narrow in his learning and has little knowledge; he will die from overstudying." Ning sat up and scolded, "Wild fox! You drag your tail and flee, how dare you speak rashly!" The woman also scolded, "Rustic fool! How can I be wrong? If you truly read and understand, you should know about our family's virtues and achievements. How dare you make false accusations?" Ning replied, "Causing mischief within the city, borrowing the power of a tiger to establish your authority, these are your greatest skills. Where are your family's virtues and achievements?" The woman said, "You read every day, but do you not know about the story of Yu the Great marrying Lady Nüwa of the Tu Mountain clan? Their harmonious union and thriving home symbolize ancestral virtue. During the late Shang dynasty, our family moved to the Western Sea. When King Wen of Zhou was imprisoned in Youli, Shan Yisheng visited our ancestors in their humble dwelling and released the blue phoenix to resolve the matter. These great achievements are recorded in history. How have you not studied them deeply?" Ning said, "Indeed, these are true. But your kind play tricks by lighting lamps and spread debauchery on the bed; you are not good people in the end."

The woman said, "Death is associated with moral concepts, and even great sages admire their benevolence. Certain animals or phenomena can predict the weather. Han scholars respected their wisdom, and when virtue reaches its peak, mythical creatures like the nine-tailed fox appear. The *Shanhai Jing* is a reliable source, clearly stating these facts. You have indeed read but not fully understood them!" Ning pondered for a long time, then respectfully said, "Originally, I considered you all as unworthy and vulgar, but now that I have heard your profound insights, I wish to be your scholarly friend." The woman smiled and agreed. They spent their days together, writing and revising, and in the evening, they would sit by the lotus pavilion, discussing the *Yi Jing*. Suddenly, she asked, "What is the interpretation of the chapter on 'Heaven and Earth'?" Ning replied, "The upper part mentions 'Lì,' which means 'to be close.' When people are close, men and women feel a mutual attraction, and this should be received with 'Xián.' However, 'Xián' cannot be directly received, so the text then discusses the way of husband and wife, leading to 'Héng.'" She laughed and said, "Then is the mutual attraction between men and women something that sages avoid discussing?" Ning answered, "Yes!" She continued, "But if men and women unite, all things are born. What is the explanation for that?" As she spoke, her eyes sparkled and her cheeks turned slightly red. Ning's heart was stirred, and he responded, "Do you have an intention? Let us divine it using the *Yi Jing*."

The woman casually drew the hexagram 'Wei Ji.' Ning said, "Wei Ji signifies misfortune when moving forward; the matter will not succeed." The woman replied, "A small fox wets its tail, though it is not in the right position, the interaction of strong and weak elements is appropriate—what harm is there?" Ning was perplexed by this, and from then on, they lived together. Within half a month, he became spiritually exhausted and physically weak, gradually unable to bear it. A friend visited and questioned him, and Ning tried every means to conceal it. At night, the woman came, and Ning told her about his illness. She said, "You are hardworking in writing, and therefore you gradually become thin and weak. Sima Xiangru often fell ill; how can we know it is not due to the book 'Fengchan'? Otherwise, Sima Xiangru's wife, who had not yet been married, how could she have developed diabetes?" Ning deeply agreed, and thus abandoned his writing tools. He spent each day sitting together with the woman in the same room.

Another full month had passed, and Ning's illness had grown more severe, leaving him lying motionless on his bed. A friend visited again, and Ning gradually revealed the truth. The friend sighed, saying, "This is the best strategy to flatter people. Those who flatter with their beauty see their affection wane when their beauty fades, and those who flatter with money see their relationships severed when their wealth runs out. Only by pretending to be a gentleman and secretly spreading the tricks of a villain, choosing what the other person likes and offering it, do the methods of flattery become more varied, and the poison of flattery become more lasting!" Ning suddenly realized and regretted his actions. The friend quickly called for a sedan chair and carried him back home under the stars at night. The girl also died. After half a year, Ning died of consumption. His will was lost, and it could not be verified later. Duo said, "This is a mirror reflecting the heart of a guest in a noble household. It shatters the beliefs and practices of many people, and it is also a great fault of our kind." Tiger Fool A woman named Huo Xiaoqing from the Qin region had extraordinary beauty. Her father had a dispute over land boundaries with a powerful local official and was falsely accused of other matters by the officials, eventually being killed in prison. The mother cried bitterly, saying, "There are no sons in the family, who will avenge my husband? I fear that my husband's bones will be buried in vain, and he will remain in eternal darkness forever!" The daughter, with tears in her eyes, stepped forward and said, "I am unworthy, still young and inexperienced, but I cannot become a Zhao family girl. If anyone can kill the enemy, I will serve him as a servant." The mother saw her sincerity and prayed to the gods on the western mountains every day.

One day, I heard that someone had entered the city to congratulate the county magistrate on his birthday, but on the road out of the West Mountain, a tiger suddenly appeared in front of him, bit his throat, and killed him. The mother and daughter were just raising their hands in celebration when suddenly another tiger, dragging its tail, came and directly climbed onto the hall. The mother and daughter changed color and retreated. The tiger wandered around and looked around, showing no ill intent. The mother closed the door and spoke, "Wasn't it you who killed him on the road today?" The tiger nodded in agreement. The mother said, "I am grateful to you for your righteousness and avenging my previous grudge. The lonely mother and daughter will certainly worship with incense and flowers to repay your great virtue. I do not know why you have descended your noble feet, but what do you intend to do?" The tiger glared with anger, seeming to hate those who broke their promise. The mother said, "Do you think I have broken my word? The resting place is there, and it would have been appropriate to respectfully serve the young girl with clothing. But our daily lives and eating are different. How could we ever become husband and wife? Besides, I am nearing the end of my life, and our family has no sons or daughters. We rely on the son-in-law for support. You seek vengeance as a ghost, but do you not wish to bestow kindness on the widow? I will express my sincere feelings and ask for your mercy and forgiveness." The tiger, upon hearing her words, seemed dejected and lowered its head as if about to leave, but it hesitated, looking back repeatedly, showing reluctance to go. The daughter, standing firmly, said, "Sir, please stay. I have one thing to say, and I hope you will listen. I previously offered myself to you, and I would never go back on my word. I know that sharing a bed is not possible, and you must also understand that. If you do not forget our old agreement, I will clean a room and live with you for the rest of my life, maintaining the name of husband and wife." The tiger nodded in agreement several times and accepted her proposal gladly.

The woman then led the tiger into the tent and prepared a rabbit fur robe beside the embroidered bed; they ate from the same sacrificial vessel and lived in the same room. Every morning when the woman got up to dress, the tiger would hide itself in a box and peer at her sideways. At night, after the woman undressed and went to bed, the tiger would lie down under the bed, staying awake all night, afraid that its snoring would disturb her clear dreams. Sometimes, when food was not available, the tiger would bring deer jerky to her. If the woman fell ill with a slight ailment, becoming anxious and restless, the tiger would roam inside the room without stopping, constantly present. When she recovered, the tiger would rejoice as before. The woman became accustomed to this. Her mother, being old and without support, often blamed the woman for her poor judgment, and her treatment of the tiger also became less respectful. One night, the tiger suddenly left. The mother wanted to arrange a marriage for the woman. The woman said, "Betraying virtue is inauspicious, and repaying kindness is not fortunate; how much more so, when a woman has given her heart to someone, must she necessarily consider physical form?" She refused steadfastly. Later, the woman died of melancholy and was laid out in the hall. The tiger suddenly howled and wept, tears streaming down, and those who came to view the body all saw it. Then she was buried beside the ancestral tomb, and the tiger visited three times a day. On the four seasonal festivals, the tiger would bring mountain fruits to pay respects. For three years, as if it were one day. The mother, being poor and unable to speak for herself, the tiger still brought mountain deer and wild rabbits to support her family.

铎曰：“有情痴者，必无傲骨。虎而痴，是失其虎性矣。然一言不合，掉头竟去，不依然虎性之难驯乎？痴而能傲，是为真傲，傲而能痴，是为真痴。” 鸡淡 吴郡娄门外鸡坡，吴王收鸡处也，至今居人以养鸡为业。有祝翁者，豢雌雄两头。一夕，闻墙下喧呶不已，怪而听之。 闻雄者曰：“尔我蒙主人豢养，数米而食，凿垣而栖，有何不乐？而胶胶膊膊，终夕絮聒？”雌者曰：“我怪汝喜则颈，惮则断尾，全无一点丈夫气。而犹绛冠金距，骄人昏夜，能不使人气愤？”雄者曰：“夫不雄飞，妻终雌伏。汝何所长，而翘我短处？”雌者曰：“堂上争虫，笼中抱卵，成家之道，舍我其谁？况秦穆公得我而霸诸侯，百里奚仗我而邀富贵。妆惟与宋处宗辈，作窗下清谈，否则沟畔涂膏，镜中学舞。恐曹阿瞒弃之不惜，尚得牛刀一试，冀他年大用也哉？” Duo said, "Those who are emotionally attached or infatuated will lack a sense of pride. If a tiger is infatuated, it has lost its tiger-like qualities. However, if it turns away at a disagreement, isn't it still showing its untrainable nature? Being infatuated yet maintaining pride is true pride, and being proud yet being infatuated is true infatuation." Chicken's Indifference The Chicken Hill outside Lou Gate in Wu County, where King Wu collected chickens, is still today a place where the residents make a living by raising chickens. There was an old man named Zhu who kept a male and female chicken. One evening, he heard a commotion under the wall and was curious, so he listened. The male chicken said, "We are being raised by the master, eating grains, and living in the wall. What is there to be unhappy about? But we are noisy and chattering all night." The female chicken said, "I am annoyed by you because you strangle when happy and cut your tail when afraid, showing no masculine spirit. Yet you still wear red caps and golden spurs, showing off at night, which can't help but make people angry." The male chicken said, "If the husband doesn't fly high, the wife will always crouch low. What is your strength, and why do you highlight my weaknesses?" The female chicken said, "Competing for insects in the hall, hatching eggs in the cage, the way to build a family, who else but me? Consider that Duke Mu of Qin gained dominance over the feudal lords with me, and Bai Li Xi became wealthy and powerful with my help. I only engage in clear talks with people like Song Chuzong, otherwise, I am smeared with grease by the ditch or learn to dance in front of a mirror. I fear that Cao Cao will discard me without hesitation, but I still hope to have a chance to show my worth and be used in the future."

The male bird said, "You are the kind who only knows to stay in the nest and has not seen the strength of a male. My superiority over others lies entirely in my ability to make a startling debut! Zǔ Tì heard of me and took the lead, Yàn Dān followed my example and escaped from a great calamity. As for the official from Qi who was startled by a dream, it helped establish his reputation for wisdom; the ruler of Chu who followed military strategy did not violate military law. Extraordinary achievements and great deeds shine for a thousand years. This is why Tián Ráo, with his timely actions at night, was respected as the best among the five virtues. You, the female, do not lead the dawn; how can you know that I am the supreme male of my generation?" The female bird replied, "If you consider yourself a male, who dares not to act like one? From now on, I will let you speak first." The male bird said, "Only the family will be in distress, I fear that those who wield the knife will follow. A female taking the place of a male does not bring good fortune, but rather invites disaster!" The female bird said, "Do not speak. With my initial action, I will make those who observe signs still seek the female, and all the heroes in the world will step back when they hear of it!" The male bird retreated in a startled manner.

Since then, the female one did not stop calling every night. The family considered it an omen of bad luck and killed and cooked it. The elder Zhuba sighed, saying, "The sound of a hen flying to heaven, how can it last? Especially since its position was not appropriate! It is fitting that it suffered misfortune." The elder Du said, "The *Taixuanjing* says: 'A hen crows at dawn, and a rooster turns its neck.' Is it due to the decline of yang and the prevalence of yin, gradually causing this? I wish for all those in the inner chambers to hold my strong argument and suppress the feminine influence; do not speak softly and humbly, nurturing like a wooden chicken." The Otter's Sacrifice By the great river, there is a spiritual creature, whose name is the otter. One day, it swam along the north bank and met a bird named Zhan, who had gathered on a large rock. They gathered and spoke. Zhan said, "You are good at catching fish, and I am good at catching birds. Birds often flee in fear when they see me, scattering like electricity dispersing stars, so that only one in ten is caught. I wonder if you can gather and kill fish when you observe them in the pond?" The otter replied, "Fish fear me just as birds fear you, but do they all hate life and love death, wishing to enter the dried fish market?" Zhan said, "I have heard that you drive them away and then call them back, what divine technique do you use to do this?" The otter said, "It is said that I have another technique, like the Daoist method of controlling ghosts, but it is false."

A tiger has hooked claws, a rhinoceros has fearsome horns, a fox has charming pearls, and a monkey has brittle bones. All these peculiarities are attributed by those who record strange phenomena, but nature itself is benevolent, compensating those with horns by taking away their teeth, and those with wings by giving them two feet. Would nature allow us to add more teeth and claws, thereby exhausting all types of creatures?" The sparrow hawk asked, "What then?" The otter replied, "I drive them away and bring them back again because I do not overkill their kind. I sit and catch them easily, as if by chance. They believe there is no danger and pass by, allowing me to catch them without limit. This is the strategy of letting go to catch more, a form of controlled greed." The sparrow hawk said, "Your words are true, but birds are more cunning than fish. Fish are the most docile, merely following the current. Among birds, some like the pheasant are guarded by their mates, others like the goose are patrolled by their servants, some like the cuckoo are skilled at hiding by hanging upside down, and others like the parrot are clever at avoiding danger by speaking. Others, like sparrows, often enter the curtain, swallows always stay in the hall, pigeons rely on the bells of Buddhist pagodas, and crows perch on the oars of merchant ships. With all these tricks, how can hunters capture them? If they all suddenly take flight, not capturing them all at once and letting them fly far away, would it not be too late to regret it?" The otter said, "Your ambition is great! But how about leaving an endless supply for future needs?" Before he finished speaking, a hundred birds flew across the sky. The sparrow hawk caught four or five, and the rest fled into the forest.

The falcon could not let go, flapping its wings in pursuit. Just then, a hunter lay in ambush nearby, and when he set his trap, the falcon was pierced through the neck and died first. The otter, moved by the falcon’s foolishness, set up a sacrifice on the northern bank of the river and called its spirit, saying: “The kite soars to the heavens, the fish leaps from the deep. Only I and you, who take killing as your field. If one is honest, one takes little; if one is greedy, one shares the loss. Why did you not cherish your life, carrying hatred to the nether world? Now I abandon my trade, washing my hands by the riverbank, rather let my belly be empty than lose my life. Greedy people destroy their kind; this has always been the case since ancient times. All you noble gentlemen, look upon this falcon.”

Duo said, "Annihilating a clan, the sparrowhawk is indeed vicious. Yet wanting to be greedy while appearing frugal, the otter's plot is even more vicious. Yet heaven alone rewards the sparrowhawk, not the otter. Is it that cursing fish into a bowl is not forbidden by the Buddhist door, but also the merit of repentance at the riverhead?" **Ant Mound** In Wu custom, transactions involving land and houses, the intermediary is called "ant." There was an old man named Jia, who had been in this profession for over thirty years, and his family was prosperous. He bought a kitchen maid who gave birth to a son. He asked the scholar-official Chu Shao to calculate it. Chu Shao was good at joking and used subtle words, playing with him by saying, "I see that your son will be very noble and wealthy, you will become a lord of a fief." Jia said, "Our profession is humble, how can we be known in the official records?" Chu Shao said seriously, "That is not the case! In ancient times, scorpions were called generals, fireflies were called dignitaries, butterflies were called lords of fragrance and powder, and bees were called governors of flower platforms. All insects are noble, how can the life of an ant be considered lowly? Moreover, the Dao is in the ant, Zhuangzi envied it, and hoped that the butterfly would practice it." Jia did not know it was a joke and told the scholar-official's words to his peers, who all raised their hands to congratulate him, saying, "Chunyu Fen burned incense to the point of burning his fingers, carrying a million ants to heaven together!" Jia was very happy and began to consider himself a lord of a fief every day.

A Child's Innocence and His Father's Hopes The child was simple-minded, and by the age of eighteen, he could barely memorize three pages of *The Great Learning*. When someone asked, "Is your son reading *Zuo Zhuan*?" the father replied, "He has already mastered *Zuo Zhuan* and is now reading *Right Zhuan*!" He claimed that the child recited the first and second chapters of *Right Zhuan* daily. By the time the child reached twenty, he remained as dull and unresponsive as before. The father, fearing that his previous statement was incorrect, consulted Mr. Chu, a scholar of integrity. Mr. Chu laughed and said, "Even if he has a noble destiny, how quickly can it be achieved? It takes five years for a termite to turn black, ten years to turn red, and thirty years to turn white. There is a fixed order to things, so I will wait and see." The father nodded in agreement. Later, the child became increasingly addicted to gambling and gradually neglected his studies. On the occasion of the father's eightieth birthday, guests gathered to congratulate him, and Mr. Chu was among them. The father brought up his previous claims again. Mr. Chu said, "Your titles are already prestigious. Why rely on your son to gain further honors?" The father asked what titles he had. Mr. Chu replied, "He passed the imperial examinations as an ordinary candidate, was promoted to a position overseeing land transactions, was appointed as governor of a prefecture, was awarded the title of official responsible for documents, and was elevated to the rank of draft proposal official." The guests burst into laughter, and the child also hid his smile. Mr. Chu said, "In the future, your son might benefit from your influence, but he will either become an ambassador to a teahouse or a clerk in a trading office." The father finally understood that it was a joke, and his hopes of being honored by his son's success were dashed.

Duo said: People from Wu mock those with low-ranking officials by saying, "A big future for ants," yet ultimately, one's future prospects can also come from humble beginnings. Is it necessary to rise to high positions as described in the *Xijing Ji* or achieve noble status as depicted in *Nankemeng* to realize that earlier statements were not mere jokes? The predictions of "Jia Lao" may not always come true; this is akin to the one-sided desire of ants for mutton, where mutton does not desire ants in return! The statement of the bureaucrat from Qiyuan is even more severe than that of Chu Zi Guang.

The Turtle Mirror Jiujiang Tang became wealthy through the practice of divination, seeking land and inquiring about houses, becoming the richest in the entire prefecture. Those in the same profession vied to visit him, asking what secret techniques he possessed that always led to success. Just then, a turtle limped up the stairs. Tang pointed at it and laughed, saying, "This is my teacher. You all ask for advice from me, but it would be better to ask the turtle." Those in the same profession asked why. He replied, "All the skills I use to navigate the world are taught by this creature." Those in the same profession said, "Is the art of physiognomy as important as the wisdom of the turtle?" Tang answered, "It is not that the art of physiognomy is less important than the wisdom of the turtle. The practice of divination is the most difficult, and one must embody the form of a turtle to teach effectively!" The crowd asked for a detailed explanation. Tang said, "We use our skills to travel the world. Without the support of influential people, how could we succeed everywhere? Some scholars and officials, by befriending their servants, gain access to their households, which is called 'relying on a strong back.' The turtle relies on its sturdy shell for protection. Entering a grand house is the hardest part; a red gate and high threshold, if misstepped, can cause one to stumble. I manage to get a letter of recommendation and a visiting card to introduce myself, which is called 'taking advantage of the opportunity.' Once inside, there is no fear of falling. When entering, walking with a high head and raised tail is despised by the wealthy, and fawning with a lowered shoulder and a servile smile is looked down upon by servants. One must walk slowly and unsteadily, being substantial and not frivolous, like a turtle dragging itself through the mud, which is called 'putting on airs.'"

The turtle, with its rounded front and rounded back, balanced left and right, is indeed the body of a turtle. When discussing physiognomy, if one accidentally hits the mark, they act like a turtle with its head up and tail wagging, feeling pleased with themselves. This is called "soft fire囤." If one fails to predict something, they act like a turtle retracting its head and tail, quietly retreating. This is called "convenient rest." Those who admire me should use the turtle as a mirror. Form alliances with the 28 constellations and use the 72 drilling techniques. Despite having six eyes, they are all blind, but the four feet are still busy. A bowing turtle has spirit; when it meets someone who dislikes it, it will bow respectfully. A sleeping turtle does not breathe; when it is insulted, it will endure the anger. The way of physiognomy is now practiced. This is why I have learned from the turtle. You all should use the turtle as a mirror! The crowd collectively sighed in admiration, while the turtle on the steps still shuffled away.

铎曰：尝读《史记。龟策传》，而知南辰北斗之说，为卜者言之，而相者不与焉。乃此君悟道于龟，岂李固足履龟文，李峤耳传龟息，亦《相经》所载者乎？舍我灵龟，何以相天下士？ 兔孕 俗传娈童为兔，不知始于何时。襄阳韦生，豪族也。宠姬四人，分四院以居。后眷一童，名粲儿。终年不履内院，日与粲儿坐书室调笑为乐。又得仇十洲所画《左风怀秘戏》，按谱行云，照图作雨。后庭花满，视温柔乡不在钗丛中矣。 西院姬名阿紫，美而黠，与粲儿通，而韦不知也。一日，韦他出，阿紫出帘下招粲儿私语曰：“自与君接后，红潮不至者百日矣。主人经年不御，倘一旦临蓐，诸婢于持我短长，宁仰药以求死耳！子盍为我计。”粲儿曰：“我筹之熟矣，断不误卿！” The bell said: I once read the *Biography of Tortoise and Divination* in the *Records of the Grand Historian*, and learned that the concepts of the South Pole Star and the Big Dipper were spoken by diviners, but not by physiognomists. This gentleman gained enlightenment through the tortoise. Is this akin to Li Gu walking on the patterns of a tortoise shell, or Li Qiao hearing the breathing of a tortoise, both of which are recorded in the *Physiognomy Classic*? Without my divine tortoise, how can I discern the characters of all scholars? **Rabbit Pregnancy** It is commonly believed that a young male lover is a rabbit, but it is unknown when this belief began. Wei Sheng of Xiangyang was a prominent figure. He had four favored concubines, each living in a separate courtyard. Later, he favored a young boy named Can'er. Can'er never entered the inner courtyard and spent his days with Wei Sheng in the study, joking and laughing for pleasure. He also obtained a painting by Qiu Shizhou titled *Left Wind and Secret Play*. Following the pattern to make clouds, illuminating the diagram to create rain, the back garden was full of flowers, and the land of softness and gentleness was no longer among the hairpins. The concubine in the western courtyard was named Azhi. She was beautiful and cunning, and she had an affair with Can'er, but Wei Sheng did not know about it. One day, when Wei Sheng went out, Azhi came out from behind the curtain to call Can'er privately and said, "Since I met you, I haven't had my period for a hundred days. The master has not visited me for a long time. If I give birth one day, the maidservants will gossip about me. I would rather take poison to die! You should plan for me." Can'er replied, "I have thought this through thoroughly, and I will not let you down!"

Soon after, Wei returned from outside and shared breakfast with Càn'ér. As soon as he took a bite, he frowned, held his abdomen, and suddenly showed signs of nausea. Wei quickly rose to comfort her, saying, "Did you catch a chill last night sitting in the moonlight, and part of your sleeve was removed, causing you to feel cold?" Càn'ér replied, "No. Since I have received your family's gracious love, I have been pregnant for more than three months!" Wei was initially shocked but then laughed, saying, "A rooster incubating eggs, a stallion giving birth to a foal—such things have never been heard of in ancient or modern times. You shouldn't take this as a joke." Càn'ér explained, "Don't you know? I saw that you were childless in middle age and were abandoning fertile opportunities, plowing stony fields. When would rare and valuable plants grow? I privately prayed to the God of Peaches, wishing to transform into a girl to extend your lineage. Now, the gods have indeed witnessed my prayers, and soon you will have a child. Why do you still treat my words as a joke?" Wei was delighted, patting her back and saying, "If you don't enter the rabbit's burrow, how can you catch a rabbit? From now on, just wait here, and there's no need to dig more holes!" Thus, day by day, they approached the time of Azi's delivery. Càn'ér said, "Having a child in the outer chamber is not very elegant. Please move me to the inner chamber."

All of Wang Shang's other wives were stubborn and unwilling to yield. At the time, Zizi pretended to be ill and lay on a brocade bed, calling Wang Shang to speak with her. She said, "Since you have been infatuated with the young boy for three years, not entering the inner chambers. Now you urgently seek him, it's not surprising that he doesn't respond. If you want to live in my west courtyard, you must avoid coming and going as before, and I won't allow you to pace back and forth. Wait until she is relieved of her life and then deal with the matter!" Wang Shang laughed and said, "You exclude me as an outsider, do you intend to harbor thieves in the house?" Zizi replied, "Those dressed in formal attire can be recognized as sisters. If you suspect me, why should I bother with trivial matters?" Wang Shang left and spoke with Can'er. Can'er said, "This is a good plan. The birth of a boy is originally shocking to hear. Now if I am moved to the west courtyard, once I give birth, I can pretend that the child is the offspring of Ziniang, avoiding causing trouble and leaving a stain on the reputation of a future good child." Wang Shang also clapped his hands in approval, so he moved Can'er to the west courtyard and slept alone in the outer room.

One night, it was said that Càn's son suffered severe abdominal pain, and the family was urgently called to assist in childbirth. Then, a baby cried out, and a son was born in the room. After half a month, Càn's son wrapped the infant and brought it out. Seeing his appearance, he looked very much like Càn, and they called him "like his mother's son," not knowing that he actually resembled his father. Since Càn had no milk, Azi was asked to feed him with rice water. The scent of milk filled the air all day, and Wei did not question where it came from. All the jade rings and embroidered garments were provided by Azi. If he had a slight illness, Azi would always have her trusted maid carry him into the inner chamber, taking every possible measure to care for him. Wei thought she was not jealous and instead admired her virtue. Once jokingly telling Càn, "A rabbit giving birth to a bird, truly the good fortune of a foolish child!" Càn also joked back, "The confusion of the hare and the sparrow, unable to distinguish between male and female, you too have seen the rabbit but not the bird."

After Wei, who indulged in lust without restraint, died prematurely, his concubines scattered like stars. Caner and Azhi eventually became a couple. When the child grew up, he inherited his wealth, moved to the prestigious neighborhood of Guanganli, and was recognized as a wealthy household. The bell said: "Men engaging in promiscuous behavior could render women unnecessary, but Yu Hualu was merely joking. Foolish people take jokes as reality, leading to the collapse of what was initially successful. The child was conceived through unnatural means, and the father's role in this process is forgotten, leading to a distorted lineage. The *Shen Yu* says: 'Accumulating rabbits in the marketplace, yet passing by without looking,' which is a subtle meaning of managing the household? It is indeed difficult to manage small people, let alone women. One must know that digging wells is in vain, but plowing fields is a steady plan. Do not allow the boar to enter the house, lest it steal my piglets, leading to a dangerous situation where a threat escapes and causes further harm. The past can serve as a warning, so guard against the treachery of the east pavilion. It is difficult to open the back door, so do not lightly unlock the north gate."

The Pheasant Mediator Mu the Old Man of Taiyuan, whose livelihood was keeping birds, had reached seventy years of age and was a widower. He felt a strong desire for a romantic partner, but despite numerous attempts through matchmakers, none brought him any good news. When pressed, people laughed and said, "Ideal suitors prefer young and vigorous partners. Now, your hair should be black but is white, and your face should be white but is black. Who would bring the beauty of a luxurious setting to serve an old, long-lived man with his staff and shoes?" The old man became greatly angered and released all the birds from their cages, then went out in a fit of anger. One day, he hid in a dense forest or valley, surrounded by a vast and desolate landscape, with no one to ask for directions. Suddenly, a white pheasant flapped its wings and flew, heading south and departing. The old man followed it, and at the end of the mountain, he suddenly saw a village. In the dense shade under willow trees, there was a wall with a distinctive shape, stretching for a hundred steps, with a garden gate wide open on the left side. The old man suspected it was a grand mansion and did not dare to approach, so he sneaked in quietly. Four women came laughing and chatting, saying, "Today the weather is clear and pleasant. Let's do a pole-walking game." They pulled red and climbed green, climbing one after another. One woman wore a dark red shirt, green collar, and jade sleeves, turning her back to the flowers and not speaking. The others said, "Is Ah Ying being foolish? Yesterday, Mrs. Sang performed a divination with lamp flowers, and one head with four petals, meaning we will definitely have a strange encounter today. However, a romantic and joyful gathering is shared by everyone. Why are you thinking foolishly first?" Just as they were laughing, they caught a glimpse of the old man hiding among the flowers and exclaimed, "The stars have not yet aligned for a romantic encounter, but the Southern Pole Star has invaded the flower palace!"

The old man first entered the gathering of women, his heart trembling and eyes dazzled. He wanted to explain his background but was clumsy with words, so he just stood idly by a flower, fiddling with his white beard. A messenger announced, "Lady Sang has arrived." The four girls left the old man and stood in a circle. Lady Sang asked, "Has a charming guest come?" The girls replied, "How could there be a charming guest, there's only an old fellow!" Lady Sang pointed to the old man and said, "This is your husband." The three girls spat at him and turned away, while only Aying clung to Lady Sang's elbow. Lady Sang said, "Aying is quite clever; don't learn from foolish maids to judge by appearance and cause trouble." She then guided the old man into the inner room and smiled, saying to him, "You are descendants of the ancient sovereigns of the Shao Hao lineage. You have wandered far and found refuge here, and you have a predestined connection with me. I respectfully claim the joy of the magpie, and secretly join the union of the phoenix. I hope you will not consider the poison cup ugly, and instead become a waiting mandarin duck." The old man nodded. Thus, under the light of the phoenix-headed lamp, the fragrance of the duck-tongued incense burning, and in front of the peacock screen, he and Aying completed their wedding ceremony. The other three girls crouched behind the screen to peek, laughing softly and hiding, saying, "What a fine husband like a falcon, absolutely like Wei's son picking out a green crane!"

The next day, the lady produced a single dose of purple mulberry pills, which she entrusted to Aying to give to the old man. The old man consumed them, finishing them in three days. Within half a month, his blackened skin turned white, his white hair turned black, and his beard fell out completely. When he looked in the mirror, he felt as if he were back in his thirties or forties when he was young. Three women heard of this and brought wine to congratulate him. They wore colorful and graceful clothing, spoke in a flirtatious manner, and when asked for their names, it was learned that they were Juan娘, Cui娘, and Yan娘. Yan娘 was the most playful, often spreading her sleeves and dancing in a swirling wind-like manner, or deliberately leaning and falling into his arms. Ying娘 sometimes brushed her clothes to tease him. Juan娘 was somewhat reserved but became playful under the influence of alcohol, touching her face and making flirtatious gestures. The old man, feeling rejuvenated and young again, hugged and embraced from both sides, almost wanting to pursue both the older and younger women. Ying娘 could not bear it and scolded him, saying, "You just got rejuvenated, and now you want to achieve two goals at once?" The three women also became angry and said, "A newly married woman for half a month, acting possessive, how could we, who are like owls, destroy your household?"

Eryang, having swept her sleeve and risen, said, "At first, I spat on them, then I licked them; truly, they are a rabble! I cannot bear to be near the roasted bird, to relieve your jealousy." Yan娘 said, "Do not use your honeyed tongue. I am eagerly looking forward to it, and the young master Zhang has already been accustomed to it. Would we seek the dream of peace by borrowing the wings of a neighbor's bird?" Cui娘 said, "The vinegar lady is being too coy. Between sisters, it is only playful banter!" Juan娘 said, "If she is already pecking at her own flesh, we had better return home." She tugged at her sleeve and sulked as she tried to leave. But the lady arrived, saying, "You all are not properly groomed! If you dislike the old, then you alone should allow the phoenix to rest, and if you favor the young, then you should compete to take over the sparrow's nest. Originally, you should be treated with the same severity as a hawk chasing a sparrow, but considering the harmonious relationship of the Meng family, we share a common bond. Eryang, transform your resentment into virtue, resolve your grievances, and share joy. From now on, let us fly together like geese, and do not generate more idle talk." The three girls leaped forward and thanked her in unison. The lady left. They then poured wine to make amends for Eryang, and she laughed, saying, "We with our crow-like beaks have been annoying, but I hope my sister does not forget her promise." Eryang replied, "As long as my elder sister continues to protect me, how could I dare to fly alone?" The old man heard her words and laughed heartily. The others said, "You aimed for a double victory, and now you have four phoenixes in one bed. Be careful that the mandarin ducks in the water do not get used to eating too much swan meat!" From then on, they flew side by side by day and touched necks by night, and the four girls had no words of criticism or complaint.

One day, the lady came with a changed expression, saying, "The big tree has fallen! Quickly send the young man back." The four of them held hands and wept tenderly, unable to part hastily. The lady sent a plain-clothed maid to urge him. Yingniang said, "Rather than die a thousand deaths and shatter my wings, I cannot bear being separated in the clouds. This is truly what I mean today." The old man also lingered and did not leave. The maid said, "I came to see you off, and now I will see you off again. Forcing you to stay would be of no benefit, fearing that we too would suffer the same fate!" Reluctantly, they parted with tears, stepping out a few steps, and on returning, saw the entire house in ruins. But saw only one mulberry tree, casting shade over half an acre. There was a woodcutter, holding an axe below, and four birds gathered in the mulberry tree, wailing mournfully. Just as he was about to question the maid, in an instant, they transformed into white pheasants, soaring into the sky. The prisoner remembered the virtue of Lady Mulberry, pitied the woodcutter, and left the trunk of the tree, asking the way and returning.

Duo said, "In Rugao, a single shot brought joy to Jia's wife. Thus, the pheasant, as a creature, specializes in mediating domestic affairs. Yet, Mu Duxi, at seventy, remained unmarried and never felt the need for the 'Zhi Chao Fei' melody to bring him a match. If it were Master Mu, it was likely due to his virtue of setting birds free." Romantic obsession and literary passion intertwine, entangling my gentle nature and my essence. What lies beyond words and texts? Together, we break free from the illusions of the world. Romantic passion erodes one's pride, leading to infatuation. Returning to oneself, maintain composure. Mockingly, I laugh at those seeking immortality on Mount Zhanren, year after year painting tigers and buying rouge. How can gossip and slander be resisted? Let others sing their praises at midnight, regardless. Even the most gentle and submissive may take decisive action, and even the most dominant may suffer significant losses.

Last night, a weasel wept by the riverbank, its cries echoing in the style of the *Chu Ci*—a sound too sorrowful to bear. How many greedy men lie buried in the forest, their fate unknown? Where can one inscribe a poem to mourn their autumn tombs? Though the winds carry divine decrees and the phoenix’s seal remains unbroken, do not look down on the old village man for his foolishness. A golden seal hangs at his waist, as large as a measure, yet all this power and wealth comes from the dream of *Nanko*. I would not be a bird of noble gate, white-necked and lowly, but rather be content to be disgraced and trampled in the mud. Through three levels of mastery in the black robe’s secret, I have learned the wisdom of the hemp robe—just as Master Xu Fu once bore a prophetic chart. Confusing and elusive, it is hard to describe; even the great and mighty are reduced to equal status, their former glory forgotten. A cunning den is built, the fragrant chamber sealed, yet pitifully, the one who caught the rabbit has forgotten the hoof that led him there. The pheasant’s chick spreads its emerald tail in beauty, and the lonely fish is led to the hall of conjugal joy. If you should meet a man from Chu on the road, do not hesitate to spend a thousand gold to buy a phoenix—true worth is worth its price. In reverence and gratitude, I inscribe this under the great decree of the imperial mandate. *Harmonious Bell.* Volume Two. *The Man by the Screen.* Xiang Guan was a poor girl from Jiangyin. She was skilled in poetry and prose, and also loved to study physiognomy, often accurately predicting people’s fortune and misfortune. When she reached the age of fifteen, her mother was about to betroth her. Xiang Guan said, “My features are weak, and I am not suited to be a wife. If you truly love me, then let me remain a daughter, and assign me only the small star’s task.” Her mother, impressed by her accurate words, agreed. Soon, many suitors came to her door. Her mother had her watch from behind the curtain, but none pleased her. She said, “Foolish girl, your eyes are too high. Is there not even one blessed young man among them?” Xiang Guan replied, “It is not what you think.” Her mother questioned her further, and seeing her eyes fill with tears, gave up and left the matter unanswered.

A Scholar from Hu Xi, Hong Sheng, was a man of talent. He loved the beauty of Jun Mountain and lived as a guest in Jiangyin. Upon hearing of Xiangguan's reputation, he entered her home to propose marriage. Xiangguan happened to be playing a game of money toss in the corner of the screen when he saw her. She entered and told her mother, "The guest in the hall is truly a good match for my son." Her mother came out to see him, agreed, and left. She then asked, "How does this son look to you?" Xiangguan replied, "He has a clear and refined spirit, graceful and elegant appearance, but he is not from the upper class. However, too clear and too refined can be thin and too graceful can be weak, I fear he will not live long." Her mother was surprised and said, "If he is not going to live long, why do you have a special liking for him?" Xiangguan wept and said, "I looked at myself in the mirror yesterday. My willow-like eyebrows reach the moon, and my pear-like dimples add charm. Three years after our marriage, I will be a widow. Your appearance is not favorable for the year of Jia Yin. This is truly a sign of short life, and going against it will bring misfortune. Please do not worry, Mother." Soon after, Hong prepared a grand residence and chose a date to welcome him with proper rites. After the wedding, they were very happy together. Hong was skilled in painting, and he had a busy schedule with long scrolls and short sheets of paper. As soon as he finished a painting, Xiangguan immediately wrote a poem on it. I still remember one of his poems titled "Twin Lotus," which goes:

In the serene village of Water Cloud, one sees tenderness; how many innocent children row painted boats. On the river, a lone mandarin duck labors to convey a message, flying away with flowers on its back without looking back. A sad prophecy is evident in the words. One day, sitting under the flowers, they folded short notes to play a drinking game, using flowers such as twin-headed, twin-stemmed, entwined, and leaf-bottom blooms. Whoever drew a note had to recite two lines from the *Book of Songs*; if the lines were agreeable, they were rewarded with fragrant tea; otherwise, they were punished by clapping their fingers on their wrists. Xiangguan drew the twin-headed flower and said, "May we be united in good times and bad, and our wives and children be happy together." Hong smiled affectionately and said, "The phrase 'nightly union' is naturally beautiful, truly a wise-hearted person!" Next, Hong drew the twin-stemmed flower and said, "May your household be happy, a sign of good fortune for men." Xiangguan replied, "May there be good fortune for men, and it be shared by both. As you say, it is also a sign of success!" Then, Xiangguan drew the entwined flower and said, "The road is long and difficult, but spring brings warmth and light." Hong said, "The words 'Changchun' (long spring) and 'entwined' form a fitting text, truly a coincidence!" Finally, they drew the leaf-bottom flower.

Hong said, “The sound of axes ringing as wood is felled, and its fragrance begins to rise.” Xiangguan smiled and said, “Woodruff is indeed listed in the flower catalog; why did you combine it with the second character?” Hong laughed and said, “This is what is called a flower hidden beneath the leaves.” Soon after, he asked, “You spoke earlier of twin-flowered blossoms; do you know how many such instances exist within the Three Hundred Poems?” Xiangguan replied, “Driving four steeds, his face like freshly dyed vermilion; paying homage to the sea, the *gantang* tree, with its spreading shade. I think that covers them all!” Hong said, “I still have one couplet.” Xiangguan asked for its meaning. He said, “Also greatly flourishing, the millet grows in disorder.” Xiangguan looked solemn and said, “Leaning close in the presence of flowers, our joy and union were just beginning—why speak of parting now?” She leaned against the railing, standing in a daze, her eyes fixed, on the verge of tears. Hong was just offering gentle words to console her when a message from home arrived, summoning her to return! Compelled by her father’s command, she had no choice but to hastily pack and take her leave.

Xiangguan, since Hong's departure, had her makeup room remain closed, her powder boxes all collected, and she spent her days gazing into the mirror in deep thought, observing her complexion. One day, she threw the mirror aside and wept bitterly, urgently calling for her mother to have mourning clothes made for her. Her mother said, "You are too foolish! After Hong's departure, there was not even a single letter of illness, how can you be sure he is dead, and why make such an ominous thing?" Xiangguan replied, "Based on my complexion, it will surely be accurate." Her mother still refused. Instead, she was given plain garments, and day and night, she could only wash her face with tears. Within a month, the news of his death indeed arrived. She destroyed her appearance and stopped eating, almost not wanting to live. A guest brought a message from Hong's father, pitying her for being young and a widow, offering her hundreds of gold pieces and advising her to remarry. Her mother consulted with her daughter. Xiangguan angrily said, "What are you saying! I have served my husband for a short time while he was alive, but I will serve him for a long time after his death. Moreover, my return as a widow is based on my trust in him; my being a chaste widow is also based on my trust in him. In this world, are there people who have cold faces like frost and hearts colder than snow, yet go to remarry like the eastern wind?" The guest was amazed and left. She told Hong's father, and people approved of her decision. They then prepared a boat to welcome her back home.

There are sisters-in-law who ask her to talk about physiognomy, but she keeps her mouth shut and does not say a word. Family members know she is good at poetry and compete to bring out white silk for her to write on, but she all declines with illness. She says, "A woman with talent will ultimately have no good fortune; old habits are finally overcome!" Only the young maidservant secretly took her poem "Inscription on Hong Jun's Leftover Painting" to show her nephew Zhao En, who got twenty-eight lines, which read: "The red and white colors fill the railing, one can see a whole spring in the painting. When unfolded in the autumn window, it is completely unlike, the courtyard of the parasol tree is extremely cold." Although this is a small glimpse of her talent, those who read it can also mourn her aspirations. Diao said: "The *Tangshu* records that Yuan Tian Gang, when he appraised Cen Wenben, said: 'Your talent will certainly shine throughout the world, but you have a bone on your head, which may shorten your life.' Now this method is passed down to women, as a basis for choosing a husband. The short life is suitable for one sentence, it is a clear and decisive judgment. If this method were widely used, Pan Qi's *Ode to a Widow* would not have the 'sudden death' regret." Pen reduces life span Zheng Lanfen, a woman from Zhongzhou, lost her father when she was young. Her mother loved her dearly and often let her sit in the study. There were books and scrolls everywhere on the table. Her mother often teased her, saying, "This is our family's thousand-mile horse, but she is a female rather than a male!" Lanfen replied, "As long as she can gallop on the literary battlefield, it is better than Liu's pigs and dogs." Thus, her reputation in the inner quarters spread throughout the village.

Once, I created a divination text titled "Money" (《钱》卦) which states: "Money, it is useful for maintaining integrity. The image says: 'Money is square and upright within, round and upright without. Square and round are upright, the great principle of heaven and earth.' Money has a square hole, which refers to one's elder brother. Elder brothers and younger brothers, fathers and sons, husbands and wives, and thus the flow of money is smooth. When the flow of money is smooth, the household is stable. The image says: 'Gold is refined from fire. Money, the gentleman has substance within and radiance without.' For the initial nine, having money in reserve, regret disappears. The image says: 'Having money in reserve, it comes not yet in the right way.' For the sixth two, nothing is accomplished without being in the right place, and if one remains steadfast, it is auspicious. The image says: 'The auspiciousness of the sixth two, it is due to being compliant and hidden.' For the ninth three, the power of money is like the sound of a bow being drawn, with initial regret but ultimately favorable. The carefree attitude of someone who is overly dependent on money leads to eventual loss. The image says: 'The power of money is like the sound of a bow being drawn, about to be lost; the carefree attitude of someone who is overly dependent on money, they lose their family business.' For the sixth four, a wealthy family is most auspicious. The image says: 'A wealthy family is most auspicious, as wealth is accumulated through virtue.' For the ninth five, a gentleman who has money need not worry, as it is auspicious. The image says: 'A gentleman who has money, they love and care for each other.' For the upper nine, having authority like a ruler, the end result is auspicious. The image says: 'The auspiciousness of authority, it refers to the development of one's character.' Mr. Xu Xiaolian of the Fragrant Reed Pavilion included this in "Remnants of the Banana Window," and those who spoke of it were captivated by it.

Ah Kang, the maid, was intelligent and clever. One day, she went to pick flowers in the garden and did not return for a long time. Lan Fen sent his fifth son to track her down, and he found that she had been troubled by the servant Liao Er. Lan Fen then imitated the "Five Sons' Song" and wrote a satirical work titled "Regulations for the Maid" to mock her, saying: "Ah Kang holds a position without doing the work, for ease and pleasure, neglecting her duties, and all others are discontented. She roams aimlessly without restraint, playing in a desolate garden. There is the poor servant Liao Er, who, because no one was around, acted familiarly in the courtyard. Her younger brother, the fifth son, followed the master's command and waited at the edge of the garden. The fifth son was greatly resentful, recounting the master's instructions and composing a song. The first part says: 'There are teachings for managing a household: people can be diligent, but not lazy. Diligence is the foundation of a stable home. I see that even the most foolish men and women, if they do not listen to me, will lose twice. Disaster is not always obvious, so it should be anticipated.'"

I address you all, majestic like a chain controlling six horses. Those who are beneath me, how can you not be cautious! The second one says: "There is a saying, internal corruption and external licentiousness, gluttonous, lazy, and stubborn, burrowing through walls. If any of these exists, there is no way it will not perish." The third one says: "Only my high hall has this righteous way. You have defied your teachings, disrupted their rules, and thus brought about your own downfall." The fourth one says: "Majestic is my lord, the head of the family. With propriety and law, bequeath to future generations. Reciting poetry and prose, as the ancients did. Neglecting their legacy, teaching licentiousness and losing shame." The fifth one says: "Alas, return quickly, I am filled with sorrow. People are deceiving you, whom will you rely on? My heart is deeply troubled, and I feel a great sense of shame. If you repent of your faults, the future can still be salvaged." With clever thoughts and wise words, they are mostly like this.

One night, sitting by the lamp, I began writing *The Spring and Autumn of Perfumes and Powders*. Before completing a few lines, my arms grew weak and my body tired, and I lay down to sleep. Suddenly, I found myself in a hall, with a horizontal plaque above that read, "The Great Site of the Six Classics' Writings." A dignified figure, wearing a ceremonial cap and sitting upright, was present, along with several scholars in scholar's caps, correcting books at the corners. One person held a scroll and said, "This is Yang Ziyun's attempt to emulate the *Yi Jing*." The figure on the throne replied, "The *Yi Jing* has been passed down from Shang Qiu to Tian He, a total of five transmissions. Wang Bi focused on principles, and Jing Fang focused on numbers, but neither fully explored its profundity. How could you dare to imitate it! Moreover, he has already compromised himself to serve Xin Mang, so even if he has strange characters and words, they are not worth seeing." Another person said, "This is a false book by Zhang Ba." The figure on the throne responded, "The *Shu Jing* emerged from the wall of Lu, and the ancient text has not been transmitted for a long time! Mei Ze's twenty-five chapters are roughly similar, but who is Zhang Ba to dare to create it!" Another person said, "This is Shu Guangwei's *Poems to Supplement the Lost*." The figure on the throne replied, "The choice of meaning and words is also not far from the teachings of poetry. However, fish swimming in clear ponds and birds gathering in forests are purely the style of Jin Dynasty people."

"How can one unjustly claim to be a paragon of elegance and refinement!" Another person stepped forward and said, "This is Liu Xin's *Collected Rites*." The person sitting at the head said, "It is a fake version from Hejian, and it is truly difficult to discern. The *Kao Gong Ji* section is clearly a private fabrication by Han scholars, intended to supplement the omissions in the *Winter Official*." Another person stepped forward and said, "This is He Xiuyu's *Abridged Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals*." The person sitting at the head said, "The *Gongyang* Commentary is rigidly conservative, the *Zuo* Commentary is like a chronic ailment, and the *Guliang* Commentary is like a physical disability; it is just a madman's dream!" More books such as *The Deletion of Lu* and *Non-Mencius* were presented. The person sitting at the head suddenly became very angry and said, "Imitating *Zhuangzi* but turning it into a *Chu Ci* style, it still belongs to petty scholars playing with words. To cut and paste the sacred texts and teachings of the sages, rashly criticizing and attacking, should be punished by being sent to the hell of the tongue being pulled out, to show the retribution for evil deeds." Before the sentence was finished, someone hurriedly knelt down. The person sitting at the head said, "Zheng Jiaji, what do you have to say?" The person hesitated and replied, "Zheng Xuan supported and interpreted the *Five Classics*, claiming to have contributed to the education of the world. Who would have thought that a descendant of a woman would tamper with the text of the classics, treating it as a game, what can be done?" The person sitting at the head said, "This is an insult to the words of the sages, and the punishment should be increased. Considering that she is a woman and may be ignorant, let her lifespan be shortened to atone for her past sins."

At the time, Lan Fen was lurking outside the hall. Upon hearing his words, she was so startled that her heart and soul trembled; she stumbled down the steps and suddenly awoke from a dream. Under the lamp, she burned her old drafts and deeply repented. Later, she wrote a letter to a young man from her hometown, but she died three days before her wedding, which was indeed the retribution for mocking sacred words. We who treat writing as a game, can we not abandon this attitude? Duo said: "Wine is the food of scholars, women are the scholars of gentlemen; eloquent and charming speech, unparalleled in a thousand years. Yet, when 'The Peony Pavilion' is performed in the world, the author suffers one day in the afterlife, who knows if this is not the cause of this case? My family member from Hunan once wrote 'Monthly Notes on the Inner Chamber,' which includes phrases like 'lip balm melts, curtains turn into hooks. Clothes are damp with the heat of summer, powder rain occasionally falls,' showing a wise heart. But the red paper is still wet, and the yellow earth quickly covers it, self-inflicted sorrow, what more can be said? I append this note here, sighing over it!"

Proclamation Against the Cat** A student named Huang Zhi Jun, who loves to read. With books and records on both sides, he is comparable to a ruler over a hundred officials. He keeps a cat to catch mice. Its fur is spotted like a tiger, and everyone considers it a fine thing. He places it beside his bookshelf, where it sleeps all day in a drowsy manner, softly muttering as if reciting Buddhist mantras. Someone says, "This is a cat that recites Buddhist mantras." He names it Buddha's slave. When mice cause trouble in the room, they initially hide slightly upon seeing Buddha's slave. Later, they jump and lose their footing, falling to the ground. Buddha's slave pampers and caresses them repeatedly, guiding them away. Afterward, the mice fear but show no fear, gathering in groups around it. One day, they climbed onto its shoulders and back, finally biting its nose, causing blood to flow continuously. Huang Zhi Jun was about to ask for medicine to treat it. I happened to pass by and shouted, "The purpose of keeping a cat is to catch mice. But it cannot eliminate them, which is dereliction of duty. Instead, it is bitten by them, which is a loss of dignity. It is appropriate to beat it and ask why, rather than using medicine?" I ordered Huang Zhi Jun to write a proclamation against the cat, and I revised it. The proclamation reads: [The proclamation text would follow here, as it is not provided in the source text.]

The Rat-Catcher, a creature known as Buddha's Slave, is naturally timid and weak, yet feigns kindness and mercy. It recites scriptures like Snow-Cloaked Lady, pretending to follow the rules of a gentleman. In the shade of flowers, it is lazy and indifferent, ignoring the chaos around it; on the bamboo mat at night, it is sluggish, letting others do the work. It even gathers a group of chaotic followers, surrounding the Palace of the Mother of Demons, with multiple generations stacked on each other's shoulders, the Six Thieves playing on Amitabha's Cliff. Yet it remains like an old monk in meditation, seeing and hearing nothing, a puppet on stage, silent and odorless. It is indecisive and indulges in wrongdoing, allowing evil to flourish, thus suffering the misfortune of losing its nose, and instead being bitten by the teeth of others. Yama, the god of death, loses his authority, and the general fears his soldiers, losing discipline. It willingly accepts humiliation, being the worst kind of evil-doer, while the causes of disaster are all created by those who seek fame. Therefore, it arranges the chaotic array of Chu dogs and organizes the disorganized army of Cai mules. It is aided by a cowherd's whip and a horse's rope. Lightly, it is the same as holding a pig; heavily, it is equal to whipping a sheep. It hangs the promise of a fox's head on a pole, to serve as a warning for future generations; it binds it to the qilin's throne, to observe the consequences. Together, they unleash the might of the tiger, to prevent the rabbit from escaping. The Bell said: "In the past, Master Bin of Wanshou Temple considered not catching mice as an act of kindness. The crowd thought he was lying, but they did not know it was actually the Buddha's way. If a scholar becomes an official, the main task is to eradicate evil and support the good. Yet, by eating the ruler's salary, they seek their own fame, nurture the corruption in their city, and harm the people. Like Buddha's Slave, it is pardoned by the Buddha's law but must be punished by the king's law!" The Ode to the Worm

The Ten Thousand Volume Pavilion was the place where my cousin, the Prefect Jiang, stored his books. He traveled to Fujian as an official and locked away his books during the noon. Later, he took leave to return home and exposed his scrolls and volumes, half of which were damaged by bookworms. He then ordered the children to capture and kill them all until they stopped. That night, in the tower, thousands of voices cried together, almost until dawn, and the owner was troubled by it. I happened to be staying in the Pine Rhythm Pavilion, and thus wrote this essay to offer a sacrifice: Alas, O bookworms! You possess the nature of insects but do not gather around stench, and though you have the name of fish, you do not leap into the deep waters. You wander freely in the treasury of literature, relying on ink and brush as a means. Why did you not learn from the industriousness of termites and the transformation of money beetles? You love the fragrance of books and wish to discard the stench of money. I have heard that your ancestor Pulse Vision transformed into a celestial being. Using poetry and books as the forge and anvil, he hoped to preserve the family tradition. Building a nest in books and tilling the fields of words, even though you consume ancient texts but do not transform, you are pitied for your intentions. Who would have thought the owner would be so well-meaning, but the fate of things is unpredictable. They were all copied like vines, suddenly the entire clan was exterminated. To spread poison in the study and suffer injustice in the forest of books. Recognizing that disasters have roots, I will explain why it is so. Boring through the classics and histories too deeply, cutting off the connections between words and meanings, destroying chapters and breaking sentences, also tearing pages and leaving behind incomplete works. Hiding in the garden of literature, indulging in the greed for flesh and blood, considered pests of culture, thus incurring the wrath of sages. Those minor officials with pens and brushes, spending their lives in paperwork, stealing a single word from your life, then manipulating the law and wielding power. You should repent and reflect on your faults.

Not the owner's desire for violence, but rather karmic retribution from heaven. A bundle of writing materials, half a boat of ink, you shall partake of it, in this spiritual feast. Do not become a spirit of harm on the walls of dragons and snakes, wait for the turning of the wheel by the dung of dung beetles. A mound of pens, predict your long sleep; a fertile ink field, mark your new path. Invite the mourners (symbolized by flies) to pay respects, drive the ants (symbolized by small creatures) to the underworld; if the wandering spirit is unharmed, return on the back of a mosquito. After the sacrifice, the sounds in the tower have ceased. The bell said, "Clerks manipulating documents, called officials' pests, but there is no such name in the realm of reading. Yet, those who use words as amulets, rely on verses to hunt for food, can all be called bookworms. Someone said, 'These people whose words and meanings are disconnected, called bookworms, still belong to an overpraise.'" Through the window, discussing poetry. Water Painting Garden, the gathering place of scholars like Mao Bijiang, now abandoned as a Buddhist temple. Hu Shengwen from Qichang, a guest in Rugao, rented a Buddhist temple to live in. Possessing a unique spirit, respected by predecessors like Shen Jinzhai and Wang Xiyuan, he became even more pleased. He once wrote a poem expressing his aspirations, with the lines, "Am I being foolish? Just to say so, when I was young, I was no better than others." I happened to see it and said, "This is the Song and Yuan style." He became angry and unwilling to concede, then turned to question me about the origins and development of poetic studies. I nodded in agreement. He angrily said, "Are you not even willing to teach?" Flinging his sleeves, he left.

Officials and guests, their demeanor rich and their intentions far-reaching, faithfully inherit the tradition. The style of Du Fu is like a whale pulling the sea, immense and powerful, while the style of Li Bai is like a strong crane reaching for the sky, elegant and soaring. As for quatrains, they lack concrete references, and one must seek flavor beyond the basic tastes. Though Du Fu was a highly talented poet, he did not leave behind many outstanding quatrains. One cannot say that quatrains like "Yellow River flows far away" and "Grape wine in a cup" are worthy of imitation, like a badger's feast. He suddenly raised his voice and shouted, "I am the Old Man of Yuyang, having discussed poetry for over sixty years, taking Du Fu's poetry as the model. What kind of reckless youth dares to point out the essence of Sikuang Shu's poetry, leading people astray from learning?" He then laughed and said, "You are a generation of poets and a thousand years of historians. How dare you speak rashly? But the poem 'Luo Feng Slope Lamenting Pang Shiyuan' still needs careful consideration." While he was speaking, a man claiming to be Cui Budiao and another claiming to be Chen Qinián began to argue loudly. I said, "Are you disciples of the桐花 school? Famous in life for the 'yellow leaf' style, yet the two words 'red maple' are similar in meaning and style. I imagine your lifetime masterpieces are only the couplet 'spring water' and 'peach blossom,' which can barely rival 'peony' and 'rose.' As for the *Gacang Collection* of the censor, it can be compared to Xin Qiji and Su Shi; but the hundred poems about plum blossoms, they only earned the praise of Yunlang holding the inkstone, not matching the 'high branch, cold hand' works in terms of sound and resonance." The others still continued to argue, so he clapped his hands and laughed, "Mr. Mao, you have associated with a generation of poets, but in the end, they were destroyed like a simple nest in a fire, starving and filling the ditches, alas!"

In the blink of an eye, Hu burst into laughter and approached, saying, "Master, you were unwilling to teach me, but I have now fully understood your teachings." It seems that Hu wanted to hear my wild talk, so he secretly instructed a companion to hide behind the window and create this cunning scheme. I laughed heartily. Hu presented a token of respect at my door for two years, discussing literature in his spare time, and also touched upon poetry, prose, and lyrics. However, his manuscripts were not well organized, and they were often stolen by friends. Liu also closely resembles his teacher, indeed so.

The Bell said, "Bian Xiaoxian once explained a joke to his disciples; this is even more like a guest's entertainment. If the teacher is eccentric, the disciples will also be eccentric; if the teacher is lazy, the disciples will also be lazy. Eccentricity is not something to be learned, and laziness is even less so. Teacher, rest now; students, strive hard." In the spring of the year of Yi Wei, I rented a hermitage called Yuxiang An and settled there, planning for a secure life. The hermitage was the site of Miao Garden. At that time, my husband was also a guest in Rugao. I presented gifts at the teacher's door and spent two years together. In the winter of the year of Bing Shen, I moved my family south. Far from the teacher's door, for eleven years. In the year of Wu Shen, the teacher served as a pastor in Wuqi. After two years, I returned from Yuzhang and visited the teacher respectfully. The following year, he invited me into the study hall and provided guidance. I was constantly under his influence, almost without a day's break. During leisure time, I asked to see the complete works of poetry and prose, and also requested to see the large-scale compositions of music and dance. All were declined due to loss or dispersion. Only by checking the traveling box, I found over fifty pieces of 'Harmonious Bell.' After finishing reading, I then asked further: "Teacher, do you have a compassionate heart to save the world, but hide it through humor, like the ancient Eastern Manqian, why not quickly publish it, to be used as a tool for the official to spread the word?" After being granted permission, I recalled past events and collected recent news, gathering several items, divided into twelve volumes. This item was added during the carving process. This record shows the deep bond between teacher and student, lasting twenty years like one day. However, I, as a poor student, have neglected my studies and rely on others for writing. Looking back at past joys, they have become like dreams of yesterday. Alas, youth does not return, and I feel ashamed that I have not achieved anything in my prime. I have greatly disappointed my teacher's efforts to help me succeed.

On the twenty-first day of the sixth month in the year Xin Hai, I, Hu Wen Shui, who was trained by [the person], respectfully recorded this. Behind the Curtain Discussing Music Li Qiurong was a beloved concubine of Xu Gongzi from Wujiang, known for her keen intellect and profound understanding of music theory. A certain scholar from the same town, who had some talent and learning, wrote a play and showed several works to Xu Gongzi. While they were discussing, laughter suddenly came from behind the screen. The scholar then clapped and sang, and the laughter behind the screen became uncontrollable. Xu Gongzi softly rebuked, "The music master is present, it is proper to listen respectfully. Laughing and giggling, what kind of demeanor is this?" He said, "This young man doesn't know how to behave. Set up a green silk screen for me, and drive him away." After a while, a woman sat behind the curtain and invited the guest to meet her. The scholar bowed through the curtain to greet her. She asked, "Is your play based on Southern tunes or Northern tunes?" The scholar replied, "Recently, plays performed on stage have both Southern and Northern styles, and there are even mixed Southern-Northern performances. They are not different in skill, so how can they claim to be able to compose music?" She asked, "Do you know what makes Northern tunes different from Southern tunes?" The scholar said, "Southern tunes have four tones, while Northern tunes only have three tones, and the entering tone is distributed among the level, rising, and falling tones. Those who compose music carefully analyze every detail and match words to the melody, who doesn't know this?" She said, "You know that the difference between Northern and Southern tunes lies only in the entering tone, but do you also know that there are still inconsistencies in the level and falling tones?" The scholar said, "I haven't heard of that." The woman behind the curtain laughed and said, "You truly are someone who only knows one thing and not the other! In Southern dialects, the character '崇' is pronounced as '戎,' but in Northern dialects, it is pronounced as '虫.'"

The pronunciation of the character 杜 differs between southern and northern dialects: in the south, it is pronounced as "渡" (du), while in the north, it is pronounced as "妒" (du). Cases like this are too numerous to count. Moreover, the primary difference between northern and southern dialects lies in the tone of the departing tone (去声). Southern music emphasizes high-pitched tones, while northern music focuses on clear and solid expressions. One must carefully match the sound to the melody; otherwise, it can strain the voice and even break it. The student asked, "Are there differences in pronunciation even within the same rhyme?" The teacher replied, "Yes, there are. Within the same rhyme, the sound of the character 杜 is long, while the sound of the character 终 is short, the sound of the character 风 is flat, and the sound of the character 宫 is round. Within the same rhyme, the sound of the character 江 is broad, while the sound of the character 臧 is narrow, the sound of the character 堂 is coarse, and the sound of the character 将 is fine. Mastering the precise pronunciation and selecting the appropriate style is essential for creating music, making it a divine art." The student nodded in agreement. Then he asked, "What musical score or standard do you follow?" The teacher replied, "I follow the 'Great Completion Nine Palaces,' adhering strictly to its rules without making arbitrary changes." The student then asked, "What palace or key do you use in your music?" The teacher remained silent.

The person inside the curtain said: "Dividing the palaces and establishing the modes is the first step for composers. For a rich and luxurious atmosphere, use the *Yellow Bell*; for expressions of sorrow and grief, use *Nanlü*. From one example, you can infer many others. Otherwise, talking about something completely irrelevant to the situation, even if you judge the sounds correctly without error, it will be completely mismatched with the context, which is a major flaw in composing music. Additionally, Southern tunes often have a continuous flow, while Northern tunes often have more breaks or pauses. Southern tunes have a fixed rhythm, while Northern tunes often have a more variable rhythm. Southern tunes have fewer supplementary syllables, while Northern tunes have more supplementary syllables. Choosing words and setting the structure is the work of those who are skilled and intuitive in music. As for the five tones and four pronunciations, ending a sound and returning to a proper tone, these are the duties of singers, but it is not necessary to expect perfection from the composer."

The man was greatly frightened and looked at the young lord, Xu Gongzi, saying, "I did not expect your family's luxurious abode to have such wonderful talent, surpassing Zhang Honghong's trivial matters." Before he finished speaking, a person rolled up the curtain and came out. Looking at her, she was a green-clad maid. She said, "Fortunately, I have learned from a lady, but my skills are limited to this. Otherwise, if the women's army were to come, could you avoid surrendering and being bound?" The man was greatly embarrassed and left dejectedly. Later, the father of the young lord, Lingtai Mr., collected discussions from the inner chambers and wrote a volume called *The Transmission of Music*, which circulated in the world and was revered as a standard by musicians. Duo said, "Examining the poem 'Deer Cry' in *The Music Score*, in the first chapter, I am the lead, you are the chorus, he/she is the response, and the guest is the southern direction. In the second chapter, I am the chorus, you are the southern direction, he/she is the response, and the guest is the yellow direction. Thus, the various scales can be interchanged. There is no fixed scale in the world, but musicians are very particular about the notation. Is it that modern music is different from ancient music? Or is it that adjusting the notation to fit the melody can be forgiven in ancient times but not in modern times?"

**Examining the Plaque and Chasing the Rotten Ghosts** In Lou Dong, Chen Yuesheng built a retreat to the west of the Lotus Bridge. Just after the construction was completed, family members spread rumors of ghosts. Chen doubted the truth of these rumors and decided to move in. One night, he saw four scholars, each wearing a blue robe, coming in a group. They were chanting poetry, and all their limbs carried a stench of decay. There was an old man about fifty years old, another about forty, and the other two were about eighteen or nineteen years old. The old man said, "Yesterday, the rain and wind spoiled our mood. Tonight, the moon is beautiful. Why not choose a topic to discuss the excellence or inferiority of literature and art?" The three others replied, "Agreed." The old man took out several circular pieces of paper from his sleeve and ordered the young men to choose one. They opened and looked at it, and it turned out to be a complete chapter question on "to see what he is like." Each person took out their writing materials. The old man sat up front, the man about forty sat next to him, and the two young men sat at a lower table.
