The four of them closed their eyes, frowned, shook their heads, scratched their ears, and mumbled for about two hours. The old man laughed and said, "Tonight, our minds are dull and blocked, and we only managed to find one good solution. What to do?" The man next to him said, "I am also equal to you, sir." The old man took a look at his work and said, "The first sentence can be said to be a hero's perspective, but the second sentence still lacks inclusion." The man next to him asked for advice. He then took out what he had written and showed it to the old man, saying, "To see what he is like, and to observe his actions, to examine his comfort, and to see if he is thin?" The old man said, "The first sentence can be said to be a hero's perspective, but the second sentence still lacks inclusion." The man next to him was greatly impressed.

The Elder said, "In the art of writing, a slight deviation can lead to great consequences. You have consistently ranked among the top five, while I am merely attached to the bottom of the fourth tier—this is truly because the topic has been thoroughly explored." After saying this, the young man felt quite proud. Looking at the two youths, he saw that they had written nothing. The Elder said, "You are in your prime years, and writing should be done with great enthusiasm. Why are you submitting blank papers like this?" The youths replied, "The strictest punishments in the world are nothing compared to the rigors of writing. We hate life and love death because we are fortunate to have escaped this ordeal. Are we now seeking poison when we are not ill?" The Elder clapped his hands and laughed, "I was wrong. As you say, this is indeed the first way to find true happiness." Soon, a young servant carrying a wine box arrived. The youths said, "Discussing profound matters on an empty stomach—what meaning is there? On such a pleasant evening, it's better to drink heartily." They then set out wine and food on the table, sat together, and gorged themselves until everything was gone. The youths laughed heartily, holding their bellies, "This is empty and void of content—only fit to be a wine bag and rice container!" The man in his forties said, "Eating meat and being strong in spirit is exactly what we need to fill this emptiness." The Elder said, "But I fear that we see it enter but not see it exit." Having said this, they all burst into laughter. Soon after, the young servant collected the wine utensils, and the four of them agreed to meet again, leaving in a state of drunkenness and fullness. Chen began to believe in the existence of ghosts. From then on, he called friends and associates, and there was no night that did not disturb the peace.

During the annual examination period, the schoolmaster sent a messenger with an official notice to inform the candidates. The messenger passed through the lotus bridge at night, stopped at Chen's cottage, placed the official notice on the table, and fell asleep wrapped in a blanket. Four scholars entered and sat down loudly, engaging in eloquent and profound discussions as if no one else were present. Suddenly, an old man approached the table, saw the official notice, and exclaimed in shock, "The summons of death has come again!" The others looked around, their faces ashen with fear. A young scholar laughed and said, "Before our deaths, we suffered greatly because of this, leading to our transformation into something else. Now, we are half outsiders; why should we worry about the Battle of Julu, where even spectators suffer?" The old man replied, "Do not speak carelessly. The underworld has recently enacted a new law: during the annual examination period in the mortal world, the city god's office is searching for ghost scholars, who must all attend the annual examination at the Hall of Literature. Those who excel will receive the highest rewards, while those who fail will be sent to the knife mountain prison, where they will be disemboweled. It is urgent, what can be done?" The young scholar's expression also changed, and he repeatedly asked for advice. The old man said, "This is not a place of peace. If you want to avoid this trouble, each of you should discard your scholar's cap and clothes, and bury your heads and necks in the darkness of hell for five or six hundred years, so that the officials cannot search for you, and perhaps you can escape from the sea of suffering!" The scholars were all relieved and happy, removed their hats, wrapped themselves, and carried them away, following the old man as they staggered and fled. The messenger was surprised by this. The next day, he told Chen about the incident. Chen was very pleased and copied the official notice, posting it on the wall. After that, the scholars never came again.

铎曰：“曳白秀才，森罗殿犹防对策，矧敢金门待诏耶？固知李昌谷应制玉楼，惟平日呕得心肝乃敢赴绯衣之召耳！” 妙画代良医 蜉溪潘琬，字璧人，美仪容，有玉树临风之目。妻尹氏，艳而妒。 潘谨守绳墨，跬步不离绣闼。潘有别墅，在濂溪坊里。庭前海棠数株，每当含苞未吐之时，隐度其两鬟插戴处，往向枝头芟剪，及花放，折归助妆，长短疏密适合。尹尝执花睨潘而笑曰：“此解语花也，劳卿手折，益妩媚矣！”由是，封海棠曰“花卿”，而戏呼潘曰“掌花御史”。后潘以病瘠死，尹哭之哀。一日，过别墅，适海棠盛开。尹凭栏凝睇，触绪萦怀，忽忽若迷，归而病殆。 尹有族弟名慧生，善绘事，闻之曰：“此心疾也，吾当以心药治之。”遂写海棠数十本，貌潘生科头其下。旁绘妖姬五六人：有拈花者，有嗅花者，有执花在手乞潘生代为插鬓者，有狎坐膝头戏以花瓣掷生面者。画毕，竟诣床头，询姊近状。尹流涕不言。 Duo said, "A candidate who fails an examination, even in the Hall of Punishments, must still be cautious in their responses; how dare they aspire to serve in the imperial court? Indeed, it is known that Li Changgu, commissioned to write in the Jade Pavilion, only dared to accept the call of the red-robed officials after pouring his heart and soul into his work!" A masterful painting can serve as a remedy. Pan Wan of Fuxi, styled Bi Ren, was handsome and graceful, with an air of elegance. His wife Yin Shi was beautiful but jealous. Pan strictly adhered to propriety, never leaving the inner chambers. He had a villa in the area of Lianxi Lane. In front of the courtyard were several peach trees. Whenever the buds were about to bloom, he would secretly observe where his wife would wear her hairpins, then go to the branches to trim them. When the flowers bloomed, he would pick them to help her adorn herself, ensuring the lengths and arrangements were just right. Yin once held a flower and looked at Pan, smiling, "These are speaking flowers; it's a labor of love to have you pick them, making me even more charming!" Thus, she named the peach tree "Flower Official" and playfully called Pan "Master of the Flowers." Later, Pan died of illness, and Yin mourned him deeply. One day, she passed by the villa and saw the peach trees in full bloom. She leaned on the railing, gazing into the distance, and was suddenly overwhelmed with thoughts, feeling as if she were lost. She returned home and fell ill. Yin had a cousin named Huisheng, skilled in painting. Upon hearing of her condition, he said, "This is a heart ailment; I shall treat it with a remedy for the heart." He then painted dozens of peach trees, depicting Pan with a bare head beneath them. Around him, he painted five or six beautiful women: one plucking a flower, another smelling it, a third holding a flower and asking Pan to help her pin it in her hair, and a fourth playfully tossing petals onto his face from her lap. After finishing the painting, he went to her bedside to inquire about her recent condition. Yin wept without speaking.

Huisheng said, "In the past, when my brother-in-law was still alive, he once asked me to paint a scroll of leisure and joy. I feared that my sister would be angry and kept it at my place for a long time. Now that he has passed away and is buried in the netherworld, I believe my sister will forgive me, so I specially returned the precious picture." He then took out the painting and gave it to Yin. Yin examined it closely for a long time, and suddenly his face turned red as he said, "Did a faithless man do such a thing?" Huisheng replied, "Sister, you are mistaken! When a man leaves the embroidered curtain (a metaphor for the home), he should immediately soar into the clouds. If not Lian Po (a historical figure known for his loyalty and integrity), who could be so careful with the writing desk (a metaphor for being meticulous in one's duties)? Since it has already happened, it cannot be undone, let it go." Yin angrily changed his expression and said, "If so, then death would still be too late! What do I have to lose?" Huisheng pretended to advise and withdrew. Thereby, Yin's heart disease gradually eased, and within ten days, he suddenly and completely recovered. Huisheng took the painting and threw it into the fire, and ordered the family members to each hold axes and shovels and go to the villa to cut down all the crabapple trees. Dui said, "This is the old trick of Yuan Qian treating the wife of Prince of Poyang. When grief suddenly subsided, jealousy arose again. The cutting down of the crabapple trees is no different from the wife of Yuan Yi cutting down peaches. Liu Xiaobiao's three samenesses and Wang Wenumu's four fears — I know even those in the netherworld would be terrified."

Harmonious Bell. Volume Three The Young Girl Embraces Buddhism Chen Qiqin from Rongjiang, a young girl who served in a noble household, was educated alongside her female companions from a young age. At fifteen, she excelled in composing poetry and enjoyed filling her works with the style of Northern Song dynasty lyric poetry (小令). For example, in her *Farewell to Spring* poem, she wrote, "A stream of petals and the sound of flowing water stretch long; who knows this is the path of spring's departure?" This line was included in Xu Ruobing's *Ran Zhi Za Lu* (燃脂杂录) from the Nanlou family. In her *Preface to Shi Shijun's Collection*, she penned, "I lament that I was not born a scholar; in the market of wine and tea, I yield to Liu Yong, the poet, as if by imperial decree." Her graceful and free-spirited nature is evident in these verses. Afterward, she abandoned her poetic pursuits and turned entirely to Buddhism, with Buddhist scriptures like the *Leng Yan Sutra* (楞严经) and the *Nirvana Sutra* (涅槃经) piled neatly beside her mirror and cosmetics box. At the time, a strict Buddhist monk named Huigong came from Jingci Temple and took residence at Suiguang East Monastery. Chen Qiqin sought him out and asked to learn the *samadhi* (三昧法). Huigong replied, "To enter samadhi, one must first sever the six senses." She responded, "I understand." Huigong sat cross-legged on a cushion and loudly posed questions: "How is the method of having no eyes?" She replied, "With thick curtains, I tire of seeing paired flowers; from a high tower, I fear to see swallows nesting together." "What is the method of having no ears?" She answered, "Do not let the flute disturb the willows; do not allow the flute to attract phoenixes." "How is the method of having no nose?" She said, "The orchid does not bear the airs of royalty; the daylily cannot distinguish the fragrance of a maiden." "What is the method of having no tongue?" She concluded, "I am fortunate to have never plowed the dark prison; what concern is it of yours to spit forth the lotus?"

**How is the method of being free from the body?** It is said, "Habitually using the unclean to seduce Xi Shi, foolishly imitating Xiao Lian's display." **How is the method of being free from the mind?** It is said, "Only by having feelings do you create a small disaster, but because of being unobstructed, you reach the spiritual realm." Hui Gong said, "The six senses are purified, the eight defilements must be removed, and I will now give you a further teaching. What is the affliction arising from thoughts?" It is said, "Mistakenly splashing muddy water on lotus leaves." **What is the method of removing it?** It is said, "Seize a steel knife to cut lotus threads." **What is the affliction arising from non-thought?** It is said, "Letting it fly and stick to willow fluff." **What is the method of removing it?** It is said, "Untying a golden bell." **What is the affliction arising from both thought and non-thought?** It is said, "Spring silkworms spin cocoons that bind their entire bodies." **What is the method of removing it?** It is said, "Candles turn to ash and completely burn out." **What is the affliction arising from the self?** It is said, "Clouds that haven't emerged from the mountain already make rain." **What is the method of removing it?** It is said, "A tree that doesn't flower has empty branches." **What is the affliction arising from the self's possessions?** It is said, "Struggling in the rapid stream to beat the drum." **What is the method of removing it?** It is said, "It's good to rely on the current to push the boat." **What is the affliction arising from the nature of the self?** It is said, "Drilling a yew tree to get fire but burning the tree itself." **What is the method of removing it?** It is said, "Frozen water turns to ice and does not make waves." **What is the affliction arising from differences?** It is said, "Grinding ink but still finding it white."

"What method should be used for elimination?" He replied, "If you buy rouge, it becomes red." "What is the method to accept or manage suffering?" He replied, "To watch with pain as one holds the heart of Xishi." "What method should be used for elimination?" He replied, "To ask Magu to scratch one's back due to itching." Master Hui said, "This child is endearing. I will teach you the nine roots. If you can ask and answer one question, I will transmit to you the first profound truth. Where is the root of faith?" He replied, "Dragon Tooth hitting the board." "Where is the root of diligence?" He replied, "Stone Cliff setting up arrows." "Where is the root of mindfulness?" He replied, "Danxia choosing the Buddha." "Where is the root of concentration?" He replied, "Hualin binding the tiger." "Where is the root of wisdom?" He replied, "Snow Peak kicking the ball." "Where is the root of compassion?" He replied, "White Deer hanging the bag." "Where is the root of joy?" He replied, "Damo receiving the bowl." "Where is the root of renunciation?" He replied, "Tathagata suffering on the back." "Where is the root of intention?" He replied, "Heavenly Dragon raising a finger." "So, what is the ultimate outcome?" Qi Qin clapped her hands and chanted, "Eat when hungry, sleep when tired; to understand the first Zen of the lamp transmission, to scatter all the heavenly flowers without attachment, Fenggan has been talking a lot for many years." Master Hui said, "You are truly a seed of the Buddhist door. But if you use words to interpret the scriptures, you will avoid falling into mere verbal Zen!" He handed him the cushion from the seat and said, "When this object breaks, it will be the time for you to take your vow."

Qi Qín bowed and thanked with joined palms, returning home to sit in silence for a long time, remaining silent and without laughter all day, as if learning from the nine years of facing the wall in the land of India for enlightenment. Later, it was heard that the meditation cushion remained unbroken while the woman was buried first. Is this the false words of the teacher? Or will the golden coffin with both feet reveal the form of Kāśyapa and lead to enlightenment? Let's record it, along with the case of Ye Xiaoluan practicing meditation, as a good story in the literary world. Duo said: "In the past, the Fifth Patriarch used the monastic robe to save the world, among five hundred people, he must choose a dull person to give it to. Thus, we know that the realm of the Golden Lotus is not a place for clever people to tread. We who wish to practice the Mahayana path only hope to be reborn countless times, not to be sentient beings." The Poor Scholar Performs Divination Ma Dián from Wu, skilled in poetry and lyrics, but his name was not known in the countryside. Driven by hunger and poverty, he traveled lightly in Yangzhou, visiting all the wealthy and noble with poetry, but after three years, still without meeting anyone. Just as the lotus flowers on the bridge were blooming, a salt merchant hosted a banquet in the garden pavilion, inviting famous scholars from Yangzhou. Ma privately took his poems to go, but the gatekeeper stopped him. Ma pushed the door open and entered directly. The crowd shouted and asked who he was. Ma said: "I am a poor scholar from Wu, who has practiced divination since youth. Now there are many important guests, please allow me to show a small skill." At that time, divination was very popular in Yangzhou. Borrowing the sand tray and other equipment nearby, he arranged them in the courtyard. Ma wrote the talisman and burned it, choosing a servant to help with the matter. The divination board suddenly moved, writing twenty-eight large characters, which said:

A Fragrant Path Through Lotus Blossoms, Long and Winding, I intended to recite poetry and entrust it to the jade flute. I have tread every path of the flat hills, yet no one is seen, So I returned to the small table and crossed the rainbow bridge. The crowd requested him to sign his name. It was written: "I am Kang Duishan, who happened upon a poet and wandered here idly." A salt merchant fell to the ground and bowed, saying, "The First Scholar has arrived!" The learned men also knelt and pleaded, saying, "Your eloquence, the First Scholar, has long been admired. We have already glimpsed a glimpse—now we wish to see the full picture." The spirit-writing said: "Most of my old works I have forgotten, but I have four new poems from *Yangzhou New Lüyuefu*; I offer them for your critique." The first poem says: I borrow from the gods, I pray to the gods, The god of wealth grants me a loan of a thousand gold pieces. I do not accept idle office, nor do I become a merchant, But I buy a young child and teach her to sing and dance. When the child sings a song, gold piles up in the house. When the child dances once, Sichuan silk rises higher than the platform. The red candles flicker, the spring night is short, And I exhaust the wealth of thousands of households. Do not worry about your ruin—squander wealth and go bankrupt, For there will be the god of wealth as your creditor. The second poem says: In February, the east wind blows yellow dust, A flying palanquin arrives on the busy street. The front is not high, the back does not sway, A swollen belly stretches out with feet extended. Before the palanquin, attendants walk; behind it, handsome youths follow. By the roadside, an old man comments repeatedly, praising the father. That old man, in his day, was good at carrying burdens— He carried water from the east gate and sold it at the west gate. The third poem says:

The Grand House at Night and Day The noble house, with its heavy red gates, is quiet at night and bustling during the day. The golden locks and the clinking of keys echo through the windows. In the hall, half of the silver candles have burned out, and the master wakes up to order breakfast. To the left, there is a zither player, and to the right, a singer with a se. Precious flutes and pipes are arranged on both sides, and guests drink and listen to songs, enjoying the music endlessly. When the music ends and the singing concludes, the drum tower strikes the third watch, and the grand feast is cleared. The torches are gathered, and the guests bow and leave through the front gate. The fourth verse says: Nobleman, whose anger are you directing? You are not angry with the performers who come to visit, nor with the salt merchants who arrive. You are only angry with the slow-witted scholar. In his hand is a book, and he bows deeply on the empty steps. Such a scholar, so ignorant and unaware of the world, how can he be considered a good person? Do not aim too high; instead, seek recognition from Han Zhongli. After writing, the scholars all applaud in admiration, and even the salt merchant claps along. Ma looks around and smiles. Then, they see a clay cup on the table, and inside it sits a clay monk, calmly seated in the lotus position. They ask the spirit to write a poem. The spirit writes: I learned Confucianism as a child but am not familiar with Buddhist scriptures. I came here with Master Wugou from Wugong Mountain and asked him to write for me. After a pause, the spirit writes: "I am Master Wugou, the guest monk from Wugong Mountain. I was invited here by Secretary Kang. What do you wish to ask, gentlemen?" The scholars point to the cup on the table and request a poem. The spirit writes: By mistake, I steered the Buddha's boat back to sea, and the waves disrupted the lecture platform. For years, my teachings have become empty forms, and I wish to transform the lotus pond into a wine cup. The Bodhi dew drips into the wine jar, and I become intoxicated on the meditation bed without losing my composure.

A Wakeful Laughter at the Monks, Can One Drink the West River in One Gulp? Later, it was written: "The Chancellor is bringing a courtesan, the humble monk shall retreat." When asked for the name of the courtesan, it was recorded: This is Bian Shun娘, the one referred to in my poem "Inviting Guests" as 'The Green Jade Robe of Qin's Tower.' She once learned the pipa from Zhao Si娘, has a profound understanding of music and rhythm, and also enjoys studying the erotic style of *Jin Quan*. She is also quite refined and elegant. At that time, Wang Tiaoshan and Xu Xiangpo invited poets from the Jiangnan region to compose poems in response to thirty *Lü Chun Ci* (Green Spring Lyrics). Various scholars then presented their original drafts for harmonization. The oracle said: "All of you are renowned scholars, yet you are struggling to compose poetry within seven steps, and are seeking inspiration from the dance and song of courtesans? Unable to help it, I will write a poem on your behalf." It was written: West of the Ruan family's wall, east of the Song family's home, a row of sparse curtains seems like a dream. In the deep courtyard, the rain of the sparrow-hen brings forth flowers, and the wind of the willow hangs over the painted railings. The jade vase, tired of its plainness, adds purple mountains; the step screen, hating the cold, replaces it with sea-red. Year after year, the grass by the southern bank turns green, yet the parting sorrow troubles Wen Tong. The lotus palace is separated by layers, and the phoenix returns but never meets again. The water of the belt drowns the moonlit river, and the sword peak cuts through the rain and cloud peaks. Why is the face hot when washing? The wine to drown sorrow is not strong enough. Stealthily walking under the money-flipping hall, the chess tray and medicine cauldron are all covered in dust. Suddenly, following the plum and willow, crossing the spring river, suddenly seeing the peach root leaning against the painted boat. Again calling for Xue'er to play the brocade zither, urging Yunmu to spread the gauze window.

At the tips of her shoes, peacocks bowed in a thousand reverences; beneath her sleeves, eighteen pairs of mandarin ducks were embroidered. Beside her, in the hall behind, she found compassion to dwell—when would this demon of love ever be subdued? Coldly she laughed at the jiao liao bird, clinging to a single branch; build her a golden house, do not mind the delay! Peach blossoms encircle the house of the morning star; peonies bloom at the steps on the Shangsi Festival. From the northwest tower, she watches the sunrise; in the southeast, the peacock flees the wind’s touch. With embroidered camels, she moves her furnishings; incense manuals and tea classics are etched in snow-like verse. The pavilion is delicate and near the emerald heights; to set up a bed and grind grain is not entirely wrong. The screen opens like a turtle shell, inviting floral companions; the curtain rolls up like shrimp whiskers, revealing the swallow’s gate. Her twenty-five-stringed zither is difficult to play; her thirteen-lined script is imitated in a thick, flowing hand. At times, she smiles as she picks up Han Yan’s treasures; she strikes up the yellow oriole to fly in pairs. Her comb, shaped like a rhinoceros horn, rounds the ice; nine beams of flowers are inserted into the two empty hairpins. Her refined spirit is too lofty to learn the cicada’s hairstyle; with lowered hands, she worries as she adjusts her swallow-like skirt. Her short hair is disheveled after picking vegetables; her shy brows are smoothed at the beginning of her first bloom. Beneath the crystal curtain, there is no more space; in her eagerness to watch her comb her hair, she forgets the book. With gentle steps like a lotus, she needs no support; where the鲛绡 is undone, her icy skin is revealed. Frowning, she longs for three years of mugwort; in good spirits, is she ready to pay a measure of pearls? Relying on her shoes, she is still useful to be commanded; when she lifts her slippers, she still resents the lack of affection. The old poem of Gan Zhen, the husband once read; it is good to paint the image of the Luo River’s silk socks. Only seven poems were written; the learned men vied for brushes and inkstones, copying them with heart and hand, yet the spirit moved so swiftly that the rest could not be fully recorded. Only these are recorded:

A device with bent iron hooks to prevent moisture from entering the eyes, a secret transmission of something intimate tested in the wind. The child experiences the evening breeze as flowers bloom at night, and the continuous rainy season passes after the spring equinox. The wish has been fulfilled, and the plan in the heart has come to fruition; the wound on the arm has healed, and the snake doctor's mark has faded. A dish with five spicy ingredients offered amidst fragrant flowers, a charm written in the "Six Jia" script tied around the waist. A medicine for longevity grown in a fragrant room, a lamp that prolongs life guided by the colorful sleeves. A plant that returns in the night rain, flowers that are not useful in the spring breeze. Looking into shallow water, the desire to build a strong tower to block the blue sky. My friend Liu Dongli was present, and he presented his painting of "Picking the Herb of Immortality" and asked for a poem. The spirit medium replied: "My child's wrist has weakened, why don't you understand? And this is not my forte. The play 'The Wolf of Zhongshan' by Dongjun has spread among the chrysanthemum troupe, why don't you still ask me to write?" The spirit medium remained silent for a long time, then wrote: "How laughable is this foolish child, always escaping literary debts. I will take up the remaining courage to respond to Liu Jun's request." The poem reads: Myriad rare flowers and grasses fill the level fields, rushing towards the east wind, returning to the green mountains. Tilling the fragrant soil and passing through the dew, a small basket carrying clouds. Who is the true friend? Only a handsome mountain boy who sweeps the path. The rain of flowers drifts, the birds startled by the cold stand on the tree tips, the silk threads sway, the woodcutter treads leaves across the flat bridge. Who can describe this serene scene in the sky? Half a life of picking herbs is unknown to others. Unknown to others, the master points out the mountains.

As a Taoist monk, I was originally from the Liu family, why not fish in the Xiang River? My courtesy name is Dongli, why not pour wine along the chrysanthemum path? All day long, I pass by the front stream, gather jade shoots, follow the fragrant bank, and search for aromatic herbs. The winding mountain valleys are like a riddle, my steps have taken countless turns. It is not that I am wasting effort seeking trouble, but because I have been a hero for half my life. Debts from drinking and poetry, wandering the lakes and seas, I end up being melancholic like Song Yu, often ill like Sima Xiangru, with my temples gray. Where can I find the money to buy a mountain retreat? The path through Zhongnan Mountain is clever, I look forward to the elixir that preserves youth, and the formula that extends life. I have thrown away my brush, planted a flower marker, a small stage, alchemical equipment, a grand life, mythical fruits, and they have made the young mountain boys' heads shake. Please, sir, mock me, let's all clap our hands and laugh heartily at the foot of the mountain. Guess the mystery behind these words, but don't overdo it. Haven't you seen lazy Ji Kang's efforts at longevity being ineffective, and old Huang Gong's fasting being futile? It is natural to think, the burning of the red snow elixir: there is no need to grind the mysterious frost in a mortar. Various kinds of phoenix and crane glues, who will they mend? Why bother to draw the pursuit of immortality on paper? This is mostly due to the lively music and festivities in the spring, the opulence of the wealthy, and the poverty of the poor. There are a few cultured young people who also stand by the red bridge, listening to the jade flute. They rush to the painted boat, float on the fairy table, accompany the official pavilion, write poetry, but they do not recognize the old mountain with its idle breeze.

The melody. Therefore, withdrawing from the painting early, he wrote several layers of green mountains gently outlining the waist, added a few more green trees with ten thousand tender leaves, and jumped out of the trap of sorrow. He called it *The Gathering of Chrysanthemums*, which is material for becoming a deity. Solving this riddle, surely make you laugh. Already picked up by a scholar who had buried his books, spreading the truth widely. After finishing the poem, Liu bowed and thanked. The salt merchant said, "The状元's literary talent, cannot be delayed." He ordered Ma to write a talisman and send it. Soon after, he greeted the guests and seated them, ordering Ma to sit by his side. At the table, they chattered incessantly, praising each other. Some stretched their throats and pressed their throats, singing in a long, drawn-out voice. Ma could not bear it any longer and said, "The writings of the乩仙, absolutely lack the startling phrases of Xie Tiao, why do the gentlemen need to praise them so much?" The crowd shouted, "A frog in a well dares to criticize, this is also a madman!" The salt merchant said, "I think he doesn't understand the matter. How could there be any mistakes in the writings of the状元?" Ma said, "The gentleman considers me an outsider? I have a humble manuscript, I wish to present it for your critique." The scholars just glanced at it and said, "This is the work of a poor scholar filled with pedantry, what is there to talk about in poetry!" They read several more, all saying it was not good. The salt merchant said, "How could a beggar write poetry, what wonderful points could there be? The gentlemen need not waste their eyes." The scholars also criticized with their mouths and hands, thoroughly belittling him. Then, when they read the later part of the manuscript, the previously written couplets and the four new poems from *New Music Bureau* were clearly listed, and they fell silent, looking at each other in shock.

Ma slammed his hand on the table and stood up, saying, "You all are mediocre, truly like frogs in a well criticizing the ocean. I, though not talented, foolishly take pride in my literary skills. I didn't expect that after three years of wandering, I haven't met a single true appreciator. I suspect that recent celebrities only seek poetry under the hats of officials, ascribing their names to the top scholar to increase the value of their writings. They marvel at strange talents when they write and call anything rhymed a masterpiece. They falsely attribute their names to beautiful women and falsely use Buddhist terms, to make the blind followers follow suit without understanding. Attributing names to poems is an ancient and persistent habit. How could even a discerning judge like Jiǔ Fāng Gāo recognize qualities beyond the obvious?" The scholars were sweating and dejected, hiding their faces against the wall. The salt merchant laughed heartily and said, "The Jiangnan youth is indeed cunning, as expected." They quickly invited him to sit at the head table and competed to offer him large cups of wine, and they asked him to keep quiet about the matter. Ma replied, "Criticism of poetry is common everywhere, not just among you." Thus, they took turns toasting and drinking, enjoying themselves until they dispersed. Later, the scholars unanimously elected Ma as their leader. The salt merchant's family vied to invite him and often gave him a thousand gold coins to support his family. But as his reputation grew, his methods became crude, and he could no longer sing 'The City of Wei' (a metaphor for refined poetry).

The speaker said, "If someone can help me out of this predicament, even those with great ambition still have regrets, how much more so if it is used as a strategy to alleviate poverty? At first they belittled each other, then they began to form cliques. The poetic spirit is impure, when will it be cleansed? I will lament the loss of the Kang family drum and compose 'Yuyang Three Variations.'" **Old-Faced Ghost** When my teacher, Mr. Zhang Chumen, was teaching at Dongshan on Dongting Lake, Yan Aiting and Qian Xiangling had not yet joined the literary circle, and they studied together in the same room. One evening, as they discussed literature by the light of a lamp, a ghost peeked in through a loose lattice. At first, its face was like a winnowing basket, then it resembled an overturned pot, and later it grew as large as a watermelon. Its eyebrows were like brooms, its eyes like bells, and its high cheekbones were piled with five dou of mundane dust. The teacher looked at it with a smile and took out his work *Jumobian*, saying, "Do you recognize these characters?" The ghost remained silent. The teacher said, "If you don't know the characters, why bother putting on such a big face?" He then flicked the ghost's face with two fingers, producing a sound like rotten leather. He burst out laughing and said, "With such thick skin, it's no wonder you don't understand the situation!" The ghost was greatly ashamed and shrank to the size of a bean. The teacher turned to his students and said, "I thought he was always putting on such a big act, but in reality, he's a faceless person, coming here to cause trouble." He took out his dagger and slashed at the ghost, which fell to the ground with a metallic sound. He picked it up and saw that it was nothing but a small coin.

The Bell said, "The transformation of money into a deity, scholars expounding, is indeed as it is. When Cangjie created writing, ghosts wept; when Zhou Jing minted coins, ghosts laughed. Do ghosts not know how to read but love money, is this a common trait? Yet there are those who know how to read and still love money; I cannot fathom what kind of malevolent spirit they are!" The Deity of Obstructed Vision In Wu County, there were two bureaus, one in the north and one in the south, each with a machine room hall. Beside the hall, there was a statue of a deity known as the Deity of Obstructed Vision. One night, a guard saw the deity emerge, top-hatted and sash-wrapped, swarming out. Several days later, while staying in the hall, the guard saw the deity return. The deity was dressed in green robes and had an uncovered head, looking as if covered in soot and ashes. The seated official asked about it. The deity replied, "Just now, someone took off my hat and sash!" The official asked, "Who was it?" The deity replied, "I don't know." The official asked, "What crime did they commit?" The deity replied, "I don't know either. Earlier, at the hall's veranda, I met dozens of people in green robes, who offered me a thousand gold pieces, leading me to a place where outside the walls were thorns, and red decorations hung on the door. In the middle was a golden plaque with characters. The people in green robes led me inside, where I saw hundreds of small houses on both sides, people shouting and ringing bells, not knowing what they were doing. Soon, I ascended two levels of steps to a hall, which had a very solemn layout. There were two elderly people sitting on both sides, and two long tables set up below, covered with red carpets. On the carpets, there were piles of something, but I don't know what they were."

A group of people sat in a circle, arguing loudly. Someone dressed in green urged the narrator to cover their eyes. The narrator then used their left hand to cover the upper part of the hall and their right hand to cover the lower part. Shortly after, a man dressed in blue arrived and asked, "Who are you?" The narrator replied, "I am the person responsible for covering the eyes in the machine room." The blue-clad man angrily said, "Your deceptive tricks are only suitable for the marketplace! How dare you come here? And it is now midday, with those in charge having clear judgment. How can such people manipulate things?" He ordered a man in red to remove the narrator's headgear and belt. Then, a blue-faced ghost took a brush, dipped it in ink, and painted the narrator's face several times, chasing them out of the door. The narrator hurriedly tried to find the person in green, but they had already fled. The narrator returned in a disheveled state and sought shelter under a roof. The upper seat holder thought about it for a while and said, "Such an unusual event is also beyond my understanding. The person and place can be investigated." Suddenly, the guard sneezed, and the sound then disappeared. Later, the narrator recounted the story to their peers, who discussed it in a confused manner, and no one could recognize or understand the story.

Diao said, "This place is where we used to hang out, but no one knows it, whether they ask, answer, recount, or listen. People in the past considered not reading books as being a happy immortal, but this is where they suffer." Cheating in the imperial examinations is a crime that the law must punish. Since he is a commoner, the punishment can be slightly reduced. But to dip the pen and smear the face, resulting in a stupid-looking face, instead adding a few more strokes of writing, and drawing a ghost in blue, can be considered rewarding evil. Someone said, "Those who fail due to greed are called 'black' (墨), to serve as a warning!" Ou Jia'an, a student, adds this note. To make a name by burning or recording something.

Shi Yunyu, courtesy name Zhi Ru, was famous for his literary talent, yet he was actually a person of the Way (Dao). He once told me, "Our writings cannot support the established teachings, but whenever we encounter books that offend these teachings, we must destroy them thoroughly. We should keep a paper repository at home, called 'Sea of Sin.' This is to throw them into the turbid stream, hoping to prevent them from spreading their influence." One day, when he came across the *Records of Four Dynasties* from Fujian, he was so enraged that he slammed the table. He quickly consulted his wife, took off the gold bracelet from his arm, pawned it for fifty thousand cash, and searched all the bookstores, finding over three hundred and forty copies, which he intended to burn. I happened to pass by his study and was surprised, so I asked him about it. Shi said, "This book contains accounts of the past dynasties and writings by famous scholars, which are无可訾议. However, it includes a report accusing Zhu Wen Gong, which is absurd and unreasonable. It describes acts of disobeying one's mother and deceiving the emperor, seizing power and forming factions, and even private, scandalous affairs. These are things that a petty person would never do, yet they are presented in official documents, defaming our upright and virtuous people! Moreover, the compiler anticipated that future generations would not deeply believe it, so he included a table of Wen Gong's apology to substantiate the accusations. Alas! How could a petty person be so shameless as to go this far?" I replied, "What is there to be surprised about? Those who enjoy great fame are inevitably envied by many. Especially since Zhu Wen Gong in his youth engaged with the classics, became overly influenced by Buddhism and Daoism, and small scholars are easily tainted by the stench of the hall. Later, he achieved the true path of Neo-Confucianism, with high and steep gates, and was supported by brothers like Pai Xishan and Zhen Jingyuan. Those who clung to him were all driven out of the gate. Thus, he turned shame into anger, wanting to ruin his reputation but finding no opportunity."

During his stay at Xianhe Hall, Wen Gong wrote two letters containing phrases such as "ministers fail in their duties, and villains seize power," which displeased the emperor, and also accused Tang Zhongyou of illegal actions. These criticisms offended high officials, leading him to carefully frame his words before rashly submitting them to the emperor. One reason was to avenge the resentment of being dismissed, and another was to please the officials in the capital. This behavior clearly revealed the heart and liver of a villain. The stone replied, "Then why did Wen Gong not defend himself?" I responded, "Wen Gong served during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, who summoned him three times to the imperial court and received three sealed memorials from him. He laid bare his heart and liver, criticizing close ministers. Emperor Xiaozong was open and receptive, ordering him to hold the positions of governor in Zhejiang and Jiangxi, and later gave him the command to teach in the imperial academy. His favor became even thicker, but the jealousy of others grew deeper. At the time, officials in the capital requested his dismissal, even suggesting that he should be executed. A wise gentleman preserves himself, and often praised for his eloquence, he was subjected to humiliation. Moreover, the balance between reason and desire is subtle, and every detail must be distinguished, still fearing that doubts might arise and mislead future scholars. In terms of conduct in court and personal behavior, there will always be a public opinion. It is like holding a passerby and pointing snow as black and pitch as white; even the stupidest person would know it is wrong, but to argue endlessly about it?" The stone said, "Your argument is indeed good, but this can be told to the wise, but is difficult to explain to the common people!" Finally, he burned the text. I said, "You can be called brave in upholding righteousness!"

That year, Shi passed the provincial examination in the southern region and, in the Gengxu cycle, took the metropolitan examination, becoming the top scorer at the grand assembly. Was this his merit in supporting and upholding the teachings of Confucianism? Du said, "The burning of the imperial library by Qin Shi Huang, the First Emperor, is a grievance that lasts for a thousand years, as it led to the destruction of the classics. If Ding Yi, who labored without reward, were not remembered with a good name; and Zhu Rong, who received undeserved rewards, became a celebrated figure, then we must destroy indiscriminately, without fear of spreading the ashes of Qin Shi Huang's burned library!" Reading Can Be Ridiculous Xu Cong, courtesy name Zhifu, was an orphan who grew up poor. He had just started reciting the Four Books when he could not afford a tutor, so he borrowed reading from the upper hall of the Moon Sound Monastery. The monk Yin Yuan was a remarkable person. In his spare time from reciting scriptures, he would sit cross-legged on a cushion and listen to Xu read. Whenever Xu reached a point he liked, Yin Yuan would clap his hands and praise him, ordering the attendant to give him tea, bamboo shoots, fruits, and pastries. If Xu ever expressed gratitude, Yin Yuan would stand up respectfully and say, "You are a gentleman who reads, and this humble abbey is simple and unrefined; please do not take offense." Later, Xu became a student in the Imperial Academy and continued to read at night as before. However, Yin Yuan closed his eyes and lowered his eyebrows, seemingly not paying much attention. When Xu would hold a book and recite aloud, Yin Yuan would rush to the meditation bed and lie stiffly under the blanket. Subsequently, when Xu visited, Yin Yuan did not engage in any conversation.

In the year of Wu Zi, Xu Dengxian wrote to visit the hermitage to offer congratulations, and the footprints nearly filled the place, yet Yin Yuan remained as lonely as before. At that time, Xu was preparing for the imperial examination, diligently studying and often reading until dawn. Yin Yuan suddenly shouted, "The barking of donkeys and dogs, the persistent chatter; please retreat three lodgings, do not mix me with you." Xu was startled and said to Yin Yuan, "Although I am unworthy, I have been praised by my teacher. Why have you become so arrogant now?" Yin Yuan replied, "When you first came, you read the wise sayings and teachings of ancient sages and virtuous men, so I was deeply impressed. Since you became a scholar, you have read superficial and redundant words, which are devoid of meaning and have already become tiresome to listen to. Now that you have achieved high honors in the imperial examination, the things you read are getting worse and worse, almost like simple folk songs and pastoral tunes, unbearable to the ear. Your humility before and arrogance afterward, this is self-inflicted, what do I have to do with it?" Xu said, "You are an outsider, not understanding the secrets of reading. We have established methods for reading. As children, we started with the Four Books and Five Classics, and as we grew older, we read the historical works of the Han dynasty, the *Chuci* (Songs of Chu), the writings of Han Yu, Liu Zongyuan, Ouyang Xiu, and Su Shi, and practiced for the imperial examinations. We read the works of Chengzu, Hongwu, Longqing, Wanli, Tianqi, and Chongzhen, and the contemporary examination essays. After passing the preliminary exams, we studied the examination papers from the two seasons of the imperial examination, mastered them thoroughly, and then could pass the imperial examination. Why are you confused and talking so much?" Yin Yuan said, "Originally, Confucianism and Buddhism are different. Buddhism aims for continuous improvement, while Confucianism is a step down at each stage!" Xu was speechless.

Yinyuan pondered for a long time, then suddenly burst into laughter and said, "You use your own methods, and I will continue to read my books. Scholars have their own established ways; just don't be misled by monks." He gently agreed and withdrew.

Duo said, "Buddhism has its own view of existence and non-existence, while Confucianism permeates from top to bottom, all being part of the same principle. Why should we create artificial divisions between them? Scholars often criticize monks, and monks also criticize scholars. In reality, those who criticize monks are adhering to the methods of Buddhism, and those who criticize scholars are following the methods of Confucianism." Mirror Play Feng Yeha from Wuhu was known for his integrity and courage in dealing with people, but he was uniquely constrained by his wife. In his middle age, lacking an heir, he purchased a concubine and forbade her from sharing his bed. Once, they whispered privately in a secluded place, and his wife saw them. She called out to heaven and beat the ground, cursing in many ways. Feng was intimidated by her and dared not speak up. One day, a scholar visited his home. Feng invited him to sit and asked where he came from. The scholar said, "I am a person from Qintai, good at recognizing people's courage. Having experienced the world for a long time, I have seen that those who read books lack the courage to write, those who polish shields lack the courage to break through enemies, those who wear official robes lack the courage to speak frankly and offer advice, and those who wear fine clothes lack the courage to entrust their wives and children. I have heard of your high integrity, so I came to see your courage and strategy." Feng was very pleased and wanted to demonstrate his courage to the scholar. The scholar said, "You truly have a courageous and righteous spirit, which I can clearly see. But you must strengthen it with wisdom and spirit, so you won't lose your courage!" Feng replied confidently, "Although I may not be as strong as Changshan Gong, who was all muscle, I have been enduring hardships for many years. I am sure I won't be afraid like a baby in a swaddling cloth." He clapped his hands and spoke enthusiastically, showing great self-confidence. The scholar admired him greatly.

Not long after, a loud and angry voice came from the inner chambers, but Feng remained unconcerned and continued to talk and laugh as usual. Soon after, the sound of a pot breaking in the kitchen echoed, like the collapse of a copper mountain and the response of a bell in Luoyang. Feng still struggled to control himself. Shortly, he heard the sound of beating and punishment in the hall, the cries of those being beaten, and the noisy and clamorous sounds of the maids and servants trying to mediate. Feng's face gradually changed color. Then, an old woman ran to tell him, "Madam has rolled up her sleeves and is standing hidden behind the screen with a pestle." Feng slowly stood up and moved away from his seat. Suddenly, the sound of the pestle hitting the ground behind the screen, a harsh and loud voice shouted, "Who is the reckless boy who is leading men astray and acting so boldly?" Feng's face turned pale as paper. The scholar glared angrily and said, "Strange indeed: it started large like an egg, then became small like a mustard seed; if threatened again, it will surely break!" He quickly stood up and wanted to leave, but Feng forcibly held him back. The scholar said, "I came because I thought you had courage and strength, to see the general outline. I did not expect you to be just a cowardly and timid man!"

Before the speaker's words were finished, a stick flew out from behind the screen, striking the scholar's left arm with a resounding sound, and transforming into an ancient mirror. Upon picking it up and examining it, they saw the inscription "zhao dan" (lit. "illuminate the gallbladder") on its back, recognizing it as an artifact from the Qin dynasty. The woman took the mirror to look at herself, and her gallbladder appeared as large as a jar, still emitting anger. When she looked at Feng, it was as thin as half a grain of millet, with green water dripping. Upon checking, it turned out to be broken! The speaker said, "He is a man, I am a man, why should I fear him? She is a woman, I am a man, how can I not fear her? Record this as a warning for those who fail to be men. Those who are weak and cowardly only feel ashamed of their incompetence, which leads to fierce and violent behavior. But wise and intelligent people fear that the ugliness of their family will be exposed to the outside world, often suppress their voices and endure insults to maintain a good reputation. Thus, they establish their authority and spread it throughout the world. Yuan Zhi caught a foot, Taifu closed the curtain, Wang Maohong's ox cart, Fang Xuanling's poisoned wine, can serve as a warning. However, the Five Tigers of Yanping, even ghosts fear them. Without the medicine of Duranxiang to heal wounds, it is not easy to save all beings. I still remember in the year of Gengyin, while recuperating in the Red Peony House, I wrote the legendary play 'The Golden Band' to predict the punishment of a domineering wife, performed in the hall of Song Guancha. As soon as it was performed, all the men in the audience turned pale and left. The domineering wife's jealousy has not been punished, but the coward's courage has already fled. How dangerous!"

**The Mockery Note** Chen Xiaowu, a man from Zhu Zhu Li in Wu, was arrogant due to his talents and often trampled on others. One day, someone delivered a note to his door. Upon reading it, he saw that it was from a fellow student named "Someone" who respectfully submitted the note. Chen was surprised that he had never met this person before and wondered why they had come. The guest, wearing a golden hat and luxurious clothing, entered abruptly, bowed respectfully, and took the seat of honor. Chen asked about the guest's family background, and the guest replied, "I am from Gui'an in Zhejiang. I have searched everywhere for refined people, but never met any. Now that I have heard of your high talent, I have come to visit." After speaking, the guest confidently stroked his beard, laughed arrogantly, and remained self-assured. Chen looked at him sideways and laughed, saying, "Ah! How strange! In this world, there are people who don't know a single word of literature, yet they can recognize my high talent. This is truly a remarkable thing!" The guest was startled and said, "Although I may not be talented, how can you know that I don't understand a single word just because we met by chance? Why do you openly insult me?" Chen replied, "A person's lack of knowledge is not determined by their ability to discuss literature and classics. Even looking at your name on this note, where is a single word of literature?"

The guest asked for his explanation. Chen replied, "Although you are a distant descendant of noble lineage, my family has been farming for several generations, never having been listed in the official records. The term 'year' in your address is unclear in its meaning. As for referring to us as kin, there is no familial connection between me and the nobility, so this term should also be omitted. You studied in Zhejiang, and I attended school in Wu; the term 'classmates' is entirely fabricated. I am only thirty-two years old, but your hair and beard are already gray, and calling yourself my younger brother is too modest. Just now, when I saw you enter, I bowed, and you immediately took the seat of honor. I haven't even performed a full bow, how can you say I should bow? Consider whether any of these terms in your address can be justified. To say you don't understand is clearly true, how could I be insulting you?" The guest said, "You are truly inexperienced, this is just a common practice for name cards!" Chen replied, "If you treat me according to this common practice, how can you expect to find refined people in the world?" Turning away, he went in. The guest hesitated for a long time, collected his name card, and left in a hurry. Duo said, "Regulations should be widely understood, etiquette should follow customs, especially for something as trivial as a name card. And yet it caused ridicule. Only then did I realize that Mr. Zhengping, who had his name card with the middle character erased, kept it in his pocket and did not hand it in, showing great insight."

A Single Coin Leads to Dismissal A man from Nanchang, whose father was a deputy teacher at the Imperial Academy, followed him to the capital. Once, passing by Yanshou Temple Street, he saw a young man counting coins to buy the *Lüshi Chunqiu* (Master Lü’s Spring and Autumn Annals) at a bookshop, and a coin accidentally fell to the ground. The man secretly stepped on the coin, waited for the youth to leave, and then picked it up. An old man sitting nearby stared at him for a long time, then suddenly got up, asked the man his name, and left with a cold laugh. Later, the man, having passed the upper scholar examination, entered the transcription office and was selected as the magistrate of Changshu County in Jiangsu. He packed and prepared to take up his post, presenting his credentials to the higher authorities. At that time, Governor-General Qian'an Tang Gong was in charge of Jiangsu. The man visited him ten times but could not get an audience. The guard delivered a message from Tang Gong, ordering the man not to take up his post, as his name had already been listed in the impeachment edict. When asked what he was accused of, the guard replied, “Corruption.” The man thought to himself that he had not yet taken up his post, so how could he have any embezzled funds? There must be some mistake. He urgently wanted to explain in person. The guard went inside to report, and soon returned with another message from Tang Gong: “Do you not remember the incident at the bookshop many years ago? As a scholar, you valued a single coin as if it were your life; now that you have been fortunate enough to become a local official, how could you not reach into bags and open chests, becoming a robber under the official hat? Please immediately resign, and do not make the whole road cry!” The man finally realized that the old man who had asked for his name was none other than Governor-General Qian'an Tang Gong. He felt ashamed and resigned from his post. Being impeached before even taking up his duties was indeed an unexpected event. This story is recorded to warn those who are careless in their conduct.

The bell said: "The god of money can take on countless forms, each tempting people into making mistakes. Do not think that one coin is insignificant. A small stream that is not blocked will become a river, and a small fire that is not extinguished will become a great conflagration. I wish those who uphold ritual propriety to start with one coin." **Two Fingers Inscribed on a Banner** Zhao Rongjiang, before he had passed the imperial examinations, was staying at the home of Lu in the east city. At that time, the mistress of the house was a widow with a seven-year-old son who was studying under Zhao Rongjiang. One night, while reading by candlelight, he heard a knock on the door. He opened it and admitted her—it was the mistress of the house. He asked where she had come from, but she smiled without saying anything. He firmly questioned her. She said, "You have been away from home for a long time, sleeping alone in loneliness. Tonight is a good night with fine weather. I dare not think about it, but I offer myself to you, to pass this good night." Zhao Rongjiang sternly said, "A woman values her reputation and chastity, a man values his integrity and moral boundaries. If one does not love oneself, one will lose each other. You should return quickly, people's words are very frightening!" The woman stood firmly and did not move. Zhao Rongjiang pushed her out the door, but the woman turned around and entered again. Zhao Rongjiang quickly closed the door, and two of her fingers were caught in the gap. She let out a loud cry of pain. When he slightly opened the door, she slipped away. The woman returned home, closed the door, and lay down to sleep. She suddenly thought, "How could a widow of a respectable family do such a shameful act, to the point of being so degraded?" She tossed and turned in her bed, feeling both shame and regret. She quickly got up, took out her dagger, and cut off her two fingers. Blood flowed profusely, and she nearly died but revived. She secretly took her two fingers, mixed them with lime, and carefully stored them. Zhao Rongjiang was unaware of this, and the next day he packed his belongings and returned home.

After his son Cheng had been promoted and entered a government office, he requested a posthumous honor for his mother. At that time, Rongjiang, a high-ranking official, was already in a position of great importance. The request was repeatedly submitted and repeatedly rejected, and Cheng did not understand why. Upon returning home, he recounted the matter to his mother. His mother smiled and said, "I know now." She took out a small box made of sandalwood, sealed it, and handed it to her son, saying, "Go present this to your teacher, and there will be a revelation." Cheng, following his mother's instructions, presented the box to his teacher. Rongjiang opened it and saw two severed fingers lying side by side inside, with faint bloodstains still visible on the soil. He then suddenly realized the truth and, on the same day, submitted a formal request for the posthumous honor. This event is recorded in the *Zhao Family Genealogy*, and it was related to me by his close relative, Mr. Shen.

The Bell said: "It is easy to be in poverty and lowly status, but difficult to be in wealth and high status. When Rongjiang had not yet passed the imperial examinations, he shut the door to repel visitors, displaying a dignified and awe-inspiring presence that was hard to offend. Wasn't this the conduct of a person who maintains their integrity and dignity? Yet this woman managed to preserve her late virtue, while Rongjiang ultimately joined the ranks of the treacherous, harmed by his desire for fame and success. Isn't this also what is meant by 'focusing on trivial matters and losing sight of the bigger picture'? For we who read books and discuss the world, we must broaden our perspective, not protecting the faults of the virtuous, nor obscuring the strengths of the unvirtuous. For example, Li Guangbi's defiance of imperial orders is essentially disobedience to authority. Wen Tai-zhen's tearing off of his robe is essentially disrespect to his parents. The deep resentment of Xie Dao-yun towards Wang Lang is essentially disrespect to one's husband. Xu Pu's giving fertile land to his elder brother while secretly taking the title of xiao lian is essentially disrespect to one's elder brother. Wang Zhong-hui's angry beating of his son, not allowing him to mourn a fellow student's death, is essentially disrespect to friends. As for the greatest traitors in history, none compare to Cao Cao, yet Mi Heng did not kill himself, which cannot be said to be anything but appreciating talent, and Wen Ji's distant redemption cannot be said to be anything but upholding justice. Qin Hui's poem 'Inscription on the Ode to Bo Yi' clearly shows the noble intention of Yu and Ji. Yan Fen-ying's ten years of studying at Qianshan Hall, enduring cold and snow, is not much different from the high moral standards of a virtuous person. Yet the virtuous always remain virtuous, and the unvirtuous always remain unvirtuous, for a single fault is not enough to overshadow great virtue, and small good deeds cannot cover up major faults. Thus, I recall the story of Zhao Rongjiang and its related events." Harmonious Bell, Volume 4 The Prohibition of Alcohol Old Master Deng, whose name is lost, sold soup in the market of Handan. One evening, a disheveled servant carried a gourd to the old man to get wine.

The Old Man gazed at him intently. He said, "You were recently entrusted to a fragrant neighbor, do you not recognize me?" The Old Man ignored his question. After more than a month, he no longer came. Later, they met at the Lu Sheng Shrine, where the Old Man forcibly invited him into his shop, recounting their parting and reunion, and offered him a cup of "Wangtou Lihua Chun" wine. A disheveled servant hurriedly grabbed his arm and laughed, saying, "Do not mislead me again. Let me tell you honestly: I am the Willow Immortal under the Pure Yang Master. In the past, when I followed my master to Yueyang, I saw him drunk three times, and his throat would always itch. My master was stingy and gave me not a single drop, so I went to buy wine every day to quench my thirst. When my master went to the City of Fragrant Flowers for a flower-washing banquet, he ordered me to guard the medicine furnace. Bored by the solitude, I drank the old wine from the gourd and got drunk, then lay drunk beside the furnace. When my master returned, he blamed me for failing to guard the furnace. I defended myself by saying I was drunk, and my master became angry. I said, 'My Eastern Master spends his days in the land of wine, why does he alone impose a ban on me?' My master replied, 'What I drink is wine; what you drink is not wine, but a curse! Is there any difference?' My master said, 'My wine is made from the millet fields of Yanzi near the city wall, with the chaff and weak grains removed, measured with a skillful measure, stored in a wise container, kept in Cao's book warehouse for months, then soaked with clean and yielding water, poured into Fan's clean cauldron, distilled with the distant Trisula fire, using good medicine as the mash, and straight wood for the vat. When it is completed, it is measured with Yao's bell, Confucius's gourd, and Zhong's jar. Therefore, it can be holy when clear and virtuous when turbid. Your wine, however, is nothing more than the stolen millet of Zhi, the greedy man's spring water, the prince's stove, and the foolish child's vessel.'

Mistakenly drinking it, even the honest become greedy, the cautious become reckless, those who fall into wells perish, those who curse others bring disaster upon themselves, suspicion of treachery at the stove, accusing the pot as a thief—these are minor faults! If you do not take heed of these warnings, will you still blame the master for your own mistakes? The man then had a great realization. The master said, "If the root of impurity is not removed, later it may resurface." He drew his sword and carved out my intestines, washed them all away with water, and put them back in—there was no longer any suffering. Then he bestowed upon me the golden tray of dew he had brewed, and I drank it on my knees, remaining drunk for seven days. Afterward, when I passed by a wine shop, seeing the overflowing barrels and jars, I touched my nose and did not know what it was, and thus I no longer came. The old man was greatly shocked and fell to the ground to bow and say, "If your master has the formula for brewing wine, why not give it to me to see?" The willow spirit produced a brocade bag and gave it to him, then laughed long and departed. Upon opening it, he saw a large "water" character written inside. Looking up at the wine in the shop, it had all turned into water. The old man then abandoned his trade of selling wine and went to the shrine of Lu Sheng, becoming a perfumer and hermit.

The Bell said, "Attempting the impossible harms one's life, drooling and losing one's reputation, if I die, bury me, what good is it to act as a great man? How can one take gold and leave the plow, jade toad, and wash it all away with water from the Western River? This is a single wakeful madman's argument, a scholar or connoisseur well-versed in wine and literature, from now on, seal the jars and cover the pots." Huan Wen was present, given a daily allowance of two liters; Jing Bo attended the banquet, strictly adhering to the three cups of wine. Entering not a friend of wine, the road leads to a place of fermentation. When happy, the fragrance of incense confuses the heart, Chunyu Kun drinking together in disorder; when angry, the wheel of the carriage binds the neck, Gao Jishi using power to detain guests. The state of intoxication overpowers others, in the world of drunkenness, familiar with beauty. One must thirst the Qiang people by the pond, tie up and throw into the pond; shackle Bi Zuo at the edge of the jar, ask him to enter the jar.

**Notes Appended by a Student of Xu Yuankai** **The Color Taboo** A certain scholar from Yuanpu was fond of pursuing beauty. His wife was both beautiful and virtuous, but whenever she advised him, he would scowl at her. In the year Gengwu, he went north to take the examination, but failed and returned home, passing through Gongjia City. A woman was plucking flowers outside the gate, glancing at him with a sidelong look—she was extremely beautiful. He deliberately let his whip fall, dismounted slowly, and picked it up, saying, “The Zhengyang scholar has dropped his whip—why has Lady Qianguo not invited him into the courtyard?” The woman seemed not to hear him, took the flower, and entered by lifting the curtain. He was greatly disappointed and left in a sullen mood, whipping his horse away. That night, he stayed at an inn and tossed and turned, unable to sleep. As soon as he lay down on his pillow, he saw a guest with a tall hat and long sword, wearing an apricot-yellow robe, coming in an arrogant manner. He got up to invite him to sit and asked his name and family. The guest said, “I am the Yellow Robe Guest. Since the son of the Huo family buried his jade, I have hidden with the Dragon-bearded Kunlun and others in the sea. Now I have an urge to act again, to step once more into the world.” The scholar was surprised and told him what he had seen, discussing it privately with him. The guest said, “Is it not the fifth house on the south side of the city, with a ginkgo tree outside, with a green vine entangled above, producing purple flowers?” The scholar replied, “Yes.” The guest said, “This is a woman from a good family, her husband is also a scholar, and her family status is equal to yours. She is not a willow by the Zhangtai Road, to be plucked by anyone.” The scholar insisted on seeking a strategy. The guest said, “I will comply with your request for now. But I have a bold and impertinent manner—I hope you will not hold it against me.” And so he left.

Not long after, the guest brought a woman with him. Lighting the lamp, he saw her with loose hairpins and disheveled hair, which only made her more alluring; he was overjoyed. He wanted to embrace and fondle her, but was hindered by the guest’s presence. It seemed the guest sensed her youth, and said, "I have also brought a beautiful woman with me, one who is on par with the woman before you. You may lean close to beauty, and I shall rest beside charm — there is no harm in both." Having already fulfilled his desires, he no longer cared who the guest’s companion was. He asked the guest to go to a separate bed in the eastern pavilion, and then himself carried the beautiful woman to the bed, indulging in the most indecent and obscene acts. When the deed was done, he secretly went to the eastern pavilion and peered through the crack in the window. There he saw a beautiful woman lying beside the guest, sharing a pillow with him. Soon after, he overheard them whispering: "My husband is too clumsy and playful, always chasing after women and leaving me alone. Now that I have the chance to sleep with you, I wish to remain faithful to you for the rest of my life." The guest reached out to adjust her pillow and smiled, saying, "Your words are most insightful. But a green headpiece is about to be given to your husband! What can be done?" The woman replied, "He has his own karma to face; what is there to regret?" Upon hearing this, the man realized that the woman was jealous like his wife. Enraged, he pushed open the door and entered directly, saying, "What kind of reckless fool are you, defiling someone’s purity?" He drew the sword from his bedside and lunged at him. The woman suddenly fled. The guest rose to greet him, smiling and saying, "Do you also know what it means to defile someone's purity? 'What you yourself do not desire, do not impose on others.' Your wife is still in bed, so you should also leave your husband some room!" The man was speechless, gripping his sword and glaring furiously. Suddenly, a man with a timid appearance rushed into the inner chamber, seized his wife, and dragged her out, barefoot. He quickly entered the eastern pavilion, found the man, and seized the sword to kill him. The guest intervened on his behalf, but the cold blade of the sword was already at the man’s throat.

The person was extremely frightened, shouted wildly, and woke up. He then sighed and said, "Those who defile others' wives will also receive defilement in return. Adultery is close to murder; can one continue to test this with one's own life?" After returning home, he became very close with his wife, and in brothels and entertainment houses, there is no trace of the person anymore.

The Bell said, "Promiscuity in guesthouses invites bandits into the inner chambers. Now that we have these round coins, there's no need for the old man on our heads to worry about the calculations. Beware! Beware! What you do to others will come back to you. Moss on the wall is hard to sweep away; even this saying can make a worthless broom worth a thousand gold pieces." Gong Sun Mu, leader of the inner chambers, and Han Xizai, a beggar in the inner court, though they indulge in wild behavior, do not stray far from their quarters. If one crosses boundaries and invades others' territories, they will inevitably love wild ducks while losing their domestic chickens. Heaven rewards good and punishes evil, and people's words can be feared. Know that this day of promiscuity on the bed is the beginning of a signpost for selling a wife; it is not until the next year when bandits are invited into the inner chambers that one realizes the gradual self-immolation by fire! Appendix by Xu Yuan Kai, a student. Admonition on Wealth In the City God Temple of Lucheng, Shanxi, there resides a strange guest who calls himself the Son of the Five Great Mountains. His voice suggests he is from the region of Yan and Zhao, and he plays with flowers and trees, reversing the seasons. One day, some enthusiasts in the neighborhood gathered to invite him to perform a trick. The guest said, "Yes." He took out a green coin from his sleeve, stuck it in the courtyard, and made a sign with his fingers as if writing a talisman. Soon, the coin grew as large as a carriage wheel, astonishing everyone. The guest said, "Just now, Cheng Lianzi delayed me by the sea, so I must leave temporarily and return tomorrow morning." As he was leaving, he pointed to the coin and smiled, saying, "This object brings good fortune if you have it, misfortune if you don't; do not underestimate it." He then left, and the crowd gradually dispersed.

A lazy person associated with someone wandered until night, stroking for a long time, and then sneakedly peered through a coin hole. Inside, he saw jade towers and green pavilions, embroidered railings and patterned windows, glass screens and coral beds, pearls, jade, and precious objects, all present. Soon, several beautiful women appeared, wearing fine clothes and accessories, and came gracefully. Each held a musical instrument, unlike the traditional Chinese instruments commonly known. Shortly, one of the women said, "The *Purple Clouds Return to the Music Hall* was stolen by Ah Huan and hasn't been played for a long time! Why not play it?" The others replied, "Agreed." They then spread a carpet on the ground and began to play. After finishing, one said, "Ah Man's charming demeanor is unparalleled; let's perform the bending-waist dance, shall we?" One of the women stood foolishly, as if not agreeing. The others laughed, "You foolish maid, you've been ravaged by White Family's young master; your waist has become coarse!" The woman's face turned red, and she reluctantly shook her sleeves and danced. Peach petals fell rustling in front of the hall, like falling red rain. The person in the coin hole, initially just peeking in, later gradually entered a better state and unconsciously moved closer to the waist. Suddenly, he heard a scolding voice from the hall saying, "What a dirty slave, C, peeping into a woman's inner chamber!" They dispersed in a loud, chaotic manner. The multi-story buildings and layered pavilions vanished without a trace. The person felt the coin hole gradually shrinking, binding all around below the waist, unable to advance or retreat. The binding caused extreme pain, and he shouted wildly for help. The villagers gathered and looked around, finding no way out.

At dawn, the guest returned and scolded angrily: "You wretched beggar, you, a destitute wretch, recklessly long for the beauty of palaces and the service of concubines, to the point of piercing through the coin's hole, constantly drawing blame. This calamity is of your own making—there is no saving you!" The crowd pleaded for mercy on his behalf. The guest said, "In the world, the four virtues—ritual, righteousness, integrity, and shame—and the four vices—wine, women, wealth, and anger—are like Zhuge Liang's Eight Trigrams Formation. Integrity is the gate of life, wealth is the gate of death. He has already entered through the gate of death—still does he hope to exit through the gate of life?" Upon hearing this, I wept bitterly. The guest smiled and said, "If you have a heart of repentance, perhaps you may yet be saved." He then took a large brush, dipped it in ink, and painted over the coin's hole, removing it. The coin immediately shrank to its original size, and he returned it to his sleeve. He said to me, "For now, you are saved by the stroke of the brush; henceforth, do not be willing to risk your life for a single coin." I bowed in gratitude and left with the crowd. To this day, the temple attendants still tell of this story.

The Bell said: "A high-minded person may buy a mountain, a talented person may flatter a tomb; even if one is as廉洁 as Liu Cong, they will still select large coins to accept. How much more so for someone with a癖 like He Qiao? However, when examining the Nine Treasuries' circular law, where the outer circle represents heaven and the inner square represents earth, drilling through the coin hole is no different from burying one's head in hell. Water can drown the body, a knife can take a life. Taking this metaphor, it is still considered far removed. Silver is obtained from the direction of Gèn, which does not flow, and money is given with a spear, which brings death nearby. A廉洁 person adheres to the principles of integrity, while a贪夫 takes advantage of opportunities for personal gain. How could one know that the Deng family's copper mountain is buried in dust with hungry ghosts, and the Shi family's Golden Valley is stained with the blood of wandering spirits? They serve as cattle and horses for half a life, and throw away their homesteads for a smile. Carving out a thousand gold caves in a rocky mountain, why is this so? Lamenting the marketplace of wolves and ghosts, where a million silver slaves are now, where are they now?"

A Note Attached to the Teachings of Xu Yuankai Admonition Concerning Emotions Yushan Mountain's old man Zhuang, in his sixties, finally had a son. Just one year old, his second wife, Geng, loved him as if he were a precious jade. Once, a neighbor's daughter invited her to attend a white robe gathering, and she took her child, handing him to Zhuang to hold, giving him repeated earnest instructions before getting into the carriage and leaving. Zhuang, holding the child, went into the study and began reading *The Brief History of the Qin and Han Dynasties*. Upon reaching the part about Qin Shi Huang burning books, he banged the table in anger and said, "How foolish, First Emperor! You want to make the common people completely stupid, but who will recognize the stele of merit at Langya?" The child was startled and began to cry, but Zhuang ignored him. Continuing to read, he reached the part about the assassination attempt at Bolangsha and banged the table again, saying, "Alas! Heaven did not abandon Qin, and the secondary carriage was only barely hit. Otherwise, the stench of a rotten fish would not have lasted for thirty-six years!" The child cried again, but Zhuang continued reading as before. When he reached the part about Liu Bang entering the pass and escaping from the Hongmen Feast, he became extremely angry, banging the table and standing up, saying, "If they had retreated at this time, what would have happened afterward? Not understanding Fan Zeng's strategy, the old man with double pupils should have been removed!" The child cried uncontrollably. Later, when he read about Liu Bang's promise to share power with his allies, Zhuang's anger filled his chest, and he overturned the table, saying, "Parents act like this, how can ministers know? To cook the hunting dog, what is strange about that!" As he was still angry, he looked at the child in his arms and saw that his face had turned blue and his breath was blocked; the child no longer made any crying sounds.

A woman returned home and saw something that shocked her to the point of wanting to die.
