Despite this, her husband still clenched his fists and rubbed his palms, glaring at the book and declaring, "Where is the sword that cuts through snakes? I shall take the son of the Red Emperor and slay him!" The woman spat at him, quickly took their child and lay down to sleep on the bed. They called a doctor, but he could not save the child. Grieving over the death of her child, the woman searched through the books on the shelves and threw them all into the fire. Her husband became furious. From then on, he lived in a separate room from her, and thus the descendants of the old man were cut off. The bell said, "To act out of a momentary fit of anger and sever one's lineage for a hundred generations, how can one rashly stir up such fury? Yet, heroes brandish their swords, commanding the winds and clouds; renowned scholars wield their pens, moving the five great mountains. Do not take the old man as an example, and do not become a man without spirit." Rigidity often leads to disaster, while patience and restraint bring success. One should emulate the man who retrieved the slipper on the bridge, and not be like the man who drilled holes in his pants to sell the cloth. If one allows oneself to become stale and outdated, or pretends to be crazy to gain freedom, then on a grand scale, one might become like Mi Heng, beating drums on the banks of the Parrot Island and losing one's life; on a smaller scale, one might become like the man who tore his clothes in the *Cherry Ode* and was dismissed. Therefore, one must learn to remain calm and unangry, following the example of the truly brave, and avoid acting in a petty, resentful, or irrational manner, which is unbecoming of a true gentleman.

Appendix by Xu Yuankai** **The Courtesan Who Teaches Loyalty** Fang Zhi, a female scholar from the Qinhuai River, had keen eyes and could recognize heroes. Her reputation surpassed that of Dun Wen and Sha Nen, and she had the closest relationship with Li Zhenli's daughter, Ah Xiang. Ah Xiang rejected Tian Yang's proposal and agreed to marry Hou Gongzi. One day, Fang Zhi visited her room and said, "Sister, I have found a man to entrust my life to! But famous scholars are only admired for a short time. I want to find a loyal and righteous man to share a long life with." Ah Xiang laughed at her. Guizhu Yang Wenchong heard of her name and visited her. Fang Zhi asked him to paint a plum blossom. Yang Wenchong quickly sketched a full sheet. Fang Zhi was delighted and eventually agreed to a lifelong commitment with him. At that time, Yang Wenchong was allied with Ma and Ruan, serving as a low-ranking official close to powerful figures, and was disdained by the literati. When they heard that Fang Zhi had agreed to marry him, they were greatly disappointed, and even Ah Xiang secretly laughed. On the night of their engagement, Fang Zhi approached him seriously and said, "Do you know what I mean by entrusting myself to you?" Yang Wenchong replied, "I don't know." Fang Zhi said, "Before, I saw your painting of a plum blossom. The petals were all in a charming and graceful state, but the old trunk and horizontal branches sometimes had thunderous and strong bones. I know you are smooth and adaptable to the customs, but you still have a backbone. I want to assist you in your great principles and preserve your integrity until the end. That's why I brought a strange treasure in my bridal chest, which I will give to you in the future." Yang Wenchong replied indifferently.

Shortly after, a national disaster broke out, and officials Ma and Ruan were all executed by decapitation. Hou, carrying Li Xiang, fled far from the turmoil. Amidst the chaos of war and thorns, thousands of households were terrified. Fang Zhi brought out a beautifully crafted golden box and calmly advanced, saying, "I promised you a rare treasure in the past, and now it is time to test it!" Yang opened it, and inside was a long grass rope, about two zhang in length, with a shiny object beside it, which turned out to be a half-foot-long small dagger. Yang was startled and hesitated, undecided. Fang Zhi spoke sternly, "A man leaves behind either a good reputation or a bad one, and what we are fighting for is just this moment. How can you live a cowardly life and be laughed at by children and women?" Yang also rose up with enthusiasm, intending to hang himself. Fang Zhi said, "Stop! Stop! A guilty minister should not wear formal attire!" She quickly removed it. Yang then wore simple, unadorned clothing and tied himself to the window lattice. Fang Zhi watched as his breath ceased and clapped and laughed, saying, "My lifelong aspirations have finally been fulfilled!" She then took the dagger and stabbed herself in the throat, dying. Later, Luan Xiang heard about the incident and sighed, "Sister Fang, you are a hero among children and women. Your actions are so unpredictable, is it like this?" She asked Hou to write a biography, but it was not completed. And in the unofficial historical records and folk tales, there is no account of this event.

The Bell said, "A word spoken by a child can be remembered by heroes for a thousand years. Who would say that there is no certainty in the brothels? A poem about a single move in a chess game that has ended, I pity Liu Miwu!" **The Young Actress's Filial Piety** The music department of the Pear Garden, most prosperous in Wu Gate, had a young actress named Yin Lan, who was twelve years old and looked like a maiden. Her father was a scholar, but he had died early. Her mother observed her widowhood, and suddenly fell ill with hemoptysis. The family was extremely poor and could not afford medicine. Yin Lan, after careful consideration, had no other choice but to join the Huaxiu troupe as a Pear Garden disciple. She covered her head with a brocade handkerchief and learned to walk with a green hook. Her charming voice and graceful appearance were outstanding at the time, and she earned money, which she spent all on medicine. The rest of her earnings were used to buy fine dishes and fruits to enjoy with her mother every day. She woke up early, asked for her mother's well-being several times, and then went to the singing field. She returned late at night, took her waist drum and wooden clappers, and sang the Linchuan tunes by the bed. When her mother was asleep, she would quietly lie down behind her feet. If her mother was slightly unhappy, she would lay out mats and tables, prepare herself, and perform various plays like "Xiao Qing's Inscription" to make her mother happy. When wealthy families invited her to luxurious banquets, she would leave before the candles were burned out, using an excuse. If they locked her in to make her stay, she would weep and beg to return, saying, "I'm afraid my old mother is waiting by the gate." Because of this, everyone admired her filial piety, and even in the evening, they did not insist on keeping her. When given money, she accepted it without thanking, but when given hairpins and earrings, she would bow twice before accepting them. People were surprised by this. She explained, "Those who give me money know I am poor, and those who give me hairpins and earrings know I have an old mother."

For seven years, the mother's blood disease suddenly broke out and she died. Lan was so grief-stricken that he nearly wanted to die. After he had the coffin carried and buried with his father, he took the old green feathered phoenix hairpin, and all the embroidered handkerchiefs and flowered shoes, and threw them all into the fire. He knelt for a long time at the tomb and swore, "If anyone else follows this old habit, may they be struck down." People laughed and said, "You have already enjoyed fame and profit from this, why start and then regret it?" Lan shed tears and said, "You do not understand my heart. Although I am unworthy, I was raised in a noble family; how could I stoop to learn this vulgar skill? I started it because I needed to support my mother; I regret it now because I fear it will tarnish my father's reputation." The Minister of Revenue, Yang Gong, admired his filial piety and invited him to the capital to teach him the scholar-official arts. Due to regulations, he could not take the exams, and he was recommended to serve as a secretary to a certain Military Governor. Once he attended a theatrical banquet and, upon returning, wept bitterly, saying, "The old way of life was vividly before my eyes. In the vast underworld, who will I serve? The lyrics of the fallen whip on the stage were all echoes of 'Lao E'!" He was depressed for several days. From then on, when asked to enjoy music, he used evasive language and never went again. The bell said, "Since ancient times, eccentric and outstanding people have sometimes humbled themselves and lowered their aspirations, without needing the world's forgiveness. Alas, alas! Who does not have parents? Yet it is only one person, Yin Lan, who has become known as a filial son!" Someone said, "Yin Lan's filial piety is only known because of his career as an actor." That is true. But why are there no scholars and officials who are not actors, who can also be known for their filial piety?

丐妇殉节 In Qingzhou, there was a beggar woman named Xiao Miao'er. She had a slightly dark complexion, and her eyebrows and eyes were graceful and charming. She accompanied her husband, Wang Wu, who begged in the Huai River region. Wang was lazy and violent, often lying in the Yellow Public Shrine during the day. He would order his wife to go out begging, and if she returned with few alms, he would immediately beat her, saying, "Where did you play, and why did you only get this much?" If she returned with many alms, he would beat her again, saying, "Who did you have an affair with to swindle this money? If it is revealed, your father-in-law will not forgive you." Even minor offenses would prompt Wang to sit on the steps, pull her down to kneel, and slap her cheeks himself. The woman did not argue back but cried in silence and accepted the abuse. One day, a wealthy man named Master Zhang sent his servant to invite Xiao Miao'er. Worried about being suspected of infidelity, she went with her husband. Master Zhang ordered her to sing the "Picking Dates" tune. After she finished singing, he whispered to his servant for a long time, then led Wang out to the outer room and gave him wine as a gift. Privately, he told the woman, "With your beauty like this, why worry about not finding a good match? To become the wife of a beggar, and I heard that he beats you in the morning and humiliates you in the evening. The affection between husband and wife has ended! Why don't you plan for yourself early?" The woman angrily replied, "A beggar's wife only knows about her husband! How could she know about his morning beatings and evening humiliations? And as a woman, I am loyal to one man until death, so what planning is there to do?" Master Zhang laughed and said, "You don't plan for yourself, but I have already planned for you." He led the woman out to the outer room, where her husband was already hanged by a short rope around his neck and dead. Realizing that she could not match him in strength, she feigned anger and said, "Cursed slave, I have followed you for more than ten years. What enjoyment have I had? He often beats me with a red stick."

"Now that it is like this, it is a retribution from heaven!" The man was greatly pleased. The woman said, "Killing it is certainly good, but when the dog or horse dies, it should also be buried with a canopy. If even a small piece of land is provided to cover it, it is truly a great virtue of the lord." The man believed her and ordered the servant to guard the woman, then went out to a deserted place to look for a suitable spot. The woman took the opportunity to tell the servant, "Do you know my wish?" The servant replied, "I do not know." The woman said, "I am just a beggar's wife, suddenly becoming a wealthy family's wife, I am not used to the food, clothing, and daily life. If I can have someone like you to serve me, then my wish will be fulfilled!" The servant was delighted, and then asked, "What about the master?" The woman said, "It is not difficult. Quickly report to the authorities, then the master will definitely be imprisoned. You and I will flee together, go to another place and engage in a small trade, it is better than bowing one's head under the eaves!" The servant greatly agreed and hurriedly opened the back door and left.

When the man returned, he had lost his servant. He questioned his wife, and she said, "I did not see you come, so perhaps he followed his traces and went away." The man embraced his wife in an attempt to be intimate. She said, "This is also very laughable. How often have you seen someone whose flesh is not yet cold, yet already trying to force himself upon a woman?" The man persisted in forcing her. She spoke sternly, saying, "Because he treated me cruelly, I have forced myself to serve you. If you force me, it would be replacing one form of violence with another—how different would it be?" While she was still resisting, suddenly she saw her servant leading several men holding ropes, rushing in violently. They bound the man and took him away, and the wife followed him to the magistrate's office. After reporting the matter, he confessed upon a single interrogation. The man was sentenced to death, and the servant, having first reported the crime as a co-conspirator, received a lesser punishment and was also imprisoned. An order was given to allocate a square foot of ground to cover the body of Wang Wu. When the burial was complete, the beggar-woman stepped forward with a knife in hand. Those around her tried to dissuade her, saying, "He treated you so cruelly back then, yet now you are repaying him with virtue—this is already excessive! Why must you go this far?" The woman sighed and said, "The relationship between ruler and subject, and husband and wife, is the same in terms of duty. My death will make the world know that those who fulfill the duties of a wife must not use their husband as an excuse for excessive behavior, and it will also shame those who treat their subjects as worthless and dare to treat their ruler as an enemy." After speaking these words, she cut her own throat and died.

Duo said, "A brave man sacrifices his life, doing his utmost within his power. This is why Zhu Lie Shu died to repay the favor of Ju Ao Gong. The general opinion of ordinary people versus the high standards expected of a national hero; Yu Rang was simply unaware of worldly complexities!" **Guarding Loyalty** In the household of Zhang Tai Shi in Hai Ning, there was a servant girl named Chong Nu, who suffered from a condition characterized by red spots or rashes on her skin. She had a dark complexion with patches of black and had legs or feet described as "lotus boats" about one foot in length. A military officer named Lu, who was poor and had not yet married, hired her as his wife. One day, a wealthy individual plotted to kidnap the wife of a poor household, and Lu, upholding righteousness, came to her aid. The wealthy individual became angry and bribed the city guard, who then removed Lu's name from the roster. From then on, Lu became even more impoverished. When Wu Liu Qi was not yet prominent, he begged for food at the home of the upright scholar Zha Yi Huang. Lu had previously met Wu. Upon learning that Wu had been appointed as a deputy general, Lu joined his ranks. Wu was known for his hospitality and often hosted many extraordinary scholars. Among his guests was a man called Hai Ou Zi, who was skilled in spiritual arts and tasked Lu with serving him. One day, Hai Ou Zi looked at Lu and smiled, saying, "You may not have the fiery spirit and noble bearing of Ma Zhou, but you can still achieve greatness like Zhao Wu, who rose from poverty to wealth. However, your wife's condition is unusual, and I fear she may not be able to bear children. Fortunately, the lines of good fortune on your temples have turned red, so we can still use divine power to help." He then handed Lu a black pill. Lu did not fully believe him, but he respectfully accepted it.

After following General Wu to suppress bandits, Lu achieved military success and was appointed as a subordinate general. He then again suppressed sea bandits, capturing the chief rebel alive and presenting the captives to the emperor. Upon reporting his success, General Wu was appointed as the commander-in-chief, and Lu was promoted to a town commander. Lu expressed his feelings and requested leave, returning home by night. He first visited the Grand Historian, inquiring about his favored servant. The Grand Historian smiled and said, "Do you still remember the past, noble person? Regardless of status, they are unequal, ugly and repulsive. Even considering age, she is now a sixty-year-old aunt! Still able to hold bedding and call herself a new wife?" Lu replied, "Not so. From humble to noble, my life is hers, and to her repulsive appearance, I was well aware when I first married her. If we consider old age, the sin of wasting time should be attributed to me, not to her!" The Grand Historian then spoke seriously and said, "You are truly a righteous man, deeply respected by me." Since Lu had not yet built a residence, he married into the family of the Grand Historian that very day. On the night of their wedding, the favored servant wore a plain headband and looked straight ahead, dressed in ceremonial robes and a pearl headdress, looking like a noblewoman. When she undressed and went to bed, a few strands of white hair scattered over her head, from forehead to feet, barely resembling a human form. Lu respected her without diminishing. The favored servant advised him to take a concubine. Lu said, "I will live with you for a hundred years, which is still only thirty years of bedding. How can I let others divide my love again?" However, the favored servant was finally worried about not having an heir. She then took the black pill given by Hai'ouzi, gave it to Lu, and he swallowed it. Within a month, her menstruation returned. The following year, they gave birth to a son, named Gongshou. People said it was a reward for adhering to righteousness.

Duo said: "The story of Wu Yan entering the palace and Meng Guang bearing the tray shows that those who value a woman's virtue do so not for her beauty. Yet, in this world, if there is no Ting Shi, who then would be the worthy husband? If a warrior is like this, the son-in-law of the Yuan family should be ashamed to live!" **Tao Yao Village** Jiang Sheng of Taicang, a man in his early twenties skilled in writing, followed a merchant on a sea journey, and was carried to a place where mountains rose like screens and rivers gleamed like paintings. There were no walls or city gates around, only tens of thousands of peach trees arranged in a circle like the layout of a prefectural capital. It was the middle of spring, and a fragrant breeze blew through the air, with tens of thousands of trees in bloom, their blossoms like brocade and embroidered canopies arranged on either side. Jiang was delighted and, accompanied by the merchant surnamed Ma, walked slowly through the blossoms. Suddenly, he saw dozens of small embroidered carriages approaching in a swarm. The women were dressed in various styles, some plain, some beautiful. Among them was a woman with a sunken face, folded ears, protruding lips, and sharp teeth, yet she was adorned with pearls and jade, looking like a daughter of a wealthy family. She wiped her face with a cloth and concealed her sleeves, feigning a charming posture. Jiang and Ma both laughed at the sight. At the end of the procession came a single carriage, in which sat a young woman with a beautiful face, wearing a simple hairpin and a plain robe. Her natural beauty was like jade blossoms and white flowers, a grace that was hard to describe. Jiang was astonished by her and followed her from behind with Ma.

The wheels and axles made a loud noise, and the carriage moved as swiftly as the wind and lightning. They arrived at a public office, and everyone got out and entered. The scholar was puzzled and asked the locals. They replied, "This place is called Taoyao Village. During the second month of spring, when young men and women are getting married, the local official first records the women of the people, ranking them according to their appearance. Then he records the men of the people, testing their literary and artistic abilities, and ranks them accordingly. After that, he combines the two lists, pairing the best with the best, the second best with the second best, and so on. Thus, the beauty of the women matches the talent of the men, fitting each other well. Today is the women's examination, and tomorrow will be the men's. If you do not have a wife, why not come along and enjoy it?" The scholar nodded politely and rented a house to live in. He thought about the young lady in the carriage, believing her to be the most beautiful; he also thought to himself that his literary talent was outstanding, and he did not think of being second best. If fate is favorable, truly fulfilling the wish to seek a phoenix across the world. The horse also thought about the young lady and wanted to hurry to the examination. He discussed this with the scholar, who laughed and said, "You are not familiar with this, why bother to put up a sign and gamble for money?" The horse insisted on going, and the scholar could not stop him. The next day, they entered the examination hall. The scholar wrote without adding a single stroke, completing his essay in a short time, while the horse merely scribbled carelessly. After the examination, they returned to their lodging. A person came to deliver the chief examiner's command, demanding three hundred strings of cash, promising to make the scholar the top candidate. The scholar was angry and said, "Whether my bag is empty or not, it is not enough to satisfy the greedy. Even if I have a house full of gold, would I borrow divine power to make my writing lose its spirit?" The person was ashamed and left. The horse followed him, taking out the money from his pouch and giving it to him.

When the case was revealed, the horse ultimately won the championship, while the protagonist humbly came in last. The protagonist sighed, saying, "Words have no power; it is indeed not worth lamenting. But to lose a beautiful woman and gain an ugly one, what can be done?" Not long after, the examiner paired them in order and ordered the woman who came in last place to marry into the protagonist's family. The protagonist thought surely it was the woman with a sunken face, twisted ears, crooked lips, and sharp teeth. When she uncovered the veil and looked at her, her dark, fragrant beauty shone like a candle—it was the beautiful-toothed woman from the post. The protagonist carefully questioned her. She said, "I come from a poor family, sold pearls to repair the house, and had no time for even daily tasks. The examiner, seeing this, demanded a large bribe from me, promising to list me at the top, but I scolded him to go away, and therefore he held a grudge and listed my name at the end of the list." The protagonist laughed and said, "The old man of the border lost his horse, who knows it might not be a blessing. If I had given three hundred strings of coins, I would have been listed at the top, and how could I have been together with a beautiful woman tonight?" The woman also laughed and said, "Right and wrong are reversed, and this is how the world is. Only those who remain true to themselves can ultimately attract good fortune." The protagonist greatly admired her. The next day, he went to the horse's house to congratulate him. The horse's appearance and spirit were dejected, and he did not say a word. It was because he married the champion's daughter, who was the woman seen before, covering her face with a handkerchief and hiding her sleeves, and forcing a flirtatious attitude. The protagonist laughed and investigated the reason. This woman offered a thousand strings of coins to the examiner and was listed first, and the horse also climbed the ranks to the top of the list, therefore got this treasure. The protagonist laughed and said, "Gained a great reputation but lost substance, this is self-inflicted, what blame?" The horse was depressed and disappointed, lived for half a year, and sailed back across the sea. The protagonist was devoted to his wife, finally settled in the overseas, and did not return.

Duo said, "Money manipulates people, and right and wrong are inverted. Who would have thought that fate manipulates people, leading to a reversal of the power dynamics related to wealth! Yet this woman is generous and has a noble spirit, truly extraordinary, and can be called an excellent match." **Thicket** Zhou Mengquan of Kuaiji, at the age of infancy, his father was a guest in Guangdong for twenty years, with no news or communication. Zhou, following his mother's command, went to inquire about his father's whereabouts, enduring great hardship with wind and rain, traveling for more than two months, still far from the border of Guangdong. Suddenly, he took a diverging path into a valley, filled with countless thorns, obscuring the sky and blocking the earth. Several well-dressed individuals were struggling through the thorny terrain, pricking their feet and snagging their clothes, as if they did not feel the pain. Zhou pulled up his clothes and wanted to enter, seeing an elderly man approaching with a cane, asking where the guest was going. Zhou replied that he was looking for his father. The elderly man said, "You are a filial son. You should take the broad and smooth path, not enter the thicket." Zhou asked, "What are these people doing?" The elderly man said, "These people are obsessed with wealth and status, taking the hot path, taking shortcuts, boasting of their strong strides, so they ran into the thorns, causing them to twist their toes."

The questioner asked, "Why don't they feel the suffering?" The old man replied, "The world is full of invisible thorns; only the bystanders can see them, while those who enter do not know." Zhou said, "Why don't you make a compassionate vow and cut them down for these people?" The old man smiled and said, "In the thorns, there were originally two paths; I have already cut down one path for the passage of loyal ministers and filial sons. But these people do not take the right path." Zhou inquired about the location, and the old man said, "Facing the thorns, turning back is the way." Zhou turned around and indeed saw a broad and level road, as smooth as a plain, so he followed the path. On both sides were bamboo and trees, beautiful and lovely in the wild. The old man said, "These are the filial bamboo of Prince Zi Han and the yielding trees of Zhang Maoxian." They arrived at a ferry called "Ferry of Righteousness," where a boat named "Boat of Compassion" was moored. The living water from the source flowed around, with no ripples or waves. The old man took Zhou aboard the boat and reached the shore. On the shore stood a clean stone with golden and green large characters engraved on it, resembling ancient script, which Zhou could not decipher. The old man said, "Commonly known as the shore of enlightenment, which is what Confucianism calls the shore of the Way." They walked another few miles and came to a gate inscribed with "Gate of No Duality." From afar, it was as level as a grindstone and as straight as an arrow, with no side paths on either side. The old man said, "Go from here, do not retreat, do not take a wrong path, persevere, and you can live your life without thorns!" And so they left.

Zhou entered through the gate, and the path he trod was all stone, its surface so bright it could reflect shadows, yet not even the slightest smoothness. He walked gracefully and lightly, initially without much effort. Suddenly, a sheer cliff loomed before him, with old trees entwined by vines reaching up to the heavens. Zhou climbed up, but slipped and fell like a falling wild goose. When he rose to look around, he saw fine grass on a flat slope, wildflowers in the path, as if another realm. A woodcutter, carrying firewood, walked and sang as he passed. Zhou asked him, and the woodcutter replied that it was the western slope of the mountains in Guangdong. Zhou hurried into the city, searching for his father's whereabouts, and found him in the East Courtyard of Puli Temple. His father had been traveling as a guest, wandering aimlessly, and felt ashamed to return home. They met, each recounting events from the past twenty years, and embraced, weeping bitterly. The people of Guangdong were moved by Zhou's filial piety, spreading the story throughout the village and neighborhood, and provided him with funds and tools to return home. The commentator said, "A broad and smooth road is even cleared from among thorns. One can see that the paths of good and evil are not far apart. Dangerous indeed!" Harmonious Bell. Volume Five. Evil Farewell A man from Zhijiang named Lu had a cousin who served as the Prefect of Didao State. He went to depend on him, but two months ago, his cousin had been promoted to Governor of Western Commandery. With no money in his purse, Lu temporarily resided in the Sha Ni Inn. Fortunately, he had learned martial arts as a child and temporarily taught boxing for a living. In front of the inn stood two date trees, with a circumference that could be embraced. At the time when the fruit was ripe, those who beat the tree for fruit numbered in the hundreds each day. Lu smiled and said, "Fitting hooks and sharpening sticks, isn't that too roundabout? I will think of a way for you." He unbuttoned his clothes and hurried to the left tree, embraced it, and shook it. It felt as soft as a bed of thistles, and the dates fell one after another to the ground. The crowd was amazed.

Beside him stood a bearded man who smiled and said, "What is there to be surprised about?" He also undressed and walked to the right side of the tree, embracing it with both hands, but the branches and leaves hardly moved at all. Lu watched him. The bearded man said, "What you practice is external skill, while I practice internal skill. This tree will wither and die in an instant once I touch it." Lu doubted that it was true. Shortly after, the leaves turned yellow and the branches fell off, falling down in clusters, carrying the dates with them. The trunk stood stiffly upright, as if it had been a dry tree for a thousand years. Lu was greatly startled. The bearded man said, "The boy is also worth teaching." He asked about his family background and whether he was married. Lu replied, "I am poor and humble, and for most of the year I rely on others. I have not yet found a wife." The bearded man said, "I have a simple-minded daughter who is quite a good match for you. Do you know if you would accept her?" Lu said, "My life is like a floating萍 (a type of aquatic plant) or a流浪 (a wandering person). To receive protection and support from an elder is indeed my wish." The bearded man was pleased and took him with him to return home, dressing his daughter and having her come out to meet. That night, they immediately performed the wedding ceremony. The next day, Lu paid respects to his wife's relatives. There was an old woman with a limp and a cane, who was the daughter's grandmother; a woman with a coarse robe and bald sleeves, tall and slender, who was the daughter's stepmother; a woman with a short jacket and tight pants, with large feet like a basket, who was the daughter's biological mother; a woman with wildflowers in her hair and no makeup, who was the daughter's widowed sister. Lu found his wife's gentle and compliant nature to be quite acceptable.

After half a year, I observed that the bearded man’s behavior was secretive and suspicious—most certainly not a man of virtue. Seizing the opportunity while he was away traveling and had not yet returned, I privately told the woman: “I have long known the nature of your household’s affairs. But killing and seizing goods will ultimately lead to ruin. Once fire consumes jade and stone, how will you treat me then?” The woman replied, “My actions will follow yours; how dare I decide?” Lu said, “As for the present situation, the only course is to report this to the elders and return to our hometown together, in the hope of avoiding regret in the future.” The woman said, “Then please go ahead and speak.” Lu conveyed his intention to the old woman. After pondering for a long time, the old woman said, “Since your father-in-law has not yet returned, it is proper to wait quietly. But since you have already resolved to leave, a farewell ceremony will be held tomorrow.” Lu was pleased and relayed this to the woman. The woman, however, looked troubled and said, “Our family’s customs are different from yours. What is meant by a farewell ceremony? It involves passing through the inner chamber, the main room, the hall, and the gate, with each person holding weapons to guard. Only if one can break through each door can one escape and return to one’s hometown. Otherwise, there is no mercy between kin under the blades.” Lu was greatly embarrassed. The woman said, “I have already thought this through thoroughly. My elder sister is short but strong and capable, though she is not my equal. My stepmother has recently been ill in her arm, but she can still manage to hold her ground. My birth mother is strong enough to fight ten thousand men, and since I am truly her child, she will not be overly harsh with me. But my grandmother, with her single iron staff, is like Mount Tai pressing down from above. If there is even the slightest mistake or oversight, my head will be crushed and destroyed. I will do my utmost to protect you, but I cannot yet know what fate has in store.” They looked at each other in anxious silence, unable to sleep all night.

Early in the morning, she packed her belongings and secretly carried weapons with her as she left. Just as she was leaving the inner chambers, her elder sister stepped forward, holding an axe and said, "Brother-in-law, you are leaving, please eat this silver knife sliced meat!" The woman replied, "Sister, stop playing tricks! Remember when your brother-in-law passed away, the cold night and lonely quilt, for three years I held your sister's back."

Today's affair, I hope you've left a little face for my sister." The elder sister scolded, "Foolish maid! You ran away from your father, yet dare to pretend to be a mediator?" She took an axe and struck her face. The girl drew a hammer from her waist and blocked it. After just three exchanges, the elder sister was sweating profusely and panting, threw the axe, and fled. Upon reaching the outer room, the legal mother greeted with a smile, "Dear guest going far, I have nothing to offer, a bamboo cane as a token of good luck." The girl knelt and pleaded, "Mother, you have been grieving the loss of your sister-in-law all year. Though I am from a different mother, I should also consider her." The legal mother became angry, "Evil maid, talking too much, first deal with you." She raised the whip, and the girl's hammer sprang up. They fought for a while, and the legal mother dropped the whip and scolded, "Cruel child! Taking advantage of my sick arm, only using the Shaha Meteor Method, pressing hard on someone!" She shooed them away. Looking afar at the main hall, the biological mother was weeping. The girl also cried, stepped out to see, and dragged Lu to kneel together. The biological mother said, "You are too heartless, wanting to abandon me?" After two words, she choked and couldn't speak. Lu pulled the girl to leave, but the girl held onto her clothes and wept. The biological mother said, "A woman follows her husband as the standard, I won't keep you. However, the custom of farewell cannot be neglected." She took a green-silver spear from the rack, with several gold coins and a string of pearls attached, and deliberately stabbed them into the girl's bosom. The girl casually picked them up, and they suddenly fell off, revealing it was a silver-like wax spear tip. She pretended to call out, "Son, too domineering, finally escaped the Lady's City!" The girl understood her meaning and dragged Lu away quickly.

As she was about to reach the gate, a single iron crutch flew down in front of her. The woman used all her life's skills, quickly taking out a pair of hammers to block, while Lu rushed out through the door and fled. The woman prostrated herself in apology. The old woman threw her crutch and sighed, saying, "A daughter has an outward orientation, and now it is true! Hurry up and follow your husband, do not pretend like this!" The woman followed Lu back to his hometown, sold her gold and jewels, engaged in small-scale trading, and could mostly support herself. Later, the bearded man was caught after failing, and the entire family was beheaded in the marketplace. Only the mother of the girl, alone, fled far away, shaved her head, and became a nun at a temple of herbs, dying at the age of eighty. She left a letter for the daughter. The woman traveled with Lu to find the temple, saw a single horizontal Zen staff on the bed, which was still the same as the old spear shaft. The woman and Lu both wept greatly, buried her coffin on the sunny side of East Mountain, lived in a hut by the grave for three years, and then returned together. The bell said, "Heaven's blessings start with filial piety and kindness. The daughter knows how to love her mother, so she does not become an egg in a overturned nest. The mother knows how to love her daughter, so she does not become a swan with a severed neck. Those who are devoted to their daughters, why do they not follow their husbands? Those who mourn their mothers, why do they not mourn their fathers? The wise say, 'This is why they become thieves.' Alas, there are many in the world who do not become thieves, and even thieves are like this."

**A Strange Marriage** Wenden, styled Dao’an, was a native of Wukang in Zhejiang. At seventeen, he enrolled in the local school and was betrothed to a certain family, but the bride died before the wedding, leaving him depressed and discontented. He wandered aimlessly, seeking a wife. By chance, he arrived at Fengyang and met a Daoist on the road. The Daoist questioned his purpose, and the man explained his intent. The Daoist said, "If you are seeking a fine wife, go fifteen li to the southeast and you will surely meet someone." The man believed him and went as he pointed out. Upon arriving, there was a theatrical performance on the Spring Platform, and the crowd gathered like bees and ants, leaving no room to stand. Looking back, he saw willows hanging low, revealing a corner of a small red building. A woman drew the curtain, her clothes half-folded, peering sideways from the side. Her powdered face and shadowed brows struck him in the eyes. He turned and looked back and forth, nearly unable to control himself. By the time the slanting sun tilted westward and the music at the performance ended, he was still gazing upward at the empty building, not realizing that the crowd had already dispersed. Suddenly, a man slapped his shoulder and shouted, "What kind of foolish boy are you, peering into someone's private quarters?" The man looked up and saw a tall, dignified figure. The man seized his arm and dragged him into the hall. Wenden’s legs trembled, and he changed color, wanting to run. The man then laughed loudly and said, "So cowardly, and yet you pretend to be romantic? Let me tell you truthfully—the woman in that building is my daughter. If you have no wife at home, I am willing to form a marriage alliance with you." Wenden’s fear turned to joy, and he replied, "Yes, yes, as you command."

The candle had been lit, and the woman was dressed formally when she and the man exchanged bows and entered the private room, ready to engage in intimate conversation. Soon, the woman’s mother called her away. The man sat alone in the dim light, feeling bored and restless. At the second drum beat after midnight, he saw the woman coming alone from the eastern side of the painted screen. She stood before the mirror, removing her green phoenix-shaped hairpin and the pair of golden peony flowers, then swiftly taking off her lotus-patterned robe. She leaned against the bed frame, taking off her sleeve-like covering and her intricately embroidered shoes, replacing them with small, soft red slippers. She lowered her head and smiled, and the light filtered through the thick curtains. The man felt as if he were burning with desire, unable to control himself, and rushed to the bed to embrace her. But the room was empty, with only embroidered pillows laid out and a quilt partially spread on the bed. He was greatly startled, unable to understand why. He covered himself with the quilt and slept alone, restless throughout the night. Just before dawn, the woman returned. The man questioned her, but she remained silent.

By night, the protagonist first hid in the brocade tent. After the watch had ended, the woman appeared, wearing a short red jacket with a gold phoenix and purple silk belt. Her hair was loose and disheveled, tied with a black cloth. Her lower body was adorned with embroidered skirts but not fully covered, revealing red silk trousers with straight patterns. She wore embroidered gold shoes and stockings, and she came, pulling back the curtain and climbing onto the bed. The protagonist quickly grabbed her arm, and she turned and moved with the ease of a red snowflake falling into the mountains of Wu. When he searched for her, she was gone without a trace. Is she a fairy or a ghost? Her traces are even more mysterious. At the height of the sun, the woman did not come. Yinggu, the woman's sister, happened to pass by her room. The protagonist, feeling lonely, was licking the brush to write by the mirror. Yinggu looked at him and said, "Have you also read books and learned to write?" The protagonist replied, "Although I am unworthy, since I was young, I have studied in school. How could a scholar not read and write?" Yinggu sighed and said, "I pity you, young scholar, your death is imminent, but you do not know it." The protagonist knelt down to ask her, "Our parents specialize in using witchcraft to rob people's wealth. Before they act, they must first kill one person to sacrifice to the gods and clear the way. They often use my sister as bait, calling it a marriage, but in reality, they have no contact. Since I have been aware, I have seen countless young men who were killed on the bed and then placed on the altar. Tonight, when the stars are bright, it will be your turn."

The young man was in extreme distress, prostrating himself and begging for help. Yinggu said, "How can I save you? To relieve your dire straits, you still need your elder sister." The young man asked for advice. She said, "The reason I immediately disappeared after climbing onto the bed is that there was a charm of the Six Jia wrapped around thirty-six strands of green silk at the bottom of the mattress. You must search for it and discard it, and she will certainly not be able to escape. If you can make her your wife, then you can plead with her using your emotional ties, and she will be able to save you from trouble." The young man carefully followed her advice. Yinggu secretly led her away. The young man opened the mattress and indeed found the charm as described, and he quickly discarded it. At night, the woman came, waiting for him to undress and climb onto the bed, she approached him naked. The woman seemed to sense something, saying, "The maid spoke too much, ruining our plans. Although, it was probably fate." She relaxed her body and embraced him, and they consummated their union. Afterward, she lay naked at the foot of the bed, pleading with him to help her. The woman said, "A lifelong marriage, a thousand deaths in following, what need is there for your words?"

He rose quickly, tied a rooster to the top of his staff, and instructed the man to carry it on his shoulder, saying, “Travel north for about thirty miles. When the rooster crows once, leave it behind and run. Then go another twenty or so miles, and when I come, be ready to go together.” The man carefully remembered and departed. The woman pretended to tell her parents. Her father became greatly angry and rode his horse to pursue her. She said, “If you cannot catch her, it would be better to fly a sword and cut her down.” The father followed her words, and threw the sword into the courtyard. It vanished like a white silk ribbon. Soon after, a flash of lightning appeared, and the sword clanged as it fell to the ground, its bloodstains still wet. At that time, the man had left through the northern gate of the city and had traveled about thirty miles when the rooster on his staff suddenly crowed loudly. He quickly dropped it to the ground. A white light shot down, and the rooster was silent and hot—lifeless. He continued another twenty or so miles, his strength exhausted, and rested beneath a tree. He saw a crane fall from the clouds, and the woman had already been riding it. When she folded it, it was just a paper crane! She laughed and said, “The great calamity has passed. Please return to your hometown.” The man asked, “What can I do about your father?” She replied, “There is no long-term strategy in unorthodox ways. He cannot reach us beyond fifty miles.” They waited until dawn to travel, and within a month, they returned together to their old home. The man locked himself in to study, and whenever he had spare time, he would play games with the woman, enjoying their hidden lotteries. One day, a woman suddenly rushed in. Looking at her, it was Yinggu. They both rose and asked why she had come. Yinggu said, “Since my sister left, my parents forced me to take her place. I refused to do so, and so I encountered their anger, and have been beaten daily with whips.”

Fortunately, my father went to the assembly of demons, and I took the opportunity to escape. Reflecting on my weak and vulnerable state, with no relatives or acquaintances to rely on, I traveled along the way and came here to seek refuge.” The woman was very pleased. The man said, “Your arrival is also very good. But she is neither ordinary nor exceptional; how will you place her in your family?” The woman smiled and said, “I am trustworthy, and you should repay kindness. Follow the example of those on the stage; it is natural to do so.” She then took out hairpins and earrings to adorn Yinggu. Yinggu blushed and refused, saying, “I came here just to be a guest in a cold and lonely place; I do not wish to join a pair of mandarin ducks with a wild duck!” The woman gently persuaded her, and Yinggu finally had no objections. Just as they were about to have them exchange bows, a Daoist came from outside and laughed, saying, “Did the words of the wife prove true?” The man respectfully thanked him. The two women looked at each other in shock and said, “It looks like our father’s teacher!” The Daoist replied, “Yes! Your father failed to achieve immortality and fell into heretical practices. He even used the magical techniques from my teachings, such as flying talismans and divination, to commit evil deeds daily. Despite severe admonitions, he had no intention of reforming. He would inevitably be destroyed one day. Out of pity for the innocent women, who would also suffer a cruel fate, I brought Wen Lang into the fold and helped each other through various means to save you from the fires of disaster!” The woman asked, “Are my parents safe?” The Daoist said, “This conversation now is the time when your whole family will be captured.” The two women burst into tears, and the Daoist said, “This is their deserved punishment; why cry about it?” He waved his sleeve and left.

After the young man secretly investigated the matter, it was indeed on that day that the official army searched and captured him, beheading him in the western suburbs. This even more convinced the Daoist priest of his divine power. The bell said, "In the city of transformation, there are not many demonic barriers, yet the children of the ghost mother will eventually enter the lotus bowl of the great bodhisattva. One day they will look back and together rely on the path of the Dao. The two women's escape was due to the miraculous guidance of the immortals, allowing them to leave the suffering of the Buddhist realm. How many people throughout history have taken such risks and gambled their fate?" A Demoralized Scholar Xiasummer from LinTong, whose name was Qitong, had a dull and slow nature and studied to become a candidate for the imperial examinations. Every time he produced a piece of work, the group would surely mock him. Once, he happened to take the examination for young scholars and plagiarized old texts to enter the local school. Later, when he took the annual examination, he thought he would surely be placed in the lowest rank. He met a diviner in the market and consulted him, who gave a prophecy: "It cannot be heard or seen, and it cannot be seen or touched. If a gentleman consults the divination, he will surely get his name." The diviner raised his hand and congratulated him, saying, "Your writing will certainly be the champion." Xiasummer was delighted and boasted to the crowd. They said, "Even if the examiner's two eyes are blind, they can still smell the good and bad. Below the third rank, you might have a chance to be the champion." Xiasummer was greatly ashamed. At the time, a certain official was appointed to oversee education in Xi'an and, before leaving, bid farewell to his mentor, a certain Secretary of State. The Secretary of State was from Xi'an and thought he had some favoritism towards scholars, so he tried his best to ask for advice. The Secretary of State's lower part of the body was slightly exposed as he stood up. The official suspected that there was some instruction and hurriedly asked him. The Secretary of State said, "There is nothing else, just that the lower part of the body is connected!" The official nodded and thought that "Xiasummer Qitong" must be a close associate of the Secretary of State, so he carefully remembered it.

After the official visited Xi'an, there was indeed a student named Xia Sheng, whose name was Qitong. After the closed examination, the official carefully read his scroll, finding the words and reasoning to be incoherent and truly laughable. Due to the earnest instructions from his mentor, out of necessity, he was forced to give a rating, placing Xia Sheng at the top of the class. When the results were revealed, the students were greatly agitated. They then thought that the official, originally a Hanlin scholar, must have true judgment in reading the text. Xia Sheng was a poor scholar, completely unable to find any connections or favors, yet he ranked at the top with inferior work, which they could not understand. After completing his term, the official entered the capital and reported to a certain Shangshu (Minister). The Minister was puzzled and thought deeply for a long time, then suddenly laughed and said, "You are mistaken! That day, my lower qi (energy) happened to be released, so I made those remarks. I never gave any instructions!" The official realized this and also laughed. Later, the story was passed on to Xi'an, and the students' doubts were resolved. Alas! Pursuing fame and success due to the release of qi, even if laughed at by the scholarly world, is not better than those filled with the stench of money? Duo said, "Ancient people naming had their own meanings. A bright star in the chest, Li named Taibai; a green mountain asking for an heir, Cui called Zilang. Gao Lin responded to the sign of obtaining a treasure, and Huan Wen fulfilled the prophecy of testing his son's cry. I don't know why the person named Xia Sheng chose such a name, to be a good omen for the future! The *Physiognomy Classic* says: 'Abundant in fertile land, literary fortune is smooth.' Then, for those who pursue fame and success, this is the best name."

The Transformed Beggar In the Western Shu region, Li Taishi of Mò Zhuāng met at the Wu Jiang governor's official residence. At the time, many guests were present, each recounting past events. Li Taishi said, "In my hometown, there was a mad beggar named Jin Manzi. He traveled with his wife, begging for food in Wu, and lodged in the left corridor of the Ten Kings Hall. One day, he begged at a wealthy household and returned home, weeping bitterly. His wife asked him why. He replied, 'We are all equal in our seven feet of height. They feast on rich and fatty food, wear embroidered clothes, and daily enjoy beautiful wives and concubines as their pleasure, while I am so cold and hungry. Why is the merciless Lord Yama so unfair?' Afterward, he still stayed in the corridor. He saw the Ten Kings summon him inside and said, 'Do not complain. I will transform you.' They ordered the ghost judges to first transform his tongue, saying, 'This was the tongue used by General Qu Lianghan to eat camel hump roast. If you transform it, you can feast on all kinds of delicacies forever.' Then they transformed his shoulders and back, saying, 'This was the robe of green phoenix feathers worn by Emperor Zhao. If you transform it, you can wear splendid robes and be warmed by the heat of the sun forever.' They also ordered the transformation of his lower body, saying, 'This was the place where Emperor Han entered the land of softness and beauty, occupying three thousand beauties. If you transform it, you can have beautiful women forever.' The mad beggar was overjoyed and knocked heads in gratitude before leaving."

Soon after dawn, the wife brought leftover food to the beggar. The mad beggar became very angry and said, "I will feast on delicacies, do not let this soil my tongue!" Then she brought a worn-out robe. He became even angrier and said, "I will wear fine brocade, do not let this disgrace my body!" The wife scolded him, and the beggar became even more furious and said, "I will soon have a golden house to store a beautiful woman, see how you, ugly woman, will send bedding to your husband." The wife was startled and asked for advice, and the beggar spoke loudly to describe his plans. The wife laughed and said, "If so, then you still forget one thing." The beggar asked, "What is it?" The wife replied, "You have changed everything, but not the fate of being a wealthy man like Shi Chong." The mad beggar was speechless. This might be a playful talk by a historian, but in the world, those who are not like the mad beggar are few. The speaker said, "Only because he is mad does he speak nonsense, and only because he speaks nonsense is he foolish." Yan Lao made this play to stop nonsense, and the historian made this statement to wake up the foolish.

The Ghost of Caihua Sanlangzi In the northern countryside of Yixing, there was a female spirit known as Caihua Sanlangzi, a folk belief that she was the wife of the third son of the "Five Saints," who followed and haunted people. This ghost never entered the city, but only troubled and deceived rural men. One day, an old man from a village had a son named Fulong. In the spring, Fulong was walking alone in the fields when he saw a woman with slightly older age but charming grace. They passed by each other arm in arm. Fulong secretly stroked her wrist, and the woman laughed sharply. She then took him away with her. They arrived at a place where there were no doors, halls, or rooms, but only a small wooden bed placed in a bowl-like structure, with blankets and bedding all present. The woman pulled him to lie down beside her and removed her undergarments to meet him. Fulong was just starting to cut, and his knife did not hit the right spot. The woman led him in, and he tried his best to complete the act. Soon after, he felt trapped in the center and turned his weapon against himself, retreating. The woman laughed and got up, while Fulong sank into a deep sleep. The old man lost his son and searched for him in the fields. He found Fulong lying naked and drunk in a vegetable ditch. He helped him back home, and after a long time, Fulong woke up. That evening, he saw the woman quietly enter the room and said, "You foolish boy, spoiling my mood. Now we will raise flags and set up drums, and predict a long night of battle with the lady soldiers." She climbed onto the bed and got under the covers, resuming their encounter. Fulong had a particularly fearful and hesitant attitude, and the woman's wild behavior resumed. She moved the shield to the spear and forced a charge. Fulong lost his bow three times and broke through the encirclement, fleeing. The woman sneered, "With such training, you will always be afraid of the enemy, you are a tired soldier!" She quietly got out of bed and left.

The next night, she returned, carrying with caution and care, feeding with tenderness, charging through the encirclement and plundering the ranks, fighting all night long. The woman said, "Having something to rely on and thus not fear, the child can still be taught." From then on, there was no night without disturbance. Fu Lang's body was emaciated and his face gaunt, day by day, he became as thin as a withered tree, and neither talismans, exorcisms, nor rituals proved effective. At that time, Fu Lang had a sister who had married into the Li family in the city, and she was affected by the third son of the Five Saints, also on the verge of death. The husband-in-law ordered a strong woman to carry her to her father-in-law's house at midnight, to avoid a curse or evil influence. The father-in-law was just worried about his son-in-law's death, then saw the woman being carried inside, increased his anxiety even more. Around the first watch of the night, he saw the woman enter the son's room and leave, a short while later, the third son also arrived, found the woman, embraced her in his arms, and was about to commit adultery. Suddenly, he saw the woman come out of the son's room, the third son became very angry, grabbed her hair, threw her to the ground, and said, "Fugitive woman, I have been searching for you for over ten years, and now you are committing adultery here!" He hit her cheeks with his palm over a hundred times. The woman fell to the ground and wept bitterly. The third son looked at the woman and sighed, "I have committed adultery with many of you. This woman's unfaithfulness is also how the heavens are punishing me. Please return quickly, and continue your marital relationship."

From now on, I shall cut down the evil woman and retreat to the wilderness; I shall have no face to enter your house. After saying this, he dragged the woman away, and the evil spirits of both families vanished. The author says: "To use evil spirits to drive away evil spirits, it is a novel occurrence. Fortunately, the enemies met on a narrow path!" The beautiful wife was disgraced, and the reckless man was reformed. Those who commit immoral acts, why not let ghosts be their mirror! The Grass Shoe Four, whose origin is unknown, is the younger brother of the Grass Shoe Three. In Wu customs, after paying homage to the gods before New Year's Eve, the Grass Shoe Three and others are worshipped with incense and silk. Although not a main god, they belong to the category of lesser deities like Zigu and Magong. The younger brother relies on his elder's power, making demands and causing trouble in the area. A certain girl from Lindun Village, orphaned at a young age, remained unmarried at nineteen. One night, she saw a man in a cotton robe and black hat, resembling a high-ranking official, but dragging a pair of straw shoes and staggering. The girl was about to scream, but her tongue was blocked, and her limbs were weak, unable to resist, so she was dragged onto the bed and subjected to light touches. He said, "I am the Grass Shoe Four, and I have a connection with you. If you follow me, I will bring you good fortune." Before dawn, he hurriedly left.

On a bright night, the host gathered with four or five guests, setting up a banquet in a high hall and seating his wife at the end. Among the guests was one who was silly and playful, frequently kicking the woman's double phoenix shoes with his foot. She blushed and shrank up to stand up. The host noticed this and scolded the guest. The guest replied, "Your wife's embroidered shoes and brocade socks only fit to match black boots. To be a companion with a person wearing straw shoes, is particularly unrefined." The host glared at him. A guest beside said, "The straw shoe party is indeed lacking in elegance, I fear that recently the broken boot party is also not a good guest." Everyone clapped hands and laughed loudly. The host's anger slightly eased, and he then poured a large cup as a rule: after that, if anyone played with his new wife, he must finish three cups. Soon after, the guest went mad again, picked up the fruit plate and threw it at the woman's face. The host took the cup and made him finish it. The guest took out a hundred coins and placed them on the table, saying, "I cannot drink, I wish to redeem my sin with this." The host laughed and put them in his sleeve. The other guests said, "How vulgar! Selling the new wife for a hundred coins, truly the nature of a straw shoe person!"

Suddenly, a commotion arose, and they all dispersed in a hurry. The four men, deeply ashamed of their straw shoes, tossed and turned in bed, discontented. They left for four or five days and did not return. Suddenly, one night, they arrived wearing fine leather boots, clinking as they came, and lifted their feet to place them on the woman's knees, looking around with pride, saying, "Now I understand the advantage of fine leather boots! Once put on, the toes immediately rise!" Just as he was about to take off his boots and go to bed, a man with a black face and red beard burst in, shouting, "You rascal, give back my boots!" The four men panicked and fell to the ground. The man with the black face and red beard said, "I am Zhong, who passed the imperial examinations after studying, and I wear these fine leather boots as commanded by God to enhance my appearance. You, a scoundrel from the market, should have followed your brother's example and stuck to your humble origins, but you dare to usurp my noble status, pretending to be a scholar, and spreading debauchery in private quarters. How can your crime be forgiven?" He ordered the boots to be removed and held the feet upside down with both hands, saying, "Such a pair of muddy legs, how much fortune can they bear?" With a loud sound, his body split in two, and he was devoured. The man then took his boots and left.

A woman was so shocked that she fainted and remained unconscious for half a day before regaining consciousness. Later, she married a certain man from the village and advised him to never wear black boots, as if to warn against a similar disaster. The official said, "An uneducated person loves to wear black boots, and this item is not prohibited by law. However, how can the sins of a cow's hoof and a dog's paw be atoned for? Fortunately, Master Zhong has long guarded the rear door; otherwise, when entering the hall and the inner chamber, wouldn't the sound of footsteps be clearly heard?" **Litigation and Its Deception** In the south of Jiang, there are many litigious people, and Wu is the most so. There was a father and son, both greedy and cunning, skilled in writing legal documents. One day, they dreamed that ghostly officials led them to the hall of Yama. The king, leaning on the desk, first interrogated the father, saying, "Scholars, farmers, artisans, and merchants, each have their own constant occupations. Why do you write legal documents?" The father replied, "Am I fond of litigation? People tempted me with gold and silk, and I initially refused, but my eyes glared with greed, and I could not help but agree." The king then interrogated the son, saying, "This is your fault! If I had been born with unfeeling hands, how could I have written legal documents?" The king asked, "What tricks do you use to make black and white seem reversed?" They replied, "It is not difficult. Laozi Xi sat with a woman, and we fabricated a charge of rape. Guan Zhong received a large estate, and we applied the law on the seizure of property." The king said, "This is to accuse the innocent of guilt! And what about twisting the truth to make it seem right?" They replied, "It is even easier. Oao Xiang killed his brother, following his father's command; Chen Ping helped his sister-in-law, which can be called saving someone from drowning."

King said, "Indeed, that is so! What about those who listen to lawsuits?" The person replied, "To deceive by means of a certain method, then Yanzi picks up dust, is misled by one's teacher, Zeng Mu throws her shuttle, also doubts her son. How much more so, then, for those who adjudicate cases facing south, how many have a bright mirror hanging high? Opening and closing doors, pushing and waiting, creating many branches to dazzle, providing side evidence to entangle, being supported by upright and kind officials, or surrounded by greedy and cruel ones. My methods will surely succeed!" The king was furious and ordered the Ox-Head to pry out his father's eyes and cut off his son's two arms, still commanding the ghost servants to send them back. When they woke up, the father and son each experienced their dreams as reality. They reported this to the responsible officials, who said that since these people had already been punished by the underworld, their lawsuits could be somewhat lessened. A few days later, the officials ordered the clerks to go and observe them, and they saw those who came to sue, holding gold and currency, surrounding the hall. The father sat cross-legged on a mat facing south, closed his eyes and whispered instructions, while his son had a table beside him, using his toes to hold a five-inch tube and writing as fast as the wind. The clerks returned and reported this, and the responsible officials sighed and said, "If all the prefectures and counties were to become living Yama (King of Hell), these people could not be eliminated either. How terrifying!" Duo said, "In the *I Ching*, 'The upper trigram is Qian (Heaven) and the lower trigram is Kun (Earth), which represents a lawsuit.' The image says, 'Heaven and water go in opposite directions.' Alas! That vast sky, is it also a lawsuit? I do not know the teacher who does this, is it in accordance with Heaven, or against Heaven? And its hexagram says, 'There is faithfulness that is obstructed.' Therefore, to avoid obstruction, one must seek a teacher."

**Famous Courtesan Selling Her Reputation** Yellow Bamboo Marsh, Qi's Bai Ye Sheng. On his way to the capital, he passed through Wuqiao County and visited a friend who was staying at the county office. The friend said, "There is a famous courtesan named Zhu Qingniang here. Have you seen her yet?" Huang replied, "Not yet!" So they went together. When they arrived, the residence had white-walled red-doors, unlike the thatched huts and earthen walls of the northern regions. An old man with a gray beard invited them to sit and offered tea. After the tea was served, an old woman came out, briefly inquired about their well-being, and then led them into the inner room. The walls were adorned with inscriptions and dedications from famous people, and in the center hung a painting titled "The Two Qiaos Observing Military Books." A black leather table and an incense burner were set up, and a red plum blossom branch was placed in a vase, its bud still closed. Soon, a young maid came forward and said, "Madam Zhu is suffering from a hangover and has just woken up. She is dressing by the window now. Please wait a moment." After a long while, another maid came out and said, "Madam Zhu has finished dressing, but she is feeling tired and has fallen asleep again. She will wake up and change into her formal attire to see you shortly." Judging from her demeanor, she seemed highly esteemed, and Huang, eager to meet the famous courtesan, patiently waited, his gaze fixed on the curtain, not looking at anything else. After a long time, the old woman unfolded the curtain, and two maids helped Madam Zhu arrive.

Huang quickly glanced at her. Her face was splattered with flour, and her lipstick was smeared. She had a large, heavy belly, as big as a three-stone jar. She walked with large strides, as if a grain transport boat was passing through a lock. Huang was greatly surprised and turned to his friend, saying, "Such a courtesan would shame the streets of Chang'an." His friend regretted having spoken rashly and secretly fled. However, Qingniang showed no shame and calmly said to Huang, "How do courtesans compare to scholars?" Huang replied, "They are equal!" Qingniang said, "If so, then why should I feel ashamed of being called a courtesan? Scholars wield their three-inch brushes, galloping across the literary arena, making people around the world yearn for their charm, valuing their inner talent. My reputation, though seemingly earned through superficial means, is actually based on the real skills in the bedroom." Huang smiled and said, "What do you mean by 'skills'?" Qingniang replied, "There are openings and closings, there are slow and fast moments, there are grasping and releasing. These are the secrets of scholarly writing, are they not?"

Huang Daoyue became intimately entangled with Qingniang. Later, he said to her, "The land of tenderness truly has genuine joy. Even if you remove Xi Shi's eyebrows, cut off Pan Jinlian's feet, and desecrate the essence of the courtesans, it is still like purchasing the *Twelve Beauties of Jinling* and embracing them every day—it cannot truly captivate the soul!" Within half a month, he lost all his resources and, before he could take the imperial examination, returned home in disarray. When his friends learned of this, they sighed and said, "In this world, those who gracefully claim the title of 'scholar' are certainly adept at insincere arguments to seek fame and fortune. Unbeknownst to them, even courtesans do the same. Huang Daoyue fell into their trap without realizing it. The so-called scholars' intelligence is even inferior to that of a courtesan. Alas!"

The Bell said, "Throughout history, scholars and intellectuals have spoken of ancient learning, mentioning the Song, Tang, Jin, and Han dynasties, and no further. Yet this courtesan's skill was taught by Tianmu to the Yellow Emperor. An ancient song says, 'The mythical maiden is my teacher, her gracefulness fills a myriad of ways.' This is true ancient learning, this is a true scholar!" **The Mud Puppet** In the west of Guangxi, in Liuzhou Prefecture, there is a temple dedicated to the land deity. In the corridor, a statue of a deity was sculpted, with an unattractive appearance, wearing a black robe with a sash. The locals called it a mud puppet. When the county magistrate was honest, the statue’s hands were tucked into its sleeves; if a corrupt magistrate governed the prefecture, the statue’s hands would extend as if begging for money. Once, a certain official came to govern the prefecture, and he was excessively corrupt and greedy. The statue’s hand protruded from its sleeve by one foot. The official tried to hide his corruption by secretly ordering his servant to pull the statue’s hand back into its sleeve at night. The next day, he saw that the hand had protruded even further, about five inches, and the fingers were tightly clenched. The official was greatly ashamed and prepared sacrifices to appease the statue. Within a few days, the statue’s hand suddenly retracted, and after a few more days, it gradually returned to its sleeve. The official privately rejoiced, thinking that the spirit had also accepted bribes, but he did not know that he had already been reported, and the new county magistrate, Peng Gong, had arrived. Peng Gong’s name was Tingji; he was my father’s sister’s husband, who rose from a position in the central government to become a county magistrate. He was a strict and upright person, so the spirit had foreseen his arrival. One day, the statue’s hand began to gradually protrude from its sleeve again, and the official was greatly alarmed. He privately investigated and found that his subordinate officials had presented him with two buckets of lychees, containing three hundred gold pieces, which he had unknowingly accepted. He quickly returned the bribe, and the statue’s hand also suddenly retracted. From then on, he remained incorruptible throughout his term, not taking even a single coin.

Bell said, "According to physiognomy books, 'those who can reach their knees with their arms are destined for great wealth.' Hmph! Puppet, you are a god of great wealth." Stone Turtle Two brothers from the small bridge in Wu Gate went out for a spring outing to Canglang Pavilion. Passing by the school office, they saw a turtle at the base of a stele, not recognizing it, mistook it for a tortoise, and rubbed its head, saying, "What heavy sins did you bear in your past life, that you disgrace yourself so much in front of people today!" They laughed loudly and left. Later, on the occasion of their mother's birthday, they performed the miscellaneous drama "Feast at Hong Gate" at night, with many guests present. Suddenly, on stage, Fan Kuai appeared, lifting a knife and moving forward, causing the host and guests to turn pale. He shouted, "I am the god of the turtle! Originally a son of a dragon, Heaven pitied me for being brave but lacking in culture, so I was ordered to carry stones to the school, where I could slightly glimpse writing and learning. Unfortunately, my form is stupid and crude, and the servant mistakenly thought I was a tortoise, mockingly laughing at me. You, a rascal from the marketplace, usually do not maintain your morals and conduct, and you are also drunk, truly what is called spiritually similar but not physically similar. You do not measure up, yet you say I disgrace myself in front of others. Today, with guests celebrating, I will argue this case with you." After finishing, he lifted the knife to kill them. The two brothers crawled and begged for their lives, and the guests also pleaded for mercy on their behalf. He then threw down the knife and laughed, saying, "Preserve your bones and become noble, rather than live dragging your tail on the road, I will temporarily forgive you." Letting go, he returned to the stage, continuing to perform the script of "Feast at Hong Gate," still as Fan Kuai. When asked, he did not understand. The story spread widely in Wu, and the two brothers were placed on the list of disreputable people. Later, they were imprisoned by example, and people still called them "Clothed in Brocade and Honored as a Tortoise."

The Bell said: "Many words, even if they are just talk, can be feared. But it cannot help being jesting and cruel!" Harmonious Bell. Volume Six Elimination of Evil Spirits at the Upper Pure Palace Di, a resident east of the Sanmao Temple in Wu Prefecture, was a servant in the household of a certain司马. The司马 had a daughter who was afflicted by an evil spirit, and despite numerous attempts to get rid of it, it remained. Therefore, he wrote a short note and ordered Di to go to Longhu Mountain to ask the Celestial Master to deal with it. Upon arrival, the courtyard was spacious and the palaces were imposing, with flags hung everywhere, inscribed with the names of divine generals for protection. The gatekeeper went inside to report, and after about two hours, Di was summoned to meet. The judges escorted the Celestial Master out, who sat on a tiger skin chair in a lotus canopy, with a gold seal and precious sword displayed on the altar. Di knelt at the eaves and presented the case to the judge. The judge then presented it to the Celestial Master. The Celestial Master carefully read it, shook his head, furrowed his brow, and pondered for a long time, whispering something to the judge, but it is unknown what was said. Then he gave a piece of paper to Di and instructed the道士 at the Upper Pure Palace to perform a ritual.

Dí was on a mission and left, encountering a Daoist who was wearing a cloth cap and short robe, carrying manure in the fields. He then took out the scripture of the Celestial Master and showed it to the Daoist. The Daoist looked at it and suddenly burst into laughter, saying, "The Celestial Master sells talismans and earns money by the thousands, yet he never considers the consequences. When it comes to killing and harming lives, he always troubles us, which is also very laughable." The Daoist then took out a small wooden basin, poured cold water into it, placed a copper mirror upside down on top, dipped a brush in ink, and applied it to the mirror's surface several times. Soon, the water boiled like hot water, and a thread of hot air rose to the heavens. Suddenly, a sharp cracking sound was heard, and the hot air descended, the water scattered like jumping pearls, and the basin was empty. The Daoist said, "It's done!" Dí was delighted and told the Daoist, "Go back and tell your master, I will certainly give you a generous reward." The Daoist merely laughed coldly and said nothing, gathered his tools, and continued carrying manure, walking away briskly. Dí then returned to the Celestial Master, took the path back home, and reported to Sīmǎ. Indeed, on that very day and time, in Sīmǎ's house, a thunderclap shook the room, and blood rained down, and the evil spirit was immediately eradicated. Later, Sīmǎ offered one hundred taels of silver, a set of cloth robes, and personally visited the Upper Pure Palace to express his gratitude.

He encountered something that remained unseen, and thus sighed and returned. Duo said, "Those with great abilities do not boast about them. Even among common people and in humble occupations, there can be extraordinary skills. Yet people seek extraordinary individuals among the wealthy and powerful, which is like buying charms from under lotus curtains." In the Hall of Judgment, souls were summoned for judgment. Mr. Li Bao, a successful candidate in the imperial examinations from my hometown, served as the magistrate of Huining County in Gansu and was later appointed as a professor at the Confucian academy of Anqing. He traveled alone to take up his new post. However, his predecessor's family had not yet returned, so he temporarily stayed at the corridor of the Ten Kings Hall. One night, he heard loud voices in the hall. Li got up and peeked inside, seeing a bright light with officials standing in two rows; a red-bearded man with a tall hat and a sash held a book and stood at the eastern corner. Soon, a king with a ceremonial headdress appeared, followed by officials paying homage. The king said, "It has been thirty years since the records of the dead were checked, and this has led to accumulated corruption. Now we must carefully review them and not allow any concealment or leniency." The red-bearded man immediately presented the book. Then, people carrying shackles and chains entered the hall in a line. After their names were called, they left the hall in a dispirited manner. Next, the soul-calling register was checked, and their names were called several times, but no one responded. The king said, "There are 87,000 soul-calling ghosts, why is no one here?" The red-bearded man stepped forward and reported, "We received orders from the Wheel King of the Rear Palace.
