Men have been sent to become doctors, and women to become courtesans, all reborn in the human world."

The king frowned solemnly and said, "To take souls and spirits, the underworld has its own fixed limits. If these beings continue to spread their poison throughout the world, there will never be an end to those who fall victim!" He then opened the ledger of hungry ghosts. Immediately, a clerk approached, knelt down, and reported, "The guards at the threshold between the living and the dead failed to guard and inspect properly, allowing the hungry ghosts to escape. Now, they have all escaped to the world of the living." The king asked, "What are they doing in the world of the living?" The clerk replied, "Most of them are acting as county magistrates." The king said, "These beings have endured hunger in hell for thousands of years. Now that they have gained power, they will surely consume everything, leaving no survivors!" The clerk asked, "Should we return them to the underworld?" The king pondered for a long time and said, "This will be a great deal of trouble. Those who can endure hunger, let them stay; but if they cannot, the more severe ones should have their official records removed, causing their descendants to be relegated to lowly positions, and the less severe ones should be demoted to cold and insignificant offices, suffering from cold and hunger for life, and restored to their original form." Li, who was watching from the side, could not help but burst out laughing. Suddenly, all the lamps and candles went out, and there was no sound whatsoever in the hall.

Later, I met him at the Qinhuai guesthouse, where he detailed the story and entrusted me to record it. Duo said, "I have heard that when Mr. Li was in Huining, he wore military attire to fight against bandits and achieved significant merit. His decision to change to a scholarly position was also a wise and strategic move. He once wrote a couplet that read: 'Scholars with learning are all my peers, ordinary officials can only reach this level of office.' This gives us a glimpse of his character! Is this perhaps just a playful remark?" Su San Liu, whose given name is Wei and courtesy name is Wanru, took the imperial examinations in Nanjing in the year Jiyu. He stayed at Ding's Water Pavilion. Before that, a certain nobleman from Jinling spent a thousand gold pieces to secure a courtesan. Among the courtesans in the entertainment district, there were those known as the 'literary top scholar' and the 'literary third-place winner.' The 'literary third-place winner' was a woman named Su Xiuying, who followed her mother's surname and was called 'Little Three' because she was the third daughter in her family. She admired Liu's reputation and asked her fellow townsman Mr. Zha to introduce her to him, wishing to be invited for a visit. Liu smiled and said, "The romances of Xi Shi and Fan Li, the tragic love stories of Zhuge Liang and his wife, and the once vibrant Qinhuai River have long been overgrown with grass. How could there still be old beauties from the past who can sing 'White Silk Skirt'?" Despite Zha's persistent encouragement, Liu declined and left.

After walking a few steps, Liu encountered an old acquaintance, Huang, who strongly invited him to visit his residence. As soon as Liu entered the hall, he saw a lady with her hair piled with jasmine flowers like snow, wearing a thin, gauzy shirt, exposing both sides of her body, with a hint of a red wall; she wore a silk skirt that draped down, and her bare feet were in butterfly-shaped shoes about eight inches long. Seeing the guest, she was not very responsive, and she rubbed her gold bracelets on her arms, making a clinking sound. Liu was repulsed by her. Huang said, "Do not look at her with contempt, this is a certain lady who was the literary champion of the Qinhuai River." Liu smiled and said, "Indeed, the reputation of a状元 is extraordinary. But you are used to it, I cannot grovel at the feet of such a lady." They hurriedly bid farewell, eager to return to his lodging. Zha said, "Not having reached Peach Blossom Spring, how can you talk about returning?" Liu angrily said, "If the状元 is like this, what about the探花? I would rather recognize heroes beyond the ranks of the successful, I dare not seek talent under the flowers of the successful!" He waved his sleeves and returned. Zha recounted the story of the third person, lowering his head and remaining silent. Afterward, he sighed and said, "The members of the Revival Society during the Ming Dynasty, who passed the imperial examination under the list of Zhou Yanru, compared to defecating on the head of a Buddha. I am unfortunate to be associated with such people, should be spurned by the noble and talented!" He threw himself on the bed and wept bitterly, tears streaming down. Zha comforted him, and he stopped.

The young scholars had finished their exams and returned to their hometown together, setting up a table on the eastern side of Wuding Bridge. There, they saw a young woman with a sickly and sorrowful expression, leaning on a railing by the water. Her faint traces of clothing and shadow seemed to make the lake water fragrant. Liu glanced at her and, turning to Chai with a smile, said, "Where did this startled swan come from, gracefully appearing at the Luo River?" Chai replied, "This is the literary prodigy I recommended." Liu deeply regretted his actions and said, "Discarding the fragrant for the foul, rejecting the phoenix for the crow—I realize my fault!" He quickly tied up his boat and visited her, apologizing for his past mistakes. Xiao San said, "A gentleman observes people by their kind; a learned person holds views without being swayed by superficial appearances. If only I can eventually receive your favor, why regret meeting so late?" Liu was greatly delighted. Xiao San prepared a feast to entertain him. After three rounds of wine, Liu got up from his seat and said, "I am deeply attached to emotions, but visits to immoral places are something I have never experienced in life. Today's joyous banquet is already like a farewell feast. Please leave some words, to serve as a trace of a bird's footprint on snow and mud." Xiao San laid a white handkerchief on the table. Liu took up his pen and wrote a section of the poem "Water Tune: Prelude," saying: ### Translation of the Poem: **Water Tune: Prelude** Upon the eastern bridge, the water flows, A startled swan, her grace unfolds. By the railing, her sorrow softly lies, Her shadow faint, the lake's sweet sighs. Liu's gaze, a glance, a fleeting glance, Chai's smile, a word, a tender chance. The literary prodigy, her name, A swan's flight, a gentle flame. Discarding the fragrant for the foul, Rejecting the phoenix, the crow's howl. Liu's regret, a deep, remorseful plea, A visit made, a past to see. Xiao San's words, a gentle tone, A learned heart, no shallow bone. Favor received, no late regret, A joyous feast, a heart's regret. A white handkerchief, a table laid, Liu's pen, a verse, a heart displayed. The Water Tune, a prelude's start, A fleeting trace, a heart's art.

Knocking down the delicate hairpin, the fine brocade does not need to be plucked. Speaking of the intimate love and conflicts between lovers, one confides to the mythical phoenix in the mirror. I am a seed of literary talent, and you are a beauty with a connection to the divine. Together, we serve in the imperial assembly. A minor exile for three thousand years, our journey is confined to the mortal world. Beyond the precious railing, the dew is cold. The blooming flower of a young lady, do not get too close to the precious railing. Yesterday, drinking in Qingzhou; today, seeking pleasure in the pleasure quarters; tomorrow, acquiring a mountain. Living in seclusion together, two phoenixes returning to the moon. After finishing, the boatman hurriedly urged to untie the ropes, and we boarded the boat and left. The scholars of Baixia passed it down as a beautiful tale, even composing long songs to commemorate it. Since then, the name of the flower-seeking scholar is only known in the garden, while the so-called literary状元has few carriages and horses at his gate.

The Bell said, "As soon as one steps out of the ink pool, one immediately climbs the snowy mountain. The evaluation of people in the world, all learned from the streets of Shanheshang. Indeed, to judge people based on their reputation, one must combine iron from all nine regions to create a single, unbreakable piece. If Liu Sheng is like this, he can be said to be able to attract talented people!" **Ge Jiu** At the Water Pavilion of Ding's family, living with Liu Sheng was Cheng Sheng, whose courtesy name was Zhenlu. Cheng was known for his chivalrous spirit, his writing was extraordinary, and his actions were all unique. A courtesan came from the Han River, named Ge Jiu. In her early years, she fell into the world of courtesans. Later, she washed her heart and purified her conduct, cutting flowers and selling shoes, and filially supporting her parents. Suddenly, her two parents passed away one after another, and she had no means to bury them. Unwillingly, she resumed her old profession. A well-wisher recounted this to Cheng Sheng. At the time, it was pouring rain, and Cheng held an umbrella and wore wooden clogs, visiting her in the dead of night. Ge Jiu was immediately smitten upon seeing him and prepared the bed for him to rest. Cheng laughed and said, "There's no need. I am not a dreamer of the Red Building, but I came to see you in the rain to help you raise funds for the funeral expenses!" Ge Jiu was moved to tears and then asked for advice. Cheng said, "Recently, the young men who indulge in pleasure are all like blind men riding blind horses, rushing to the brothels. You, with your simple dress and bun, although you have a calm and elegant appearance, may not be able to earn a thousand gold coins for a laugh. Only by relying on the power of your writing, placing a sign to raise your price, might you attract a large amount of money and achieve your goal." From his sleeve, he took out several feet of砑虹绫 (a kind of fine silk), and because she was the ninth, he playfully picked nine characters to fill a quatrain of the "Golden Threaded Quilt" (《金缕曲》), saying:

Walking by the Twenty-Four Bridges, strange that the east wind casually blows past, on a fine night of the fifteenth. Again looking from the thirteenth floor, casually covering the four surrounding red doors. Lacking a good dream, once every ten years. Counting the sixty-six peaks of Wushan, the third peak, left as the path for wandering clouds. The two stars shine, the seven crossings are crossed. In the three paths of three lives, a script is written. Leaning before the flowers, the six-curved railing, the sanxian (three-stringed instrument) softly speaks. Playing the "Six Beats of the Flower," half of the soul is consumed by the dance. Adding a strand, the beauty of Xie's eyebrows. Thirty-six mandarin ducks circling the four corners, adding two parts, the bright moon at the third watch of the night. Do not yet write the Four Sorrows. After finishing, the rain deepens and the night grows dark, Ge repeatedly pulls his sleeves, brushes his robe, and finally returns to his guest quarters. The next day, Ge decorates with a painted screen, inviting guests to sit. Those who love fame vie to spread the news, the noble sons and gentlemen of the city, the feasts never cease. The alley of the Pichang gate, almost matches the legendary Micolou of Gu Mei. Within a few days, gold fills the chest, he orders his younger brother to carry it back, burying the two coffins. Sending a letter to invite Cheng, but Cheng does not come, treading on silk as if it were grass.

One day, just after she had finished her morning makeup, she accompanied a noble guest at a banquet under the *lukui* pavilion. Suddenly, someone arrived carrying a white wooden box. When it was opened, it contained a golden pair of scissors, along with a monk's hat, robe, and shoes. Inside was a short note that read: "Ancient people who dishonor their bodies are not filial. I pity your ignorance, so I will temporarily dishonor myself to fulfill filial piety. Now that the matter is resolved, my heart has been fulfilled. Turn back early, seek the shore of enlightenment, and do not sink into the sea of desire or fall into the dust of flowers. Can the person in the grave close their eyes? Upon receiving this message, quickly cut off the roots of karma and return to your original attire. Outside the window where eyebrows are drawn, there lies the field of choosing Buddha. Rowing at the lakehead, it is all part of the path of compassionate ferrying. If you can cover late, cleanse your past sins, and do not hesitate, you will not be bound like a silkworm." Upon reading the note, Ge Luan had a great awakening, cut off her own hair in front of the mirror, and changed her appearance to resemble a monk. The noble guest hesitantly retreated. After a while, Cheng came laughing, clasped his hands, and directly ascended the seat. Ge Luan prostrated and worshipped. Cheng, imitating an old monk, recited a verse: "That beautiful person, anyone can marry. I will now guide you, to transcend the muddy soil, kick over the peach leaf ferry, and jump out of the lake of sorrow. From now on, let go of the shore of the lotus, where is there no lotus in the fire?" Upon receiving the message, Ge Luan called a boat at night and returned to Yangzhou, offering her body to the *Tanhua* Upper Temple. Later, she asked Han Manting to write the *Prostitute Hall Converts to Buddhism* painting, which she hung in the pure room, to remember the old virtue.

铎曰：“昔日卞玉京成为女道士，偶尔有赞助者。由此可知，莲花的本性虽是天生的，但莲丝难以割断，也是女子的常态。然而，一片纸飞来，六根清净，这个孩子的智慧事业，必定非凡。如果不是当头棒喝，那么柔花弱絮，又将漂泊到哪里呢！像这样的金粉如来，在风流薮泽中应当处处用丝线绣之。” 奇女雪怨 线娘，是夏邑士族的女儿。擅长诗词歌赋，兼通帖括。每当创作一艺，老师宿儒都会赞叹道：“女学士易钗而栉，恐怕不到玉堂金马。”年十七岁时，父母相继去世，线娘独自一人。隔壁院落是某位学生的别业。庭院中有一株玉兰，斜倚东墙。线娘清晨起床，摘花于树上。某生看见了她，向墙下深深一揖。线娘脸红欲避。某生说：“我不是宋玉，怎敢妄想登墙？只因独学五师，愿作王逸少，执贽簪花座下耳。”随即拿出一卷窗课，嘱她点定。线娘带回内室，阅其文，才华秀瞻，间有一二小疵，碍于场屋者，直笔删去。次日，折花墙角，袖而还之。某生感佩。

After some time, the pursuit became more intense. The man wrote a text titled "Climbing Over the Neighbor's Wall to Embrace the Maiden" to provoke her. The woman responded with a text titled "Matchmaker's Words." The man laughed and said, "Responding to urgency with delay, you completely miss the point of the topic." The woman said, "I'm afraid that if I write hastily, I won't have a proper conclusion." The man felt that her words were reasonable and climbed over the wall, grabbing her arm and saying, "I have always treated you as my teacher, why don't you sit in the red curtain with me?" The woman gently resisted and said, "Scholars are most likely to act against their conscience. If you betray your teacher, will you not betray your teacher, just like Feng Meng betrayed Yi?" The man then swore by mountains and rivers, and pledged his oath by the sun and moon. The woman then consented to their union. They lived together, morning in the courtyard and evening in the room, for almost half a year. The woman urged the man to present a gift to her parents. The man promised but delayed in doing so. Later, he eventually negotiated marriage with another family. On the wedding night, the woman learned of this and stood at the wall, hoping the man would come to say goodbye. However, the man was busy building his new home and no longer remembered their past union. The woman, filled with anger, closed the door and committed suicide by hanging. The man heard about it and mourned and sighed, feeling a sense of regret.

After taking the provincial examination, he just picked up his scroll to begin thinking, when he saw Xian Nang coming gracefully. He was terrified she would seek revenge on him, shaking with fear. However, Xian Nang had no angry expression; instead, she smoothed the paper and ground ink, urging him to focus on his writing and explaining the main theme of the question before leaving. That year, he was recommended for the provincial examination. Later, when he took the central government examination, Xian Nang came again. She smoothed the paper and ground ink just as she had done during the provincial examination. She replaced any inappropriate or awkward phrases in his essay, and that year he passed again. He was ranked second in the palace examination and served as an official in the Ministry of Agriculture. Xian Nang often came to him and said, "You hold an official position in the capital, earning only a meager salary. How can you fill your official pouch? Why not seek a post outside the capital? You can quickly become a high-ranking official with a salary of two thousand stones!" He nodded in agreement. Within two years, he was promoted to governor of a prefecture. Originally a poor scholar, he suddenly gained the status of a high-ranking official with a large retinue. He exploited the common people and enriched his own pockets. Not long after, he accepted bribes and allowed criminals to go unpunished. When his crimes were exposed and reported to the emperor, he was sentenced to public execution. The night before his execution, he saw Xian Nang coming to him with her embroidered headscarf, her hair loose. She said, "After years of grievance and anger, justice has finally been served. I helped you achieve your career because scholars bury themselves in their studies, how could they suffer a major calamity? It was necessary to place you in the officialdom so that you could uphold the law, and now the time for justice is not far off." She laughed and left. Duo said, "One act of betrayal, ten years of dejection. Who would have thought that even under the mirror of the lotus, there could be a place for a woman to seek revenge? It is only a lenient punishment for a man who is unfaithful or cruel to his lover to be removed from the list of successful candidates!"

A Scholar Repaying a Debt of Gratitude In the Plain Province, there was a censor named Liu Gong. As a young man, he was poor and humble, teaching at the Guan Temple in the East Village. At the end of the year, he disbanded his academy and entered the city to visit his sister. She gave him a young chicken. On his return, he slaughtered it, intending to use it for the New Year celebrations. Coincidentally, a student's family had lost a chicken and began to secretly discuss their teacher, gradually turning to riddles. Suspecting him, they questioned him. The clever ones smirked, while the less intelligent ones answered truthfully. Liu Gong became very angry and called the fathers and brothers of the students, buying incense and candles, and vowed before Guan's shrine: "If I am a disgraceful person, I will stumble out of the temple gate." After making the vow, he left. His clothes caught on the threshold, and he stumbled and stood up, his feet raised as if in flight. Everyone laughed. Liu Gong looked up to heaven and exclaimed, "A hero humiliated in the mud, not only regretted by the common people, but even the righteous god does not understand. It is so unjust!" Determined to continue his studies, he hastily borrowed furniture, gained five gold coins, and finally went to the capital. He worked as a copyist under Li Lan Tai. Whenever he had free time, he studied diligently. With a high ranking from Daxing, he entered the school and won every battle. Within a few years, he became a censor. At this time, the Celestial Master came to court and wrote a petition on paper, asking to investigate past events. The Celestial Master submitted a document to the Guan Temple. After several days, the reply arrived, stating: "On a certain day, a certain person received an imperial decree from the Jade Emperor, summoned to the Hall of Supreme Clarity to discuss the campaign against Chiyou. Not in the hall, there was an old badger behind the temple, impersonating a deity, occupying the public seat. Thinking that among the poor and lowly there must be no extraordinary people, it played tricks to amuse itself. Ordered Zhou to expose its lair and斩之 with the Azure Dragon sword."

The Celestial Master recounted the events to the various censors, who had asked for leave and returned home in the middle of the night, summoning their old fathers and brothers. They eventually arrived at the temple. Indeed, there was a desolate grave, sunk into the ground about seven feet, with an old badger whose head and neck had been severed, lying bloody and dripping in a pool of blood. The crowd's doubts were finally resolved. The Master then sighed, saying, "To suffer misfortune through play, although he brought it upon himself, but without this one encouragement, I would have ended up as a village scholar. Where do honor, fame, wealth, and power come from? The addition of adversity in the world is precisely the reason why the unrighteous bless the righteous. This thing perhaps perfected me like jade!" He quickly ordered a vacant spot to be chosen and the badger to be buried there, setting up a stone slab and naming it the Tomb of Gratitude. Ah! You are also enlightened. Duo said, "When heroes are in dire straits, to pity and humiliate me are all deep sources of gratitude. Otherwise, the repayment of kindness with a thousand gold pieces in Huaiyin, how is the slight injury to a young boy equivalent to the meal given by a washerwoman? Fickle and shallow people often use petty injuries to repay resentment, it is just a small matter!" To build a tomb is to repay gratitude, indeed. But how much better would it be not to kill it? Someone said, "The badger's death was due to the Holy Emperor, not to Master Liu." Alas! I did not kill Bo Ren, but he died because of me. In the unseen realm, I bear this good friend, and I can use this example to hold him accountable.

**Appendix by Xie Pu** **Dream Within a Dream** Zeng, a scholar-official, went to the imperial examinations at the Nan Gong, taking an old servant with him. They traveled north and at night, stopped at the Liuzhi Temple. It was already dusk, and they dismounted and rested immediately. He happened to step out the door and saw willow trees lining both sides of the riverbank, a long red bridge, and the bridge crossing the water at an angle. Alongside, there were dozens of apricot trees, with green birds chirping among them. Zeng crossed the bridge to the other side and saw a garden gate wide open. He walked slowly inside, where the garden was deep and secluded, with embroidered pavilions and terraces arranged unevenly. Following the winding corridor, he reached the inner chambers. Behind multiple layers of pearl curtains, ornate hooks were slightly bent. Behind a crystal screen, a coral bed was set up, with a red silk curtain hanging down. There were a pillow and quilt made of fine materials, and the scent of musk and orchid was abundant. To the left was a small table for dressing and grooming, with a mirror case not yet put away and a half-open powder box. Inside a small vase, delicate peach blossoms were neatly placed beside the cosmetics. He heard the sound of a phoenix arriving in a scattered manner and was startled, hiding behind the curtains. He looked and saw his wife in the boudoir. Zeng asked, "How did you get here?" She smiled and said, "This is our new villa that our husband recently purchased. How could you have forgotten?" Zeng did not remember either. They sat together and chatted intimately. Suddenly, there was a commotion outside with horses galloping and people bustling. He got up to inquire and found out that they were welcoming a new official to a banquet in the apricot garden. Zeng immediately mounted his horse, and the attendants led him away. Ten miles of flower-dusted roads, ten thousand households with jeweled towers, with golden reins and jade bits, he looked around with pride. After the banquet, they returned home, and his wife greeted them at the door. They burned incense and lit candles, talking about their years of cold-window night reading, and both had happy expressions.

After retiring to bed, Zeng privately thought that his wife, who was somewhat older, now lived in such wealth and honor. Why not have many concubines to fill the ranks of the secondary wives? Just as he was lying down, a man presented his card at the door, claiming to be a wealthy person who had befriended a newly powerful official. He offered ten measures of pearls to buy four beautiful concubines to serve as secondary wives. Zeng was greatly pleased and immediately ordered them to be summoned. Shortly after, Zeng herself applied her makeup, and the concubines stood in attendance all around. There were those slender like Yan and plump like Huan, all of whom were exceptionally beautiful. Zeng feared that his wife might be jealous, so he led them into another courtyard and asked for their pet names. The plump one was called Juan Juan, the slender one was called Chu Chu, the one with bright eyes and a beautiful smile was called Qian Tao, and the one with hanging hair styled into a slanted bun was called Chun Liu. Zeng ordered Juan Juan to spread the bedding, Chu Chu to hold the quilt, Qian Tao to arrange the embroidered pillows, and Chun Liu to help remove their hats and clothes. Zeng first undressed and entered the curtain, then turned to see the concubines quickly remove their clothes. They unfastened their silk robes, loosened their embroidered skirts, took off their black-headed socks and happy shoes, unfastened their red ribbons, and pulled up their peach-patterned undergarments. Their white flesh and pink skin rushed to join in the night. In a moment, fragrances filled the air, and their beautiful bodies were laid out in an arrangement like eight-petaled fragrant lotuses, elevated and competing to raise. Zeng's heart fluttered, not knowing where to go.

Suddenly, he heard the lady behind him calling out loudly, and his pleasant dream abruptly awakened. He grew greatly angry and said, "Why are you so nagging? A moment of convenience, and I would have been in the warm embrace of dreams long ago." The lady also scolded him. Zeng was very angry and said, "When I was poor and lowly, every step was subject to restraint. Now I am fortunate to be greatly wealthy and noble, and the Han Dynasty has its own system. How could it be due to the whims of a flirtatious and capricious person?" The lady dressed and stood up, weeping against the wall and said, "Do you remember the ungrateful child? When you lost your position, by evening, you couldn't even have a bowl of porridge. I took off the hairpin that pressed against my temples, pawned it for a bowl of porridge. Now you have suddenly achieved success and often give me the cold shoulder. Where is the affection we shared from our youth?" Zeng was proud of his new status and, hearing the lady point out past faults, was even more resentful and unwilling to yield. He pounded the pillow and shouted, "A set of five-colored edicts, how dare you bear it with such a face?"

Suddenly, a laugh was heard at my ear, saying, "Are you having a nightmare, sir?" Turning to look back, I saw an old servant catching lice at the lamp. After a moment of concentration, I laughed heartily under the quilt, while the servant remained puzzled. The bell said, "When one awakens from a spring dream, there is no one who does not laugh; how could one know that they are still in a dream? Only the enlightened have no dreams, because they have no desire for wealth and honor; likewise, only the foolish have no dreams, because they lack the fortune for such desires." **Body Beyond the Body** When Mr. Historian, whose name is not given, had not yet passed the imperial examination, he heard that the old monk Fazan at Lingyin Temple had attained the essence of Zen Buddhism. He approached the monk and asked to be his disciple. The old monk calculated using the heavenly stems and earthly branches for a long time and said, "Your bone structure indicates you are destined for the Buddhist path, and your fate still suggests nobility. Do not be hasty!" The scholar pleaded with him, but the monk replied with a smile, "What business is it of an old monk's? Just receive twelve years of wealth and honor, then come back." The scholar wept and refused to leave, but the old monk threw his divine staff and drove him away. The scholar stumbled down the steps, but quickly got up and fled.

Upon returning home, he seemed dazed, spending his days reading and his nights as if sitting under the guidance of an old monk, reciting scriptures and listening to sermons. He then returned to the temple, but the old monk closed the door and did not admit him, saying, “If you wish to seek enlightenment here, you must return my divine staff to me.” The man was bewildered. Later, after passing the provincial examination, he returned to the temple again. The old monk still closed the door as before. After passing the metropolitan examination in the year Jiwei (己未), he became an official in the Hanlin Academy and later served as the chief examiner for the Hubei provincial examination. He entered the Jade Hall by day and the Red Tent by night, yet the cushion and Buddhist lamp of meditation were never absent from his dreams. Twelve years passed slowly, and with his old vows fulfilled, he requested retirement and returned to his hometown. He then rushed day and night by post, arriving in the Zhejiang region within a month. That night, he stayed at a Kui family inn, calculating that he was no more than fifteen miles from Lingyin Temple. Despite his restlessness, he could not fall asleep. Clutching his blanket in anxious thought, he suddenly felt his foot slip off the bed. When he looked, he found himself in the main hall of Lingyin Temple. A small shrine with flickering lights illuminated the Buddha’s seat. A patched robe, left and right sewn together, lay nearby. Touching the top of his head, he found it smooth, devoid of any hair. Greatly alarmed, he hurried to the old monk’s seat. The monk had closed his eyes and lowered his eyebrows, deep in meditation. After about two hours, the old monk emerged from his meditation. The man prostrated himself on the ground, begging for wisdom. The old monk smiled and said, “You have been ordained here for twelve years, yet you still speak so much?” The man suddenly realized the truth.

The next day, at the Kuai family inn, the public (referring to the protagonist) was not seen to rise. Lifting the cover to see, there was only a Zen staff; greatly alarmed, they searched everywhere but could not find it. Hearing that the public had an appointment with an old monk, they traced him to Lingyin Temple, where they saw the public in tattered clothes and a dirty hat, indeed an old monk. They questioned him, and he said, "I feared alarming you all, so I secretly came here. Entrust a message to the relatives, telling them not to think of me." He then presented the Zen staff to the public. The public laughed and said, "Foolish trickster! Twelve years of wealth and honor, you have replaced them. From now on, guard the Zen gate carefully, and do not jump back into the mundane world again." The servants did not understand what was being said, and sighed as they left. Duo said, "The immortals have the ability to divide their bodies, but the Buddhist door does not; it is because great compassion does not wish to deceive the world with illusions! Is it the public or the staff? Is it not or is it? How can I know?" **The Perfume Powder Hell** Yang Shilun of Henan, a son of a prominent family, was arranged to marry his uncle's daughter from a young age. When his uncle was appointed as the prefect of a southern county, Yang, following his mother's command, went to marry, but fell ill in the inn. In his illness, he vaguely saw a ghost servant holding a warrant to summon him. When he arrived at the underworld, the king questioned him, and his hometown and surname did not match. He scolded the ghost servant, saying, "I ordered you to summon Wang Shilun of Hunan, how could you be so wrong?" He severely beat him and ordered Yang to return to the mortal world.

Yang descended from the hall and encountered his deceased friend Yin Zhongqi, who was surprised to see him there. Yang explained the situation in detail. Yin said, "I have been serving as a low-ranking official in the hall of the Prince of Chujiang. Now that I have a bit of free time, I thought you might not know the way back, so I will accompany you." Yang was delighted and they left together. After about three miles, they came upon a place with beautifully decorated windows and ornate buildings, arranged like fish scales. Outside the door, women were applying makeup and hiding their sleeves, and they did not seem particularly afraid of the guests. Yang found this strange. Yin said, "This is the 'land of perfumes and powders'—a brothel." Yang asked, "Who are these women?" Yin replied, "In the human world, officials who are exposed for corruption and cruelty face the law. If they manage to escape, the underworld records their young wives and daughters and sends them to brothels to repay their karmic debts. The women who lean on the door and sell their smiles are all the precious beauties of the inner chambers!" As they were lamenting this, a woman appeared from the left door, seeming to be acquainted with Yin. She smiled and said, "You, a noble person, have not set foot in such a lowly place for a long time. Now, fortunately, the wind has blown you here, but you are passing by without entering? She forcibly pulled at Yin's sleeve. Reluctantly, Yin and Yang entered. Two courtesans then emerged, laughing and greeting them. Yang asked for their nicknames. Yin said, "This one is named Cui Juan, and this one is named Sai Nu. They are both the best in the north district." Soon, the old woman brought out food and wine, and they sat in a circle, dressed in blue and red robes. After three rounds of wine, Yin asked Cui Juan to sing to accompany the wine. Cui Juan passed the request to Sai Nu, who looked displeased.

Cui Juan repeatedly urged her, but Sai Nu said, "You rely on your father as the county magistrate, and yet you bully me, the daughter of the county recorder? In the mortal world, there are hierarchical relationships, but in the underworld, only sisterly etiquette is observed. You cannot command as you please, making others uncomfortable." Cui Juan's face turned red, and she forced herself to clap her hands, singing the song "Dream at the Terrace." Sai Nu said, "The rhythm was off, and it was very unpleasant to listen to." Cui Juan grew angry and said, "I grew up in a noble family and was not accustomed to such behavior. How can it be like your father, a merchant from Shandong who sells dates, who bought two pairs of sharp-edged wings and taught himself the song 'Hanging Branch,' whispering it to himself during the break?" Sai Nu was speechless, furiously stood up, but Yin and Yang repeatedly tried to mediate, and finally, they sat down again. Suddenly, there was a loud commotion outside. The ghost servants followed the order of Lord Yama, leading a woman who had just entered the brothel. With loose hair, she cried pitifully, and her beautiful face was disheveled. Yang quickly stood up and looked at her, recognizing her as his aunt's daughter and his betrothed. He was greatly shocked and asked about the details. The woman said, "My father-in-law accepted stolen gold worth eight hundred taels, defamed someone's reputation, and sent me to this place as punishment to cover the stolen money. Now that you are a guest here, would you not lend a hand?" Yang consulted with Yin, who said, "The underworld is different from the mortal world, and it cannot be resolved through bribery! What can I do?" Yang was racked with worry, unable to think of a solution, and felt like he was about to die.

It is said outside, "Three attendants from the Nine Hells Palace are coming." The old woman respectfully welcomed them in, and Yin and Yang both stood up from their seats. The attendants smiled and said, "We heard that your family has welcomed a new money tree, so we especially prepared several pieces of silk headbands and one pair of gold hair ornaments to set up a bond with the new bride." The old woman repeatedly expressed gratitude and ordered the girl to go into the room to prepare herself. The girl was extremely embarrassed and had nothing to say, falling to the ground and weeping bitterly. Yang, seeing this scene, felt his anger burning inside, at a loss for what to do, and pleaded with Yin to speak on his behalf. Yin called the old woman into the inner chamber, explained his intentions, and showed great reluctance. He then offered her a lot of money, and the old woman finally relaxed; she went out and whispered to the attendants, but it is unknown what she said. The attendants left angrily, and Yin also urged Yang to leave. Yang said, "My wife has been so unfortunate, suffering such a great humiliation. How can I face the world again?" The girl also wept. Yin said, "If we don't meet in the underworld, how can we ever meet again? There must be some heavenly destiny in this. Please let us use the brothel as our bridal chamber for now." He ordered the east room to be cleaned, had the girl stay with Yang, and himself accompanied Cui Juan and Sai Nu to the west room. They stayed late into the night, almost forgetting about the underworld.

One day, a black-clad official arrived holding a document, announcing that a certain prefect had donated 800 gold coins to establish six gates of charity schools. Yan Jun, the King of Hell, approved the report of the City God and ordered his daughter to return to the mortal world, carrying her on a humble cart, and they hurriedly departed. Yin raised his hand to congratulate Yang, saying, "The lady has gone, and you will also depart from here!" He then bid farewell to the old woman's family, accompanied Yang for thirty to forty li, and turned back when they reached the inn. Yang also felt as if he had awakened from a dream. After resting for ten days, he packed and went to his uncle's office, asking in detail about the charity schools. His uncle said, "I initially had this idea but had not yet implemented it, how could you know?" Yang explained the beginning and end, and his uncle was startled. The next day, they chose a good day to perform the ceremony.

On the wedding night, Yang recounted past events as a jest, but the woman firmly refused to acknowledge them, saying, "You have fulfilled your dream, how could I possibly have this?" Yang remained bewildered for a long time, and when he sought spring at the cave entrance, there was no longer any red flower on the blood-stained bed! The moralist said, "Women being forced into prostitution by official decree, the harsh policies of the previous dynasty, one would not expect that this law still persists even in the underworld. Father's greed for silver, daughter falls into the brothel, the officials' bags full of millions, all the luxurious silk in the women's chambers. Yet, if one repents one day, one can preserve one's virtue for a thousand autumns. The strict laws of the underworld, never fail to allow one to redeem oneself, strive for it!" **Cycle of Faces** A scholar from Jingjiang, named Zeng Yi, was talented and beautiful, highly esteemed in the artistic community. However, when he looked into the mirror, he felt ashamed of his appearance, so he wrote a poem on the wall: "I want to write a letter to ask the King of Hell, the face of Mount Lu might not be true. If you say that Left Si has many ugly features, who else throws fruit by the roadside?" After finishing, he lay down in anger, and in an instant, he found himself in a place resembling a royal palace, with a three-room building beside it. Above the door hung a golden plaque inscribed with "Cycle of Faces." In his astonishment, a scholar emerged from inside, wearing a tall hat and a long robe, carrying two books. Upon closer inspection, it was his old friend Kang Xi Hou.

Kang was a famous scholar from Zhejiang, who became a guest of a feudal lord through his paintings, and Zhao once formed a close friendship with him. When they met, Kang asked about Zhao's recent situation, and Zhao also inquired about Kang's whereabouts. Kang said, "Brother, don't you know? I've been tired of this world for a long time! Because I was good at painting in life, I was summoned by the Wheel-Turning King to serve as a guest in his court. All beings, I first paint their ears, eyes, nose, and mouth, and then they are born into the world." Kang then took out two books from his hand and showed them to Zhao, saying, "Brother, if you look at these, you will understand the difficulty of my creative effort." Zhao first looked at the first book, which was titled "The Appearance of the Noble," and the figures depicted were mostly ugly and clumsy; the slightly better ones were also coarse, black, and fat. Then he looked at the second book, which was titled "The Appearance of the Lowly," and the figures were beautiful like women, though lacking in grace, each with a self-pitying expression. Zhao then angrily said, "Brother, you wield the power of creation. Why do you make the noble and the lowly so easily interchangeable, and beauty and ugliness so inverted?" Kang smiled and said, "Brother, how low your view is! The high-ranking officials of the imperial court certainly have inner beauty that is worth seeing. Why must they rely on their appearance to gain honor and prominence? Only those of low social status, who are poor and unable to support themselves, can benefit from having a good appearance. They can bask in the favor of the nobility and also insert themselves into the world of courtesans, secretly enjoying the love of the kind and passionate.

This is my compassionate heart to save the world and my skillful method of shaping it! For example, your face is invaluable. If you only refine your appearance to please people in the present, you might remain poor and lowly forever! How could you rise to prominence in the literary world or pass the imperial examinations?" Zhao replied, "Your words are mistaken. Throughout history, there were beauties like Zhi Anren and Liu Yiqing, and Zhang Liang, who was as good-looking as a woman, but they were not all poor and lowly." Kang said, "Zhi Anren and Liu Yiqing, who drank wine, still left a bad reputation; Liu Yiqing was watched to death by passersby, and Zhang Liang, if he did not follow the path of immortality, might have faced a similar fate to the Duke of Zhou. In summary, those who strive for perfection will inevitably invite the jealousy of fate. Why not leave some imperfections to enjoy life?" Zhao remained silent. Kang said, "If you wish to reduce your fortune, I can still refine it for you." Zhao was delighted and asked for advice. Kang took the pen from the desk and made a few strokes on Zhao's face, saying, "It's done!" Zhao asked again to have it refined. Kang said, "I have been friends with you for ten years and cannot bear to see you become a starving beggar." While they were talking, they suddenly heard the sound of a carriage approaching. Zhao hurriedly left and soon woke up from his dream. Afterward, his appearance gradually improved, but his literary talent decreased; he stumbled in the examination hall for over thirty years and ultimately grew old as a student.

The speaker says, "The dignitaries, their appearance has already been heard of, but I do not know what the appearance of the rich is. He says, 'There is a method of physiognomy: those who become rich are like donkeys or horses.'" Zhou Gong cut a disaster, Confucius wore a mask of a demon, Gao Yao cut a melon, Fu Yue planted fish fins. This knowledge, the gentleman must have learned from the *Xunzi* chapter on "non-appearance." A skilled craftsman labors with his heart, is it not self-praise?

受业陈元瑛识 能诗贼 长洲顾兰畹先生，居毛氏废园，杜门却客，吟诗自娱。 一夕，薄饮而卧，闻击桌声甚厉，醒而视之，一人在灯下翻闽诗稿，吟咏再四，拍案起立曰：“妙哉！青莲、浣花之嗣音也。”急下床揖之，兼叩踪迹。其人曰：“实相告：予北郭之偷儿也。亲老家贫，无以供甘旨。入先生室，冀有所获，适见案上诗，触予夙好，不觉狂吟》，有惊台驾。”先生曰：“汝既耽此，必有以教我。”因即诗稿评论之，曰：“集中诸作，俱有盛唐风格。惟《春兴》律中‘杏花寒食终朝雨，杨梆人家尽日风’已落晚唐卑调。”又指其《题长恨歌后》“如何私语无人觉，却被鸿都道士知”曰：“此亦儇薄，有伤忠厚。李义山‘薛王沉醉寿王醒’，非不尖新，而终失诗人敦厚之旨。”先生曰：“汝论诗已见一斑，未识有佳作得赐教否？”曰：“自遭家堆，所作尽投楚炬。不得已，为先生一吟。”遂拍手而歌曰： A Record of Chen Yuanying's Students A Thief Who Can Recite Poetry Mr. Gu Lanwan of Changzhou lived in the ruined garden of the Mao family, shutting himself in and refusing visitors, reciting poetry for his own amusement. One night, after a light drink and going to sleep, he heard a loud thumping on the table and woke up to see someone under the lamp flipping through Fujian poetry manuscripts, reciting them four times, slapping the table and standing up, saying, “Marvelous! The echo of Li Bai and Du Fu!” He quickly got out of bed and bowed, also asking about the person's background. The person said, “Let me tell you the truth: I am a thief from the northern suburbs. My parents are old and poor, unable to provide for their needs. I entered Mr. Gu's room, hoping to find something, and just saw the poems on the table, which touched my long-held interest, and I unconsciously recited them, startling you.” Mr. Gu said, “Since you are so fond of this, you must have something to teach me.” He then commented on the poetry manuscript, saying, “All the works in the collection have the style of the Prosperous Tang Dynasty. Only in the regulated verse of ‘Spring Awakening’ with the lines ‘Plum blossoms and cold food rain all morning, willow flutes and homes all day in the wind’ have fallen into the low tones of the Late Tang Dynasty.” He also pointed to the poem “After the Song of Eternal Sorrow” with the lines “How can private words go unnoticed, yet the Taoist from Hongdu knows,” saying, “This is also light and frivolous, harmful to sincerity and kindness. Li Shangyin’s ‘Prince Xue is drunk, while Prince Shou is awake’ is not lacking in sharpness and novelty, but ultimately loses the poet's essence of sincerity and kindness.” Mr. Gu said, “Your discussion of poetry has shown me a glimpse, but do you have any good works to share with me?” The person replied, “Since I suffered a family disaster, all my works were thrown into the fire. Out of necessity, I will recite one for you.” He then clapped his hands and sang:

A Path to the Golden Gate is Obscure, Wondrous Hands of the Young in Vain Do not compose a poem in the style of "Gao Xuan Guo," guard against losing a pearl in a brocade bag. The scholar said, "With such poetic talent, how have you fallen to this state?" He sighed and said, "I cannot compose poetry, nor have I fallen to such a state. You should still encourage yourself." As they talked, it was already dawn. The scholar prepared his weapons and watched him depart, saying, "Thank you for the poetry exchange, I wish to keep your name." The other person said, "No need! No need! In the future, just call me 'The Poetic Thief'." After saying this, he left with his rice. The bell said, "Zhuangzi recorded 'poetry and ritual unearthing the grave,' reading the chapter on 'green green wheat,' it is indeed the echo of the three hundred poems. In the lineage of thieves who can compose poetry, this thief is the last descendant! But at night in the horizontal pond, figures like Dai Ruosi and Shi Chong, all possess extraordinary talent. He can only compose poetry, hence he is a petty thief." The Dog That Knows Letters When I was a child, I kept a small dog named Jinsheng. Later, when I entered the school, I always carried him along. Occasionally placing him on the desk, he would watch me read, often gazing intently as if pondering something. I found this strange and playfully wrote "Jinsheng is not allowed to enter the school" on a piece of paper, attaching it to the side of my seat. The dog stared at it for a long time, then lowered his head dejectedly and left, not daring to enter the school for several days. When I called him, he would come. I found this even more strange and added the character "Hui" to his name, calling him "Hui'er." The dog wagged its tail joyfully, showing gratitude, much like a scholar who loves to be called by his courtesy name.

The Literate Dog After the dog learned to read, it became quite refined in character, choosing its food carefully and selecting its sleeping place with care. When it occasionally wandered into the streets, it calmly and disdainfully refused to associate with common dogs. Leftover food and roast meat, it would kick away and glare in anger before turning away. A scholar-official from the neighborhood, Zhou, heard of this and found it strange. He paired it with a female dog, yet the dog would not share food or lodging with her for an entire year. The dog had no particular liking, except for lying in the study, guarding the books on the shelves for me. Later, I followed my grandfather into official service in Huaidian, and I brought the dog home with me. Whenever I sent an old servant back on a journey, the dog would always carry the servant’s clothes as if to inquire about his well-being. Only after I showed the servant a letter stating he was safe would the dog then joyfully leap about. For nearly twenty years, it lived this way, until one day I heard that it had suddenly fallen into a fit of madness. It would warmly greet and play with those in ragged clothes, but would bark wildly at those dressed in fine garments. I sighed and said, “Accumulated oddities become habits; this is common among eccentric scholars. Yet going against its nature, it may bring disaster from now on!” No more than half a year later, the dog was killed by a neighbor’s son who lured it with a poisoned bamboo bow. Because I had raised the dog, my family buried it beneath a mulberry tree, marking the spot with a stone tablet inscribed with the words “The Literate Dog.” Soon after, I heard that the female dog had spent the entire day howling and, in the end, ran into a wall and died. I sighed deeply and said, “In life, they lived apart; in death, they are buried together. Was this dog one who suffered and remained virtuous? Or was it the retribution of a literate person who had abandoned his integrity?” I sent a letter instructing that the female dog be buried alongside her mate, so as to fully honor the dog’s wishes.

The Bell said: "Knowing characters is what the Creator abhors, how much more so when fallen into the path of animals, daring to rely on one's talents to be arrogant? Going against one's nature invites disaster, truly a world-revealing statement. It began with madness leading to death, and was later rewarded for his integrity. Perhaps the Creator abhors his life but not his death. The crane is still marked, but the dog does not know characters; a case of wrongful imprisonment was entirely saved by the dog's inability to read. If this dog were to face it, it would inevitably be tested by the sword of Chenhao. A dog that knows characters, truly a source of disaster." Qing Dynasty. Shen Qifeng. Harmonious Bell. Volume 7. The Rooted Girl The eldest daughter, Hui Sun, lost her mother at a young age. At eleven, she followed her uncle, Lin Li Cha, to study in the Lan Ye Study. One night, a young servant girl led a young lady in purple robes through the curtains. Lin questioned her origin. The young lady said, "I have a couplet here, and I ask the Scholar to complete it." She took out half a sheet of Xue Tao's paper from her sleeve, on which was written: "Carrying a basket outside the fence to pick orchids, I was stopped by a man in blue robes." Lin had not yet responded when Hui Sun casually answered: "Holding a brush to inscribe on the wall, I was forced to complete it by the girl from the Azure Sky." The young servant girl looked at the young lady and smiled, saying, "This girl's speech is truly like my esteemed aunt, Fei Qiong." The young lady said, "Indeed! Indeed! Sister Fei Qiong played in the world for twenty-six years, and only returned to her original state yesterday. She once said there was a lotus root left in the Bathing Fairy Pool. Eleven years later, it will sprout and grow leaves, and surely manifest a wise form in the air. This is it."

Hui Sun was about to speak, but the woman took the letter and hurriedly left with her maid. Duo said, "By the stone where one rides a cow, spirits have once appeared; by the post station of Chousang, the name 'Miaozi' has often been called out. When emotions reach the point of utter desolation, such illusions often arise." Office Without Vital Energy In the capital, at the Lülü Factory, there was an old man who posted a sign in the marketplace, saying, "I can discern a person's official position by observing their vital energy." Soon, those who had entered the ranks of officialdom came in such numbers that their shoulders brushed against one another. The old man invited them to sit and had them all exhale their breath. He then examined them closely from the side and said, "This is the energy of metal—fit for the Hanlin Academy; this is the energy of wood—fit for the Ministry; this is the energy of water—fit for the middle-level literary officials; this is the energy of fire—fit for the censors; this is the energy of earth—fit for the Guozijian." Everything he said matched perfectly. Then, a man exhaled for a long time. The old man pondered for a while, seemingly unable to determine what office he held, and said, "Strange! It resembles the energy of metal but lacks refinement, the energy of wood but lacks vitality, the energy of water but lacks clarity, the energy of fire but lacks intensity, the energy of earth but lacks thickness. Could it be that he belongs neither to the scholar class nor the official class?" When asked, the man explained that he was a candidate being considered for the position of county magistrate, seeking guidance. Thus it became known that these cold and idle offices are all held by men without vital energy. Their fate, it seemed, does not belong among the five elements at all.

Duo said: "How dare I act recklessly, nor am I trying to be distinguished. Only I know myself, so I speak from my own experience. While temporarily holding office in Xingjiang, I composed a Four Books essay in the style of the 'Broad Literature' Master as a jest, and I append it here for your amusement: 'I do not hesitate to take on a minor office. Study, and the salary will be in it. Indeed, the great danger for people is that they like to be teachers. Why do you study without tiring? Teaching also has many methods. This may be one of them. Ancient people never did not desire to hold office; those who have authority over land and people and hold office are called the parents of the people. Suppose there is a person here, chosen from the people, but without wealth, he is lowered by one rank. Since he cannot command, it is better to study. At that time, he prepared his belongings and went to a state. This is also governing; please try it. As he was about to enter the gate, he said, 'I am here.' The halls and rooms had not yet been set, so he led his wife and children, as if the poor had no place to go. After waiting for the person, he then came out and then dared to enter. He repaired his walls and houses, and those who followed him came like people returning to a market. Among the common people who held office, there were six or seven. Stupid but fond of using his own way. Eating full all day, he never spoke with them about their actions. A tenth (tax), he let them assign fifty acres to themselves, but they all lost their records, and no one knew their hometowns; although there were some who survived, it was rare. As he was about to go out, he wished for carriages and horses. If the means are insufficient, it cannot be considered as joy. To change is the most valuable thing. He did not wait for the carriage to be ready but went immediately. In auspicious months, he would wear formal robes. After many years, though obscure, it became more and more evident. He begged from his neighbors, and his height was half a person longer. Three months without knowing the taste of meat. In spring and autumn, when sacrificing to the public ancestors, he would prepare the offerings fully and present them. Not eating without working—this is one joy! Not even a single coin would he take from others."

If a student presents a gift, why should I refuse it? It's just a small token! Isn't it about forgiveness? I have never failed to teach him! He likes to ride horses and test swords, which makes him unsuitable for the group, but to educate him, is that what I want? For now, set aside your studies and follow me. Beware of contention. I rarely see him alive! He calls himself a young boy, judging by the time. When he advances, it should be as natural as ascending, and he should prepare for the next year. He lives in the suburbs, to wait for an opportunity. The villagers all dislike him, and learning is not being discussed, so why is he so restless? Is he seeking wealth? He went to mourn the death of a child. The people of the country all look down on him.

How is it with you, Zeng Dian? To call it a gradual process! It is so urgent! For those who act, there is a way to gain the support of the ruler; one must abandon and leave it behind, looking around and not finding any immediate profit. To look back and move to another place, then one will surely achieve fullness or success. How difficult it is! Those of lower status should not offend the great families. The age of one's parents, their offerings should be made according to ritual; to receive it without reciprocating, to borrow more and add to it, this is a sign of estrangement. By the time three years have passed, to calculate, and when they are old, why not leave them? To pity these lonely ones, to want to stop but cannot. Virtuous and capable people in power, they are distinguished but not utilized! To continue as before, as if for life, what can be done? Let it be, let it be, do not disgrace yourself! Return, return, this is indeed what is desired! From now on, there is a great way to generate wealth, why must one read books? The gentleman has nothing to compete over, follow what I enjoy. If someone responds to me, to tell that person: "Without a sense of shame and disgrace, then to pursue learning, a beggar would not stoop to it, but you say I desire it?"

**Ghostly Wife Managing the Household** Lu of Lanxi, a man in middle age, lost his parents. His wife, Leng, was a harmonious and respectful partner. They had one son and one daughter, who were just weaned when Leng fell ill and died from emaciation. Lu then remarried a woman surnamed Ouyang, who was beautiful but fierce. She was particularly cruel to the children, often scolding and cursing them. Even the slightest displeasure would be met with whipping and beating. Whenever Lu showed any anger, she would gibber incoherently, continuing for days on end. Lu could no longer endure the situation and went out in a fit of anger. Caught in the rain, he ran into a forested valley. Suddenly, he stepped into a hole that collapsed, and he seemed to fall onto a roof ridge. He heard noise and shouting of a thief, and one person tied him up and brought him down. Upon seeing him, the person was revealed to be the deceased servant Mu Yi. He said, "Who do you think I am? It's your young master." Mu Yi released his bindings and hurried inside to inform the others. Not long after, both parents came out, embraced each other, and wept. The father said, "It's a strange thing that you came here; let's spend a little time together." He then led Lu into the room, where they saw the deceased wife threading a needle to sew shoes. Lu went forward and held her slender wrist, intending to recount their separation. The ghost wife pulled away and said, "What kind of rude guest are you, so reckless!" Lu stared in confusion, not understanding. The mother asked, "Have you remarried?" Lu replied, "Yes." The mother said, "Any man who remarries a new wife has no more affection for the first wife, so they do not recognize each other when they meet again." The mother went inside and whispered to the ghost wife, who then suddenly understood and burst into tears, asking about the family matters in detail. Lu said, "The fields and property are still intact, but the children are suffering daily from cruel treatment. What should we do?" The ghost wife turned to the wall and wept, and Lu also lost control and wept bitterly.

Father said, "You have already borne a child, yet you do not think of the young phoenix, recklessly inviting the owl. It is fitting to destroy the nest and take the child away. The trouble is self-inflicted; why regret it?" Mother replied, "He is indeed not worth sparing, but we should still consider the continuation of the family line." Father said, "To ensure the continuation of the lineage, it depends on my virtuous wife." Mother said, "The deceased wife has long been recorded in the register of the dead. How can she help the child?" Father said, "Even if she is not a virtuous wife, I will bring her here. You should gently instruct her early or late. Let the deceased wife follow the child, using her hands and feet to manage household affairs. Wait until the children are married before coming back here." The wife said, "Living in the same house, how can I bear to speak of separation?" Mother also wept bitterly. Father said, "You come here as a filial daughter-in-law, and leave as a kind mother. This is fully justified in both ways; why be reluctant to part?" He then ordered someone to go out with the wife, build a ladder at the corner of the house, and climb up it. They looked down into the hole and still saw their parents looking forward eagerly from the eaves. Reluctantly, they took the wife and followed the path home.

Just as he reached the door, the wife flew in swiftly ahead. She saw the children gather and rush to tell their story: "After Father left, the stepmother struck us with an iron rod. Suddenly, we turned pale and fell to the ground, becoming stiff." Before they finished speaking, Lady Ouyang walked out slowly. The children trembled with fear and tugged at their father's clothes, making fearful gestures. Lady Ouyang walked up to the father, stroked him repeatedly, and sobbed softly, saying, "I left you all not even three years ago, but I didn't expect you to be so emaciated." Her voice strongly resembled that of the former wife. The father was greatly pleased and told the children, "This is your former mother; do not be afraid." The children looked at each other with bright eyes. The wife asked the daughter, "Did I use the gold bracelet from your arm to make you a hairpin?" The daughter replied, "Mother, the hairpin you wear in your hair was made from the gold bracelet that was removed from me." The wife said, "Why do I need this?" She then removed the hairpin from her hair and gave it to the daughter to wear. She asked the son, "I made a three-foot brocade with a pattern of flowers and a phoenix for you to wear as a sash. Why aren't you wearing it?" The son replied, "Father made it into a lotus cover for Mother!" The wife said to the father, "A foolish man loves his second wife; it's no wonder the children have been mistreated." The father bowed his head to apologize, and they all went back into the room together.

Seeing the medicine stove and tea stove, as well as the place for sweeping brows and placing mirrors, all were no longer in their old positions. The woman sighed deeply and said, "Once a person retires from service, everything is left to the descendants, truly it is painful!" She unlocked the box and found peach-yellow shirts and purple silk trousers, brightly piled up, while not a single piece of old clothing remained. She asked someone about this. The person replied, "The new clothes fit well; do not think of the old ones." The woman said, "The heart and intentions of a man are revealed in his words!" The man regretted his lost words and tried to pacify her repeatedly. The woman then leaned against the window and gazed out, saying, "The old peach tree planted, where has it been transplanted now?" The man said, "Since you left, it has been cut down daily, and the tree has withered and died." The woman sighed, "If the tree is like this, how can a person bear it?" She looked back at her children and could not hold back her tears. She then lifted the water jar and drew water, holding the stove and cooking. The man tried to persuade her not to work so hard. The woman said, "These are the bodies and hair of the future generations, they should be cherished by you. Otherwise, since I entered your family, I have never sat idly perfumed." The man's expression turned pale, and he held his breath, unable to speak. The woman said, "I came here according to my father-in-law's command, it is not necessary to criticize you for every mistake. But hiding resentment and pretending to be happy, this will harm a woman's virtue, I cannot help but express my anger!" The man nodded in agreement. From then on, they began to live harmoniously, managing household affairs day and night. After twelve years, the children had all grown up.

A woman married a local scholar named Zheng, and their son married the daughter of a scholar named Qian. Their family was harmonious and free from discord. One evening, they set up a feast in their private quarters and drank deeply until they were drunk. The woman said to her husband, "I dreamt last night that my father-in-law summoned me, and now I must part forever. Our marital bond will end here!" Her husband wept and said, "Our family was separated, but you rebuilt it. We should have lived together until old age, but you still left me!" The woman replied, "I came to take care of your children and went to serve your parents. If you insist on keeping me, it would be disrespectful to your parents." The husband turned to a corner and wept bitterly. In an instant, the woman lay down on the bed, her breath ceased, and she passed away. Just as the husband was in shock, the woman suddenly sat up and said, "My sister has returned, and I will take her place!" Upon hearing her voice, it was clear that she was still the same person, an Ouyang. The husband was startled and pale. The woman said, "Do not doubt or fear. I spent twelve years in the presence of my father-in-law and mother-in-law, and only then realized that what I did before all went against the way of a good wife. From now on, I will strictly follow my sister-in-law's established rules and follow her advice for several years to atone for past sins." The husband was overjoyed and called their son to inform him. The son was moved by both sadness and joy. The woman said, "After leaving here for ten or so years, I almost grew up and got married. Please do not hold onto old grudges, still should help your father manage the household." The son replied, "The hard work of the former mother is indeed the body of the latter mother, how can there be any old grudges that I dare to forget?" The woman was also overjoyed; from then on, she managed the household and educated the children, showing great kindness and righteousness, and was highly praised by the neighbors and relatives as a virtuous wife.

The bell said, "The old man obtained his daughter-in-law, who flatters and obeys blindly, nurturing arrogance and ferocity, until she destroys the nest to take the chicks. This is disobedient to parents and cruel to children. If the dead could rise, where would the earth even go? Alas!" A Man of Lowly Status Teaches the World A man from Xianan carried a thousand threads to Wu Gate to do small business. Later, his family became prosperous, trading cloth for silk and accumulating wealth in the tens of millions. He often boasted, "There is a secret to getting rich, which foolish men do not know!" Several people came to his door, begging the old man to teach them. The old man said, "This secret is not to be passed on. You all bring a hundred coins each, to serve as my conversation material, and I will teach you." At night, they brought the money and arrived together. The old man had them sit down and said, "It is not difficult to become rich. First, deal with external enemies, then internal enemies. The way to start a family. You have thought of more than half!" They asked, "What are external enemies?" The old man said, "External enemies are five: eyes, ears, nose, tongue, and body. Eyes like to see beautiful colors, beautiful wives and concubines, who cannot be stored in a golden house. I spend several strings of cash to buy an ugly wife, which can also continue the family line. Ears like to hear good sounds, music departments, which cannot be provided without money. I climb Le You Yuan to listen to folk songs, which can also serve as silk and bamboo music. If you place a treasure tripod, buy dragon's saliva, it is all a burden for the nose. I close my nose and do not smell the fragrance, lying on a pile of horse manure all day, which is also pleasant. To obtain mountain delicacies, to arrange sea errors, it is all a deception for the tongue. I eat without distinguishing the taste, eating sour leek porridge all day, which is never unsatisfied."

To the point where one is isolated and alone, the disaster is even more severe: in summer, wearing fine gauze, and in winter, heavy furs, all for the sake of others' beauty, squandering one's own hard-earned wealth; I follow the ancient imperial system, using leaves for clothing and grass for headgear, from head to toe, not worth a penny. These five are the secrets to dealing with external enemies." The crowd asked, "What are internal enemies?" The old man said, "Internal enemies also have five: benevolence, righteousness, propriety, wisdom, and trustworthiness. Benevolence is the primary evil; to widely give and help the masses, even Yao and Shun found it difficult. I swear before the gods to never perform a single act of kindness, saving much waste. A common man upholding righteousness, bankrupting his family, also brings self-inflicted suffering. I see profit and forget, thus enjoying life. As for reciprocity in gifts, offering silk and linen, ancient people were too reluctant; I give without expecting to receive, taking advantage first. Wisdom is detested by nature, leading to poverty; one needs only to remain simple and ignorant to enjoy a long-lasting average fortune. A promise worth a thousand gold pieces is even more useless; one can speak generously but harbor ulterior motives, so that the world knows I am untrustworthy, never to be invited again. These five are the secrets to eliminating internal enemies. Refine and understand them, not caring about face, reputation, integrity, or criticism. With this approach, becoming a millionaire is as easy as turning over a palm. Those with ambition should do so.

The crowd agreed, and offered money, placing it on the seat. The old man looked at it, and saw that it was all paper money ashes. He shouted, "I have taught you with all my heart, why are you using this to play a joke on me?" The crowd replied, "Your teachings are indeed excellent, but they might not be feasible in the human world; they should only be used to teach ghosts." Before the words were finished, they all showed ghostly appearances. The old man turned around to escape. The crowd said, "In the animal realm, there are 48,000 ghosts, awaiting the old man's teaching; let us go together." The old man was startled, and then wept, saying, "Please wait a moment, allow me to settle my family affairs." Searching through the left box and right cage, almost everything was checked, but there was nothing to take. He then sighed, saying, "Having been a rich man all my life, I still have to go among the poor ghosts and play tricks." The crowd started mocking him, and the old man also fell down suddenly.

Bell said, "The rich always call on their ancestors, while the poor always invoke ghosts. Therefore, I know that at the gate of the afterlife, there are no strange books of wealth for sale. If one can have this ancestor ascend the platform to teach the Dharma, even in the darkest of prisons, one can achieve the highest status and wealth!" **Insect Writing** The girl from Jinping, Ye Peixiang, possessed innate wisdom and began studying at the age of seven. She had a deep understanding of subtle meanings. Once she told her teacher, "Ancient people created characters through meaning and form; but sometimes there are many errors." The teacher asked her to explain, and she said, "The character for 'short' (矮) clearly consists of a bow and arrow (委矢), and should be read as 'to shoot' (射). The character for 'to shoot' (射) clearly consists of an inch and a body (寸身), and should be read as 'short' (矮). Now that the meanings of these characters are reversed, isn't this a mistake made by ancient people?" The teacher was amazed and told her father, "Tong Wu, at nine years old, could understand profound texts. This daughter of yours, with her wise nature, should not be inferior to Tong Wu's successor." Her father sighed and said, "Tong Wu was very intelligent but died young before he reached adulthood. I fear that even a precious gem cannot be held in one's hand forever!" At the age of sixteen, she suddenly fell ill and died. She was buried in the garden under a green phoenix tree. Countless green insects gathered on the leaves, biting to form small characters, which, when read, often formed beautiful verses. There was a poem called "Eight Scenes of the Afterlife." One of the poems, "Looking at the Moon at the Ghost Gate," reads: "The ashes have cooled, and the night robe is not warm; the clear blue moon shines on the wandering soul; the whole body is drenched with wind and dew, making it hard to bear, yet it is strange that the pear blossom still has a trace."

《奈河桥春泛》云：Tears fall like mist over waves, symbolizing long-lasting sorrow or separation, and the oars are rowed out of a small pond or lake, possibly symbolizing departure or transition. Do not follow the flow of time or life away from a place of significance, as one fears encountering a fox spirit in the human world. 《望乡台晚眺》云：Where can one lean on the winding railings? The imposing structure of the evening tower rises high; one realizes that their soul is like a swallow in autumn, flying over twelve layers of jade towers, symbolizing the journey of the soul through different realms. 《孟婆庄小饮》云：The soul returns home at night, with a jade pendant swaying, symbolizing a sense of peace or connection, and holding a fragrant wine by the stove, possibly symbolizing a moment of reflection or communion. The sorrowful rain during the Cold Food Festival falls, and tears are shed on the food offered to the dead, symbolizing mourning or remembrance. 《剥皮亭纳凉》云：A chilling wind brings evening coolness, possibly symbolizing the harshness of the afterlife, and the soul's blood-soaked clothing, symbolizing the lingering effects of past life. One remembers playing under a bean flower trellis, possibly symbolizing a cherished memory from a past life, and gently waving a fan to catch fireflies, possibly symbolizing a moment of innocence or joy. 《恶狗村踏青》云：Small dogs with bells around their necks are heard in the sound of water, possibly symbolizing the presence of judgment or torment, and the soul's journey without the constraints of the physical world, symbolizing freedom or detachment. Female companions play with flowers, possibly symbolizing a moment of innocence or community, but the soul's inability to return to the land of the living, symbolizing the finality of death. 《血污池垂钓》云：A channel is formed by the blood of many, possibly symbolizing the collective suffering of humanity, and the transformation of blood into a red carp, possibly symbolizing rebirth or transformation. The fish is caught on the line and then let go, possibly symbolizing the soul's rejection of past life, as one fears that the fish might contain a letter from home, symbolizing the soul's longing for its past life. 《点鬼坛饭僧》云：The sounds of Buddhist rituals are heard in the afternoon, possibly symbolizing a moment of spiritual reflection, and rain falls on a flower-scattering platform, possibly symbolizing the impermanence of life. The soul acts of repentance for past deeds, symbolizing spiritual purification, and offers one's burial clothes as a sacrifice, possibly symbolizing the soul's final act of devotion. 其他诗词不能备载。 一日，作书别其父母曰：One day, the soul wrote a letter to its parents, possibly symbolizing a final farewell or message.

The Child's Farewell Letter As a child, I was loved by my family; my boundless thoughts are hard to repay. I still remember the serene scene after the rain, with flowers blooming in the small pavilion. Calling for my father to teach me, seeking my mother to comb my hair, pulling on their clothes to make them smile, playing without knowing sorrow. I thought that the willow trees in spring would last long, the pear blossoms would be eternal, living to old age, remaining as innocent and pure as an infant. Who would have thought that a shocking dream and a sudden calamity would occur; the heavy sins of the mountains and hills affect the parents. Since our parting, the swallows have left their nests, the mandarin ducks still mourn at the grave. Like a silkworm spinning silk after death, a candle continuing to burn after the wax is spent. My spirit wanders in the night rain, sings in the autumn graves; it is inevitable to have feelings, so a short song replaces the cry. Yesterday, I visited my old home and looked at my parents from afar; the flourishing cypress and peony symbolize their good health. My younger sister, now grown and happy, has spent years in the inner chambers, adding incense and holding the inkstone. I hope to open my old chest, pick out the remnants of my makeup, the two hairpins, the embroidered scarf; small dowry, left as a memento. I have recently been summoned by the Queen Mother to serve as a secretary, to cultivate virtue without a matchmaker, there is a path to heaven; according to the old rules of the Jade Hall, this is just to relieve mockery. But once we are separated, for thousands of years, the shadow is divided, the endless long sorrow, what does this mean! I only hope you will eat well, devoutly collect herbs. If the fire is ready at the stove, the elixir is completed, then at the edge of the city of perpetual night, the conference of immortality, we may not meet again! The weak river has no fish, the mountain has few geese; a final farewell, unforgettable for ten thousand lifetimes. In a hurry, I bow a hundred times to my parents.

The parents received the letter and were greatly grieved. The green insects in the back garden all disappeared, and no more characters appeared on the wu leaves. The author said, "I read Zhou Yigu's *Miscellaneous Records* and had some doubts about the story of the fairy traveler Dong Lang. However, talented people's spirits do not perish; they寄托于昆虫草木，以抒发其郁结的情感。It is possible that this is the case. But the beautiful woman ascended to heaven, and the green insects disappeared. Is it like Liu An lifting his house, where even chickens and dogs became immortals? Alas! This is indeed strange!" *Beast Genealogy* The trend of genealogical records was most prevalent in the Jiangnan region. There was a certain family whose status had declined, and they rose to prominence through trade. They wanted to associate with the scholarly elite and consulted with the magistrate Wu. Wu was good at satire and said, "I have a story to tell you." The person listened respectfully, and Wu said, "In the past, the celestial official He Gu borrowed money from the Jade Emperor for his wedding and was exiled to live in the constellation Shang.
