He worked hard in farming and accumulated tens of thousands of coins, which he tied onto the back of a cow and took to the Heavenly Gate to pay in advance. But the cow suddenly ran away to the lower realms, and he looked at himself and felt ashamed, unable to face the world. He then thought that the things on his back were quite abundant, and it would not be difficult to attach himself to a noble family and boast in the village. He went to the East Sea to meet the Qilin and told him his intention. The Qilin said, 'My horns are those of the royal clan; my hooves are those of the noble sons. Moreover, I have one horn and five hooves, which are different from ordinary animals. How can you, who can write on walls, mix with our kind?' He scolded him away. He also went to the Western Regions and bowed before the Blue Lion, but before he could introduce himself, the lion saw his clumsy appearance, roared loudly, left feces everywhere, and sent him flying thousands of miles away, where he wandered aimlessly in the wilderness."

Suddenly, I remembered Long Ears from Mount Lu, with whom I had shared the camaraderie of traveling together in a carriage. I decided to consult him. Long Ears said, "There is a person named Money Leopard in the southern mountain. Although he pretends to be a hermit in the mist, he actually makes many friends. I will introduce you, and you will certainly be accepted." So, we went together to the southern mountain. Long Ears first expressed his sincerity. Money Leopard said, "People of a kind gather together. Those I associate with are mostly fat and prosperous." Long Ears highly praised the possibility, and we led a cow to meet him. Upon entering, the cow felt constrained and awkward, finally feeling ashamed of not fitting in. Money Leopard initially rejected it, but then saw the money the cow carried and laughed, saying, "Your back is rich beyond words. And the reason my family is called Money Leopard is because my back has patterns that resemble coins! Even though you are not naturally a thief, you can still achieve it through human effort." He then ordered the cow to be led out, and the fur and hair were arranged into patterns.

Not long after, the object transformed into a colorful and brilliant form, with golden light flickering, suddenly becoming a miniature version of itself, unlike looking through a tube and seeing only a part. The long-eared man looked at it carefully and laughed, saying, "Once the miser's purse is opened, it becomes a fine thing. Even if Jie Ge Lu were to come, he would not be able to distinguish it clearly!" He then left. The leopard began to consider the object a peer, and the cow, too, wagged its tail proudly, following behind the leopard, shining brightly in the lush forest. Within a week, the cow's money was spent, but its fur remained the same. The leopard became angry and said, "Such a disgraceful appearance tarnishes my noble lineage!" It chased the cow away noisily. The cow, aimlessly wandering without knowing what to do, returned to the temple. The River Drum struck the cow's back a hundred times with a coral whip. He then asked where the cow's money was. The cow told him everything. The River Drum said, "Foolish beast! What you desired to associate with you was because of your tens of thousands of coins! Once the money runs out, would they still consider you a worthy descendant of their ancestors?" He tied a chain through the cow's nose and confined it in a cage. Later, people named the River Drum as "Tian Niu."

I heard that sweat poured down my forehead, and the practice of compiling genealogies has come to an end! The bell said, "Even a lowly laborer may be the descendant of a frugal official, and even a lowly servant may have noble ancestry. Yet they disparage their own ancestors and improperly claim descent from others, and I cannot understand their motives. However, when the chancellor promotes officials, and when various officials hold power, they are not of the same family, yet they still take association with the powerful as a source of honor. How can one be surprised that people of the same surname discuss genealogy?" Black-clad Grand Eunuch Mr. Zhang, the Filial and Upright of Mubian, named Bangbi, was invited to join the staff of the magistrate of Fenyi County. One day, he leisurely went to the market, just as the town was holding a festival, and all the city's people, both men and women, gathered like clouds and mist. Mr. Zhang stood under the eaves waiting to watch. Soon, the sound of drums led the way, followed by banners, flags, and parasols, moving slowly in an orderly manner. There was a golden canopy plaque with the words "Grand Eunuch of the Chancellor's Office" written on both sides. Mr. Zhang did not know what kind of deity it was. Soon, fragrant smoke wafted, and in the warm sedan chair sat a deity with a plump purple face, a beard like a halberd, wearing a net hat, a black straight robe, a phoenix belt, and pointed black boots. Mr. Zhang found it strange and followed the procession to the temple, where offerings were set up in abundance, and the lights and candles shone brightly. The crowd knelt and bowed before it, all wearing sleeveless jackets, short brimmed hats, frog-headed shoes, and speaking in a formal manner. Then they proclaimed the blessing text, which included phrases like "May the divine spirits protect, from the highest to the lowest officials, gatekeeping has power, responsibilities are heavier."

Zhang was particularly amazed and asked what kind of deity it was. The response was, "This is the second sir, the head servant of Vice Minister Fan's household." Zhang became very angry and declared, "The traitor Yan sold official positions privately and ruined loyal ministers, and all of you fawned upon him and helped him grow evil. Now the traitor Yan's name is defamed in the annals of history, what kind of dog servant dares to openly worship him in a temple?" He climbed onto the altar and wanted to slap him across the face. The crowd was greatly surprised and pulled him down, saying, "Are you crazy? A poor scholar who reads a few lines of books always acts like a pedant. If you were born in the time of a chancellor, you would have followed people like Yan and Zhao to visit his door, seeing Mr. Niu with his shoulders hunched and fawning smile, you wouldn't know what you would do! And everyone has their own master. Scholars worship the God of Literature, just to seek blessings and hope to pass the imperial examinations. Butchers and vendors sell candles and silk daily, praying to the God of Wealth, they also want to earn tenfold profits and be called wealthy men in their neighborhood. Now we worship Mr. Niu, it is also like the God of Literature for scholars and the God of Wealth for merchants. What is there to blame?" Zhang knew that these people could not be argued with and spoke to the county magistrate, and the temple was immediately destroyed. From then on, the ghost of Niu Xun became even more fierce, transformed into countless forms, and became a blood-eating deity across the land.

Duo said, "Among the five sacrifices, the gate is the first. In later generations, this sacrifice was not passed down, and the hungry ghosts sought food everywhere, thus appearing as generals and chancellors during the day! Why does the deity who presides over cattle still wear a black robe? Someone said, 'Those who wear the hat of a scholar and carry feathered arrows are merely its transformed appearance!'" **Maiden in the Study** Jiang Sheng from Guian was in his early twenties and could only memorize the Four Books and half of the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents). He was poor and wanted to become a secretary, so he entrusted himself to relatives and friends. They all thought his talent was limited and did not recommend him. However, there was a county magistrate who was promoted to the position of magistrate of Fuyang County, and he urgently wanted to find a friend to manage his correspondence. Thus, Jiang Sheng was hired. The magistrate was illiterate, and Jiang Sheng deliberately made grandiose claims to deceive him. There were many errors in the letters and memorials, but the magistrate ignored them. It happened that the birthday of the Dowager Lady of the governor's office was approaching, and the magistrate wanted to praise her. He asked Jiang Sheng to write an essay. Jiang Sheng pieced together an old collection of parallel prose and embellished it. However, he did not understand the allusions, and the text mixed in phrases typically used for men's birthdays, such as "celebrating the poet's birth year" and "praising the elder's age." These inappropriate words filled the entire text. The magistrate did not understand, so he asked someone to write it on screens and banners, and personally delivered it to the governor's office. The governor read it and laughed heartily. The magistrate, seeing his pleased expression, believed that the birthday essay must be exceptionally good, surpassing everyone else. Upon returning, he told Jiang Sheng, who received an increase in his salary.

The following year, Governor Pu was celebrating his birthday and still asked someone to write an essay for him. Jiang, the young scholar, once again compiled paired phrases from his secret notes and mixed in words of longevity for women, such as "shining like the North Star on the eastern wall, descending the Queen Mother to the western pool" and "adding luster to women's headgear, increasing longevity in the jade chamber," which were particularly laughable. The county magistrate still carried and delivered the gift. Governor Pu laughed uncontrollably and asked, "Is Mr. Someone still at your office?" The county magistrate nodded affirmatively. Reflecting on this, he thought, "He is just a clerk, yet he has been consulted by the governor, so he must be a famous scholar in Zhejiang." He then recounted this to others. Jiang became even more proud of himself, privately thinking that with such talent and reputation, why should he settle for a position in a county office? If he were to greet guests at the governor's gate, he could gain ten times more than he currently has. He made an excuse to resign and went to the governor's office, submitting a petition to request an audience. Governor Pu summoned him in. Jiang expressed his gratitude to his close friend. However, he observed that the governor's intention seemed not very welcoming. He then explained that the longevity article for the county magistrate was written on behalf of a certain person. Governor Pu understood the reason for Jiang's visit and laughed, saying, "Mr. Jiang, you have great talent, which I greatly admire. But you have been misled by the ancients. My mother is not a 'poet,' and neither am I a 'woman' with 'headgear.' " Jiang was greatly embarrassed and withdrew. From then on, Jiang was called a "female guest scholar" in Zhejiang. He sought recommendations everywhere, but finally, no one hired him.

铎曰：“庾兰成的‘春旗芝盖’一联，子安颇为相似。名士写文章，有时也会模仿他人。但‘一一鹤声飞上天’，却不是那些愚钝之人所能模仿的。金根的误解，弄獐的误写，固然成为千古笑柄。为何‘弋人何篡’，《法言》中可以误写；‘垂杨生肘’，《南华》中不妨错解。名下之人喜欢题诗，词坛的弊端，古今相沿，对蒋生又能如何？” 鲛奴 茜泾景生，喜闽三载，后航海而归。见沙岸上一人僵卧，碧眼蜷须，黑身似鬼，呼而问之。对曰：“我是鲛人，为水晶宫琼华三姑子织紫绡嫁衣，误断其九龙双脊梭，因此被放逐。如今漂泊无依，若能蒙您收留，感激不尽。”生正苦无仆，便带他回乡。此人无所好，亦无所能。饭后赴池塘一浴，即蹲伏暗陬，不言不笑。生以其穷海孤身，亦不忍时加驱遣。

On the Buddha's birthday, I went to the temple of the昙flower to express my joy. I saw an old woman leading a young girl to pray before the statue of the Buddha. The young girl, with her hands pressed together in prayer, moved gracefully, her movements like the play of shadows and light, as bright as the moon emerging from thin clouds. After the prayer, she followed the old woman away. I followed them into a narrow alley and asked the neighbors. They told me that the young girl was from Wu, her family name was Tao, and her given name was Wan Zhu. She had lost her father at a young age and was mistreated by the community. Three years ago, she moved with her mother to this place. I intended to marry her due to her mother's widowhood and poverty and offered a large dowry, but her mother refused. I said, "Your mother is holding out for something impossible, and will you let your daughter grow old unmarried?" The old woman laughed and said, "She is as valuable as two precious pearls from Lantian; what objection could there be to a high dowry? And since her name is Wan Zhu, she must receive ten thousand pearls to accept the proposal; otherwise, even if one tries to weave a fine net with a thousand threads, it would be futile!" Disappointed, I returned home, thinking that even if I were to bankrupt myself, it would be impossible to gather ten thousand pearls. Day and night, I wrote in the air and dreamed, and after a week, I fell ill and could not get out of bed. Doctors diagnosed me with a complex illness but attributed the primary cause to unrequited love. I lay weak and close to death. My companion, a mermaid, came to inquire about my health. I said, "Like Wang Boyu of Langya, I am destined to die of love. But you have been with me for half a year, and if I die first, where will you go?" The mermaid heard my words and cried, her tears flowing onto the ground. When I looked down, the tears sparkled like jewels, each one a如意珠.

The man started up suddenly, saying, "I am better now!" The mermaid was surprised by his sudden recovery. He said, "The reason why I was sick and nearly died was because I lacked one pair of your urgent tears!" Then he recounted the whole story. The mermaid was pleased and picked up the tears, counting them, but they did not fill her quota. She sighed and said, "You also look beggarly, showing sudden joy upon getting something valuable. Why not wait a moment longer to let me weep for you fully?" The man said, "Can we try again?" The mermaid said, "We laugh and cry from the heart, unlike the mechanical people of the world who always put on false faces. If not, then tomorrow I will carry a container of wine, climb the sea-viewing tower, and help you out." The man followed her advice, and early in the morning, he took the mermaid to the tower to look out at the sea. He saw the surging waves stretching endlessly to the horizon.

The mermaid-like creature raised a cup and drank to intoxication, performing a dance in a palace surrounded by swirling waves, featuring fish, dragons, and other mythical creatures. Gazing south to the red cliffs and north to the celestial ruins, ancient cities such as Zifu and Jieshi were all shimmering in the sea waves. She sighed and said, "All around is desolate and bleak. Where are the old homes?" Raising her sleeves and becoming impassioned, she felt a strong desire to return home and wept bitterly, her tears falling like beads. The man took a jade plate and placed the tears on it, saying, "It is sufficient." The mermaid-like creature said, "Grief arises from within and cannot be cut off." Letting out a loud cry, her tears ceased. The man was greatly pleased and invited her to return home together. The mermaid-like creature suddenly pointed east and laughed, saying, "The dawn rises over the red city. Twelve mirages, near the bridge of the alligator, and the fairy of Qionghua is marrying the sea deity of Coral Island today. My calamity is over; please let me depart!" She leaped into the sea and disappeared. The man returned alone, feeling a sense of loss. The next day, the man presented a precious pearl and went to present a dowry. The old woman laughed and said, "You are truly infatuated with love. I was just testing you. How could I really sell my daughter to shamefully seek a livelihood?" She refused the pearl and gave her daughter to the man. Later, she gave birth to a son, named Dream of the Mermaid, to remember the connection that brought them together.

Duo said, "Borrowing the cry of someone in a desperate situation, to help the poor scholars, the skill of the mermaid is extraordinary. I find it even more extraordinary that the mother first requested a dowry and then rejected the pearls, allowing a woman of unparalleled beauty to exude grace and charm in the home. Otherwise, accepting a hundred taels of pearls from the Shi family, though raising the price, would be no different from a farmer trying to sell vegetables at a higher price for profit." Dog Servant During the reign of Emperor Qingping, the Wuchang official requested leave and returned home. His wife wanted to buy a servant. A poor woman brought her daughter, who had a pale face and thin body, with bright and sharp eyes like a dog. When asked about the price, she demanded one hundred taels of gold. The wife laughed and said, "Your daughter is so ugly and clumsy, what is her special talent that you consider her a rare treasure?" The poor woman replied, "This girl may look plain, but she has naturally sharp eyes and can see things clearly in the darkness, as if it were daylight." The wife said, "Let's keep her and test her." The poor woman left. That night, the girls were embroidering the official's court attire by the light of a lamp. She was asked to thread a needle in the dark, and she did so easily, as if it were as simple as throwing a mustard seed. The wife was pleased. The next day, she paid the full amount. She named the servant "Xir" (Joyful). Xir was outwardly plain but inwardly wise, good at understanding the wife's intentions. The wife favored her, almost treating her like a daughter. At night, she often used her for amusement, sometimes taking out gold bracelets and silver rings and scattering them in the dark, and Xir could retrieve them without missing a single one. Once, she took a thousand coins and scattered them in a dark room, asking Xir to pick them up, and she did not leave a single coin behind. She once told the official, "Redline holds the pen, and the graceful girl recites the *Lulingguangdian Fu* (Palace of Lu Luling's Light), but none of them can compare to my Xir, who is even better than the green-eyed foreign merchant."

One night, Dàcháng held a candle in his inner room, writing a tombstone inscription for a certain official of the Ministry of Personnel. He urgently needed to consult the *Ban* (Book of Han) and *Shi* (Book of History), so he sent Xǐ'ér to the bookshelf to fetch the specific volume and chapter. Xǐ'ér left with a loud cry, returned several times, but came back empty-handed. When questioned, she stood there in a daze, silent. Dàcháng said, "Feeling one's way in the dark is not an easy task." He then got up, holding the candle, and went outside to find the book on the shelf. The book was clearly visible. He smiled and told his wife, "Your family's blue-eyed merchant is now also confused by the five colors?" The wife did not understand but blamed Xǐ'ér's laziness. Xǐ'ér replied, "Madam is mistaken! When my mother was past middle age and unable to bear children, she prayed for offspring at the temple of Yang Taifu and was told that a dog under the seat would be reborn as a girl. Thus, I am all lowly bones, except for my bright eyes. But dogs, when they encounter gold and silver objects, can see them even in the dark. If it comes to articles and calligraphy, even in broad daylight, staring at them, I wouldn't know what they are, let alone in the dim evening." The wife sighed and said, "To abandon a person for a dog, one should be clear about small things but ignorant about dogs." From now on, she knew she had made a mistake. Dàcháng said, "Not so! Those who are busy and preoccupied, are they not like this? Anyone who encounters wealth and possessions sees clearly, but when it comes to words and texts, they cannot recognize even a single character, all of them are like dogs. The price of a male servant is double that of a female servant, but this is not true understanding." The wife then laughed, and Xǐ'ér's favor remained unchanged.

The passage states: "The bell of Zhu's family, the melody of plum blossoms, the dog of Lu's family, the message delivered in Luoyang. Who would say that writing and literature are beyond the reach of animals? Yet, to perform music without understanding its meaning, to deliver messages without comprehending their content, to rely on dogs instead of people, is ultimately not a wise strategy."

During the Qing Dynasty, Shen Qifeng wrote in Volume 8 of his collection *Harmonious Bell*: **Hands Emerging from Coffins** Chen Jingchu from Xiaoshan had been staying in Tianjin for a long time. After packing to return home, he passed through the border region of Shandong Province. At that time, there was a severe famine, and countless poor people died. Inns were deserted and would not accommodate guests. He took shelter in a temple and saw over thirty coffins piled up in the east wing, while one coffin stood alone and firmly in the west wing. After midnight, hands began to emerge from the coffins. All the hands were scorched, thin, and yellowish, except for one hand from the west wing, which was slightly fatter and whiter. Chen, who usually had courage and strength, looked around confidently and smiled, saying, "You poor ghosts, you must be short of money. Are you all begging for money from me?" He then unfastened his green bag and gave each a large coin. The hands from the east wing all retracted, but the hand from the west wing remained extended. Chen said, "One coin might not satisfy you; I will add more." He increased the amount to a hundred coins, but the hand remained motionless. Chen angrily said, "This ghost is too pretentious, truly greedy and insatiable!" He then lifted two strings of coins and placed them in its palm, and the ghost's hand suddenly retracted. Chen was surprised and moved the lamp to shine all around, seeing that the coffins in the east wing were marked with the names of certain starving peasants, while the coffin in the west wing bore the inscription of a certain county magistrate. He firmly said, "The starving peasants have no great aspirations; one coin can satisfy their wish. But the four officials are used to receiving book gifts and won't accept until the full amount is reached."

Shortly after, a sharp metallic sound rang out. It was because the coffin seam was quite narrow, and the ghost's hand inside was trying to pull something, but it couldn't get in, making a loud snapping sound as the string of coins broke, scattering money all over the ground. The ghost's hand came out again, frantically searching in all directions, but it didn't manage to grab any money. Chen looked sideways and smiled, saying, "You're too greedy. All you have left is empty hands, and that's worse than the petty-mindedness of others, who at least left behind a single coin to look at!" Yet, the hand continued to search fruitlessly. Chen clapped his hands and shouted, "You received two strings of coins in life, and you were beaten in private, serving wealthy families like a dog or a horse. Where did all that accumulate? Why do you act so strangely now?" Before he finished speaking, he heard a long sigh from the ghost in the east wing, and the hand also retracted. At dawn, Chen set off on foot, using the scattered coins on the ground to pay the temple monk for his lodging. Duo remarked, "The lower the official, the greedier they are. How can one describe the ugly behavior of such people! They live like ghosts but die as humans, is there no rule in the afterlife? The sigh from the east wing suggests that the ghost has already lost its soul!" Mirror Reflections of the Heart In the Xingjiao Temple in Yangzhou, there lived a man who shook a tiger support, calling himself the Mirror Grindstone Old Man. He wore an ancient mirror at his waist, which looked as if it had been around for a thousand years. When asked about its purpose, he said, "The human heart has seven orifices. Those with little wisdom have their orifices blocked. I use this ancient mirror to reflect on them, knowing where their sickness lies, and then give them a wonderful medicine to open their orifices and increase their wisdom." Thus, foolish and dull people competed to receive the medicine, and it had very good results.

A Certain Wealthy Merchant Had a Son, Who at Sixteen Years Old Could Not Distinguish Between Beans and Wheat. The merchant invited an elderly scholar to his home and knelt for a long time, requesting treatment. The scholar took a mirror to examine the boy closely, shook his head, and stood up, saying, "The illness is too deep; I cannot treat it." The merchant asked for the reason. The scholar replied, "I can treat later-acquired illnesses, but not innate ones. Your son's heart is externally wrapped in the smell of alcohol and meat, a problem that arises from later-acquired habits and can still be cured. However, internally, he is wrapped in the smell of gold and silver, a problem that stems from innate factors and cannot be cured." The merchant firmly requested methods to address the issue. The scholar said, "For the sake of trying, I will treat it." He ordered the boy to be confined in a room, fed rotten scraps when hungry, and given bitter water when thirsty. This regimen lasted for half a year. The scholar then took the mirror to examine the boy again and said, "The smell of alcohol and meat is completely gone! But the smell of gold and silver is blocked by innate factors—what can be done?" The merchant asked, "What do you mean by innate?" The scholar replied, "When your wife was pregnant, gold and silver were piled in the inner room, and your son was affected by that atmosphere, leading to his confusion and blockage of the seven orifices. Externally, it seems like gold, but internally, it is the stench of copper. To seek a cure, hurry to the Temple of Literature's reverence for written characters, take two measures of paper ash, mix it with several dou of ink, make pills the size of tallownuts, and boil wisdom-enhancing soup to take morning and evening. Finishing all of it might help." The merchant followed the scholar's method diligently.

Within three months, the old man took a mirror and looked at himself again, seeing that he had six keen senses, but only one remained dull and blocked as before. The person sought medical treatment again. The old man smiled and said, "This is called the literary sense. You are a wealthy man; it is not appropriate to have a seed of reading. Opening it might invite the displeasure of the creator. Leave this one sense intact, to return it to its original state in your family. Otherwise, if it is cut back too much, what benefit would it bring to you?" The person dared not ask again, and the old man also left. Later, his son was adept at social interactions, wiser than before, but he could not read and recite. The person paid a fee to obtain an official position and ended up as a provincial administrator. The narrator said, "The *Dijing Tu* (Earthly Realm Map) states, 'The energy of copper, when seen, knows the blue sky.' This child was born from a copper mine, yet cannot ascend to the heights of success, why is that? It is because he was born in a time of clear skies and bright sun, his energy is not as strong! The closure of the literary sense, or perhaps it is not his fault."

**Mengpo Village** Lan Rui was a courtesan from Handan who played the zither and sang. Her sister, Yu Rui, had made a solemn oath by biting each other's arms with a man named Ge in the village. Ge was poor, and the madam of the brothel demanded a high dowry, which made him feel hopeless. Lan Rui had many wealthy patrons and gave all the private gifts she received to Ge, using them to pay for Yu Rui's entertainment fees. Ge was grateful to her. After Lan Rui fell ill with a wasting disease and died, Ge felt even more desolate. Not only did he dare not speak of the dowry, but even the thought of spending one night with her made him feel ashamed of his empty pockets. His spirit was broken, and he died of sorrow. He was taken to the underworld, where the judge, moved by his innocence, ordered him to be reborn. He arrived at a place where vines were tied together to form a shelter, and stones were laid out as a table. He saw hundreds of men and women competing for spoons and ladles, approaching the stove to drink a potion. Feeling thirsty, Ge also went to join them. Suddenly, a woman came out from behind the shelter. It was Lan Rui. He was shocked and asked her how she came to be there. She told him everything. He asked her why she was there, and she said, "You died of your love for me, so how could I live alone?" She shook her head and warned him not to drink the potion. He asked her why. She waited until the others had dispersed, then said, "Don't you know? This is Mengpo Village! She went to celebrate the birthday of the Queen, and I am temporarily in charge of the spoons and ladles. If you even taste a drop of this potion, you will forget your past life, and there will be no way to return to life. Now that you still remember your past, why don't you escape back to the world of the living and fulfill our old promise with my sister?" Ge said, "It's too late to keep our old promise, and being reborn is useless. What can you do for me?" She said, "I will help you plan something."

She led him to the back of the tent, where he saw a row of stone jars arranged in the corner of the wall. The woman pointed and said, "This is called the Intelligence Soup, and those who drink it gain wisdom. This is called the Longevity Soup, and those who drink it live longer. This is called the Harmony Soup, and those who drink it bring joy to others." The man asked, "What do you drink?" The woman laughed and said, "This is all made from the chaotic soup of burning heart fire and tear spring!" Finally, they reached a jar, and the woman urged him to drink. The man asked, "What is this called?" The woman replied, "This is the Treasure Soup. You dislike life and love death, but you are missing this one thing!" The man drank a few sips, but it was difficult to swallow. The woman said, "Such a dirty thing should not go into the stomach of a scholar, but since it allows you to vent your feelings as a passionate lover, it is not so bad after all!" The man hesitated. The woman said, "I urge you to finish your cup, for once you pass the Yang Pass, you may not see old friends again." The man smiled and drank half of it. The woman said, "That's enough!" Then she led him out of the tent and showed him the way back.

The time since death had been five days, but because there were no funeral items, the body was left on the bed, with only an elderly woman from the kitchen guarding and watching. Suddenly, the body leaped up, frequently calling out in abdominal pain, probed the throat and vomited violently, like a gushing spring, with a faint glow as if mercury was entering the ground. They ordered to store shovels and picks, dug into the ground several feet, gathered thousands and recruited tens of thousands, and among them were all statues of a Buddha or bodhisattva. They hurriedly went to the brothel owner's house. Yu Rui received news of life and death, and stopped eating for three days. The person revealed the truth, and all were greatly pleased. Then they paid with gold to hire her and returned home. Because of gratitude for Lan Rui's virtue, they moved her coffin to bury her with proper rites. Later, the descendants of the Ge family multiplied, and they ordered to perform sacrifices in spring and autumn, perpetually established as an example.

Duo said, "Ten hu of pearls, a net woven over a thousand miles. With no golden house in the home, where would Lady A-Jiao store herself? Thus, in the land of comfort and indulgence, how many cold scholars have been trapped. Overwhelmed by desires, one sinks into the underworld, buried in bones; beyond the Gate of Ghosts, standing alone in the vast emptiness. In the end, who will eat the money soup? Alas!" The Temple of the Ten Aunts The Temple of the Ten Aunts is located in the western part of Duzhi, its founding era unknown. It boasts ornate beams and pillars, pepper walls and orchid curtains. Inside, ten women are sculpted, adorned with jade feathers and bright pendants, all of extraordinary beauty. A certain scholar from the upper house passed by this place, entered the temple to pay homage to the images, and returned home, feeling a dream. He suddenly found himself standing under the veranda. At that time, the autumn river stretched across the sky, the dew was thick on the ground, sparse stars flickered, and a corner of a red building was barely visible. Suddenly, he saw a group of five or six beautiful women, holding several red silk lanterns, leading a group of beauties down the steps. One woman sighed, looking up at the sky, saying, "Tonight, the Palace of Eternal Cold is closed. I wonder how lonely Chang'e is in her solitude?" The others said, "Do not worry for her. We are alone without a husband, and we are no less than the Lady of Qingxi." As they laughed, a maid moved the lamp and trimmed the coal, seeing someone crouching in the shadows under the veranda, she exclaimed, "Where is this reckless child, peeking at the national beauties here?" The group hurried to see, laughing, "We just said we have no husband, and suddenly there is a guest. This greatly relieves our embarrassment." They invited him into the room, arranging seats for two or three people in order.

In a short while, delicious dishes and fine wine were arranged on the table. The eldest sister said, "Drinking alone and feeling depressed, tonight we are fortunate to have a good guest, why don't we play a game of refined poetry?" The others laughed and said, "Still, the host is not ordinary, the moment he speaks, he mentions something elegant." The eldest sister said, "How dare I claim to be elegant? Just take a phrase from the Four Books, follow it with an ancient person's name, if it matches, they don't have to drink, otherwise, they must drink according to the rules of the Jin Valley." The others said, "Yes!" The host took a large cup of wine to toast someone. The person toasted back, saying, "Guests should not take away the host's role." The eldest sister took the cup and drank, turned over the palm and stood up, saying, "Mencius meets King Hui of Liang — Wei Zheng." The others all praised, "Wonderful! The concise and elegant poetry, the poetry competition of the Han scholars, is no more than this." Then it came to the second sister. The second sister said, "Can be used to compose a poem — Xu You." The eldest sister said, "Later, they yield to the superior, the great sorcerer suppresses the small sorcerer." Then it came to the third sister. The third sister said, "Crops do not grow — Tian Guang." The fourth sister continued the game, saying, "Put down weapons — Bi Zhan." The fifth sister looked sideways and laughed, saying, "The second sister's skill is equal to the first, they can be called two queens on the poetry stage!" The fourth sister looked at her with a white eye (disdain). The fifth sister playfully tickled her face with a feather, saying, "Sit in mud and charcoal — Hei Dun." The fourth sister twisted her abdomen a few times, saying, "The girl really has a left-handed bias." The game came to the sixth sister. The sixth sister stammered, saying, "Alone, alone, alone..." The third sister said, "Who among us is not alone? You need to say many words." She took the cup to punish.

Aunt the eldest said, "Phoenix, phoenix, still a phoenix, what harm?" Aunt the sixth blushed and giggled, saying, "I enjoy bravery—Wang Mang." Aunt the seventh lowered her head and smiled; the group questioned her, and she replied, "I have a command, but it's not very elegant." Aunt the eldest said, "Little mischievous girl, always playing tricks. With guests present, don't speak recklessly." Aunt the seventh could no longer hold back and said, "As straight as an arrow—Yang Hu." The group covered their ears, unwilling to hear. Aunt the eighth turned to Aunt the ninth and said, "I'll go with you to get the jiegu drum to cleanse the foolish girl of her impurities." Speaking seriously, she added, "Taibo can truly be called the highest virtue—Yu Rang." Aunt the ninth said, "The relationship between friends—Di Wu Lun." Aunt the tenth stood up and said, "My sister is young, let her try to save face for all of us."

Even if there were a thousand people, I would still go forward — Yang Xiong." Just as I was deeply pondering and had not yet finished, I heard the tenth aunt speak, and suddenly had a great realization: "The water of Niu Mountain once tasted so good, like a single stone of fine quality." After speaking, I felt quite proud. The eldest aunt said, "A talented person with extensive learning, not afraid to delve into obscure matters, how could they even mention *Water Margin*?" I retorted loudly, "How could I not recognize the channel's illness and the Guan Suo's plight, and not understand the reckless bravery of the Three Langs?" Everyone suppressed their laughter. The eldest aunt said, "You are mistaken! What he said refers to Yang Xiong of the Cao Yuan Pavilion." The seventh aunt said, "Lazy scoundrel, only knows about stealing a bow and being called a thief, what does he care about Yang Xiong's clouds and rains?" I felt embarrassed. The third aunt said, "With so many mouths against mine, it's better to just drink three rounds." I raised the cup and finished it in one go. The eldest aunt laughed and said, "Your knowledge is quite limited, but your capacity for wine is luckily quite wide!" The whole room burst into laughter. After the exchange of wine and food lasted for a while, the fifth aunt suddenly stood up and said, "Today's gathering cannot be without poetry." She ordered the maids to bring writing tools. The seventh aunt said, "The fifth aunt is used to playing with her knowledge, but today we only need to collect old phrases from ancient people and compose a poem in a set form." The eldest aunt said, "I didn't expect this mischievous child to have such a system in their heart." She ordered the maids to move the lamp to the wall and first picked up the brush to write: [Note: The actual poem written by the speaker is not provided in the source text, so it is omitted in the translation.]

Jià dé Xiāo láng ài yuǎn yóu, měi yīn fēngjǐng què shēng chóu. The woman, married to Xiao Lang who loves traveling far, often feels sorrow because of the scenery. Táohuā liǎn báo nán cáng lèi, tóngshù xīn gū yì gǎn qiū. With a delicate peach-blossom face that cannot hide her tears, and a solitary willow tree that easily feels the sadness of autumn. Làngyuàn yǒu shū duō fù hè, huà píng wú shuì dài qiān niú. In the mythical garden of immortals, many books are carried by cranes; on the painted screen, she lies awake, waiting for the Cowherd. Páng rén wèi bì zhī xīn shì, yòu bào qīng qīn shàng yù lóu. Others may not know her inner thoughts, yet she again wraps herself in a light quilt and ascends the jade pavilion. Er yí tí yuē: Er yí tí yuē: The second aunt wrote: Mèng lái hé chù gèng wéi yún? Bǎ jiǔ táng qián rì yòu hūn. Where did the dream come from, only to become more like clouds? Holding wine at the hall, the day again turns dark. Liào dé yě yīng lián Sòng yù, kěn jiào róngyì jiàn Wén jūn. I suppose she should also pity Cóng Yù, but would she easily show herself to Wén Jūn? Pāo cán cuì yǔ chéng luán shàn, chóuchàng jīn ní cù dié qún. She has discarded her green-feathered phoenix fan, and laments the golden mud clustered butterfly skirt. Qǔ cì huā cóng lǎn huí gù, dàn hóng xiāng bái yī qún qún. Picking flowers carelessly without looking back, a group of pale red and fragrant white blossoms. Sān yí yuē: “Èr zǐ gōng lì chán mián, zhēn sì Lǐ dū wèi 《yuānyāng cí》 yě. Mèi cóng hé chù zhe bǐ?” Yì zhàn mò ér shū yuē: Sān yí yuē: “Èr zǐ gōng lì chán mián, zhēn sì Lǐ dū wèi 《yuānyāng cí》 yě. Mèi cóng hé chù zhe bǐ?” Yì zhàn mò ér shū yuē: The third aunt said: "The second sister's work is exquisite and lingering, truly like Lǐ Dū Wèi's *Yuānyāng Cí*. Where should I begin my writing?" She dipped her brush and wrote: Ben lái yín hàn shì hóng qiáng, yún yǔ Wūshān wǎng duàn cháng. Originally, the Milky Way was a red wall, and the cloud and rain of Mount Wushan were futile heartbreak. Yǔ wǒ zhōuxuán níng zuò wǒ, wèi láng qiáocuì què xiū láng. To interact with me, let it be me; for the sake of my husband, I wither but am ashamed of him. Xián kuī yè yuè xiāo jīn zhàng, juàn yǐ chūnfēng bái yù chuáng. Quietly gazing at the night moon in the luxurious tent, tiredly leaning against the spring breeze on the white jade bed. Shéi wèi hán chóu dú bù jiàn, yīshēng yíngdé shì qīliáng. Who is the one who bears sorrow alone? All she has won in life is sorrow. Èr yí yuē: “Miào sì liánhuán, qiǎo tóng yù hé. Sū huì zǐ huíwén zhījǐn, wèi sān niáng zuò hòu chén yǐ!” Sì yí tí yuē: Èr yí yuē: “Mi

Returning, how could one know they would return to the moon? Seated, though close, they are far from heaven. When will this gathering of wealth be convened? Each time one reflects, a sense of aimlessness arises. The fifth aunt said, "Even the Yellow Crane Tower's poetry, the Immortal Poet (Li Bai) would be stumped. Reluctantly, I will compose a poem." The poem reads: A golden house built to house A-Jiao, the scent of wine and the warmth of a red quilt stretch through the night. On the Yingtai, in the dark moonlight, two phoenixes fly; in the deep spring of the Copper Bird Terrace, the two Qiao sisters are confined. There is a charm that can serve as proof, but no news has reached today. It is better to follow companions back to the mountains, where a clear stream winds to a curved bridge. The elder sister laughed and said, "This child has great resentment." She looked at the sixth aunt. The sixth aunt quickly wrote, and all watched as she wrote: Misty smoke lightly shrouds a spring, her skin like jade, her spirit like ice. Leisurely leaning on a screen, she smiles at Zhou Fang, not letting the fairy dog bark at Liu Shen. When will we meet again? The beauty of the nation and city is not in any one person. Looking back, one can rely on the place of music and dance, but the dust of travel is not the same as it was in the past. The seventh aunt said, "The sixth sister uses her pen as her tongue, speaking so eloquently." The sixth aunt glared at her. She then smiled and wrote: Farewell, the spring wind at the lakeside pavilion, the grace of Chu waist in the palm of my hand. Half awake, half drunk, I wander for three days; we live and die as one. I don't know when the golden hairpins fell from my bosom; at my pillow, a fallen hairpin often lies. Awakening, tears drip into the Xiang River, and the painted screen cannot capture the scene.

The eldest sister said, "The young girl speaks of romantic and poetic matters as soon as she opens her mouth, truly, she is madly passionate to the point of death." The seventh sister said, "Who is like my sister in her scholarly demeanor, just wanting to 'hold a light quilt on the jade tower'?" The eighth sister said, "Flattering words charm, it is also the nature of a woman." She then composed a poem and wrote it on the wall: At midnight, the swing is in the midst of wine, west of the painted hall, east of the osmanthus hall. On Li Hua's knees, much can be remembered, by Feiyan's skirt, one bows in admiration. Sorrow increases, joy decreases, when it comes, there is no trace, when it leaves, there is no sign. Now alone, I am filled with melancholy, the face of a person and the color of peach blossoms reflect each other. The ninth sister said, "To drink wine and sing, to make this weeping of a Chu captive, my eighth sister has ruined the scenery!" She then took the pen and wrote: There is wine in the pot, let us drink together, do not pour long sorrow into short verses. Before the night-blooming jasmine, everyone is drunk, under the painted window, the moon is setting. The embroidered brocade tent is tied with a heart-shaped belt, the beautiful woman presses down the gold bracelet on her arm. Who will report the news to Wang Chang, a thousand gold pieces cannot buy the heart behind the curtain. The eighth sister said, "Graceful and refined, your ninth sister is truly delightful." The tenth sister said, "I cannot compose poetry, can I ask my ninth sister to write for me?" The others did not allow it. The tenth sister turned her back to the wall, quickly wrote: I have never understood longing, do not let the clever one sing my words. If there is wine, pour it only on the soil of Zhaozhou, no one understands the poetry of the Bo family. Often use the *White Snow* tune to accompany Su Xiaoxiao, do not use gold to cast Xu Zhongshu.

I am the one who wields the colorful brush in dreams, can it be fitting for all of humanity? The crowd laughed and said, "Do not say that the ten aunts are kind and thick-skinned; this poem is full of playful mockery." They then took a brush and handed it to me. I was drenched in sweat, my hands trembling as if carrying a massive tripod, and after sucking the brush dozens of times, I faced the wall, my breath as heavy as a cow's. The eldest aunt said, "When inspiration is at its peak, putting pen to paper is a joyous event in the poetry world. Why are you so troubled?" The third aunt said, "Studying *The Capital* for ten years and refining *The Metropolis* for a generation is also a common example for scholars!" The seventh aunt said, "As you say, it is also good. Tonight, let us mix in a million extra hours, and see if Master Wen can achieve success." I felt that cold words were penetrating and forced myself to write seven characters on the wall: "Since the beginning of time." The eldest aunt was startled and said, "Do you wish to compose about the universe? And where does this phrase come from?" I replied, "It is the ancestor of folk songs that has been hung at the gates of the country, never altered even by a single word." The eldest aunt said, "Incorporating folk songs into poetry, the poetry world loses its luster!" The seventh aunt said, "Recently, poetry masters, for the most part, revere folk songs as the ancestor, and set them to music; ladies in the inner chambers listen with great interest, is it not better than exhausting oneself?" The hall erupted in laughter, and I looked dejected, speaking hesitantly, "My previous words were meant in jest! Please allow me to revise it." Thus, I pretended to recite and revise the writing. The fifth aunt examined it from the side, and it was the first line of *Qianji Shi* (Poems of a Thousand Families). However, the character for "noon" was mistakenly written as "cow," and she covered her mouth and laughed. I held the brush and pretended to ponder deeply.

A sudden person in official attire appeared, and I seized the opportunity to put down my brush, while Ten Aunt hurried to attend to his left and right. The person sat at the table and said, "I am Du Fu, the Lost Remembrancer of Huaxi Stream! Since the Tang dynasty, I have been worshipped here, but it never occurred to the villagers that they were ignorant and mistook 'Lost Remembrancer' for 'Ten Aunt.' Thus, they allowed women to crowd in, making a mess of makeup and sitting arrogantly. I tolerated you because you were somewhat knowledgeable and cultured, so you were allowed to stay temporarily. But you have been leading young boys to defile my walls with filth. From now on, quickly avoid me by three li. Do not think that Du's white-handled long hoe is less sharp than the edge of the Pingzhang sword!" Ten Aunt fell to the ground to ask for forgiveness, but his anger was not yet released, and he ordered me to go out first. I said, "What kind of evil guest has come, expelling the poet?" Ten Aunt whispered in my ear, "This is Du Shaoling of the Tang dynasty." I asked, "Who is Du Shaoling?" Ten Aunt angrily said, "Even if you don't know Du Shaoling, you should not come here to talk about poetry, causing trouble for us." She then slapped me ten times across the face. Suddenly, I heard a loud shout from the hall, "He is just an outsider; why bother to talk further?" The scolding did not stop, and I suddenly woke up, still not understanding who Du Shaoling was. Whenever I met someone, I would recount my dream, and everyone who heard it would laugh.

Later scholars completely destroyed the image of the woman, but they still worshiped Du Fu at the temple. Those who passed by the place, wanting to inscribe poems on the wall, would always cite a certain upper-class scholar as a precedent. Duo said, "Lesser Du wanted to shelter all poor scholars with ten thousand houses, but the upper-class scholar could not even temporarily stay under the eaves. To see how those who love talent as if it were their life, there are none who do not avoid the world like enemies. The whitewashed wall is easy to paint over, but the long hoe is hard to offend. It is clear that guarding the temple of Huanhua Creek is also not an easy task." Counting classics before the carriage Fan Heng of Yuanhe, the son of the prime minister's son. Returning from a test at the Ministry of Rites, passing through the borders of Jingzhou. A person with a sleeve over their head, tidying their shoes, came unsteadily to the carriage and begged for several ingots of silver. Fan smiled and said, "What skills do you have, yet you have such extravagant hopes?" The person replied, "I am a poor man, but rich in books." At the time, a cowherd stood under a willow tree, using a bamboo pole to play with bats. Fan said, "Use this to test the matter. If you can name one classic, I will give you one ingot of silver. If you are truly rich in knowledge and learning, even if I give away all my silver, I will not hesitate." Fan thought that the bat was a rare and obscure subject, so he used it to challenge him.

The man replied, "Yes." He cited passages from *Er Ya*, Xu Shen's *Shuowen Jiezi*, *Xuanzhong*, *Shuyi*, and other records, as well as from *Shenyi Mijing* and *Wutai Shici An*, about seven or eight points in all, speaking confidently and fluently. Fan was amazed and said, "You truly have a wealth of classical texts. But do you not know that in poetry and verse, you can also cite a few examples?" The man replied, "The line 'The broken pearl curtain lets bats fly through,' is from Bai Juyi's poem. 'Playfully watching bats fly towards red banana leaves,' is from Qin Guan's poem. Huang Tingjian's poem says, 'Strange that my body looks like a dried bat, having worked hard at the riverbank last night.' In *Feng Huai Er Bai Yun*, a predecessor wrote, 'A light breeze turns bats, and the candle goes out as the cricket rests.' In *Dongxiange*, there is the line, 'Mistakenly thinking it's a cool breeze, the bat brushes against the eaves.' Using ancient examples to prove modern points, how could one exhaustively describe them? Let me mention a few things in everyday language, please do not laugh at me." Fan asked him to elaborate further. The man replied, "It is not difficult to explain. I am afraid that your money will run out on your journey, which would inevitably increase my guilt!" Fan counted the total number of items, gave him twelve ingots, and bowed deeply before leaving.

At night, Fan checked into an inn and heard that there was a man entertaining prostitutes in the adjacent courtyard, drinking to excess and causing the walls to shake with noise. Fan hurried over to see and found a man seated in a prominent position in front of a carriage, with four prostitutes standing around him. When Fan arrived, the man smiled and descended the steps, inviting him to sit down and ordering the prostitutes to play the pipa and sing. For each song, Fan was asked to pay one silver ingot. After only three rounds, the silver was already exhausted. The man then rose, brushing his clothes and said, "The money for laughter is gone; I have spent it for you!" Fan replied, "You are also a poor scholar; why not stay a little longer to support yourself?" The man said, "I got it myself, I lost it myself; what is there to regret?" Fan rebuked him sternly. The man then laughed and said, "My tongue is still intact; there is no need to worry! Besides, the world's transient wealth is only meant for people like us. If we indulge in it, we will either become ill or go mad! What are you advising me to do?" Fan greatly approved and continued drinking, enjoying themselves until they parted ways. As they were leaving, Fan asked the man for his name and surname, but the man smiled and did not answer. A knowledgeable person said, "This is a certain young nobleman who once scattered ten thousand gold among the local people, relying on begging for food to mock the world." Fan sighed and said, "Indeed, there are extraordinary people in the world. From now on, whenever I meet a vegetable seller, I will seek them out! How dare I judge the heroes of the world with ordinary eyes!"

The Bell said, "Selling poetry and books to seek drunkenness and fullness, those with aspirations would not stoop to. Yet, not accumulating unexpected gains, is it any different from not accepting food given with contempt? If there are such people in the world, I will transport them in my rear carriage." Riding a Mule and Discussing Books Xie Yingluan, a guest of his uncle Mr. Wentao in the Linzi county office, later served as a deputy magistrate in Feixian, responsible for handling documents. One day, he was riding in a sedan chair to visit a guest, and he wrote a note and gave it to the subordinate Li Sheng to call for a vehicle to wait. When he went out to see, it was a mule cart. The young man angrily scolded him. Li said, "Just now, I received clear instructions, which only mentioned preparing a vehicle, not a sedan chair." The young man said, "You are truly slow-witted. A vehicle is the same as a sedan chair. Because the word 'sedan chair' is not classical, it is generally referred to as a 'vehicle.'" Li laughed and said, "In the past, Prince Huainan's 'Memorial Against Attacking Minyue' had the phrase 'vehicle and sedan chair crossing the mountains,' how can one say it is not classical?" The young man was startled and said, "I did not expect such a well-read person among your kind." He then dismounted and rode the mule, ordering Li to follow behind on foot, and said, "You have a wealth of knowledge in your mind, do you know about the affairs of this place, Wucheng?" Li said, "This is my hometown, how could I not know?" The young man said, "In the 'Biography of Confucius's Disciples' in the 'Records of the Grand Historian,' it states, 'Dantai Mie Ming, a native of Wucheng.'"

While recording the place where Ziyu lived in Wucheng, it is uniquely distinguished as "South," suggesting that there were two Wuchengs in the state of Lu at that time. Is this true? Or false?" Li replied, "Common belief is that Ziyu lived in Wucheng of Feixian, while Zengzi's South Wucheng is in today's Jiaxiang County. This claim is false." The student asked, "What evidence do you have for this?" Li said, "The *Spring and Autumn Annals* records that in the nineteenth year of Duke Xiang's reign, 'Wucheng was built.' The commentary states: 'South Wucheng of Mount Tai.' In the twenty-third year of Duke Zhao's reign: 'The people of Wucheng... defeated the army of Zou, capturing the land of Chu Ruo.' In the eighth year of Duke Ai's reign: 'The Wu army... attacked Wucheng and conquered it.' The *Mencius* records: 'Zengzi lived in Wucheng, where there was a Yue invasion.' Since it borders Zou and lies on the route to Wu and Yue, it is the Wucheng of Feixian today. The *Qi Chronicles* also states, 'I visited the old county of stringed music, located between Feixian and Teng County.' There is no mention of two Wuchengs." The student said, "If that is the case, why does the *Records of the Grand Historian* specifically mention South Wucheng?" Li said, "In my opinion, Dingxiang has a Wucheng, and Qinghe has a Wucheng. The term 'South' is used to distinguish it from these two places. As in the *Biography of Lord Pingyuan* where it says 'he was enfeoffed at Dongwucheng,' it is an example of the same." The student greatly admired this. Upon returning, he reported it to the magistrate of Feixian, who also found it remarkable. The student was exempted from his duties and appointed as a clerk in the local records office. Within a year, he accumulated a fortune of a thousand gold pieces, becoming a wealthy household in the village.

After Mr. Wentao finished compiling the *Linzhi County Gazetteer*, he summoned the student to assist him. The student took Li along as a consultant. However, the topics they discussed about the old records of Linzhi all pertained to Zichuan County instead. The student was surprised and asked Li about this. Li replied, "In my humble collection, there are only records of Zichuan, and none of Linzhi." The student was greatly puzzled and quickly examined the secret books. It turned out that *Shuiling* had two volumes, both fragmented and incomplete, with only several dozen pages of *Shandong Archaeological Records* and a few pages of *Fujian Chronicles* remaining. The discussions on sedan chairs and the studies on Wucheng were only mentioned in a few pages! The student then sighed, "The destiny of a scholar leads to great fame and abundant blessings, and this is what it is like." His uncle heard this and laughed heartily, rewarding him with money and tools, and sent him back with expenses.

The bell said: "A person with a narrow intellect who pretends to be knowledgeable about the *Zizhi Tongjian* (Comprehensive Mirror to Aid in Government), acting gracefully and finding himself in a high-ranking position in the central government, is merely enjoying a long period of good fortune! Otherwise, he might lose his status, with different patterns on his official robes, and even sigh in regret, how much more so for regretting the reading of *Nanhua* (Tao Te Ching)!" **Dead Marriage** Qing'er was a female performer in the household of Liang Si in the Pearl Market. Liang Si's wife, originally a courtesan from Wu, was skilled in playing the pipa. After marrying Liang Si, she bought young girls to train in the theater to make a living. Qing'er was disdainful of this and often ran away from school. Her adoptive mother beat her daily, and her sisters tried to persuade her. Qing'er said, "If you follow me, you must change from a female role to a male role." They asked why. She replied, "I am unfortunate to be born a woman, with grievances I cannot express. If I play a male role, I can at least use the face of a hero to vent my inner frustrations!" Thus, in plays like *The Thousand Gold Record*, Qing'er stood out uniquely.

The Filial and Upright Official Zhan Xiangting, appointed to wait on imperial summons in White Gate, lived with his friends in the residence of the Liang family. One night, they performed the play "Qian Jin Ji" up to the segment "Parting with Yu Ji." All the women were attracted to the character Yu Ji, but Xiangting alone found the leader of the Chu forces, Xiang Yu, with his two pupils in one eye, to be charming, and everyone started laughing at him. After taking off their costumes, they saw Xiang Yu's appearance, which indeed stood out above the beautiful women in the tent, and Xiangting was impressed. The next day, they set up a feast under the crabapple tree, with men in green robes and women in red dresses sitting in a circle, mixed together. Qing'er, originally from She County, and Xiangting, also from Wuyuan County, each spoke their local dialect to express their admiration for each other. The other guests, however, smiled at each other, competing not to understand what the others were saying. Later, Xiangting had a plan in mind but could not become a scholar-official, and all his friends returned to their seats, while Xiangting packed his things but did not leave yet, not forgetting Qing'er. He thought of buying a boat to take her away with him. However, the Liang family was holding her as a valuable commodity and wanted to keep her as the top performer; it would take a million taels of silver to persuade them otherwise. They looked at each other with tears in their eyes, thinking intensely but having no plan. Qing'er whispered privately, "How clumsy are your plans! The reason they are holding me as a valuable commodity and not selling me is because I am their money tree! If you leave, I will surely not live. If you stay and buy me, it definitely wouldn't be worth a thousand taels of silver!" Xiangting was deeply saddened. Unwillingly, he bid her farewell with great respect.

Not yet two months had passed since my return when I heard that Qing'er was ill and on the verge of death. Xiangting said, “A promise made beneath the flowers — is it not as binding as holding fast to a pillar? You have not betrayed me; how could I dare to betray you?” He hurried to Jinling, bought a coffin with three hundred taels of silver, and returned to bury Qing'er north of Tongjing Bridge. Madam Wang, Cao Moqin, inscribed her tomb; I requested renowned scholars to compose elegiac poems in her honor; I composed the legendary drama *Gan Jin Xiao* and entrusted it to the music department. Alas! Unable to bring life, yet returning through death — is this not the ultimate thought of the devoted lover, compelled by no choice but to act thus? And so, Qing'er, too, has thereby attained immortality! Duo said: “A man carries a seven-foot body, yet lives an unremarkable life with no extraordinary virtue, and in the end, rots away like grass and wood. How then, in the secluded world of the inner chamber, can there be one who is remembered? Only by not breaking a death oath while living can one die with vitality. At the same time, there was a woman named He'er, who gave a small portrait of Ma Xianglan to Zhao Yueting of Wujiang — also a woman of keen and thoughtful heart. Later, she followed a wealthy youth from her village, and within half a year became a widow. She then relied on her adoptive mother to support herself by selling the pipa. Alas! On Xue Tao’s grave, the peach blossoms have already fallen; at Guan Panlou’s tower, swallows return in vain. The life of He'er is no match for the death of Qing'er!” **The Lament of the Living** A silk merchant from Jiangning, engaged in trade in Wu, had long been fond of leaf games. One day, he invited several guests to his hall to compete for victory. A messenger arrived from Shengze, saying that someone of the surname Chen was coming. The merchant, deeply attached to the game, had no time to turn his shoes upside down in eagerness to welcome him, but since they had long been on friendly terms, he ordered his servant to bring the visitor in.

Chen appeared before him, his tears and snot mingling on his face, grabbing his arm and weeping bitterly. The man doubted his sanity and continued playing his cards as usual. Chen then said, "Your time of death has come! I am about to leave, and by the time I return, I may not be able to pay my respects. Therefore, I have prepared a modest offering of paper and silk, paying my respects to you now." After speaking, he directed his followers to place incense and paper money on the seat, taking out a memorial offering from his sleeve and asking the man to accept it. The man found this even more absurd and continued playing his cards as before. Chen then changed into white clothing and a hat, approached the man at the field's edge, and bowed deeply. Weeping and crying, he seemed overwhelmed with sorrow. The man suddenly flew into a rage, stood up, and said, "I have always considered you a trusted friend, believing that you traveled a hundred miles to give me wise counsel. Why would you do such an ominous thing, acting like a curse?" The guests at the table also criticized him. Chen responded calmly, "Am I lying? Because during my illness in early spring, I entered the underworld, where I saw a sign hanging beside a hall. On it, I saw your name, already being accused by someone, and a judgment was made for you to be heard on the second day of the seventh month." The man asked, "Who is accusing me?" Chen replied, "A certain woman." "What is she accusing me of?" "It concerns an incident on the nineteenth day of the ninth month last autumn. A nun who was involved has already been tied up in the hall." Upon hearing this, the man's expression turned gloomy, his cards falling from his hand like autumn leaves scattered by the wind, covering the ground. He then stood up, took Chen's hand, and wept bitterly as well.

The guests inquired about the details. The person said, "This is a matter of my own unworthiness; why repeat it?" Chen shed tears and departed. The person also hastily packed and, under the stars, hired a boat to return to Bai Xia. Later, it was heard that the person indeed died on the second day of the seventh month. The guests were greatly surprised and privately visited Chen to inquire about his whereabouts. Chen smiled and said, "My old friend failed to cherish his precious jade, leading to divine retribution. You all should encourage yourselves; why ask?" He then sighed and left. Duo said, "The jade ring tarnished its integrity, but no laws were cast; the formal attire betrayed its heart, but it luckily escaped the state's punishment; the nine depths and eighteen hells exist to fill the gaps in the legal net. It is difficult to deceive in the dark, for the warning of history is close at hand. Wise men who preserve themselves should still strive!" The Magician Drives Away Flies My uncle Minggao, styled Chuhe, served as the governor of Baoding Prefecture in Zhili Province, known for his strict governance and reputation as an able official. At the time, my sister's husband, Shao Nanchu, held the position of censor and recommended a guest from the capital. His surname was Xiong, styled Zijing. He was extremely unattractive, barely literate, and spent his days eating, sleeping, and sitting alone, never engaging in social interaction. After half a year, he left. Before departing, he told the host, "I have troubled your kitchen for a long time. Now I bid farewell, and I would like to offer a skill." The host agreed, and he summoned guests from his entourage to watch.

During the height of the summer heat, countless millions of flies swarmed in the hall. Some flew, others settled, and some clung to necks and pounced on faces, buzzing and swarming incessantly, like sand being scattered and beans thrown. A young servant was ordered to fan left and right to drive them away. The bear pulled out two chopsticks from his sleeve, catching each fly as it flew, missing none. He gathered all of them into his left sleeve, laughing and chatting, and went to the host's farewell banquet. After finishing the meal, he opened his sleeve and released them, saying, "If you do not disturb me, I will not catch you. Go quickly! Go quickly!" In a moment, countless sparks like meteors scattered in all directions, and the hall was completely free of flies. The onlookers were all terrified. The host offered him gold, but he refused it. He said, "May the virtuous governor govern the people as I have governed the flies. Then the entire prefecture will be blessed with much good fortune!" After finishing his speech, he waved his sleeve and left. Duo said, "Eagles and hawks chase sparrows, yet they are ultimately called kind mothers. This shows that severity must be balanced with leniency. Those who are called capable officials because of their martial strength and harshness will treat the people like ants, not just like flies." A Strong Man Taming a Tiger Yizhou's rugged mountains are home to many fierce tigers. The local magistrate often ordered hunters to capture them, but they were often eaten by the tigers. There was a man named Jiao Qi from Shaanxi, who sought relatives but found none, and ended up living in Yizhou. He was always extremely brave, able to lift the stone tripod in front of the Thousand Buddha Temple and leap onto the left ridge of the Great Hero Hall. Therefore, people called him Jiao Shiding. Knowing that there were many tigers on the Yiling, he would walk into the mountains daily, killing any tiger he encountered with his bare hands and carrying it back, making it a regular habit.

One day, entering the mountains, he encountered two tigers leading a small cub. Jiao's spirit arose, and he killed the two tigers consecutively, shouldering them on both sides, and captured the small cub alive and returned. Everyone scattered in fear, but Jiao laughed and spoke as if nothing had happened. A wealthy person admired his bravery and set up a feast to entertain him. On the seat, Jiao described his past experiences of tying up tigers, and those who listened all became pale. But Jiao further exaggerated his words, speaking and gesturing, proud and self-assured. Suddenly, a cat climbed onto the table and grabbed food, dripping greasy juice everywhere, making a mess on the table. Jiao thought it was the host's cat and let it eat heartily and leave. The host said, "A nuisance from a neighboring house, how disgusting it is!" Not long after, the cat came again. Jiao rose up quickly and fought with fists, overturning and breaking all the dishes and nuts on the table, and the cat had already jumped and hidden in the window corner. Jiao was angry and chased and hit it again, breaking all the window lattices. The cat let out a loud cry, dragged its tail, slowly walked, and went over the wall of the neighbor's house. Jiao had no idea what to do and just stood there staring blankly. The host clapped hands and laughed, and Jiao was greatly embarrassed and left. As for being able to tie up tigers but not cats, is it really brave against big enemies and cowardly against small enemies? It is also because the proportions do not match. A cauldron large enough to contain a cow cannot be used to cook small fish, and a crossbow weighing a thousand catties cannot be used to hit a small mouse. Those with talent should know, and those who use talent even more should know.

The Bell said: "Grand Commandant Bao Ji was concerned about the oxen's breathing, but he did not address military affairs, criminal justice, finance, or grain supply; it was not that he did not address them, but rather that he was unable to. How do we know this? We know this from the story of Jiao Sheng tying up a cat." Qiao Wu Meng Qian Ren Jun, a native of Mudu in Wu, loved to buy books, but did not follow the rules of Yushu, and he was so busy that he had no time to spare, yet he copied books by hand, almost filling his house with books. He heard that Mount Tai has many Qin and Han inscriptions, so he packed his writing materials and went to explore. In a village, he came across a fork in the road and had no one to ask for directions. Suddenly, he saw a thatched cottage beside a bamboo fence, with a woman picking flowers by the fence, followed by a blind old woman. Qian hurriedly asked for directions, but the old woman did not answer. The woman laughed and said, "This young man is very bookish, why ask the blind for directions?" She picked a flower and pushed open the door to invite him in. After a while, an old man came out and said, "Where is the distinguished guest from? You are lost here. If you do not mind, my humble abode can still accommodate you." Qian was delighted and followed the old man inside. The old man asked where he had come from. Qian replied, "I am a famous scholar from Wu, and I love to read rare books from all over the world. Today, I wanted to explore strange caves to enrich my knowledge, but I did not expect to get lost on this forked path!" The old man said, "This remote village, with its humble dwellings, is fortunate to have a distinguished guest. I am ashamed to be a simple countryman, and I am not worthy of your instruction. My foolish daughter has a basic knowledge of the classics and history. Please let her listen to your profound ideas to broaden her knowledge. Please do not laugh at her." He then ordered the blind old woman to bring out the woman and seated her beside him.

A thousand-petal bottle on the table had a branch of *Sulmeiren* inserted into it, its beauty and charm captivating. The man laughed and said, "This is the spirit of Xiang Yu's tent." The woman replied, "Xiang Yu should be called 'West Chu,' not just 'Chu.' Is your knowledge of history really so lacking?" The man's spirits sank. The old man said, "Common usage has been passed down, what's there to be surprised about? 'After that, he presented the *Fanghe* painting and asked for an inscription.' The man, proud of his extensive talents, quickly wrote, "The tail is entirely black." The woman hurriedly stopped him, saying, "You've made another mistake! The crane's tail is not black; what is referred to as black is the area where the wings are folded. Have you only seen a standing crane, not a flying one?" The man felt even more ashamed. The old man said, "This young girl doesn't understand the matter at all. The first chapter of *He Ming* is interpreted this way; how could it be your fault?" The man then smiled and said, "We scholars rely on commentaries to explain texts. How could we delve into trivial matters and fall into the pitfalls of neglecting the classics? That's why I bear the reputation of being well-versed; because I truly possess the knowledge of thousands of books in my mind!"

During the conversation, a young boy with braided hair entered carrying a school bag. The old man said, "This is my youngest son, just four years old. He has a slight understanding of the sentence structure of *The Great Learning*, and I request that you teach him." Mr. Wan began to explain the first section of *The Great Learning*, and as he recited it once, a blind old woman clapped her hands and laughed loudly. The old man scolded her, saying, "Old servant, you have gone mad! Clapping and shouting, what kind of scene is this?" The woman replied, "I am blind in sight but not in hearing. Now, having heard the opening line, I have already found five or six misspelled characters. I wonder how many tens of thousands or millions of misspelled characters there are in a thousand scrolls in one's heart!" The old man asked, "What do you mean by misspelled characters?" The woman said, "According to the pronunciation of the Central Plains, the character '大' in *The Great Learning* is read like '岱,' the character '道' is read with the rising tone, the three '在' characters are all read with the rising tone, and the character '善' is not read with the falling tone. Now, the character '大' is not known to be read as what sound, and the four rising tone characters are all read with the falling tone. Is this not laughable?" The old man said, "You, sir, are from Wu, and you cannot help but use your local accent. Otherwise, in the world, there are no learned scholars who would read so many misspelled characters in the first line of *The Great Learning*." Mr. Wan felt deeply ashamed and quickly got up to bid farewell. The old man said, "Your wild words are not definitive conclusions. I have some humble advice, and I hope you will consider it." Mr. Wan bowed and stood to ask. The old man said, "Those who love to learn widely often become scattered. Those who are obsessed with the unusual gain nothing. From now on, just take the Five Classics, *The Analects*, and *Mencius*, and read them for ten years at home. There is no need to travel long distances to seek Qin steles or Han stone inscriptions." Mr. Wan nodded and left.

From this point on, he deeply immersed himself in practical learning, ceasing to act as a mere clerk who copied documents. Zhao Kanglei governed the world, requiring only half of the *Analects*. Thus, the books on Zhang Jiuling's shelves, marked with ivory bookmarks and numbering in the tens of thousands, could all be considered waste paper. However, those who are obsessed with copying texts and book collectors generally boast about their wealth through extensive collections. Especially, one fears that the libraries of Lu Yu and Xu Heng might all be ruined by literate scholars who overinterpret or misread the texts.

The Wife of the Man from Jingde A man from Jingde was driven out by his fellow villagers and hid in Weiyang, where he married a woman from a certain family for a thousand coins. Later, his family prospered, and he was able to support maids and servants. He donated several hundred coins to buy an empty official title, and inside his home, red hats hung high, bamboo whips were arranged in pairs, and prominent seals displayed his authority, resembling that of a noble family. However, he did not engage in commerce or trade, and the source of his wealth remained a mystery. He left home in the evening and returned in the morning, his behavior being unusually mysterious. His wife asked him about it. He replied, "Merchants are hosting a night banquet for distinguished guests, and I have been asked to serve as the host." In Yangzhou, it was customary for merchants to hold night banquets that lasted all night, with dozens or even hundreds of guests attending. The wife believed him, but throughout the year, she never saw a single guest. The wife wanted to investigate her husband's movements. One evening, he left dressed in fine clothes and a hat, looking very distinguished. The wife followed him discreetly and saw him hurriedly enter a dilapidated temple. Shortly after, he returned in a simple outfit, with his hair tied in a spiral, and walked quietly through a secluded alley. He approached a high wall, took out an axe, and began chiseling away at the bricks. After a short time, the bricks fell like rotten wood, and a small hole appeared, just big enough for a person to fit through. The man stuck his head in and slithered inside. The wife quickly returned home, gathered her maids and servants, and had them all dress in men's clothing. She herself put on a high hat and fine clothes, pretending to be a night patrol officer. She had the maids and servants put on red hats and took bamboo whips with her as she left the house. When they reached the secluded alley, they waited at the base of the wall. Around the fourth watch of the night, the man emerged from the hole. The group seized and bound him, making him kneel in front of them. He dared not look up, and they beat him twenty times with the bamboo whips, lifting up his trousers afterward. They searched all around, but the officials and guards had disappeared. The man returned to the dilapidated temple, changed back into his fine clothes, and waited until dawn to knock on the door and return home.

The wife asked, "Where did you go last night?" The man replied with an account of a night banquet. She then inquired, "Did you perform any plays?" He answered, "It was the old entertainment troupe from the Hong family. They performed the complete version of *The Palace of Eternal Life*." The wife said, "I heard that last night, they only performed miscellaneous plays. The opening act was *The Swallow's Letter* and *The Dog-Hole*, and the closing act was *The Leather Boot Inspection* and *The Bamboo Broom Beating*." The maids and servants all hid their laughter. The man realized he had fallen into the wife's trap, his face flushed with embarrassment, and he dared not speak a word. The wife, angered, said, "Such behavior at night is understandable, but how could you disregard shame to such an extent? Please leave now, and do not trouble me in the future." She waved her sleeve and was about to leave, but the man pulled her back to sit for a moment. The wife pointed to the sky, gestured wildly, and scolded him harshly. The man then placed the stolen gold on the table. The wife examined it for a long time and suddenly burst into laughter, saying, "To gain a little at the expense of a lot, it seems worth it.
