From now on, follow me day and night, and I will not find fault with you!" Later, when the man was caught for stealing the gold, he was imprisoned and died. The wife fled with all her possessions, and no one knew where she went.

铎曰：“在坟墓旁乞讨食物的人，不是个好丈夫。而家中庭院里有人哭泣，却是在嘲笑，这说明家里有贤惠的妻子！这个女人先是哭后是笑，心里藏着祸心，等到她把箱子翻倒，把珠子都扔出去，丈夫遭受毒害，难道不应该惩罚她吗？因此，王孺仲之所以能保持高尚的操守，很大程度上是因为妻子的支持。” 村姬毒舌 内姑丈陈公永斋，在己丑年成为天下第一的状元，得到假期后返回南方。行至甜水铺时，旁边有一个小村庄，绿树成荫，野棠花盛开，他看到后感到非常高兴。于是独自漫步，忘记了路程的远近。 当走到村子尽头时，他看到一排竹篱笆，左边有一扇黑色的门，一位女子斜倚在门上，手里抓着风中的柳絮，咯咯地笑着。陈公看她一眼，心神荡漾，于是上前搭话。女子既不生气也不回答，只是喊她母亲出来。不久，一位驼背的老妇人走出来说，问女儿在做什么。女儿说：“不知道从哪里来的一个粗鲁男子，烦死了。”陈公感到尴尬，便谎称要讨口水喝。老妇人说：“这么小的碗哪里能坐下客人。小慧，去拿一盏凉水来！”女子大声答应后进来了。陈公问：“令爱今年多大了？”老妇人说：“只记得她出生那年是虎年，不知道现在多大了！”又问：“女婿是谁？”老妇人说：“我年纪大了，只有一个女儿，留在身边照顾我，不想让她去侍候别人。”陈公说：“女孩子有了家，留在身边不是长久之计。”

The maid took cool water and came, overhearing my words, and loudly told the old woman: "This guest does not have good intentions; do not talk much!" The old woman laughed and said, "If you wish to listen, then listen; it is truly up to me. Why do you have to be so trivial, servant girl?" Chen then praised the top imperial examination candidate to entice her. The old woman pondered for a long time and said, "What is a top imperial examination candidate?" Chen replied, "By studying and becoming a Jinshi (a high-ranking scholar-official), being named the top on the golden list, entering the imperial academy and overseeing imperial edicts, beautifying the country with articles, and being the number one person in the world, one is called a top imperial examination candidate." The old woman asked, "Do not know how often the number one person appears?" Chen said, "Every three years." The maid, from the side, slightly smiled and said, "I thought a top imperial examination candidate was the number one person for all time, but it turns out only one appears every three years! Such a person also talks incessantly to people; it is indeed a strange thing!" The old woman scolded, "Little demonic servant with a sharp tongue, always finding fault with others." The maid said, "What business is it of yours, the foolish child brings illness upon himself!" She smiled and left.

Chen remained lost in thought for a long time, then said to the old woman, "If you do not disdain it, I respectfully leave a small gift." He took two pieces of gold from his bag and gave them to her. The old woman touched and smelled the gold several times, saying, "It has no fragrance, and when held, it feels cold. What is this thing?" Chen replied, "This is called gold. If you have it, you can make clothes for cold and food for hunger. It is truly a treasure of the world!" The old woman said, "Our family has a hundred mulberry trees and half a qing of land, so we are not worried about cold and hunger. I fear it has no use here. Let the scholar keep it for his own use." She threw it to the ground and said, "What a pity for the foolish boy! He completely lacks any refined appearance and merely uses wealth and power to intimidate people!" After speaking, she closed the door and went inside. Chen stood there in a daze for a while, then sighed and returned.

The Bell said, "Young people with abundant gold and powerful officials—how many men throughout history have had their proud spirits ground down by these things, who would have thought there would be such a witty person in the inner chambers! Under the pomegranate skirt, one should kneel three thousand times!" Dipped Woman, Ice Heart Ouyang Xiu Zhu, a poor girl from Pingjiang, was closest friends with Gao Xiucan's sister Shu Sun. Shu Sun had been promised to the Zhou family but became a widow before marrying, and her brother ordered her to remain loyal at home. Ouyang Xiu Zhu's husband died after a wrestling match, and her father forced her to remarry. After returning home, she still visited Shu Sun. Shu Sun's brother, who was upright and stubborn, scolded her, saying, "A woman who has remarried, do not enter my house! And there is a virtuous woman in the inner chambers, do not lead others to immorality with your licentious ways!" Ouyang Xiu Zhu wept and said, "I grew up in a humble home and also knew the rules of the inner chambers. Only because my father was left without support, I could not preserve my chastity while fulfilling filial duty. My lack of virtue is due to fate!" Gao replied, "The pot is already broken, yet she still boasts of being whole, it's just pretending, how can it be valued?" Ouyang Xiu Zhu was speechless and left. From then on, her anger turned into resentment, and she died within a month.

Shu Sun lived with his brother's family, feeling gloomy and unhappy, and gradually becoming thin and weak. On the brink of death, he saw Xiu Zhu standing by the bed. Shu Sun asked, "Is my sister coming to guide me away?" Xiu Zhu replied, "No, that is not the case! Before, due to being reprimanded by my brother in the courtyard, I died of anger. Now, my living soul has already wandered in the wilderness, and I want to temporarily attach myself to your body, to take over the thirty years of hardship you have endured. To make it known that my previous unfaithfulness was due to my father's command, not because I wanted to be a wife of Hejian." Shu Sun said, "If so, then my unfinished matters in life, relying on your support, even if I die, what regret would there be?" After speaking, he smiled and passed away. His brother and family members surrounded and cried. Suddenly, his body leaped up and said, "Prepare the mourning attire, prepare a plain carriage, go to the Zhou family to uphold your vows." The brother suspected that the wandering soul was not settled, so he pretended to agree, but the woman was very anxious and impatient. Unable to refuse, he reached the Zhou family, and they carried her away.

The Woman of the Zhou Family Upon entering the Zhou family, the woman was in a state of tears and disheveled hair, refraining from applying makeup. Once, while carrying a jar to draw water, a neighbor's son envied her beauty. Upon returning, he immediately held a knife to her face, instantly destroying her beauty. Day and night, she cleaned the broth, carrying plates and basins to serve her in-laws. Thus, her reputation for chastity and filial piety spread throughout the village. The old man pitied her and chose a worthy boy from the clan to be her heir. She supervised him to study, diligently spinning and weaving to provide for his education. Her heart was weary and her body exhausted, enduring thirty years without a smile. Later, when her son studied at the school, he requested an imperial edict in honor of his mother's chastity. The woman urgently stopped him and said, "To serve as a minister with loyalty, to serve as a son with filial piety, to serve as a wife with chastity, are all matters within one's duty, why go to such lengths?" The prefect heard of this and praised her resolve, presenting a plaque with music and drums. On that day, relatives from both families filled the door to congratulate her. The woman alone called her brother into the inner room and asked him, "How do my actions compare to those of Zhang's daughter?" Her brother said, "She is an unclean woman, speaking of her is a stain on one's mouth, how could she be compared to you?" The woman replied, "Ah! Brother, you truly lack the ability to judge people, that's what they call judging heroes by their success or failure!" Her brother said, "What do you mean?" The woman said, "Zhang's daughter was compelled by her father's command, hence she could not maintain her integrity. If she were in my position, she would also end up with a clean record!" Her brother laughed and said, "You are biased, so you say such things. Brother, you cannot force an agreement." The woman said, "If as you say, will I be a chaste woman and Xiu Zhu an unchaste one?" Her brother replied, "Yes."

The woman spoke with a sigh, saying, "Narrow-minded and timid, you cannot be trusted. Let me tell you the truth—I am actually Xiu Zhu! My previous words were not understood, and I died in anger and injustice. Therefore, I borrowed a daughter's body to clarify my original intention, to make known the broken pot of necessity, which was never different from being whole. From now on, do not consider it as just putting on a brave face to laugh it off!" Her brother was stunned into silence. She said, "In the past with your younger sister, we were as close as family. Now, I happily preserved her chastity, not disgraced in the afterlife. The matter is finished. Please let me go from here. I wish to keep it secret forever, to preserve your lady's good name!" The official concluded, and she gathered her composure and closed her eyes, sat upright, and passed away. Her brother fell to the ground and bowed, saying, "I was wrong! I was wrong! I dare not use this to judge others!" Then he sighed and left, telling both parties and relatives, all called it a strange matter. Later, Feng the historian compiled the *Jie Xiao Zhuan*, still naming her Shu Sun, following Xiu Zhu's wishes.

铎曰：“已舍所天，而为人守不着痛痒之节，倘所谓李代桃僵者欤？然孀帏赍志，则生死而死生，泉路明心，则白玷而玷白。君子哀其志，亦谅其心矣！” The geomancer said, "Having abandoned one's rightful place and serving others without regard for the feelings or concerns of others, is this not akin to the idiom 'a peach tree being replaced by a plum tree'? Yet, a widow carries on her husband's unfulfilled aspirations, and life and death, death and life, are intertwined. In the realm of the dead, the clarity of one's heart may lead to the tainting of the pure. The gentleman mourns her aspirations and also understands her heart." 地师身后劫 The geomancer's trials after death 豫章王晋，清明日挈眷上冢。冢后旧有荒坟，低土平洼，棺木败录，末识谁氏。王有儿昭庆，见其地野花盛开，戏往摘之，踏棺陷足，骸骨碎折，惊而大号。王抱之出。 Prince Jin of Yuzhang, on the Qingming Festival, took his family to visit the ancestral graves. Behind the tomb, there was an old, neglected grave, with low-lying, flat, and possibly waterlogged ground. The coffin was decaying, and it was not known whose grave it was. The prince had a son named Zhaoqing, who saw wildflowers blooming in the area and playfully went to pick them. He stepped on the coffin, sinking into it, and the bones broke, startling him into a loud cry. The prince carried him out. 既而归家，儿寒热交作，王就床头抚视。儿忽色变，怒目直视曰：“吾罗汉章，堪舆大名家也。生前轩冕贵人无不奉为上客，尔一式微寒族，辄纵乳臭小儿，践我坟墓，躏我骸骨，罪何可宥！”王急谢罪，许以超荐。曰：“此恨已入骨髓，必索其命乃止。”王伏地哀泣，终无回意。不得已，保福于都城隍庙。 Afterward, they returned home. The son experienced alternating chills and fever, and the prince comforted him by the bedside. Suddenly, the son's expression changed, and he glared directly at the prince, saying, "I am Luo Han Zhang, a renowned master of feng shui. Before my death, high-ranking officials and nobles all revered me as a guest. You, a humble and lowly family, allowed a young, inexperienced child to desecrate my grave and bones. What sin can be forgiven?" The prince quickly apologized and promised to perform a ritual to elevate Luo Han Zhang's spirit. Luo Han Zhang said, "This hatred has penetrated to the bone, and I will not rest until I take your life." The prince prostrated himself and wept in sorrow, but remained resolute and did not change his mind. Reluctantly, he sought protection and blessings at the city's guardian temple.

At night, Wang dreamed of the city god summoning him away, saying, "You did not strictly discipline your son, and thus you suffered this misfortune. However, the fierce ghost arbitrarily exercised power and favor, violating the laws of the underworld." The city god ordered the arrest of Luo. Not long after, a ghost arrived, with a gaping mouth and constricted neck, certainly not a good person. The city god asked him why he was causing harm. The ghost spoke fluently and defended himself, unending in words. The city god then asked what his profession had been in life. The ghost replied, "Geomancer." The city god slammed the table in anger and said, "Since you were a geomancer in life, how could you not choose a good place to protect your decaying bones? It seems you did not understand this matter very well. In life, you were nothing but fooling around with earth and selling land, harming countless households. Today, your bones are broken and your remains are scattered, truly a retribution, not the fault of your son!" The ghost vigorously defended that it was not so. Not long after, many ghosts below the steps came to report, some saying that he was buried like a roosting chicken, injuring his remains; others saying that the black tortoise hid the head and the azure dragon lacked feet, destroying his descendants; still others saying that he had grandly talked about dragons to his descendants, resulting in the coffin remaining unburied for fifty to sixty years. The city god suddenly changed color and said, "Committing numerous evils, deserving no mercy!" He ordered the ghost to be taken to the Village of the Evil Dogs, where he would suffer boundless terror and pain. The crowd all cheered in approval, bowing and dispersing. The city god told Wang, "Fortunately, he has his own retribution. Otherwise, your son would also not be without sin. The righteous guidance, which must be strictly followed in the future!" Wang bowed and thanked him before leaving. Falling down the steps, he suddenly woke up. Looking at his son, he was speaking and laughing as before, and the illness was already cured.

Upon hearing that the decayed bones in the coffin were being chewed by wild dogs, scattered all over the ground, I finally realized that the punishment for the Village of Evil Dogs was immediate in this life, and the underworld did not have a specific hell for such crimes. I sighed for many days. Duo said: "In the fields, souls find peace, and by the lakes, bones are properly laid to rest. Mountains and rivers cannot speak; originally, one must rely on geomancers for guidance. However, if one uses this knowledge for personal gain, then even the famous geomantic tools like Dong Jia's Jade Measure and Guo's Brocade Bag, along with various geomantic texts such as 'Qingwu' and 'Chihuo,' are all written with sin! I fear that even dogs and pigs would not eat the rest. The punishment for the Village of Evil Dogs is still lenient."

The Admonition of the Virtuous Mother at Her Death A woman from Jingxi, at the age of seventeen, married into an official family. After half a year, she became a widow and gave birth to a son after a long pregnancy. She raised her child and maintained her virtue, living to be over eighty years old, with numerous grandchildren and great-grandchildren. On her deathbed, she summoned the wives of her grandchildren and great-grandchildren, who stood around her bed in attendance. She said, "I have one word to impart; you all must listen respectfully." They replied, "Yes." She continued, "You, as my daughters-in-law, may hope to live together for a hundred years, which is indeed a blessing for our family. But if you are unfortunate enough to be widowed at a young age, if you can bear it, then you should keep it. Otherwise, you should report it to the elders and proceed with remarriage; it is also a great convenience." They were startled and thought it was a foolish command from an old person. She smiled and said, "Do you think my words are wrong? The two words 'widowhood' are hard to express. I am someone who has experienced this. Let me tell you about past events." They listened attentively. She said, "When I was widowed, I was just eighteen years old. Because I was born into a distinguished family and married into an official family, and there was an unborn child in my womb, I dared not have other thoughts. However, the morning winds and night rains, the cold walls and lonely lamps, were quite difficult to endure. My father-in-law had a nephew from his sister's side who came to visit from Suzhou and stayed in an outer guesthouse. I peeked at his beautiful appearance from behind the screen and unconsciously felt moved."

At night, when her husband and wife were asleep, she intended to go to meet him. She moved the lamp out of the house, bowed her head in shame, and turned back to enter again. Her heart was restless and uncontrollable, so she moved the lamp out once more. Finally, she decided that this was shameful and sighed deeply before returning. This happened several times. Later, she finally decided to go away. Hearing the kitchen maid whispering to herself, she held her breath and returned to her room, placing the lamp on the table, tired and dozing off. She dreamed of going to the outer quarters, where he was reading by the lamp. They met and shared their innermost feelings. They faced each other, holding hands, and entered the bed. One person sat cross-legged in the tent, with disheveled hair and a bloody face, beating the pillow and crying loudly. Seeing it, she realized it was her deceased husband and shouted, waking up. At that time, the lamp on the table was faintly glowing with a bluish-green light, and the watchtower was striking the third drum. The child was crying for milk in the quilt. At first, she was startled, then she felt sad, and finally, she deeply regretted her actions. A kind of childish sentiment, not knowing where it had gone. From then on, she cleansed her heart and thoughts, and began to be a virtuous and chaste wife. If it were not for the kitchen maid's interruption, and if there had been no bad dream in the tent, could she have kept herself pure all her life, without disgracing those below? Therefore, she knew how difficult it is to remain a widow, and not to force oneself into it. She ordered her son to write this down, to be passed down as family law, and died with a smile.

In the later generations, the descendants of this family multiplied, and each generation had virtuous women who remained chaste after the death of their husbands; occasionally, there were women who remarried. For over a hundred years, the family maintained a reputation for purity and propriety, with no scandalous or improper behavior. Duo said, "Zhuo Wenjun's elopement with Sima Xiangru is still remembered with disapproval, and it may have been due to Zhuo Wangsun's demand that she remain a widow. This story can fill a gap in the teachings about women's conduct. In the past, Fan Zhongyan followed his mother to marry into the Zhu family, and after his eldest son Fan Chunhu died, his daughter-in-law also remarried Wang Tao. The Song Confucians were most strict about ritual and law, so why did no one criticize Fan Zhongyan at the time? It is better to maintain one's virtue openly than to fall into improper behavior secretly! The stories of the official at the carriage and the white-named noble of the Song Dynasty are even more remarkable as rare events in history!"

**A Round Light Above the Head** Wang Jun, also known as Kuipu, in his youth, accompanied two or three close friends on a journey to Mount Huang. They climbed vines and grasped creepers; when they reached the middle of the mountain, the slanting sun was about to set, and the twilight was dark and gloomy. His friends dared not stay any longer, but Wang, in a fit of pride, continued alone. After walking several dozen steps, the sky suddenly grew dark, the moon was obscured by heavy clouds, thunder roared, and a sudden downpour began, with lightning flashing intermittently. Amidst the storm, he followed the path upward. He arrived at a stone cave, which was about one zhang (approximately 3.3 meters) wide and several tens of xun (approximately 8 meters) high. The two walls shone like candles, and there was an old monk sitting alone with hanging eyebrows. Wang hurried to pay his respects, and the old monk merely nodded slightly before closing his eyes and entering a state of meditation. Wang leaned against the wall and waited, when suddenly, a round light appeared above the monk's head. It manifested as a person in a golden helmet and iron armor, holding a spear that was eight chi long (approximately 2.4 meters), with countless small heads hanging above. As Wang was startled, the helmet's top revealed a yellow dog, which bent its hind legs to kneel like a person and brought its front paws together like hands in prayer, as if paying respects to the Buddha. After a while, the dog grew tired and lay down. Then, above the dog's head, a high-ranking official appeared, wearing a bamboo staff and a red robe, with a tall hat and broad sash. His sleeves were filled with gold and silver, spreading out, and he had a look of joy. After a while, the official's head revealed a woman, who drew her eyebrows and painted her eyes, definitely not from a good family. She took off her red and blue striped robe, revealing a free and easy robe, held a fan in her right hand, and held a mala (prayer beads) in her left hand, making a sound. She took a cushion and placed it on the official's head, sitting cross-legged in a dignified manner.

On the woman's head, another infant appeared, adorned with jade rings and earrings, resembling a well-bred child of a noble family. On the infant's head, a sudden sound was heard, and a person emerged, bald with missing teeth, looking remarkably like the old monk. The figures stacked on top of each other, resembling a seven-story pagoda, stood layer upon layer. Wang looked up intently. After a short while, the person resembling the old monk gradually shrank and fell into the infant's head. The infant flipped and fell back onto the woman's head, then disappeared silently. The woman picked up a fan, lifted a cushion, and struck the official's head, causing his brain to spill out like a bee entering a hole. The official quickly called his dog, which pushed its head against the woman. The official rubbed his head three times and suddenly extended his foot. The dog stood on its hind legs, and its front paws fell into the general's helmet. The general grabbed the dog's head and entered sideways. The general became furious, holding a spear and striking the monk's head, causing a sudden opening. The monk escaped through the opening. The circular light on the earrings suddenly disappeared. The old monk looked at Wang with a smile and said, "Disturbance in meditation, another thousand-year calamity has occurred!"

The Old Monk said, “This is the result of my past karma. In my first life, I was General Bai Qi of Wu'an. During the Battle of Yique, I beheaded twenty-four thousand enemies, and at the Battle of Changping, I captured and killed forty thousand Zhao soldiers, and then buried several thousand surrendered troops alive. The King of Yama was furious and sent me through the six realms of rebirth, where I endured terrifying sufferings. By the Tang dynasty, I was reborn on the same day as Li Linfu. He was reborn as a cow, and I as a dog. Reflecting on my past karma, even though I had fallen into the animal realm, I devoted myself to Buddhism. The King of Yama was pleased and allowed me to be reborn as a high-ranking official during the Song dynasty, becoming Jia Sidao. Wearing the official robes, I lost my true nature; I built the Treasure Pavilion, accepted bribes widely, and mismanaged the state. After being killed at Mucian Temple, I was sent to the underworld. The King of Yama then became furious and said, ‘A corrupt official seeking gold is no different from a prostitute loving money; punish him to be a courtesan!’ In the late Ming dynasty, I was reborn as Bian Yujing. Later, guided by the enlightened monk Huizhi, I purified my heart and changed my ways, becoming a female Taoist for seventeen years. During the Flower Dust Eon, I suddenly attained enlightenment and vowed to be reborn as a man. Due to impurities in my previous life, I underwent a transformation in the realm of premature death, being reborn into the household of the Minister of Revenue in Jiangdong, named Ashou, who died at the age of seven. Now, I have finally entered the Buddhist path, devoutly cultivating good karma. Through several lifetimes, I have followed the shadow of my karma, so do not say that I am playing games on the edge of the Five Streams, for it is not a mere pastime of the heavenly demons!”

Wang was greatly surprised and astonished, bade farewell to the old monk and went down the mountain. He told his close friends about the experience and returned to the place to trace the event, but the stone steps were covered in clouds, and he finally lost track of where the place was. Duo said, "The story of the scholar in the goose cage is fantastical, but what is its significance for awakening the world? This must be the manifestation of a tall, golden Buddha figure, creating a depiction of the eighteen layers of hell, teaching the path to enlightenment in the city of Gaya to good men!" The *Lengyan Sutra* states, "Spirits, demons, and other supernatural beings all come in countless numbers during deep meditation." It is known that spirits and supernatural beings can change form, not solely due to past sins. However, even if the event did not happen, one should still view it in this way. Wang Shixiu, a student of Duo, recorded this. **Immediate Consequences of Killing** A certain magistrate of Pucheng, his family abstained from killing. However, his wife was cruel and violent, taking pleasure in slaughtering living beings. It was the magistrate's birthday, and he ordered the cook to prepare the feast in advance. In the kitchen, pigs and sheep were lined up, chickens and geese were gathered in groups, stretching their necks and mournfully crying, all about to die. The magistrate felt pity for them and said to his wife, "It is your birthday, but they are in the place of death. Since the Buddha is compassionate, I ask you to accumulate good karma." The wife scolded, "If one follows Buddhism, prohibits sexual relations and killing, then in a few decades, humans would be extinct, and the world would be full of animals! Don't say such foolish things." Knowing he couldn't persuade her, the magistrate sighed and left.

The Lady closed the door and took a daytime nap, unaware that she had entered the kitchen. There, she saw the cook sharpening knives with a whistling sound, and the maids and servants stood in a circle watching. Suddenly, her soul merged with a pig. The cook stepped forward, tied the pig's four legs, lifted it and placed it on a white wooden stool, held its head, and stabbed a sharp knife into its throat. Blood flowed out in abundance, and the pain penetrated to the heart and lungs. With a loud sound, the pig fell into boiling water. The cook plucked feathers and scraped off dirt, leaving almost every inch of skin gone. Then, the cook cut from the neck to the belly, causing extreme pain and unbearable suffering. The soul chased the liver and intestines, bursting out at once. Feeling aimless and homeless, the soul merged with a sheep. The sheep was extremely terrified and screamed wildly. The maids and servants laughed mockingly, and no one came to rescue. The cruelty of the slaughter was even more severe than that of the pig. Then, the cook cut chickens and killed ducks, and all of which were experienced by the soul. Secretly seeing the widespread slaughter, the frightened soul gradually calmed down. An old servant brought a golden carp, and the soul merged again. One maid laughed and said, "The lady loves this very much. You quickly chop it into fish balls to prepare for the evening meal." The cook removed the scales and liver, cut off the head and tail, and chopped the carp finely on the cutting board. Each cut caused pain, as if turned into countless bodies, suffering being flayed like fish scales. The soul screamed desperately and woke up after some time. A young maid said, "The fish balls are ready. Please have the evening meal." The lady immediately ordered it to be removed, recalled the terrifying scene, and sweat dripped down.

The next day, the official instructed the banquet to be canceled. The official asked in detail about the matter, and the lady fully described her previous dream. The official laughed and said, "You have never been devout to Buddhism. If you do not suffer various troubles, how could you put down the butcher's knife?" The lady also laughed. From this day forward, they abstained from meat and ate only vegetarian food, jointly observing the precept against killing. The bell chimed, "Thousands of chicken feet and tens of thousands of sheep heads, one tongue brings about the end of many lives. This family's food, with the finest mushrooms and bamboo shoots as the main dishes, and dishes named after Zongshu's pig liver and Xiaoyi's salted fish, are all sinners in the Buddhist path! To prohibit men and women and to abstain from killing, examining its adverse effects, the celestial beings and disciples of the Buddha should also have no words to respond. It is unknown whether the teachings of Confucius are for the wise and intelligent, and the teachings of Buddhism are for the foolish and unworthy. For the wise and intelligent, the teachings start with husband and wife; fishing without a net, shooting without aiming at roosting birds, making people recognize the principles in matters of sex and killing. For the foolish and unworthy, the teachings only provide the precept of abstention. In the *Lengyan Sutra*, it is said that chewing on a fly while lying down; in the *Chuan Deng Lu* (Record of the Transmission of the Lamp), it is said that offering a plum as a sacrifice. In essence, the true meaning of Buddhism from the West is not here. The wife of the Grand Master of Rites did not have a harmonious life, and it is not seen that she ascended to the lotus platform after eating vegetarian food once. However, Kumārajīva was allowed to marry, and a monk in Dengzhou ate pigeon meat from the kitchen. Reciting a verse about the heavenly bed and earth cover by Li Dan Tian, the intentions of Confucius and the Buddha in setting up their teachings, is there any difference?"

Lustful Demons Behind the Head At Qixia Temple, the Zen Master Huotang, a successor in the transmission of the Lamp, taught. I went to him and asked to study the Great Vehicle Dharma. The master said, "Your lustful demons disturb you daily; how can you lead them to the shore of enlightenment?" I replied, "I read Confucian texts as a child and have long been devoted to pure practices. Though I enjoy the elegance of literature, I have the demon of writing, but not of lust." The master said, "Do you not know? The lustful demon is a transformation of the demon of writing. If you have the strength of concentration, you can still repent and cleanse your habitual tendencies." He placed a meditation cushion on the seat and instructed me to sit cross-legged. He told me to lower my eyebrows and close my eyes, and to remain still. After about two hours, I suddenly felt my brain open, and dozens of figures in light pink and dark green appeared, linked arm in arm. At first, they smiled at each other, but then they crowded and teased me in countless ways. I sat motionless, unable to move. Gradually, I heard them whispering, "He has named and sought us out, yet he has forgotten us behind his head?" I could no longer bear it and shouted, "Where have you met me? How can you say I have named and sought you out?" They smiled and confessed. One said, "I am Zheng Yunu from the legendary *The Story of Repaying Kindness*." Another said, "I am Sun Fujie from the legendary *The Fortune of the Talented Person*." A third said, "I am Li Yingniang from the legendary *The Golden House*." Then came others who called themselves Yaoying, Zifeng, Meilan, and Xiuqin, all borrowing names from the traditional categories of female performers.

The speaker said, "This is just empty talk, how could there be you?" The others replied, "The spirit of writing can transcend the mundane and reach the realm of the divine. Thus, the famous 'Ode to the Astonishing Swallow' captures the essence of the goddess Luo Shen, and the few words of 'The Dream of the Gao Tang Mountain' lead to a dream encounter. Who says Tao Yuanming's detached and tranquil state of mind does not actually stir desires in the heart? Please turn around, do not be ashamed in front of us." The speaker remembered the teacher's words and sat motionless as before. The others laughed and said, "This child has a mouth but no heart, it is enough to criticize him behind his back, there is no need to tarnish his true appearance." They then focused intently and said, "No wonder you won't turn around, you must have gained a pair of wise eyes, and he saw through you." After they spoke, they transformed into scraps of paper, blowing away in the wind, and the speaker's eyes closed. The teacher said, "Fortunately, you have some mental discipline. Otherwise, the demon of writing can be exorcised, but the demon of lust cannot be avoided!" The teacher then remained seated and helped the speaker confess and cleanse his verbal karma. Upon returning home, the speaker burned his song sheets, not daring to use flowery language from the entertainment world, fearing that it might suggest hidden faults in his life.

The bell said, "The Confucianists have a method of correcting mistakes, and the Buddhists have a method of repentance. Is this statement about correcting mistakes? Or is it about repentance? May all the wise people in the world certify this for me." In the ninth month of the year of the Rabbit, I chanced upon the Qixia Mountain Temple and saw a poem inscribed on the wall by my teacher, which reads: "Fold your hands in prayer, listen to me confess my life. I am a foolish man from the Three Wu regions, having gained a false reputation in my youth. First, I developed a habit of reading; second, I formed a habit of loving flowers. As a side interest, I composed new melodies. Therefore, I fell into a worldly dream, unable to be awakened by a slap or a shout. Relying on the Buddha, applying divine power, turning the golden wheel, from now on, I do not recognize a single character, reading the *Book of Cattle* backwards. Meeting with Jutu and Momu, the earth forbids poetry and music, nowhere to meet with welcome or acceptance. After ten years of facing the wall, I accompany and serve in the celestial palace." Is this poem from before enlightenment? Then it is the Confucian method of correcting mistakes. Is this poem from after enlightenment? Then it is the Buddhist method of repentance. I ask my teacher about this, what do you think?

受业郏镕谨志 清。沈起凤。谐铎。卷十 道人神相 A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. A Man with Two Heads and Four Arms A man from Jiangyin, a city known for its wealth, was over forty years old and had no son. After a year, he finally had a son. One day, a Taoist hermit approached his door, and the gatekeeper shouted at and stopped him. The commotion reached the inner room. The elderly man came out to inquire. The Taoist said, "I am a rustic from the mountains, and I have heard of your name for a long time. I have come to pay my respects, but I only wish to enjoy a drink. Why was I so harshly rejected by the gatekeeper?" The elderly man invited him in and ordered his servant to prepare the wine. The Taoist drank over thirty cups of wine without showing any signs of intoxication. The elderly man was amazed and asked, "Do you have any secret arts? Can you teach me?" The Taoist replied, "I have no other abilities, only the skill of predicting a person's wealth, nobility, poverty, or lowliness, which is fairly accurate." The elderly man removed his hat and asked the Taoist to examine him. The Taoist studied him closely for a long time and said, "Your entire body is covered in ordinary bones, your five facial features carry a dull aura, and you have coarse hair on your face, accumulating to several inches. This is truly the appearance of a wealthy man."

There is a clear qi on the forehead, deeply embedded in the muscles, subtly forming a hungry grave, I fear that after this, one cannot avoid hunger and cold!" The old man said, "Haha! Your words are mistaken! With my meager property, even if I do not consider the interest, closing the door and eating from it, my descendants cannot consume it all." The Taoist priest laughed and said, "This is a matter of fate, how could I know?" He then ordered everyone to observe the family, and all remained silent. Just then, a wet nurse brought the child, and the Taoist priest was startled and said, "This is the one who will ruin the family!" The old man asked, "What does he look like?" The Taoist priest replied, "According to the child's features, at twelve he will enter school, at fifteen he will pass the imperial examination, at sixteen he will become a Jinshi, and he will serve in the Hanlin Academy. Early success, I fear he will not live long." The old man said, "If this is the case, he is a capable heir, how can one say he will ruin the family?" The Taoist priest said, "Talent and wealth are mutually destructive. The reason you possess a million while sitting idle is because for five or six generations, none of your ancestors could read a single character. Now you have a son who can write articles and enter the Hanlin Academy, I fear that the hundred-foot copper mountain will turn into nothing."

The old man casually replied, and the Taoist monk also left. As the child grew older, the father invited renowned teachers to educate him. He read the Five Classics, the *Records of the Grand Historian*, and the *Book of Han*, and understood everything he read each day; while the father daily held abacus beads and calculated, achieving no profit at all, incurring losses amounting to tens of thousands over four to five years. When the child was twelve, he indeed entered the local school; the father's pawnshop was burned down, incurring losses amounting to tens of thousands. After three years, the child was selected as a filial and upright official; the father bought seven foreign ships, all of which sank in the sea, and his relatives and associates sued in court, selling their fertile land, and through bribes, they were spared. The following year, the child passed the imperial examination and was appointed as a scholar-official; when the official announcement arrived, the father and his wife and concubines had already rented a dilapidated house. Still hoping that the child would bring honor to the family and restore their fortunes, not even half a year later, the father died in office, and the entire family died of cold and hunger. The Taoist monk's words were completely verified. The author said: "Names on the imperial examination list, gold at the foot of the bed is gone. The imperial examination list, the first talisman to ban the god of wealth. Hoping the son would manage the family affairs, willing to go bankrupt, ultimately ended up in a ditch without regret, the father is also a hero."

**The Benevolent Monk** Zhen Sheng of Taihe was in his early twenties, strikingly handsome, yet destitute, with no place to stand, and so he resided in the eastern quarter of Zhaotidi Temple. At that time, a strange monk arrived from the western courtyard, possessing extraordinary abilities. Zhen Sheng visited him privately and lamented his poverty. The monk said, "What is the problem with a scholar being poor? Is it not better to be poor and pure than rich and corrupt?" Zhen Sheng earnestly asked for advice. The monk replied, "If you wish to become rich, why not die quickly?" Zhen Sheng angrily retorted, "I wish to survive at all costs, so I beg you, Master, for your mercy! How can I dare to seek life, only to be given death?" The monk laughed and said, "Not fearing death is the way to wealth. Since you value your life, it is fitting that you remain poor!" He placed his hand on Zhen Sheng's head and waved him away. Zhen Sheng returned home and immediately fell ill, on the verge of death. Remembering the monk's words, he returned once more to the western courtyard. The monk said, "Your desire for wealth has not died; I shall let you experience a taste of suffering." He placed Zhen Sheng into his left sleeve and emerged. At that time, a certain wealthy man from a great household, who was extremely ugly in appearance yet possessed a fortune of ten thousand, had his concubines and attendants exhausted from constant service. He had contracted a disease of thirst, his breath as thin as a thread, lying on his deathbed. His family surrounded him, weeping in grief. Suddenly, the eccentric monk arrived from outside and said, "Do not grieve—I can save him!" The family prostrated themselves on the ground in reverence. The monk unfastened his right sleeve and made a gesture toward one direction, then wiped the man's face with his left sleeve, and laughed loudly before departing. Not long after, the man suddenly jumped up, looked around at all his concubines as if he did not recognize them, questioned each one in turn, and inquired about his household affairs. The family believed his soul was unsettled, hence his confusion, and quickly administered ginseng medicine to help him. In fact, it was Zhen Sheng himself.

Zhen Sheng was fortunate to be a wealthy man, and he kept it deeply secret. During the day, he wore fine clothes and enjoyed delicious food, sitting in the inner hall to account for his land and property, displaying gold and silver on the table, playing with them. Sometimes he rode a spirited horse, accompanied by handsome servants, roaming through the flower-lined streets and willow-lined alleys. At night, he was surrounded by concubines, and when the urge for pleasure arose, he would cover the seven-foot coral bed with a quilt made of Xijiang brocade, left and right adorned with hairpins and powder, holding a round gathering. He thought that the blessings he had never experienced in his previous life, he enjoyed them all here. One day, he looked in the mirror and saw that his appearance was very strange and ugly, not like the graceful and charming person he had been before. He felt quite dissatisfied. He secretly met a monk at the temple, first thanking him, then revealing his inner turmoil. The monk said, "You have sought wealth and obtained it, your wish is fulfilled! Still wanting to return to your original face in the midst of sound, color, goods, and profit?" Behind the screen, he called out a disciple. Looking at him, it was Zhen Sheng himself! He asked for his name, but the disciple smiled and did not answer, only saying, "A wet cloth robe, please put it on for me!" The monk clapped his hands and laughed. Zhen Sheng also suddenly realized, and immediately took the vows to become a monk under him. Later, he followed the wandering monk to Wuzuzu Mountain, and no one knows what happened to him. Duo said, "Without entering the sea of suffering, how can one know the other shore? These eight million and four thousand Buddhist disciples are all people who have come from the field of烦恼." A monk with a yellow face and bald head, who has not firmly planted his feet on the ground, and preaches everywhere, is not he who is dreaming?

**Hui Gu County** Dai Li, the grandson of Guan Zhai, was a man of bold and unrestrained character, careless about his appearance. He loved to read the *Classic of Mountains and Seas* and other works such as the *Records of Searching for Spirits* and the *Records of Strange Things*. One snowy day, he was drunk and sleeping at noon when a high-ranking official arrived with an imperial decree, saying, "The Lord of the County has summoned you; please prepare your carriage immediately." Dai did not ask who the official was and dressed himself neatly before going out. Outside, he saw a servant holding the reins of a small horse suitable for riding under fruit trees, ready to go. Dai leaped onto the saddle, and the official led him away. They arrived at a pavilion where Dai dismounted and rested briefly. Before the pavilion, he saw a stream of clear, emerald water with thousands of lotus flowers blooming, their delicate petals reflecting on the surface. Dai remarked, "How can there be such lotus flowers in such a severe winter?" The official replied, "This is the beginning of autumn here!" Dai scoffed at the idea, and the official laughed and said, "You are a scholar from Central China, truly seeing little and being easily surprised! Let me explain the basics to you." Dai nodded. The official said, "Our county is over forty-seven thousand li away from Central China, and it is called Hui Gu County. We count time by the sun, with morning being spring, noon being summer, evening being autumn, and night being winter. We do not keep written records of years, but we mark time by the changing seasons and plants. Now, the lotus flowers are blooming, which is the beginning of autumn here, but it is after noon in Central China." Dai was greatly astonished and wanted to ask more questions. The official, suddenly alarmed, said, "While talking with you, the northern wind has already turned cold!" Dai looked back and indeed saw that all the lotus flowers had fallen, and outside the pavilion, several ancient plum trees were budding, their blossoms gradually emerging to withstand the snow. The official urged him to continue, and they remounted and left.

Seeing a city, its signboard read: "Yan Nian." The men and women’s attire was somewhat similar to that of the Chinese, yet on their necks were all hung golden chains, which were used to pray for longevity. By then, it was already late in the evening, and he stayed overnight at an external inn. The next day, he arrived at a grand hall, where a high-ranking official accompanied Dai into the presence. The official first presented the imperial edict. The Lord of the Region said, "You were sent on a mission last summer, yet you have only returned this spring—why have you delayed so long?" The official apologized. Upon hearing this, Dai realized that one night’s stay had already felt like a year apart, and so he immediately knelt and paid respects at the ruler’s seat. The Lord of the Region rose and pulled him up, saying, "Do you know the reason I have summoned you?" Dai replied, "A humble and ignorant man like me cannot fathom Your Highness’s profound intentions. Please enlighten me." The Lord of the Region said, "I have a daughter, who has not yet found a worthy match. I admire your virtue and respectfully offer her to you as a wife." Dai prostrated himself in gratitude. At that time, a faint, fragrant breeze stirred slightly from the corner of the hall, for it was now the beginning of summer. He was ordered to bathe in the clear wave pool of the Cool-Breeze Hall, then given a robe of ice-silk and a crown of lotus flowers, and led into the Palace of Beautiful Clouds, where he was united in marriage with the princess. The sky was like brocade, the earth like embroidery; flutes and music of phoenix and unicorn filled the air. Twelve jade towers rose, and nowhere else could one find such a soul-stirring scene.

After being led into the inner palace, they saw the Princess with her hair high in a bun, adorned with a small branch of the auspicious red osmanthus. She bowed slightly and said, "The deepening of autumn is evident!" The palace maids immediately changed the attire of the groom, and they set up a banquet at the Tianxiang Pavilion. After three rounds of wine, the Princess rose, holding a cup to toast the groom, singing, "How long is a person's life? When drinking, one should sing. If one does not get drunk while singing, what is the point of such beauty?" The groom responded with the melody of "Tianxiang Guizi." The Princess smiled and said, "Do you still think it is autumn, Lord Groom?" She ordered the maids to draw the curtains, revealing icicles hanging from the eaves and snow falling on the red camellia trees. They then dismissed the banquet and lit red candles to guide them to the inner chambers. The maids gradually dispersed, urging the Princess to take her time dressing. The Princess laughed and said, "At thirty, one becomes a groom, yet you are still so impatient?" The groom smiled and replied, "In this place, you measure time by days instead of years, so a moment in spring is indeed worth a thousand gold pieces!" The Princess also laughed. They then extinguished the candles, climbed into bed, and shared their dreams under the embroidered quilt.

As soon as the morning sun rose, the palace maids reported that the crabapple blossoms had opened. The lady-in-waiting, following the order of the noblewoman, summoned the nobleman to a cherry banquet, at which all officials of the third rank and above were present. Soon, a small palace maid appeared with a tray of colorful threads, symbolizing longevity. The noblewoman immediately ordered her carriage and commanded the nobleman to join her at the Wash Horse River to watch the dragon boat races. The boats, with their ornate oars and flags, were accompanied by acrobatic performances and the music of flutes and drums. Glimpsing the willows by the river gradually turning yellow, she ordered the return journey. Along the way, the red buildings were adorned with pearl curtains raised high, and the tables were set with fruits and melons, as the young women practiced threading needles for the Qixi Festival. They stopped, pointing with smiles, and rode together slowly, the wind swirling around them. The noblewoman told the nobleman, "This truly is 'the whole city shrouded in wind and rain as the Double Ninth Festival approaches.'" They quickly rode back. Upon entering the palace, a palace maid rushed to inform them, "The noblewoman has given birth to a precious son; please attend the red-washing banquet." The noblewoman ordered the nobleman to visit his wife, who was lying on a warm bed, watching their son play with a sword and seal. Testing his cry, she found him to be a true hero, and named him Ah Ying. From then on, the nobleman spent his days in the palace, playing with his son and attending to his wife. Within a few weeks, Ah Ying had undergone the coming-of-age ceremony.

Several days later, the noblewoman passed away, and the nobleman temporarily assumed control of the government. One day, he noticed that the princess had wrinkles on her face and gray hair. The princess said, "I am growing older! Please arrange for you to take concubines." The nobleman then widely selected good families to fill the harem. At night, he sat with the princess in the鸳鸯寝, talking about past events. Suddenly, he asked, "How long have I been here?" The princess replied, "Sixty-two years." The nobleman said, "Do not jest. I remember when we first became intimate, I secretly scratched your back with my fingernail, and you hid your back and lay on your side, suddenly getting up to join me. You laughed and said, 'I wanted to protect the mountain pass, so I let you cross the Tancheng Pass.' Reflecting on this scene, it seems as if it were yesterday." The princess laughed and said, "This was two months ago, so I remember it clearly. In my eyes, it feels like I am as old as the legendary old man from the State of Jin who can remember the sexagenary cycle!"

Dai was dazed and disoriented, as if lost in thought or grief. He lowered his head and pondered deeply, suddenly missing his homeland, and thus requested to return with the princess. The princess said, "The mountains and rivers are different, and the seasons have also changed. Please return for a while, but I cannot accompany you." The next day, he entrusted the administration of the state to A Ying, packed his belongings, and prepared to return home. The princess bid him farewell at the Yichun Hall, weeping and saying, "I am now in my twilight years, and I may soon meet my end. If you do not abandon me in my old age, I wish you to come back once." She then added, "A hundred years will pass in the blink of an eye, and even if you come back, it may not make a difference!" A Ying also wept, and Dai was deeply saddened, reluctant to leave. He heard that all the court officials were waiting to see him off at the Ai Chan Station, and he had no choice but to bid farewell with tears. Upon arriving home, he found himself lying stiffly on the bed, with family members gathered around him, checking on his condition. He suddenly sat up, regaining consciousness. He asked the family, "Have I been dead for more than two months?" Dai exclaimed in shock at the strange occurrence. Due to the promise of a return, he became restless and unable to let go of the thought, often drinking excessively. Three months later, he dreamed of entering the place again. He asked the princess, who replied, "I have been dead for over eighty years. I am now buried in Cui Luo Mountain." He then asked A Ying, who said, "She has become a deity!" When he asked about the former concubines and attendants, they replied, "They are all gone!" When he met the court officials, none of them recognized him, and he returned with a heavy heart.

Awakening and sighing, he said, "A hundred years of wealth and honor are but a moment; in the world there are those who are enlightened; should they not see it this way?" Re-reading the *Shanhaijing* and the *Soushenji*, *Shuyi*, and other books, none of them mention this. He asked me to record it, to verify it with those in the world who love to talk about the fantastic. Duo said: The immortals have the method of shrinking the earth, but I have not heard of a method to shrink years. Yet, Magu's temples are half white with age, and the Green Ox Old Man has already grown old and leans on his staff. The days and months in the pot may be long, but they are but a snap of the fingers. The discussion of equating Peng and Shang is indeed not groundless. The City of Scarab Beetles Xun Sheng, courtesy name Xiaoling, had his whole body smelling of orchids, and he had the reputation of leaving fragrance for three days. Accidentally boarding a merchant ship, he drifted on the sea; suddenly, a foul wind arose, leading him to an island. Xun Sheng disembarked from the boat and went ashore; he felt a foul odor permeating, clogging his throat and pricking his nose, which was unbearable. Just as he was about to turn back, he suddenly saw an old man, accompanied by a short-haired child, coming and laughing. Seeing Xun Sheng, he was greatly startled and said, "Where is this filthy boy, sneaking into the pure land? Don't you fear that the people on the path will be frightened to death!" Xun Sheng found his smell strange and retreated three or four steps. From afar, he asked for his name. The old man also covered his nose with his hand; standing far away, he replied, "I am Copper Odor Old Man Kong. This is the name of a milky-smelling child. Because he admired the fairyland, he moved his family from the Five Filthy Villages here. He was favored by the owner of the pickled fish shop, who said that my odor is not different, and recommended me to the officials who chase odors. He was ordered to manage the keys to the north gate of the City of Scarab Beetles."

The next day, a wealthy merchant named Ma Tong invited [the protagonist] to dine at his home. They were led to a hall, which was inscribed with the words "As Fragrant as Orchids." Adjacent to it was a pavilion called "Hide Dirt," and behind the pavilion was a study room named "Tolerate Impurities." The tray on the table had no other dishes; only stale fish, spoiled meat, and pickled garlic and onions. After bathing, [the protagonist] gradually no longer noticed the smell and ate heartily. Later, [he] probed his own throat, and a foul stench spewed out. The host clapped and laughed, saying, "Good breath! Bananas and weeds can be in the same container!" Kong翁 heard of this and did not believe it, so he visited the guesthouse. When [he] saw [the protagonist], [he] was startled and said, "You truly transformed yourself into a good person. Your past foul behavior has been completely removed!" And [he] made a pact of deep friendship with [him].

The protagonist was afraid that the merchant's ship would wait too long, so he went to say goodbye to Old Master Kong. Old Master Kong set up a farewell feast for him. He led the protagonist into a back room, where he saw 36 pits filled with gold and silver, arranged in a dense row. Old Master Kong took several ingots of red gold to give to him. He also called out a woman, who was dirty and disheveled but of exceptional natural beauty. Old Master Kong laughed and said, "This is Hui Wei, the reincarnation of Xi Shi, tainted. You have no wife, why don't you take her along?" The protagonist bowed in gratitude, held the gold, and took the woman back to the boat. The merchant had missed the protagonist for half a month, anxiously waiting while the boat was anchored. When he saw the protagonist return, he was very happy. As soon as he stepped onto the boat, the foul smell was unbearable. The gold on the table was particularly foul. When Hui Wei stepped onto the boat, all the foul smells were dispelled, and everyone felt relieved. After returning home, the protagonist occasionally visited the market, where people covered their noses as they passed by. However, when he lived with Hui Wei, he did not notice the foul smell. When he tried to exchange the gold for something in the market, people got angry and threw it back at him. Three years later, Hui Wei died, and the protagonist felt unhappy and gloomy, dying while holding onto the gold.

Duo said, "The dung beetle carries dung, which people detest for its filth. Yet, when transferred to a golden pillow and thick mattress, it only hastens death! Thus, we understand that what is born of fragrance will also die of stench. The red powder is long buried, and the golden luster fades; only a stinking bag remains, which cannot be washed. Alas!" **Ghostly Indulgence** My fifth brother, Zhi Sheng, ranked first in the imperial examinations of the year Gui Mao. In the year Ding Wei, he traveled north with others on official business and stayed the night at the Fu Zhuang Post Inn. The inn was full, so they borrowed a room in the village. At that time, the moonlight seeped through the torn curtains, and the wind rustled the torn paper. Lying down, he could not fall asleep. Stepping out into the front courtyard, he turned into the back building and saw a vast wasteland garden, about three acres wide. There was an old woman with a balding head, limping under a tree, who spoke loudly, "The wind and moon tonight are quite good. Among the guests, there must be those dreaming of courtesans. Why not call them here!" Soon after, many beauties gathered. The old woman spoke with a slight anger, "You all sit in your rooms all day, gambling, cracking nuts, and being so lazy. Do you spend your long days drinking morning dew?" They all nodded and obeyed. The old woman whispered something for a long time, and the group pointed towards the southeast corner with their hands. Soon, all the guests arrived, wearing merchant clothes and scholar hats, not of the same kind. They laid out colorful carpets, sitting in a circle on the ground. The concubines moved back and forth, arranging food and delicacies, as if waiting for something. Afterwards, the wine ran out, and the laughter and conversation gradually grew tired. The old woman clapped and laughed, "The window candles have gone out! The Milky Way and the magpie bridge have been set up. The foolish cow and the silly girl are still sitting idly together!" Everyone got up. The old woman led them with a candle, and the beauties escorted the guests to a straw mat.

芷生一向以胆力闻名，便悄悄前去查看。只见里面摆着几十张竹榻，众客人各自抱着一个夜叉睡觉，鼻息声此起彼伏，红发的人依偎在肩上，血唇互相舔舐，偶尔有人枕着鬼脸在手臂上，梦中喃喃作娇柔的呼唤。正当他感到惊骇时，一个老夜叉手持一根约七寸长的铜管，向客人的脑后插入，发出呜呜的呼吸声。老夜叉摸索了几遍，最后来到一个客人面前，说：“此人没有脑子。而且全身酸痛，散发着臭气，让人实在想吐。”老夜叉揉了揉他的眼睛，将他拖到床下。芷生拍打着栏杆大声喊道：“门外有个莽汉，这老妖怪怎敢如此！”众人纷纷惊呼：“新贵人来了！”转眼间，众人全部散去。 等到天亮，芷生登上车继续上路。他看到富庄驿的众位宿客，都喊着头痛。其中有一人，眼睛肿得像桃子。询问后得知，此人是秀才，刚登记参加科举考试，打算进京谋取誊录馆的职位。芷生微微一笑，此人这次科举考试果然考中了。 铎说：“脂刀割骨，花箭穿心，一片欢场，竟是狠罗刹的汤浴！若不早回头，恐怕那毁脑之人就要来了！” 神赌 穹隆山庙，廊下有两尊神像，身着绯红袍子，腰系锦绣带，头戴乌帽，脚穿黑靴。旁边各塑有一尊夫人像，头戴珠冠，身披绣袍，俨然如同命妇。两位神像同住一院，仅隔一墙。

One night, a temple caretaker was staying in the corridor when he suddenly saw a deity from the left seat approach the right seat and say, "Tonight the night is long, and I cannot sleep, so why not play a game of dice?" The deity on the right seat laughed and said, "Swineherd! Your gambling urge has struck again? But our offerings have been sparse lately, how could we have any immediate stakes?" The deity on the left seat replied, "Let us use the counting sticks and markers, and the loser must settle the debt tomorrow. If you don't return, you should use your wife as collateral for the debt." The deity on the right seat laughed and agreed. They then used incense sticks as counting sticks, laid reeds as a playing surface, and sat facing each other. They played for about two hours. The deity on the right seat stood up and laughed, saying, "The hot-blooded one has lost. Return and rest, and tomorrow you will send your wife in a seven-scented carriage!" The deity on the left seat left dejectedly. The temple caretaker was surprised and, the next night, stayed in the corridor again. He saw the deity on the right seat approach the left seat, pressing hard for repayment, and demanding his wife. The deity's wife heard this and angrily scolded her husband, saying, "Black-hearted thief! When you were fortunate enough to get an official position through connections in the Palace of Literary Arts, you removed your hairpins and used your connections. Now, because of your gambling, you are using your wife as a last resort. Is there anyone so unfaithful in the world?" The deity on the left seat bowed his head and said nothing. The deity on the right seat pressed even harder, ranting on and on, and then cursing. Fortunately, his wife called from across the wall, and he was led away. From then on, they argued every night.

The temple attendant grew tired of it and reported to the temple director, who eventually prepared drums and music to send the left-seated lady to the right seat; the noise finally ceased. The wall that separated them was repaired and recorded. Those who knew said, "This is the new lady not forgetting her former husband." They ordered the wall-builder to leave a hole as a place to look at her husband. Since then, the wall has never collapsed again. Until today, the locals call it the Win-Lose Temple, and those who love gambling take it as a joke. The bell said, "Greedy and lustful people die, those who love gambling ruin their families. The disaster of gambling is no less than that of the powder skull! If you don't believe me, please leave the left seat empty to wait." Dreamlike Hometown A young man from Huainan, with the nickname Shen Lang, lost his parents at a young age and relied on his wet nurse's family. One day, he dreamed that a friend of his father called him away, saying, "Your father recently became the official in charge of the Eastern Peak and has a left-over house outside the eastern gate; he ordered you to take care of it and not let it fall into ruin." So they went together, about three miles, and he said, "This is my home, please rest a little."

Together they entered, and saw a young girl with braids, sitting by the window and embroidering. She playfully spat on the powdered wall and used her fingernails to create a double ring pattern, laughing at the wall. Someone scolded, "The guest has arrived! Acting coy and silly, what kind of attitude is this?" The girl grabbed her embroidery and ran away, dropping her golden scissors in the process. She turned back, laughed, and picked them up, whispering, "What kind of guest is this? He's just being a loudmouth!" The guest asked who she was. Someone replied, "This is my foolish daughter, who is fifteen years old. She was previously engaged in embroidery work for a noblewoman, creating a brocade with interwoven dragon patterns on a mountain-shaped background. The observers praised her clever heart. However, without a mother, she has inevitably missed out on proper upbringing!" The guest praised her enthusiastically. After a short while, they led the guest out the door and to another place, saying, "This is your father's retreat!" They unlocked the door and opened it, revealing a well-equipped main hall, inner rooms, and kitchen. Behind there was a three-bay building, with the middle bay storing books and playthings, the left side filled with embroidered silk, and the right side a treasury full of gold and silver, almost dazzling the eyes with its richness. Someone said, "This is your father's twenty years of hard work; guard it and do not waste it!" The guest bowed and whispered, "Without a wife, who will I share it with?" Someone replied, "You haven't been married yet? If you don't嫌弃 (dislike), I wish to offer my foolish daughter to serve as your wife." The guest bowed deeply in thanks and asked for the date. Someone said, "When the Three Stars illuminate the corner of the Swallow Tower on a clear night, I will send my new wife to you in an oil-lacquered carriage!" After finishing speaking, he left. Immediately, several servants sold themselves to serve under the guest's door. The guest ordered them to clean the courtyard and pavilion and set up tables and chairs.

The kitchen staff and the music department, along with all trivial matters, were managed by him. Exhausted, he went to bed, and in an instant, he found himself back in the old, broken bed and leather cot from his wet nurse's home. Initially suspecting it was an unreal dream, he dismissed it with a laugh. The next night, he returned to the same place, and there were servants waiting at the door, saying, "The bride's carriage has departed. Please, the new nobleman, change your clothes and wait." At that moment, the red candles burned brightly, the flutes and songs played continuously, and the multi-tiered corridors and pavilions were everywhere decorated with brocade canopies. Shortly after, the colorful sedan chair stopped, dozens of lanterns surrounded it, and flower-patterned carpets were laid out. After the exchange of bows, the couple was led into the inner chambers. In the shadows of the candlelight, she peeked from behind her fan, even more dignified than at first sight. He slowly removed his clothes, and they embraced into the heavy curtains. The joy of husband and wife, surpassing the pleasure of painting eyebrows.

The rooster crowed three times, and he put on his clothes and got out of bed. He saw the wet nurse folding clothes and searching for something near the bed. He fixated in thought, from morning until evening. Suddenly feeling tired, he lay down on the table, when an attendant in green clothes came and said, "There is a message from the inner chamber; please come, Master." The man then went to see. Upon entering, he saw those who brought good news standing around the hall. He did not understand, so he asked his wife. She said, "I heard that the Xiuwen Hall is missing an official in charge of the desk, and my father entrusted a thousand gold coins to secure this position for you. "Please change into official attire for the young master." The man laughed and said, "I once wished to become a scholar but could not, and now I see that obtaining an official position has its own methods." So, he dressed in splendid clothes and rode in a carriage to the official office of the Xiuwen Hall. He then went to his father-in-law's family to express his gratitude and returned home, telling his new wife, "Minor official positions are not sufficient for wealth; we should still engage in business." He took out the money from his pouch and instructed the servants to plan for trading and commerce, buying silk and accumulating grain, almost monopolizing the market. He spent his days in dreams, handling official duties in the morning and managing household finances in the afternoon, while his wife diligently managed the household. In less than ten years, they expanded their father's business, becoming the wealthiest family in the realm of ease.

The protagonist often boasted to the wet nurses. The wet nurses said, "Alas, this is a dream. Otherwise, a man of great wealth and honor would not make a place for earning a living here." The protagonist laughed loudly, saying, "I consider being awake as a dream, and a dream as being awake. I have lived a life of abundance, with clothes and food that cannot be exhausted! Moreover, those who enjoy wealth and honor in the world, why must they not be people in a dream?" Thus, he wrote *The Record of Dreams* to record his own thoughts. I wrote about it to inform the world of those who live in a dream. The commentator said, "I once said that people in wealth and honor are no more than having a good dream. Then, those who have a good dream should also be regarded as people in wealth and honor. Alas, good dreams do not last long, and wealth and honor do not last for long. As for the protagonist, he can enjoy long-lasting wealth and honor!" **Destiny in Marriage and Family** A young man from Zhenzhou, named Ding, was seventeen years old and engaged to a woman from the Wei family. He died before he could marry, and his family was about to arrange a marriage with a noble family. They consulted a fortune-teller, who said, "Your fate is not suitable for coupling with human beings. Later, you will marry a wife of an animal." Ding was angry and said, "Even if I am unworthy, I still have a human face! How could I marry into a family of animals?" The fortune-teller replied, "Considering your fate, it should be accurate." Despite his best efforts, he could not find a suitable match.

After making a brief trip to Chu, he anchored his boat in the middle gorge. Suddenly, dozens of young apes descended from the cliffs and leaped onto the bow of the boat. The boatmen chased them away with noise, carrying their bags and chests, and hurriedly climbed the cliffs to leave. While marveling at the strange sight, several old apes carried a sedan chair to him, pulling and pushing it, and forced him to sit down. The boatmen tried with all their might to free him but were unable to do so. They lifted the litter onto their shoulders and flew up the sheer cliff. They arrived at a cave, where stones were piled to make a door and mud plastered to make a path. The man had no choice but to get off the litter and enter. Inside the hall, an old man stood respectfully waiting. He did not look particularly strange and said, "Are you the son of Ding Qingyun?" The man replied, "Yes!" The old man said, "I was a childhood friend of your father. Eighteen years ago, I wandered here and married into the Yuan family, having a daughter who has not yet found a good match. Now that you have come, I have had my young servants respectfully welcome you. If you do not despise me for being of a different kind, I wish to unite us in marriage." The man trembled with fear and dared not accept the offer. Suddenly, an old woman appeared. The old man said, "This is my wife." The man glanced at her and saw that she had green eyes, a red and plump face, and curly hair on her cheeks like a hedgehog turned upside down. She whispered to the old man incessantly, not knowing what she was saying. She dressed as a woman, covered with a veil, and came out, pulling the man to bow to each other. She led him into a different cave. He lifted the veil and looked closely, seeing that her forehead was covered with thick and dense hair, and her human face was nowhere to be seen. The man, angry and indignant, went to sleep. At midnight, the woman secretly approached him. The man shouted, "If you want to engage in human affairs, you should wait until your fur falls off!" The woman felt ashamed and withdrew.

The next day, looking at himself in the stream, he seemed deeply resentful of his ugliness and then leaped into the bottom of the stream with all his strength. A loud cry for help was heard, and the whole family rushed to the scene, directing the efforts of others and pulling him out with great effort. He was carried back to the cave, covered with a quilt and lying motionless, feeling pain all over his body, which subsided and turned into itching. The girl scratched him several times, and his hair fell off with each scratch, accumulating like tangled threads. He was taught to get up the next day, and his face became white and smooth like jade, even more beautiful. Looking at him, he was truly a heavenly being! The man laughed and said, "Now I understand the transformation between human and beast—it's just a matter of one washing and pruning!" That night, they slept together. The next morning, he visited his father, who was so surprised and happy he was almost wild. The mother saw him and became very angry, saying, "You have given birth to a daughter who is unworthy! The old slave has ruined my lineage!" She then cursed her husband and expelled her daughter. The grandfather quickly prepared two sedan chairs and ordered the daughter to follow her husband back to their old place. After losing the man, the boatman waited anxiously for nearly a month. When he saw the man come with a beautiful woman, he was very happy and took them both away. Later, the man returned from Chu and came back to the place. The woman wanted to visit her father, but the steep cliffs and dangerous rocks made it impossible to enter. She returned with tears streaming down her face.

The Bell said, "To cut off the hair for one day, and yet to have a hundred years of beautiful companionship, one can say that the fortune-teller's words did not come true!" **The Stinking Cassia** East of the county office in Qimen, there was a cassia tree that bore flowers but had no fragrance. The locals, finding the name distasteful, called it the "Stinking Cassia." One night, a Taoist and an old man came together under the moonlight, singing and whistling beneath the tree. The Taoist pointed to the tree and laughed, saying, "This is the seventh tree in the moon palace." The old man replied, "The flowers of the moon palace are fragrant beyond compare, far more so than those of the mountain where the Buddha attained enlightenment. Why is this tree so devoid of fragrance?" The Taoist said, "I recall that eight hundred years ago, when the moon god built a new palace in the cold palace, this tree obstructed the corner of the palace, and the moon god ordered Wu Qi to move it. It was then blown down to the earthly world by a strong wind, and the god of wealth happened to pick it up and plant it on a mountain of copper. As a result, its fragrance suddenly diminished. The god of wealth disliked it and abandoned it here." The old man said, "The smell of copper is repulsive; it is good to dislike it. However, to manipulate and deceive in such a way, and yet to gain such a bad reputation, is also the result of one's own hypocrisy!" The Taoist laughed and said, "I shall cleanse this flower of its disgrace." He raised his robe and circled the tree three times. Soon after, a strange fragrance spread, and its scent could be smelled for several miles. Suddenly, a west wind arose, and golden grains fell from the sky. From the flowers emerged beautiful women, each wearing colorful, flowing robes and dancing gracefully. Among them was a woman who styled her hair like a cicada, with green phoenix feathers attached. In the beak of the phoenix was a red pearl, its light shining in the moonlight. The Taoist said, "Ah Huang, who relies on Chang'e's favor, has not been part of the Heavenly Music Department for a long time. Tonight, she shall sing for me." The woman smiled and leaned against the tree, singing:

The golden wind blows, and the jade dew fades; the Weaving Maid delays me on the west side of the Milky Way. Disliking the dragon's stench, I refuse to ride; I ride the colorful phoenix and fly recklessly. The water clock turns, and the star shadows are low; the jade palace is high and cold, and who will I rely on? I grasp the osmanthus tree and weep and sob; about to leave this place...

Return with You! After the song, the west wind blew again, and smoke and fog swirled, suddenly making the beautiful person disappear. The fallen flowers were still blown by the wind to fill the tree. The old man said, "To have a game tonight, and this flower will leave its fragrance for eternity!" The Taoist said, "The soundless sound is the true sound, and the tasteless taste is the ultimate taste. I ultimately wish to preserve the true nature of this flower without fragrance." He then lifted his robe and swept it, dispersing all the fragrance, and left with the old man laughing and talking. The bell said, "The lush bamboo groves of the Qi Garden were used to block the Hù River, and the tall pines of the Bell Temple were used as dust brushes. The wood for Cai Yong's musical instruments once burned in the stove. Returning to simplicity and purity, remaining unshamed throughout life, I believe in this tree. Bing Yuan tied money trees, which were revered in his time, leading to excessive worship. This tree is not deceived by the money god, and it should be forgotten without a good name. However, it carries this solitary fragrance and ultimately invites unique appreciation. Men of ambition should take this as an example." Omen Crow It is commonly believed that magpies bring good news and crows bring bad news. Therefore, those who hear the magpie's call are all filled with joy, while upon hearing the crow's sound, everyone will surely chase it away. But I alone like crows and dislike magpies. In the courtyard, there is an old locust tree where a crow's nest is on the top. When it rains or snows in the morning and evening, the crow has nothing to eat, so I must set out rice in the courtyard to feed it. Every time the morning light first rises, the crow stands facing the sun, cleaning its neck, combing its feathers, lifting its tail, and turning its eyes, displaying all its postures, but it is not good at singing.

At the time, I clapped and shouted to attract and tease it, but the crow remained unusually silent. On the first day of the lunar new year in the year of the Earth Rat, the crow flew and called into the room, staying for three days and nights without leaving. That autumn, I reported success. I was a candidate for the imperial examination held by the Ministry of Rites, and my family members waited anxiously for the announcement of passing, which was written in gold ink on yellow paper. We waited under a tree at dawn, hoping it would utter a good sound, but the crow turned away without looking back, and I also failed and returned home! In the spring of the year of the Water Rabbit, the crow made a loud noise. That year, my younger brother Zhisheng ranked first in the list of successful candidates. I then set food in the courtyard and called the crow, telling it, "I have been recommended five times without success, so I no longer dream of success in the imperial examinations. From now on, I will not trouble you again. Wait until my younger brother succeeds in the imperial examinations, then I will conserve my strength and strive to make a phoenix-like sound." The crow nodded its head twice. That winter, a strong wind destroyed its nest, broke its left wing, and killed it. By the year of the Fire Horse, my younger brother passed the imperial examinations and returned home. A large number of crows and magpies gathered and made noise at the door. I recalled this crow and sighed for many days. Crows only know how to help people succeed, but this crow could recognize someone before they met their destiny. I recorded this to inform those who dislike crows and favor magpies. The commentator said, "The phoenix makes a harmonious sound, while the crow makes a harsh sound.
