Is the crow the descendant of the phoenix? Why is its sound so harmonious? If it is as obsequious as the magpie, it is common, evil, and inferior, and its sound is truly unbearable. If Zhu Ci encountered it, he would burn its feathers; if Wang Jun saw it, he would certainly probe its nest."

During the Qing dynasty, Shen Qifeng wrote in the *Xieduo* (谐铎), Volume Eleven, titled "The Old Monk Distinguishes the Deceitful": When Yan Song was not yet prominent, he studied with Wang Gong, the Righteous One, at the Eastern Courtyard of Bodhidharma Temple in Beijing. One day, they read the biography of Jing Ke and came to the part where Fan Yufu committed suicide. Yan said, "This is a foolish man. Knowing whether the matter will succeed or fail, he still treats his head as if it were a trivial matter!" He then burst into laughter. Wang said, "A righteous man seeking revenge, willing to sacrifice his life, is truly pitiable!" He then wept. They continued reading until they reached the part where Jing Ke wore white clothes and a white hat for his farewell. Yan laughed again and said, "Knowing that he would never return, yet he still sent him off, Prince Dan is truly very foolish." Wang wept once more and said, "The hero's journey, with the wind whistling and the water sobbing, singing and playing the zither, still leaves a lasting pain throughout the ages!" They then read the part where Wang Nang attacked with a sword, sitting with his legs spread out and feet pointing upwards, shouting insults. Yan laughed uncontrollably and said, "This is truly an inexperienced man. He did not kill him when he had the chance by the pillars, but instead settled it with insults." Wang wept even more, his tears soaking his sleeves, and said, "A hero repaying a friend, even unto death, still shows spirit. If the copper pillar is struck, even the First Emperor of Qin should lose his courage." At that moment, the sounds of crying and laughing filled the hall. An old monk listened for a long time and sighed, saying, "To cry is human nature, but to laugh is truly unpredictable. In twenty years, there will be no remaining loyal ministers or righteous men." Later, Wang became the magistrate of Zhongmou County and gained a good reputation. However, Yan eventually rose to the position of chancellor through his ability to write alchemical texts, abusing his power and harming the country, forming factions and undermining good people, becoming the most notorious villain of the Ming dynasty. The old monk had foreseen this but could not save them, perhaps it was the Buddhist concept of a fixed destiny, or "determined fate."

"Allegedly, acts of blind loyalty and filial piety, is there any point to this? The traitors and rebels of ancient times were all exceptionally intelligent people. Therefore, scholars value virtue more than talent."

A Certain Official from Guangdong, Known as the Plain-Clothes Officer Who Captured Bandits, Served as the Judicial Commissioner of HeTang. There was a man surnamed Nie, who had been falsely convicted of a capital crime. The official cleared his name and, as a gesture of gratitude, the family offered their daughter, Shu’er, as a servant. Recognizing her sincerity, the official accepted her. His wife, strict in her management of the household, assigned her duties beyond mere cleaning and sweeping, teaching her needlework. Shu’er was unable to learn, and so she was beaten daily, yet she merely bowed her head and accepted her punishment without resistance. Later, the official was dismissed from his post due to an error and retired from public service. At that time, in the Zao Shu Forest, there was a bandit leader named Liu Biao, known as "Sai Zhang Qing," who was skilled in using "meteor balls" — a weapon that could fire five projectiles at once, each striking with remarkable accuracy. The next day, another bandit, Zhu Jian, known as "Iron Cane," was renowned for his skill in wielding a heavy iron staff, having once struck the stone drum before the Zhenwu Temple so hard that it shattered into powder. These outlaws roamed freely through the green woods, and even the bandit hunters dared not look them in the eye. The official, aware of their danger, set out with caution. By this time, it was already late in the evening, when he heard the sound of a whistling arrow from the forest. The official trembled with fear, and his wife turned as pale as earth. All the attendants and servants were visibly shaken. Shu’er, however, calmly stepped forward and said, "You petty rats, how dare you dare to offend your honor? If you wish to die, I shall kill you with my own hands." She asked to ride in front of the official and went forth bare-handed. She shouted at the bandits, "You damned dogs of slaves, do you recognize Nie Shu’er of Henan?" The bandits laughed and replied, "We care only for money and paper — what use is a girl like you?" Shu’er, enraged, said, "Your time of death has come! Dare you speak in jest?" The bandits, too, grew angry and suddenly fired a shot. Shu’er opened her two fingers with her right hand and caught the projectile; another was caught with her left hand; when the third came, she smiled and caught it in her teeth.

The thieves were startled and fired another shot. Shu'er lay on his back on the horse's back, playfully using two lotus petals to hold the ball. When the fifth shot came, Shu'er immediately shot a ball from his foot to meet it, making a clear sound as it flew thirty steps away. He leaped up, spat out the ball from his mouth, and laughed, saying, "Is this all the skill you have, scoundrel?" One of the thieves advanced with an iron staff. Shu'er grabbed it, twisted it several times, rolling it as if it were soft cotton, and threw it to the ground, saying, "Your mother's kitchen stick, and you bring it to scare people, how laughable." The two thieves were shocked. Shu'er shot balls left and right, and both thieves were killed. The other thieves knelt around the horse and begged for their lives. Shu'er said, "What are you, that you could soil my hands?" He commanded them to leave. Calmly turning his horse, he reported to the official, saying, "Thanks to your good fortune, I have not failed my mission." The official and his wife were both amazed. The official then asked, "With such remarkable skills, why can't you pick up a needle?" Shu'er replied, "When I was around twelve, I was used to playing with long spears and large swords. When I hold a needle, I don't know what it is, that's why I can't learn." The official asked again, "Why do you bow your head when beaten?" Shu'er replied, "My father sent me to repay your great kindness. A little disobedience is a way to repay your kindness, how dare I not?" Thus, the official's wife was also pleased. After returning home, she persuaded the official to take her as a concubine. She gave birth to a son who later became the county magistrate of Dian Nan. He often personally led officials into the mountains to capture thieves, greatly resembling his mother in this regard.

铎曰：吾向读《冯暖传》，而知当日无薛债之役。客无能一语，至今几成铁案。英雄寄人篱下，毕生无可插脚，恐为厮养辈下眼觑耳！书儿遇盗，其厚幸乎？有疑口逆齿噙之说为过神其技者，然不闻《列子》之言乎！飞卫学射于甘蝇，诸法并善，惟啮法不教。卫密持矢以射蝇，蝇啮得镞矢还射，卫绕树而走。则书儿此技，亦有所受之也。牛羊之眼，相儿女子犹失之，况相天下士哉！ The Bell said: I once read *The Biography of Feng Nuan*, and thus came to know that there was no incident of the debt to Xue at that time. The guest could not utter a single word, and this has now become an unalterable conclusion. A hero, dependent on others, has no place to stand throughout his life, and is likely to be looked down upon by servants and attendants! That the child should meet a bandit—could this be a great fortune? Some claim that the technique of "doubtful mouth, reversed teeth, holding it" is supernatural, but have they not heard what *Liezi* says? Fei Wei studied archery from Gan Ying, and mastered all the techniques, except for the "biting method," which was not taught. Fei Wei secretly held an arrow and shot a fly; the fly bit the tip of the arrow and shot it back, so Fei Wei ran around the tree. Then, this skill of the child must also have been learned from somewhere. Even the eyes of oxen and sheep fail to discern children and women—how much more so to discern the men of the world? 正士驱邪樵阳郡韩公，貌文秀，而性好武事，日驰马试剑为乐。未贵时，携一健奴，出游五岳。中途遇雪，投止枯庙。一更后，雪月交辉，公起立檐下，四望皎然，曰：“真琉璃世界也。”忽阴风四袭，一狰狞恶鬼，昂首直入。公拔剑相迎，健奴大惊，犬伏地下，一以两手抱公左足，见恶鬼渐长，始犹高与檐齐，继则出檐者约三丈许。仰见公状貌亦变黑面赤髯，挺身而立，身亦渐长，高出于恶鬼者又约三丈许。鬼身顿缩，伏地而拜曰：“公烈丈夫也。” The upright gentleman Han, who exorcised evil spirits in Qiaoyang County, had a refined and elegant appearance, yet he had a love for martial affairs and found joy in riding his horse and testing his sword each day. When he was not yet esteemed, he took with him a strong servant and traveled among the Five Great Mountains. On the way, they encountered snow and took shelter in a desolate temple. After the first watch of the night, snow and moonlight shone together; the gentleman stood beneath the eaves, looking all around and seeing everything clearly, saying, “This is indeed a world of crystal clarity.” Suddenly, a cold wind swept in from all sides, and a fearsome, grotesque demon strode in, head raised. The gentleman drew his sword to meet it, and his strong servant was greatly alarmed, crouching like a dog on the ground, holding the gentleman’s left foot with both hands. As they watched, the demon grew taller—first reaching the height of the eaves, then extending beyond them by about three zhang. Looking up, they saw that the gentleman’s appearance had also changed—his face was black, his beard red, and he stood upright. His body, too, grew taller, surpassing the demon by another three zhang. The demon suddenly shrank, fell to the ground, and bowed, saying, “You are indeed a valiant and noble man.”

People, regardless of their wealth, poverty, nobility, or lowliness, all have their spirits and qi elevated to a height of ten zhang. Every act of dishonesty or wrongdoing shortens one's spiritual stature by one chi. Therefore, those who appear in the world in front of us, the tallest are no more than five chi, the next are three or two chi, and the shortest are as small as a three-inch cone lying on the ground. But you alone preserve your original spirit. In the future, those who stand firm and reach the heavens, none but you will be it. Encourage yourself, encourage yourself! After saying this, he passed away. The strong servant saw you as unchanged and recounted what he had seen. You were completely unaware. Later, you reached the position of a general. You died in battle while suppressing bandits and were honored posthumously for your valor. The scroll you left behind, *Fu Gui Tu*, was burned and had a twisted beard, not in its original form. However, when evil spirits troubled the village, people would ask for your image to subdue them, and they would flee. Thus, you have gained a reputation similar to that of Zhong Kui. The bell said: "At the top of a hundred-zhang tower, Yuanlong's heroic spirit; if criticized day by day, it will be swept away completely!" Even a three-inch cone lying on the ground is not fully explained by the theory of spiritual diminishment.

The evil guest removed lust from the monk Pǔjìng of the Golden Mountain Temple. The monk kept a monkey, whose fur was entirely white, and he locked it in the Buddha hall every day to listen to sermons. One night, the monkey broke free from its chain. The monk sighed and said, "The evil beast’s lustful heart has not been severed; it will surely kill itself. Twenty years of merit and practice, all ruined." There was a certain merchant from Shaanxi who resided in Tiěwèng City. He loved to keep beautiful concubines, and even the maids and servants were beautiful. One day, a young man in a brown fur coat knocked on his door, introducing himself as Mr. Shen. He said he was tired of the noisy world and wished to borrow a garden pavilion to rest. The merchant had a long-standing preference for men and coveted the young man’s beauty, so he agreed. At night, he went to his pavilion and saw that the bed had no blankets. He laughed and said, "The bed is as cold as ice; it’s hard to sleep with just clothes. If you don’t mind my presence, I’ll bring some bedding." The young man agreed. The merchant ordered his servant to bring a brocade quilt and a yellow silk blanket and place them on the bed before leaving. The merchant pulled the young man to lie down with him and secretly engaged in intimate relations. The young man laughed and said, "You have treated me so lightly; from now on, you will wear a cap and hairpin." The merchant also laughed and said, "If you truly hide me, I will place you among the gold hairpins; how could I treat you as an outsider?" From then on, the young man could freely enter and leave the inner chambers, and the merchant did not forbid it. Gradually, he began to secretly engage with the maids and servants, and later even with his concubines. At first, he only did so at night, but later he openly engaged in sexual acts during the day, without any shame. The merchant had always favored him and could not suddenly start rebuking or expelling him. A close friend arrived, and the merchant secretly discussed the matter with him.

A friend said, "Opening the door to thieves, the fault truly lies with you. If you must eliminate the root cause, you should first sever its sexual organs." The person asked, "Should I castrate him?" The friend laughed and said, "Why use a big knife to kill a chicken?" The person insisted on knowing more, and the friend replied, "There are those who can undergo castration without holding an inch of iron, but foolish children do not understand." The person asked for a plan, and the friend said, "There is a prostitute here, her nickname is Snow Dog, and her lower body emits great poison. Why not summon her?" The person agreed. After a while, Snow Dog arrived, her lips and face powdered, a dominant figure in the world of prostitution. The person hid her in the inner chambers and ordered her to join the young man at night. The young man got Snow Dog and was indeed very happy. Snow Dog was originally a prostitute who was skilled in the arts of love, and the young man was also vigorous. They attacked the poison day and night, but he did not notice any immediate effects. After half a month, the young man's cheeks gradually turned red, and he sometimes inserted his hand into his crotch area, as if scratching an itch. After another half a month, his eyebrows suddenly furrowed, and he moaned in pain. After several days, he left. However, he would return every few days, and when he came, he would meet with Snow Dog. After several more days, he could no longer walk, leaning on a cane and hunching over, and when he arrived, he cuddled with Snow Dog, tossing and turning all night, unable to engage in sexual intercourse. Snow Dog deliberately grasped his shaft and played with it, and it popped out with a sound. He cried out in pain loudly, got off the bed and looked for a cane, stumbling and fleeing away. Snow Dog took it out under the lamp and examined it, seeing an organ about five inches long, skin and flesh stuck together, bloody and red like painted with vermillion. Afterward, he never came again.

A friend arrived and laughed, saying, "The castration has already been verified, but you have turned the embroidered curtain into a place of confinement." The person smiled and thanked him, rewarding Snow Dog with a hundred gold pieces and sending him away. Later, it was heard that on the top of the pagoda on Mount Jinshan, there was a white monkey whose lower body was ulcerated and died. An old monk buried it under the pagoda and sighed, "Who is this wicked child who has come to such a cruel death? However, all traces of lust have been removed, and he can now purify his body and return to the Three Jewels." The person instructed his friend to keep the matter secret, but Snow Dog went on to explain it in detail to others. The narrator said: "A foolish child who ingests poison will inevitably lose his life; a prodigal son who returns has already been castrated. It is fortunate to control a restless mind and not feed a greedy dog. Castration is the most degrading punishment; let all be warned by it." In the Fragrant City, there was a young man named Peng Sheng from Zhenze, who was handsome and unrestrained. He was fascinated by the story of Wen Xiao and Cai Luan, desiring an immortal companion. His parents repeatedly opposed his choice of partner. One day, he arrived at the lake in a small boat and saw a floating petal of a lotus upstream. He picked it up and looked at it, finding a short poem: "Under the gentle flow of the Xiao Fu Mountain, I remember our meeting. I wanted to pick the water caltrop, but unfortunately met the east wind. The misty rain on the midday bridge is thick; it would be better to return and dream of the curtains. East of the small building, I left the railing, half in the moonlight. The cool night has heavy flower shadows." He was astonished by this and disembarked. After a hundred steps, he found ten thousand lotus plants, spread out like a brocade tent. When he arrived, he saw a deep red door, with a copper ring closed during the day. Suddenly, an old woman in blue clothing opened the door and said, "Peng Lang has arrived." She led him inside.

East of the phoenix screen, a woman approached with deliberate and respectful steps, and Peng bowed in greeting. The woman said, "I am a spirit from the City of the Lotus, known as Fragrant Maiden. I have long fallen into the mortal world and have not yet met a worthy man. Knowing that you have long aspired to a divine connection, I have come to offer my humble writings, leading you into a hidden paradise." Peng replied, "I am honored by your divine favor, lifting up the ordinary and foolish, and I would willingly be a servant for life." The woman smiled and said, "You are truly obsessed with love." She then ordered a maid to clean the inner room, setting up two beds for sleeping. At night, the woman slept on the east side, inviting Peng to sleep on the west. Peng said, "Having seen your beautiful face, I should be close to your body. Why then do we remain so close yet distant, like the mountain of love and desire, making me feel uneasy?" The woman replied, "In the world of immortals, marriage exists only in the spirit. If one loves based on physical form, then Qin Ningyu would have had children long ago. Why then is there no sound of young phoenixes on the Xiangfeng Pavilion to this day?" Peng tried to touch her, but she remained untouched.

The woman said, "My lord, your impurities have not yet been removed, and even if you try to share the same bed and pillow, there is still a barrier between us. Tomorrow, I will prepare the transformation elixir for you. It will take three days to complete, and once you have taken it, we can finally be united." Peng could not refuse, so he withdrew to sleep in a separate bed. The next morning, the woman gathered herbs from the Three Mountains, added them to the alchemical furnace, and instructed Peng to watch over it day and night. Every day, Peng opened the furnace to check the timing of the process. The woman laughed and said, "My passionate lord, your desire is urgent." Peng replied, "A hungry person is eager for food, and a thirsty person is eager for drink. Human nature is much the same." As they bantered, a boatman arrived, bringing news that Peng's father was gravely ill, and his mother had sent a letter summoning him. Peng thought that the elixir would be ready soon, allowing him to be close to the beautiful woman and ascend to immortality. Seeing his mother's letter, he felt quite unhappy. The woman urged him to return home temporarily to visit. Peng said, "Life and death are determined by fate. What good would it do to return?"

"I'm so happy here that I don't miss Shu anymore!" The woman suddenly exclaimed, "Having feelings for one's children but lacking the nature of a father, definitely not a vessel for immortality. Even if the alchemical elixir at the furnace is ready, changing one's bones would still be futile." She immediately destroyed the furnace. Peng said, "Even though I dare not rashly seek physical pleasure, I still hope you can guide me to ascend to the celestial palace." The woman glared at him without speaking. Suddenly, the old woman in green clothes transformed into a colorful phoenix, and the woman mounted it and flew up, sighing, "This child has no heart or liver, is the Great Lao Heaven a place without fathers?" She slowly ascended into the clouds and disappeared. The flowers, trees, and buildings vanished instantly, and the boatman also disappeared. Peng was filled with regret for a long time before finding his way back. The bell said, "In the realm of immortals, couples only meet in spirit. A timeless saying that can cleanse the impurities of those like Lan Xiang and E Lv! In the imperial court and among the immortals, one must seek filial sons, but stories of people like Bo Yin abandoning their mothers and Mei Fu severing ties with their relatives are all considered fabrications by foolish people."

A wealthy family in Dingtao had three generations of people known for their virtue. Their son, aged fourteen, wanted to hire a teacher to prepare for the imperial examinations. He chose carefully and hesitated for a long time without making a decision. One night, he dreamed of someone telling him, "If you want to hire a teacher, you must hire the scholar from Broom Village in Wu County." He woke up feeling puzzled and set out for Suzhou. Upon arriving, he found Broom Village to be a remote and sparsely populated area. As he walked, an old man came out with a cane. The protagonist hurriedly approached him and asked for directions. The old man laughed and said, "The scholar you are looking for is me." He then expressed his sincerity and asked to follow the old man home. The old man replied, "My humble abode is not fit to receive a distinguished guest, but since you have honored me with your presence, let us go together." The protagonist was overjoyed and accompanied the old man back to his home, where he instructed the protagonist's son to study under him. The old man supervised the studies rigorously, working night and day without regard for the cold or heat. The student read not only the standard texts but also the old man's own works and past exam papers. If the student's work was unsatisfactory, the old man would write a model piece and have the student memorize and emulate it. In the same year, the student was admitted to the imperial library. The old man then gave him past failed exam papers to study, along with high-quality literary works considered exemplary. The student succeeded in both the spring and autumn examinations. The protagonist was delighted and held a banquet to celebrate the teacher's success. He said, "You revealed your slow and steady approach, and your student has already achieved fame. Why do you content yourself with a life of obscurity?" The old man sighed for a long time.

Someone questioned the reason, and the old man said, "Do not be surprised by what I say. I am not a human, I am a ghost. In my youth, I was not careful with small matters and felt ashamed of the moral teachings, which led to my being trapped in the examination halls for over fifty years, failing to achieve even one degree. My wife was frivolous and shallow, thinking my writing was out of step with the times, which led to my dismissal. She would mock me daily as a dull scholar, and I died with deep resentment. Now, having accumulated good deeds and blessings, I will use your virtue to give my writings a voice, to make people understand that my life of failure was not due to lack of effort; and to let the world know that those who achieve high ranks are here, not there." After saying this, he clasped his chest and wept, then fell to the ground and disappeared. The narrator was moved by the old man's virtue in educating his son and returned to the place after some time. He saw an old house with a single beam, with a coffin resting on its left side, and an old woman cooking below. He asked her, and she said, "This is my late husband, who died three years ago. He was angry when she called him a dull scholar. On his deathbed, he told me, 'Because of my thin virtue, I could not rise to high office to gain official titles. But in the future, I will leave you a legacy of literature to bring you good fortune. Remember these words and strive to live a little longer.'" Hearing this, the narrator felt even more sorrow and gave a thousand gold coins to support her family, made great efforts to arrange his funeral, and returned home. Later, when he was appointed to a county magistrate position, he brought the old woman to live with him until the end of his days. The bell said, "First comes virtue, then comes literature. Therefore, most of the successful candidates on the imperial examination lists have accumulated good deeds. Those who lose their virtue in youth are stripped of their names in old age. Even if ghosts pass on teachings, they will ultimately face retribution. Compared to those who pass the exams through three bows, they are still a step behind. Alas!"

Three Strokes Punish the Servant In the Yuanhe period, Magistrate Chang, who educated the ignorant, loved the people and valued scholars, was excellent at resolving legal disputes. In the village, there was a man who had an affair with the mistress and was hindered by his son. He incited the mistress to file a complaint against the master for being disrespectful. The magistrate investigated and found the truth, arrested the uncle and aunt, and heard the case together. On the day of the hearing, he called the bad servant up and asked, "Neither party's relatives are named; why did you file a complaint on behalf of the mistress?" The bad servant replied, "I was raised by the master and hoped the young master would start a family. I did not expect to behave so poorly. The mistress disciplined me, and I instead rebelled. I sought help from both parties' relatives, but they treated it as distant as Qin and Yue. I had no choice but to file a complaint despite the stigma." The magistrate said, "Loyal to the master, willing to endure hardship, you can be called a righteous servant." The bad servant bowed and said, "I have always been known as a good person, known by everyone in the village." The magistrate nodded. He then called the young son up, who was around 14 or 15 years old and interjected with scholarly grace. When asked about the reasons for his disrespect towards his mother, the son only shed tears without speaking. The magistrate pretended to be angry and said, "The crime of filial impiety, the law has clear provisions; the law of the three-foot ruler cannot be taken lightly." He immediately ordered a beating. The son cried in pain, and the uncle and aunt pleaded for mercy on his behalf, while the bad servant had a look of joy on his face. The magistrate looked at him and smiled and said, "Your young master is still a child; one stroke of the cane would immediately kill him."

You are the expert translator of Classical Chinese (文言文) into English. Your task is to translate the provided Classical Chinese text into English. Follow the two-stage process: ### Stage 1: Pre-Drafting Research 1. **Identify Idiomatic Expressions**: Recognize and research idiomatic or archaic expressions that cannot be directly translated word-for-word. 2. **Cultural and Historical Allusions**: Identify references to historical events, figures, or cultural practices that require contextual explanation or adaptation. 3. **Syntactic Ambiguities**: Identify grammatically ambiguous constructions or elliptical passages unique to Classical Chinese syntax. 4. **Proper Nouns**: Identify people, places, titles, and canonical terms and determine how to render them consistently. ### Stage 2: Final Translation 1. **Translate Faithfully**: Ensure the translation is faithful to the original text, avoiding omissions of difficult phrases. 2. **Maintain Consistency**: Keep proper names, titles, and technical terms consistent. 3. **Handle Uncertainty**: If a term is uncertain, choose the most likely reading but mark uncertainty briefly. 4. **Avoid Fabrication**: Do not fabricate historical facts or citations.

The Bell said: Revealing private words is indeed shameful. Using the rod as an excuse to punish the wicked is also the bitter heart of the judge! Deceptive yet just, strange yet legal, this can be said to capture the spirit of the law. The Song Dynasty was the most lenient in governing officials, hence officials like Lü Gongbi and Zhang Chongyang often used a single word to lead to execution, or even a small sum of money could result in death. Later, the power of local officials gradually diminished, and for crimes punishable by more than one year of servitude, an application must be made and approved. Only the use of stocks and canes could be decided independently, so when the crime is serious but the punishment is light, they would often let the evil go unpunished. The official used stocks and canes instead of the sword, which can be said to be a good practitioner of the law. However, even so, they were like bundles of firewood, or like butchers and executioners, and gentlemen always guard against the beginning of evil.

A Brief Statement to Protect an Infant Yuan Jianzhi, a magistrate from Qiantang, was a distant relative of my grandfather. He was appointed as the magistrate of Shangyuan County after serving in the Hanlin Academy. Known for his upright character and refusal to kowtow to power, he resolved cases by citing classics, embodying the scholarly demeanor of a literate official. At the time, a man in the village married a woman who had just given birth to a son after five months of pregnancy, and the villagers mocked him. Unable to bear the ridicule, the man sued his father-in-law, claiming that the woman had conceived before marriage. The next day, the case was heard in court, with both parties present and a large crowd gathered around. The magistrate emerged dressed formally and raised his hand to congratulate the man. The man, feeling embarrassed, knelt before the magistrate. The magistrate said, "You, a villager, can be said to have received a blessing without knowing it!" He then asked the father-in-law, "Have you ever learned to read?" The father-in-law replied, "No." The magistrate laughed and said, "Today’s lawsuit stems from the fact that neither of your families has been educated! Ancient legends speak of a white deer reincarnating and a ghost piercing a woman’s side, but such stories are absurd and need not be mentioned. Historical records, however, document cases like Lady Liang’s prolonged pregnancy and Lady Xiaomu’s early delivery—some births are delayed, others are hastened. In general, delayed births are influenced by a thick atmosphere, leading to a long life; early births are influenced by a clear atmosphere, leading to nobility. Those born to live long lives, like Emperors Yao and Shun, are well-known to you. Those born to be noble need not be sought far and wide; take me, for example, I was also born after five months. Though I am very unqualified, I was able to enter the Hanlin Academy and later serve as a magistrate. If you do not believe me, let your wife go ask your mother-in-law." The man nodded in agreement.

The official immediately ordered the wife to bring the child into the office. After a short while, the child, tied with a bell and locked with a flower-red embroidered lock, emerged. The wife knelt on the ground and said, "We graciously received the favor of the elder lady, and we are permitted to adopt the child as our grandson." The official sternly told someone, "If the child is my child, please take good care of him. In the future, do not let him surpass me in rank or achievement." He then turned to the crowd and smiled, saying, "Among you, there are those who understand reason. Please trust my heart and do not treat my previous words as empty talk." The crowd responded in unison, and thus, the shame of both families was completely resolved. Later, the child studied at the school, served the official's longevity and position, and was cared for daily. The author remarked: The saying of bearing filth and dirt is for those who govern the people; this printed legal code can only be understood by those who have studied. However, sacrificing oneself to protect the innocent is something that those who adhere to established rules cannot do. The official must have an uncorrupted body and use a wide and long tongue, preaching to all beings in the court! Huang Gai, a military man, governed Shicheng, and Kuang Zhong, a minor official, governed Wu County. Later, figures like Feng Jian and Wang Xingzong, either as recorders or as direct officials, as Wang Jinxī said, good governance does not necessarily come from the highest ranks. Yet, encountering such cases, how could they be fabricated by those without learning? Those who are not familiar with the theories of Jin Ku must suffer a flying disaster; those who do not read the *Yijing* will eventually become a wrongful case. Those who excel in learning will become officials—what profound words these are!

A teacher from Lou County named Tan, who had not obtained even the lowest degree after nearly thirty years of study, took up a teaching post in a village in the west. His teaching salary was insufficient to support his wife and children, and his students had abandoned their Confucian studies to engage in commerce. At the end of the year, as he settled his accounts and returned home, he encountered an old man on the road. The old man smiled and said, "Master, you have been dismissed from teaching. Do you have a position or a host for next year?" Tan replied, "No." The old man said, "I have a distant relative who will be hiring a teacher next year to instruct his son. If you do not嫌弃 (dislike), I will introduce you." Tan greatly appreciated the offer and asked for the old man's address. The old man said, "I will come myself on the day. Master, you need not ask further." With a bow, the old man left. After the Lantern Festival, the old man indeed arrived and offered a hundred taels of gold as a teaching fee. Tan was delighted, bid farewell to his wife and children, and boarded a boat to leave. The waterway was winding and unfamiliar. After traveling for about three days and nights, the old man said, "We have arrived." They shook hands and boarded the boat, arriving at a place with high gates and elegant houses, with a path leading to the side. The garden was adorned with flowers and trees, and there were several buildings in the middle. The old man said, "This is the study room. Please, Master, take a seat for a moment." He led the students out to greet Tan, who was dressed in fine silks and brocades, resembling noble youths. The old man said, "The master happened to be out and did not have time to welcome you properly. Please meet another day." He then brought out the books and asked Tan to punctuate them. Tan looked at them and found only one copy of *Romance of the Three Kingdoms* and several copies of *Water Margin*, with no Five Classics or Four Books. Tan was surprised. The old man said, "These young men do not aspire to honor and fame; they just need to recognize a few lines of text and slightly familiarize themselves with worldly affairs. Master, do not doubt or be surprised."

Tán was thus reassured, and the old man also departed. After half a year, the provisions and hospitality were extremely abundant and generous. One day, it was rumored that the host had returned, and a grand feast was set up, inviting the teacher to watch a play. When Tán arrived, the host wore a rooster-feathered hat and armor, solemnly welcoming him inside. The guests seated around were all dressed in military attire. Tán was filled with fear and confusion, unsure of how to proceed. The host smiled and said, “Teacher, do not be alarmed. I am a man of the rivers and lakes, a bold and fierce individual. Among my kind, we rely on strength to rob and kill, ignoring righteousness. That is why I had my son receive an education, so that he might understand the basics of propriety. Now, I am fortunate that you have not abandoned him, and I am grateful for the favor you have shown to future scholars. I have specially prepared a cup of wine to show my loyalty and respect.” After speaking, the theater troupe presented the script for selection. Tán did not recognize the names of the performers, so he tentatively chose the full play *White Robe Coat*. Not even halfway through the performance, the host’s face changed, and he stood up abruptly, ordering the feast to be taken down. He said, “I have never wronged Teacher, why are you mocking me like this? Although, perhaps it is fate.” He then prepared several bolts of colorful silk and ten ingots of gold, instructing his son to send them back at night. The next day, the bandit hunters arrived, and the entire household was arrested. The son hid in Tán’s home and narrowly escaped. Tán was moved by the old man’s intentions and raised the disciple to maturity. The old man also occasionally came to provide relief. Dùo said: Thieves also have their principles; it is not scholars who can overlook this. It is surprising that someone who treated Teacher with loyalty and respect would turn out to be a thief. Perhaps it is heaven that does not wish to cut off his descendants. Today, the idle and dissolute young nobles indulge in gambling, prostitution, and other vices, even if it means spending thousands of gold, but when it comes to providing for the private tutor, they are overly meticulous about small matters, not even as good as thieves.

A Ghost Husband from Fufeng, Qiu Shu, courtesy name Lingyi, lost his father at a young age. His mother, a strict disciplinarian, accidentally broke a jade pendant on her belt, which frightened her to flee away. She ran into the mountains at night, where the moonlight was hazy and the thorny bushes were dense, leaving her with no place to stay. She was marked for burial. Qiu Shu later married a woman from the Ji family, who was very virtuous and kind. The honors and titles he received, he also gave to his previous wife, to commend her will. The bell said: A chaste woman does not remarry after her husband's death, even if she dies, her will is fulfilled. The virtue of the later wife is also influenced by the chaste spirit of the first wife. Otherwise, the search for the old sword would provoke her jealousy, how could she have given up her honors and titles? A book spirit troubled a certain man from the Jincheng Street in Jinling. His family had been scholars for generations, but because he could not become wealthy through reading, he gave up and became a merchant. He happened to be staying alone in the shop one night and heard sighing sounds at the foot of the bed. He scolded it, and it stopped. Afterwards, he would hear it every night, but he also ignored it. One night, there was a person with a square headband and red shoes, who stepped out slowly from behind the bed, frowning and looking sad, seeming unhappy. He asked who it was, and the spirit replied: "I am a book spirit. Since I have been living in your home, your ancestors and father showed me much favor. Unintentionally passed down to you, you failed to maintain the old friendship, finally cutting off the connection. Fortunately, there is no enmity between us, but now you are bound by the pursuit of money, causing my spirits to fall. If you do not free me soon, then the smell of money will overwhelm me, and the culture will be lost. Disaster will befall you, do not regret, do not regret!" After finishing speaking, he disappeared. He quickly got up and lit a candle to look around, seeing several old, broken books tied with money strings and thrown away on the bed, for over a decade. He hated that the books were causing trouble and took fire to burn them. In an instant, the ashes flew and flames rose.

The fire spread, burning down houses, and all things in the room were completely destroyed; he eventually died in poverty. The author says: "The saying that reading cannot make one rich is true. Try asking those who do not read, can they all be rich?" But if one reads with the desire for wealth, I know he is not a true reader. We who read the books of sages, what do we learn? But with the pursuit of wealth and status, it obstructs the mind; if one fails slightly, he immediately blames the books. Try covering the book, where will you find a living? The author composed six verses of the "Qinyuan Chun" poetic form in the middle of the eighth month in the year of Jiazi. He says: "In the middle of the eighth month in the year of Jiazi, only I and the books, made a pact in the thatched hut. Let us return to harmony. From now on, please do not forget. When going out, it follows the carriage; when returning, it sits beside the table, enduring the night fires and morning roosters. I pity you, laughing as you insert the bamboo slips, personally arranged in tortoiseshell. Who knew you are inherently untrustworthy, deceiving heroes and making them gray-haired in vain. Sighing that I am starving to death, where is the thousand measures of grain? Having no place to stand even with a cone, where is the golden house? I pity you, but you truly betray me; it is not wrong to draw the sword and look at each other. Before the words were finished, the book felt ashamed and retreated, secretly leaving the door and wall."

If one fails to learn calligraphy, what else should one learn? It is not as good as being an old farmer. There is a thatched cottage half an acre in size, nestled in the curve of a pond; stone fields one qing in size, east of the reed-covered water. With a wife who braids her hair into a bun and a son who lets his hair hang loose, they pack food and walk together with hoes in hand, living in a Peach Blossom Spring, where one can see mulberry and hemp fields and hear the sounds of chickens and dogs, enjoying a harmonious and peaceful life. Alas, my path has come to an end, much like Fan Chi's failed attempt at farming. In vain offering a pig for sacrifice and learning the dialect of Qi, pulling up seedlings to make them grow faster, resulting in their withering, just like the man from the State of Song. With tax collectors at the door, no grain stored in the jar, and seeking employment under the eaves in spring. Suddenly regretting, regretting that one has always been a farmer, with hunger in it.

There is an old saying: with much money, one becomes a good merchant; why should I not be the same? Look at the young man dressed in fine clothes and riding a spirited horse, serving as a guard; or the middle-aged woman playing the zither and cittern, elegant and graceful. Just like a marquis, with gardens connected by the Qingshui River, a hundred-foot coral broken at a splendid banquet. Under the silver candles, there are unusual books on mathematics, and lawsuits over land. Our poverty is due to fate; these people are worth no more than a penny. Moreover, the foolish child He Qiao had no particular taste, and my brother Kong Lao had no particular connection. Why bother driving a carriage, when it's no different from slaughtering dogs? If wealth can be sought, it is shameful to hold a whip. Do not envy, for even the disciples of sages who engage in trade are still virtuous. "Writing on the shield boss, sealing the Xushu Pass, it is also a feat of great valor. Imagine the scene outside the city of surrender, where the frost is thick and the geese fly over the desert; on the banks of Heda Mountain, the moon shines over the dragon mound. Throwing down the pen in the army, abandoning the embroidered robe at the border, such a scholar is not easily talented. In the midst of laughter, see the head of Loulan tied, reporting victory on the platform. One day, the military office suspected and bound him, it is a great pity that he was taken away in the capital. Sigh at the high flag and large banner, the blue sky filled with gloomy clouds, pearls and yizhi berries, buried in a dark prison. The great tree falls, the Lan Tian people curse, the rabbit escapes east of the gate, returning home. From the beginning, it is better not to have studied, for the military is not the best path."

“Then what should be done? I shall follow the path of **Cè Sōngzǐ**, the immortal. **Miǎo Gū Xiānzǐ** will guide me with her jade staff, and as an exiled official, I shall sit on the frosty dragon. I shall laugh and drink the flowing clouds, and sleep drunk among the fragrant grass, gazing upon the weak waters of **Pénglái**. From now on, I shall master the secrets of the alchemical furnace and surpass **Fú Qiū**. The winds and rains of **Mào Líng** are fit for sorrow, and I shall be accompanied by the solitude of the green trees of **Lí Shān** in autumn. I lament those who have no cause, like **Hú Gōng** and **Guì Fù**; it is but a matter of course, the islands of **Fāng Zhàng** and **Yíngzhōu**. My strength is no longer that of youth, and old age is approaching; I have often erred in my pursuit of elixirs. I seek in vain, like **Wénchéng** on his horse or **Xúshì** in his small boat. “**Shū** comes before me, and since parting from you, I have become even more lonely. If you would forgive me, I shall reopen the old pavilion; if you doubt my words, I shall make a new alliance. If I were to repeat past mistakes, it would be as clear as the bright sun, and from now on, I shall rest with you in the humble grass huts. **Shū** laughs and says your words are excessive; listen to my humble opinion. Let us strive together toward the heavens, and not waste our time in idle criticism. If we were to enter the **Golden Gate** with our strategies, we would accompany you in picking up mustard seeds; if we were to offer poems at **Chángyáng**, we would accompany you in inscribing your name on the bridge. Upon returning, I shall seal you with silver mud and gold vaults, and we shall follow the path of virtue through the stages of life. I bow twice, and quickly invite you to the upper seats, respectfully holding the precious jade.”

A Sick Ghost Invites a Doctor from Caozhou, Ji Fuan, Who Was Originally a Cow Doctor. There was a wealthy man who fell ill with asthma and sought medical help without success. Ji Fuan treated him using the methods he used for treating cattle, and the treatment proved effective. He then boasted of being a renowned doctor and practiced his medical skills in the region between Qi and Lu. One day, while napping during the day, a servant came with an invitation. Ji Fuan did not ask who it was from and had the servant lead him there. Upon arriving at a hall, he saw several dozen people with pale faces and gaunt figures surrounding him, all coming to have their pulses taken. Ji Fuan looked at them carefully and realized they were not among his usual patients. Startled, he asked, "Is this the underworld?" The crowd replied, "Yes." Ji Fuan said, "If so, why have you invited me?" The crowd responded, "Doctor, you came to heal us, and now we hope you will help us leave." Ji Fuan reluctantly wrote a prescription. The crowd stared at it for a long time and said, "One dose may not be enough; please stay for two or three months." Ji Fuan wept and begged to return, but the crowd became angry, saying, "If this place is not suitable for living, why did you bring us here?" They all began to beat him. Ji Fuan also woke up in shock, feeling a slight pain on his left cheek. Upon checking, he found finger marks. The commentator said, "Using the methods for treating cattle on those with a 'cow-like' nature should yield remarkable results quickly. However, if one continues to use such advanced methods for simple tasks, alas, all living beings will suffer under the weight of such overkill."

In the Southern region, the musical and theatrical troupe of Wu was renowned for its performers who excelled in both appearance and talent, such as the famous Fat Zhang and Thin Xu, whose beauty was unparalleled at the time. The new generation of talented performers, though they had not seen the examples of their predecessors, still managed to captivate the audience with their slight advantages. For instance, Jin Dehui’s performance in “Finding a Dream” and Sun Bailin’s in “Farewell to the Shrine” were reminiscent of Jiang Caiping’s unique style in the Eastern Pavilion, characterized by a cold and reserved manner with a unique kind of sorrowful beauty. Zhu Xiaochun’s “Lamenting the Moon” and Ma Qiyu’s “Composing a Tune” were like Meng Dezhe’s elegant and graceful performances, maintaining a grand style. Zhang Lianfang’s “Yearning for the World” and Cao Yuating’s “Sweet Moment” were similar to Sun Jingyu’s unrestrained and free behavior, yet they remained natural and spontaneous. Wang Achang’s “Burying the Jade” and Zhou Er Guan’s “Splitting the Coffin” were like Xu Yuehua’s intense and emotional performances at the Qingyang Gate, where both sound and emotion were powerful. Dai Yunchong’s “Stealing the Chess” and Chen Rui’s “Stealing the Command” slightly exceeded the bounds, much like Zhao Feiyan’s bold and domineering behavior in the Zhaoyang Palace. A dance performed on the palm of the hand could change and surprise the senses. As for Zhang Xiulai’s “Yearning for Spring,” even though the beauty of Autumn Niang had faded, it still resembled the lively and playful scene of a thirteen or fourteen-year-old girl playing with coins. With the promotion of these few talented performers, the theater or performing arts could be somewhat maintained.

A performer from western Shu, Wei San'er, came to Wu, bringing with him lascivious sounds and seductive postures that intruded upon the music stage. The Lüantan troupe followed his example in a fawning manner, and even the members of the Kunqu troupe, who were supposed to be more disciplined, also imitated him. As a result, they were deeply influenced and corrupted, much like Kang Kunlun, the famous Tang dynasty musician, who had to abstain from playing instruments for ten years before he could teach, due to his mixed skill set and the corrupting elements he had acquired. I lament that literati lack conviction in the path, turning away from the correct learning and entering a wrong direction. Although they may gain praise for a time, they will ultimately be laughed at and disgrace themselves. Observing the state of the music department, one cannot help but feel disappointed and disheartened.

Duo said, "Using literature for play is the same as discussing literature through play. Singing the poem '晓风残月' by Li Yu (柳郎中), one might as well imitate Su Shi (苏学士) with a copper pipa and iron clapper, singing '大江东去' (The Great River Flows East)." North Side From Shaha Station to Pingyuan Twenty Li Road, there are countless earth-bound courtesans who have settled there. When I was a guest in Maizhou, I once wrote "The North Side's Crimson Spectrum." In the preface, I wrote, "Houses covered with white reeds, never having a building for swallows; beds made of yellow earth, rarely having silk curtains. A half spoonful of mud, tea for quenching thirst like that of Ma Changqing; a single ghostly fire, a candle burned high by Song Zijing." This was meant to be a criticism, serving as a warning to those who indulge in such pleasures. Once, while in the merchant's forest, I saw a poem inscribed on the wall of an inn, which was a gift to a courtesan named Di Lire: Her name has not been allowed to approach the flower garden, living in the eastern part of the lotus pond. She should be a former servant of the Zheng family, still being disgraced in the mud. Another poem inscribed on the wall of the inn was a gift to a courtesan named Hei Yahan: Several times dressed up by the ink pool, playing cute poses at the entrance of Wuyi Lane. Hard to find deep in the pear blossom grove, standing until the moon rises at dusk. The poetry is graceful and beautiful, but unfortunately, the person given to is not the right one. Later, when I was in the capital, I recounted this to Jin Jinshi, who laughed and said, "Wherever there is no talent, you should not look down on them. Remember at the temple in Beilizhi, I saw a woman named Gao Lanyu in the village. She had a graceful and beautiful appearance, could recite small poems by Cui Guofu, and her breath was like orchid, having the demeanor of Liu Caichun and Li Xiulan."

I am skeptical of its authenticity, so when I was heading south, I took a detour to visit, but it had already been bought by a wealthy merchant for a thousand gold pieces. Her sister, Xiu Zhen, presented a farewell poem to me, which read: Behind the curtain, the lingering light shines bright on the horse; the jade hairpin is inserted backward, and she sets off on a long journey. Near the silk skirt, the Qin zither music plays; it transforms into the sound of a flute in the mountains and passes. Thus, he sighed and said, "A woman of unparalleled beauty, originally from the north; since she has been among those with simple hairpins and coarse makeup, for several years, she has been forgotten and unrecognized. Then among the vendors of drinks and pastries, dog butchers, and fishers, how many talented people have not yet met their match?" I write this not to add to the allure of courtesans, but also to encourage those seeking talent not to follow the crowd. Duo said, "The land of Zhusu is known for producing beautiful women. But if she had gone to the eastern house first, then the story of Xi Shi returning would not be recorded on the washing stream. Thus, we understand the saying that things gather according to their kind. How many extraordinary men are buried in the dust of the world. Cow doctors and horse grinders mingle with the famous. Those who love talent like us, and yet end up below the wealthy merchants. It is also a shame." Poor children learn to flatter. During the Jiajing period, the Grand Secretary Yan wielded power arbitrarily. At night, he sat in his inner hall, and his adopted sons and foster children came to pay homage. He ordered them to enter, and they all knelt and approached. Upon entering, they bowed low to the ground, spoke sweet words, and competed to flatter him. He felt pleased and said, "If there is a vacancy for a Shilang, someone will fill it; if there is a vacancy for a Gijian, someone will fill it." The crowd bowed and thanked him. When they stood up, they moved left and right, and various postures were displayed.

A short while later, there was a rustling sound from the roof tiles. The crowd shouted and chased after the source of the noise, and one person lost their footing and fell to the ground. They lit a candle to look at him, and he was wearing tattered, patched clothing, standing dazed and speechless. The magistrate suspected him of being a thief and ordered him to be arrested and handed over to the authorities. The man knelt forward and said, "I am not a thief, I am a beggar!" The magistrate asked, "Since you are a beggar, how did you come here?" The beggar replied, "I have a hidden reason. If I am granted mercy, I wish to state my case and then die." The magistrate allowed him to explain. He said, "I am Zhang Lu, from Mengzhou. There is another beggar who goes by the name Qian Tuzu. In the spring, when merchants gather, wherever Qian Tuzu goes, people always give him money and rice. Although I sometimes get something, it is never as much as Qian. I asked him why. Qian said, 'We beggars have a flattering bone and a cunning tongue. You do not hit the mark, so how could you hope to match me?' I asked for guidance, but Qian firmly refused. Then I thought about the magistrate's household, where beggars who seek mercy in the darkness have their flattering bones and cunning tongues, which are ten times better than mine. That is why I came from afar, hiding to listen and peering through gaps for three months! Now, I have almost mastered it, but unfortunately, my presence was revealed. I hope for your great mercy and leniency." The magistrate was startled, and then he turned to the crowd and laughed, saying, "Beggars also have their ways. Your flattering bones and cunning tongues are truly the teachers of such people!" The crowd nodded in agreement. The magistrate then pardoned his crime, ordered the crowd to lead the beggar away, and had him taught day and night in rotation. Within a year, he had learned and returned, having surpassed Qian Tuzu.

Zuo said, "Zhang Lu learned from Yan, the Grand Councillor. If Yan, the Grand Councillor, had no teacher, who was his teacher? He said he learned from Yan, the Grand Councillor. The list of officials and nobles from the Ming Dynasty is just a record of beggars' origins. How strange that Zhang Lu, who himself has such a lineage, has another branch." A Scholar Humiliated by His Pride Zhong Han Tong Jun Yin Nian is a friend of mine who passed the imperial examinations in the same year. One day, he visited my study and laughed, saying, "Heroes deceive others, and famous people have habits, but sometimes they end up humiliating themselves." When I asked him about it, he said, "Yesterday, I was traveling on Wu Mountain and encountered rain. I sought shelter at a rural farmer's house. An old man came out with a fan and asked me to write something. I thought little of him and scribbled carelessly. I mistakenly wrote 'turtle' instead of 'stove,' and I wrote 'peacock' backwards. I thought the rural fool didn't understand the meaning of the words and didn't correct my mistakes immediately. I falsely attributed the writing to Huang, a fellow scholar who had passed the imperial examinations. The old man held the fan and laughed, saying, 'I have always been puzzled by the lack of a perfect match for "wine stove." Now, the words "tea" and "turtle" on the fan, are they not a perfect match?' He then examined the fan for a long time and said, 'The village fool was never well-read and doesn't know what "sparrow hole" is. Perhaps it's something like "Geng Cang" or "Lao Bo." Then he bowed respectfully and said, 'Your talent and reputation are enough to crown the universe. Why did you attribute it to Huang, when you could have written your own name? Calling him by his name still requires careful consideration, for he is actually my student.' Hearing his words, his face was drenched in sweat. He asked for the old man's name, family, and place of residence, and learned that the old man was a famous scholar from Zhejiang who had been living in Wu for ten years."

I heard this and was also taken aback. I recorded this as a warning against the frivolous behavior of talented individuals. The bell said: "Is there anyone in the world who can be taken lightly? The young man looks down on the elderly, but the elderly look down on the young even more. He is only a nominal scholar, so his ingrained habits are as they are. Both can serve as warnings." The Spirit Oracle of General Bo's Temple The temple of General Bo on Jade Peak had the most active worship. At the age of nine, I took the child examination, and by the age of fourteen, I still could not pass it. Following my father's command, I prayed at the foot of a cliff. I received an oracle that said: "After much sorrow and complaint with no way to appeal, all matters are out of sync. Only until the Mid-Autumn Festival, when I see the full moon, will I be able to return you to your original state." Below the line about success, it was noted: "If someone with the name '口木' (a placeholder for a name) helps, one's life will be smooth and successful." My father said: "Based on the meaning of this oracle, success will not be achieved this time!" At that time, the supervisor of studies was Mr. Liu Shian. My first essay was initially selected but then rejected due to the use of obscure phrases from the *Li Sao* (离骚). My father said: "This oracle indicates misfortune. If you want to enter the school, you must wait until the autumn exam." Later, Mr. Li Yinpei, the supervisor of studies in Jiangsu, held the exam in mid-March, which caused my father's concern. In the subsequent exam, the topic was "He who has seen the sea finds it hard to appreciate lesser waters," up to "The nature of flowing water." There happened to be the words "明月" (moon) in the text, and I was recognized and admitted. My ranking also matched my father's. The prophecy of "seeing the moon and returning to your original state" was fulfilled in this instance.

During the provincial examination of the year of the Earth Rat (戊子), the presiding examiners were Wáng Gōng Jìhuá and Guó Gōng Zhù. My essay was initially designated as the top scorer in the classical literature section, but due to the vigorous language in my essay on administrative governance, Wáng Gōng feared it might hinder the rigorous examination process. Guó Gōng strongly argued for my advancement, and I was ultimately ranked thirty-first among those who passed. My father remarked, "The saying about the 'mouth and wood' names has proven true today!" For Rén Gōng was named after the wood character, and Guó Gōng was named after the mouth character. Afterward, when I took the metropolitan examination, I repeatedly failed to pass. The examiners had no names that matched the earlier prediction. However, the teachers who recommended my essays, such as Kē Gōng Jǐn, Guān Gōng Bǎo, and Lǐ Gōng Zhōngjiǎn, all provided clear and verifiable support. Now that I am over forty years old, I no longer carry my books to the imperial palace. Even if the examiners were exactly as predicted, I would have no hope of achieving high status. This is not because my writing has failed me, but because I have failed the prediction of the fortune-teller. Unfulfilled hopes remain, but my ambitions are easily shattered, and my life as an ordinary person comes to an end. How sad! Dù said: "When I was in Wūyuán, I was ordered to go to the Jīnling Book Bureau. On the way, I passed by the Temple of Hú Gōng and drew a lot, which ended with the phrase, 'A significant event will occur in Yangzhou.' I thought the question and answer did not match, and laughed it off. Just as I arrived in Jinling, a letter of appointment from the salt administration official, Quán Gōng, came. The governor-general commissioned me to go to Yangzhou to compose new *Yuefu* poems. Only then did I believe that the gods do not speak in jest. However, there were no signs in the mirror, and the green-clad prophecy was broken. I suppose my life is full of misfortunes, and I am not worth making idle remarks about it."

**A False Dream at the Shrine of Governor Kuang** A certain student from Wujia, about to take the northern imperial examination, accompanied several friends to seek a dream at the shrine of Governor Kuang. Throughout the night, he tossed and turned, unable to sleep. The next day, the friends recounted their dreams, some favorable and some unfavorable, speculating on their meanings. One friend boasted, "Last night, I dreamed that I came to this hall, and Governor Kuang stood up and bowed me to the seat of honor, showing me deep respect as if I were his superior. I humbly declined. He said, 'In the future, you will rise to the position of governor or viceroy, surpassing me in rank, so do not be overly modest.' He ordered his attendants to change my attire. On the seat, there were boxes of seals and orders of command, arranged in neat rows. I felt very uneasy and left the seat, descending the steps. Governor Kuang bowed three times and saw me out the door, and I suddenly woke up, startled. I do not know what auspicious sign this might be!" The others raised their hands in congratulations, saying, "You will surely achieve great wealth and status in the future. This year, you will surely excel, just getting started." The friend replied, "If I indeed fulfill my dream, I will help each of you who have fallen into hardship." Soon after, he went to the capital for the examination, but failed the first test, feeling disheartened and wanting to return home. However, his funds were exhausted. The capital is a place where many talented people gather, and as a student, he could not find a teaching position, leaving him without means to support himself, torn between staying and leaving. Fortunately, he had enjoyed singing popular songs since childhood, and in desperation, he joined the Cuiqing troupe as a male lead.

One day, during a performance of the play *Fifteen Strings of Cash*, when the act titled "Meeting the Capital" was being performed, a man in official robes sat on the stage, with seals and arrows arranged around him. An actor portraying a magistrate entered and bowed deeply, just as the man had done in a dream the previous day. Unconsciously, the man patted the table and wept bitterly. The audience was puzzled by his sudden outburst and summoned him to inquire about the cause. He told them the truth. They then learned that the man was also from an old noble family in Jiangnan and had taken the imperial examinations but failed to pass. His uncle, Langfeng, a historian, provided him with travel expenses and sent him back to Suzhou. Zhuo said, "The books on dream interpretation from the Zhou dynasty were destroyed by the Qin dynasty's burning of books. Afterward, people like Guo Qiaoqing and Zhou Xuan each made judgments based on their own opinions. The dream of the riverbank is recorded in the *Song History*, and the dream of falling off the bed is mentioned in the *Tang History*. In the field, the crops disappeared, but in a dream, the prime minister Pau had a bad omen that turned out to be good. In the hall, looking into a mirror, the governor Cui had a good omen that turned out to be bad. Others, such as Cao Han dreaming of crabs, Zhang Zhan dreaming of mortars, Li Di dreaming of mustaches, and Han Jun dreaming of wooden clogs, are all scattered in various writings, each with its own unique signs. Yet, throughout history, countless people have dreamed, so why are only these few dreams recorded? It is known that the rest did not come true, but these dreams, which were fabricated, were all accurate without any error! The *I Ching* says, 'Good and bad fortune, regret, and shame arise from action.' It seems that the heart is the first sign, not the dream itself that brings about the illusion!"

The County Magistrate Afraid of His Wife A certain county magistrate, known for his coarse and vulgar nature, had been in office for twelve years with absolutely no accomplishments, relying solely on fawning over his superiors. A fellow townsman, who served as a governor, came to visit. When the magistrate paid his respects, his colleagues were present. The magistrate immediately announced his name at the main gate, crawled on his knees to the main hall, and bowed his head hundreds of times, causing his forehead to rise like a large egg. After bowing, he secretly placed gold and jewels under the seat and remained prostrate. The governor became angry. The magistrate then raised his head and said, "Sir, you are my father, and I am your son; if you are displeased, you can scold me." The governor's anger intensified, saying, "You have gone too far in deceiving me!" He threw the gold and jewels to the ground and ordered the magistrate to leave. The colleagues tried to mediate. The governor said, "You do not understand; he is not flattering me, but actually mocking me!" The colleagues were puzzled. The governor explained, "I know him well as a fellow townsman, and he has a well-known fear of his wife. Every morning, he wears formal attire outside his bedroom door and bows to ask for her well-being. After washing and grooming, he crawls forward in a submissive posture to the dressing area, bows to the ground dozens of times, his voice resonating like a drum, and then takes out gold and jewels to offer as hairpins and earrings. If she shows any displeasure, he holds a staff with both hands and presents it, calling out, 'Please scold me, madam.' Only after being scolded does he tremble and leave. The scene I just witnessed was eerily similar, as if he was treating me like a toy! How could it not make one's hair stand on end?" Everyone's faces turned pale in shock.

The Official laughed and said, “Do you all also have this habit? From now on, strictly uphold the authority of the husband and avoid inviting official censure. The art of flattery is only sufficient to bring disgrace!” The others nodded in agreement and withdrew. Duo said, “The rules of decorum and protocol are being used as a stage for performance in the public hall — no wonder seeking honor leads only to disgrace. In the past, Huan Fan made his wife kneel as if before the Three Dukes, but he did not bow to Lord Lu. People said he had a proud bone, but I say he had a sense of shame.” Lady of Mischief Xiao, a young man from Lanxi, was seventeen years old when he married a wife surnamed Xing, who was both beautiful and talented. She spent her days in the inner chamber, drawing her eyebrows and arranging her hair, and thus abandoned her studies. One day, he saw a small silk curtain placed beside the mirror, and inside was a young girl with a child’s hairstyle, bright eyes, and a beautiful face — graceful and beautiful beyond compare. Xiao asked where she had come from. Xing laughed and said, “This is the one I purchased for you with ten measures of pearls.” Xiao replied teasingly, “I am honored by your noble intentions — I should send her to the desk to hold the inkstone, but why then confine her in the perfumed chest, watching you apply yellow powder and green makeup every day?” Xing laughed and ordered the servant girl to move her into the study.

One night, the supervisor ordered him to study late into the night. The scholar reluctantly entered his study, lit the lamp, and held the book, placing a lady in a gauze canopy on the desk. He said, "The night seems endless, so I trouble you to accompany me in reading. If A娇 were to descend, I would store her in a golden house." In an instant, the lady emerged from behind the screen and laughed, saying, "The scholar is too dainty and lazy; he has barely picked up the book and already has romantic thoughts!" The scholar looked at her and saw that she was no different from the lady in the gauze canopy. He laughed and said, "Is it true that Cui Hui has humbled herself to descend?" He quickly moved forward to embrace her. The lady blushed and pushed him away, saying, "Do not make such a move suddenly. I am a secretary in the inner palace, and you were also a literary official in your previous life. Heaven fears that you will be drowned in the pleasures of the inner chamber, abandoning your reputation and achievements, so it has sent me to come at night to oversee your clear lessons." The scholar replied, "I have my own reputation and achievements, but if I could just touch the fragrance, I would strive for the blue clouds to repay your great virtue." The lady said, "Hasty lover, will the land of pleasure keep a tab on your debts? I make a pact with you: from now on, if you take one step forward, then plan for a night of pleasure. Otherwise, useless words are of no benefit." The scholar still wanted to force the matter, but suddenly there was a cough sound from under the window, and the lady fled from behind the screen.

From this point, the student began to study diligently under a curtain. That year, he entered the local school, and at night, a young lady came. She smiled and said, "A skilled hand at picking flowers, now tested." The student was delighted and enjoyed the encounter, asking about the next meeting. The young lady replied, "Awaiting the good news in the autumn, we will make green hemp clothes together. Those with ambition should strive to do so." The student became even more dedicated to his studies and passed the local examination that year. The young lady came again to celebrate, saying, "Since we met in the spring, menstrual blood no longer comes. If she gives birth in the morning or evening, how can she return to the register of immortals? If you achieve success in the medical field, I will serve you day and night with my personal belongings!" The student was overjoyed and studied even more diligently.

The following year, he once again succeeded in the imperial examinations at Nan Gong, passed the palace examination, and was appointed as a Hanlin. He was granted leave to return to the south. As soon as he entered the door, Xing greeted him on the upper hall with a colorful and ornate appearance, holding a baby in her arms. He asked who the child was. Xing smiled and said, "This is your virtuous heir, the heir of the scholar-official." She then called out another woman and asked, "Do you recognize the secretary of the imperial library?" He was surprised and asked why. Xing smiled without answering. The woman revealed the truth. It turned out that Xing was afraid he would neglect his studies, so she bought a beautiful woman and devised a clever plan to encourage him. The woman's charming and persuasive speech was taught to her by Xing herself. He was grateful for Xing's help in achieving success and moved the beautiful woman to the side of the mirror, saying, "To record my faults and to commend the virtuous." The commentator said, "Planting grass that bears sons and climbing flowers to pass the examination—without the ingenious and interconnected plans of the woman in the inner chambers, one might end up being a self-absorbed and idle person! What a foolish child, to have such extraordinary good fortune!" **Lü Xian Bao** Cao from Fashan Yang, a man of literary fame but with a particularly proud and arrogant disposition, traveled to Jinling to take the imperial examinations. He stayed temporarily at the temple of Lü Zǔ. Rising early to read, he first burned incense before the altar of Lü Zǔ and said, "I am a disciple of impure bone, living in the presence of the divine. The sounds of recitation day and night are noisy, please do not blame me."

One night, exhausted and lying on his desk, he saw a Taoist arrive and say, "Scholar, you're working too hard; your prosperous scholar's robe is lost this time!" Cao rose to greet him. The Taoist spoke eloquently, reciting the Five Classics, history, and the *Han Shu* (History of the Han Dynasty) as if pouring water from a bottle. Cao was amazed. The Taoist said, "When I was pursuing my career, I too studied diligently under the curtain, and with modest success, I became a Jinshi (imperial scholar). Now I have abandoned it. Recalling the midnight hours holding a book, the cold wind tearing my robe, the dim light touching the pillar, it feels like a dream from yesterday." Cao respected the Taoist as a respected scholar and asked for his advice. The Taoist read just two lines and then left. Cao said, "Did my writing offend your eyes?" The Taoist laughed and replied, "It's precisely because it cannot offend the day that I do not wish to see it. Your writing style closely resembles that of the Two Capitals, and even the lesser works are like those of Han Yu and Su Shi. If you use this to seek fame and fortune, it's like holding Shang ritual vessels and Xia tripods and selling them in the markets of the five capitals; they are not without value, but few can appreciate them!" He then took a book from his sleeve and said, "This is the treasure raft for passing the exams; please study it carefully." Cao hurriedly took it to read and found it to be full of things he had previously rejected and avoided. He became angry and said, "We write to either preserve our legacy or achieve fame in our time. To gain the examination degree so easily, I would rather die in the East Sea! How could I follow your advice?" The Taoist said, "Ha! You have a proud bone; if it is not removed, you cannot be saved." He quickly drew his sword and struck Cao's head, removing a bone and leaving. Cao was in great pain and suddenly woke up, seeing a book left on the desk, which he placed aside.

The next day, he rose and read his old drafts, but found them unsatisfactory. Selecting a book from the table, he recited it, greatly pleased, pondering day and night, feeling very satisfied. Subsequently, he entered the examination hall, and all the essays he wrote conformed to its style. When the list of successful candidates was posted, he was ranked first. One night, he dreamt of a Taoist coming again, who inserted a bone into his skull and left. Upon waking, he looked at his hometown ink, and his face turned red, his back drenched in sweat like rain. He continued to study ancient texts, and eventually passed the imperial examinations as a filial and upright official. The bell said, "Liu Fen failed the examination, yet a young man became famous. How many talented scholars, often criticized the authorities for this. It is necessary to experience it oneself, to close their mouths, and take away their spirit. Once the proud spirit is removed, it is better than removing the tongue." The Bodhisattva's compassionate ferry. Xie Mocai's sister from Qichang, widowed at a young age, swore to remain chaste like a pine boat, gave up meat and ate only vegetarian food, and daily worshiped the Bodhisattva's image. A fisherwoman entered the door, sat and talked for a long time, and hurriedly left behind a fish basket and went away.

On the seventh day of the fifth month in the year Wu Shen, a sudden and violent flood surged, causing widespread destruction to ten thousand households. The woman, considering herself without a chance of survival, suddenly saw a fish basket as large as several encirclements and quickly climbed into it. Drifting with the waves, she arrived unexpectedly at a place surrounded by purple bamboo and fragrant flowers strewing the path. Struggling to reach the shore, she looked back at the fish basket, which suddenly became small again, and then carried it with her. She saw a bodhisattva adorned with jewelry hanging with pearls, seated cross-legged on a lotus seat, who said, "Have you come? I feared you would suffer a great calamity because of your youthful efforts, so I used my compassionate ferry to save you." The woman bowed to the ground to express her gratitude. The bodhisattva then commanded the dragon maiden to wash her body with water from the willow branch and gave her new clothes made from red flowers and green leaves. She was led to a hall, which was divided into two courts on either side. The eastern court was called "Filial Piety and Chastity," and the western court was called "Talented and Virtuous." The woman first entered the "Talented and Virtuous" court, where she saw a bun-haired woman named Meng Guang, a book-holding woman named Ban Zhao, a wine carrier named Ji Que's wife, and a woman with a rectangular skirt and carrying a jar named Hao Xuan's wife, Huan Shi. There was also a woman reciting poetry without stopping, who closed her book and smiled warmly when she saw the woman, and they talked about their family backgrounds. This woman was Zhaoyun. She then entered the "Filial Piety and Chastity" court, where she saw a woman holding a jade named Zhen Jiang, a nose-cutting woman named Liang's chaste wife, a song-singing woman named Tao Ying, a face-destroying woman named Huai Qing Ba Shi, and young women with hair but not yet buns, such as Di Ying and Cao E. In the midst of sighing and lamenting, the dragon maiden said, "The bodhisattva appears as a woman to teach the Dharma, first emphasizing filial piety and chastity, then talented and virtuous, and daily using the compassionate raft to save people from the sea of suffering.
